topics.title: Sources
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEFTT744 - FOOD INDUSTRY WASTE MANAGEMENT
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Sources

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->

# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

In the context of the **KTU 2024 Scheme** syllabus for **PEFTT744 - Food Industry Waste Management**, the term **"Sources"** refers to the systematic identification, classification, and quantification of all points along the **agri-food supply chain** at which edible and inedible residual biomass is generated. These sources span the entire chain from primary agricultural production, post-harvest handling, industrial processing, distribution, retail, and final household/service consumption.

Formally, food industry waste sources are categorized under three hierarchical tiers:

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition (Module 1)**
> *Sources of food industry waste include all stages of the food chain: agricultural production, post-harvest losses, food processing (dairy, meat, poultry, fisheries, fruits & vegetables, cereals, oilseeds, beverages, sugar, confectionery), food service establishments, wholesale & retail markets, and household consumption. Each source produces a characteristic waste profile defined by its moisture content, organic load, biodegradability, and polluting potential.*

### Three-Tier Source Classification (Syllabus Anchor)

| Tier | Source Category | Representative Examples | Nature of Waste |
|------|----------------|------------------------|-----------------|
| **Primary** | Agricultural Production | Crop residues, farmyard manure, animal excreta, on-field losses | Lignocellulosic, fibrous, high-volume |
| **Secondary** | Food Processing Industries | Whey, peelings, trimmings, blood, bones, spent grains, molasses | Organic, high BOD/COD, nutrient-rich |
| **Tertiary** | Distribution & Consumption | Supermarket culls, canteen leftovers, household food scraps, market refuse | Mixed, heterogeneous, often perishable |

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-World Analogy — "The Food's Journey and Its Shadows"**
>
> Imagine a loaf of bread. The journey begins in a wheat field (leaving behind straw, stubble, and chaff — *primary waste*). It travels to a flour mill (generating bran, germ, and dust — *secondary waste*). It reaches a bakery (yielding dough trimmings and burnt loaves). It is sold at a supermarket (where stale bread is discarded — *tertiary waste*). Finally, it is consumed at home (with crusts, leftovers, and expired slices entering municipal waste). Every "shadow" cast by the bread at each stage is a **source of food industry waste**. Food Industry Waste Management is essentially the science of intercepting and valorizing these shadows.

### 1.3 Standard Metrics for Characterizing Waste Sources

The following engineering metrics are used universally in KTU-endorsed problem statements to quantify and compare waste sources:

- **Biochemical Oxygen Demand (BOD):** Mass of dissolved oxygen (in mg or ppm) consumed by microorganisms during biological oxidation of organic matter over **5 days at 20 °C**.
- **Chemical Oxygen Demand (COD):** Mass of oxygen (in mg/L) consumed during chemical oxidation of both biodegradable and non-biodegradable matter.
- **Total Suspended Solids (TSS):** Dry mass (in mg/L) of solids retained on a filter of pore size **1.5 µm**.
- **Total Solids (TS):** Sum of dissolved and suspended solids, expressed in % w/w or mg/L.
- **Moisture Content:** Expressed as percentage of wet weight basis.
- **pH:** Hydrogen ion concentration indicating acidic/alkaline nature of waste.
- **BOD₅ : COD Ratio:** Index of biodegradability; ratio > 0.5 indicates highly biodegradable waste.
- **C : N : P Ratio:** Carbon, Nitrogen, Phosphorus balance critical for biological treatment.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Board Examiner Insight**
> In 14-mark analytical questions, students are expected to quote typical **BOD₅** values (e.g., dairy whey ≈ 40,000–60,000 mg/L; fruit processing waste ≈ 2,000–10,000 mg/L; slaughterhouse blood ≈ 200,000 mg/L) to demonstrate conceptual depth. Memorize at least 2–3 benchmark values for each major source.

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->

# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Detailed Classification of Food Industry Waste Sources

### 2.1.1 Primary Sources — Agricultural & Farm-Level

Agricultural operations generate the **largest mass fraction** of food industry waste by sheer volume, although not always the most polluting per unit mass.

**(a) Crop Production Residues**
- **Field crop residues:** Straw, stubble, husks, stover, cobs (rice, wheat, maize, sugarcane bagasse, groundnut shells).
- **Horticultural residues:** Prunings, trimmings, fallen leaves, orchard biomass.
- **Post-harvest losses:** 5–40% of harvested produce in developing economies, attributed to inadequate cold chain, pest damage, and mechanical injury.

**(b) Animal Husbandry Waste**
- **Solid manure:** Cattle dung, poultry litter, pig slurry.
- **Liquid effluents:** Wash water from dairies, abattoirs, piggeries, and poultry processing units.
- **Slaughterhouse by-products:** Blood, bones, hides, paunch contents, offal.

### 2.1.2 Secondary Sources — Food Processing Industries

Each sub-sector of the food processing industry has a distinct waste signature:

| Sub-Sector | Solid Waste Examples | Liquid Effluent Characteristics |
|------------|--------------------|---------------------------------|
| **Dairy** | Whey, curd fines, butter residues | High BOD (40,000–60,000 mg/L), high fat, white colour |
| **Fruits & Vegetables** | Peels, seeds, pomace, trimmings, pulp | High BOD, acidic pH (3.5–5.0), suspended solids |
| **Meat & Poultry** | Blood, bones, feathers, viscera, offal | Very high BOD/COD, pathogenic, dark red colour |
| **Fisheries & Seafood** | Heads, scales, fins, shells, viscera, wash water | High salinity, protein-rich, fishy odour |
| **Cereals & Milling** | Bran, germ, husk, broken grains, dust | Low moisture, lignocellulosic, high BOD if wet |
| **Oilseeds & Vegetable Oils** | Oil cakes, meal, soapstock, deodorizer distillate | High BOD, oily, emulsified |
| **Sugar Industry** | Bagasse, molasses, pressmud, spent wash | Very high BOD (40,000–50,000 mg/L), acidic |
| **Beverages (Breweries & Distilleries)** | Spent grain, hops, yeast, trub, distillery spent wash | Extremely high BOD, dark brown colour |
| **Confectionery & Bakery** | Dough trimmings, burnt products, syrup waste | High BOD, high sugar, sticky nature |
| **Starch Industry** | Starch residues, gluten, fibre, process water | High BOD, high SS, white cloudy effluent |

### 2.1.3 Tertiary Sources — Distribution, Retail & Consumption

- **Wholesale markets:** Sorting rejects, damaged produce, overripe fruits.
- **Retail (supermarkets, kirana stores):** Aesthetic rejects, expired packaged foods, unsold perishables.
- **Food service sector:** Hotels, restaurants, canteens, cloud kitchens, airlines, catering — plate waste, cooking oil, expired ingredients.
- **Households:** Plate waste, expired food, vegetable peels, cooking oil — the largest urban solid waste contributor in many cities.

## 2.2 Theoretical Framework for Source Quantification

The total waste generated by a food industry is governed by the principle of **mass balance**, which states that mass entering a process must equal mass leaving as product, waste, or by-product:

$$\text{Mass In} = \text{Mass of Product} + \text{Mass of Waste} + \text{Mass of By-Product}$$

For an industry processing input mass $M_{in}$ and yielding product mass $M_p$, the **waste generation rate** is:

$$M_w = M_{in} - M_p - M_{by}$$

The **waste-to-product ratio** is a standard benchmark used in KTU problems:

$$R_{wp} = \frac{M_w}{M_p}$$

For example, a fruit juice industry typically shows $R_{wp} \approx 0.30 - 0.50$ (i.e., 30–50% of input mass exits as solid waste — peels, seeds, pomace).

## 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet (Cheat Sheet)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Master these equations for KTU 2024 Part B numerical/analytical questions.**

| # | Formula / Concept | Symbolic Form | Engineering Use | Units |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Mass Balance | $M_{in} = M_p + M_w + M_{by}$ | Quantification of waste from any source | kg/day or tonnes/day |
| 2 | Waste-to-Product Ratio | $R_{wp} = M_w / M_p$ | Benchmarking of process inefficiency | dimensionless |
| 3 | BOD₅ | $\text{BOD}_5 = \dfrac{DO_i - DO_f}{\text{Sample fraction}}$ | Organic pollution potential of liquid waste | mg O₂/L |
| 4 | COD | $\text{COD} = \dfrac{(V_b - V_s) \times N \times 8 \times 1000}{V}$ | Total oxidizable organic load | mg O₂/L |
| 5 | Biodegradability Index | $BI = \text{BOD}_5 / \text{COD}$ | Indicates amenability to biological treatment | dimensionless (0–1) |
| 6 | Moisture Content (wet basis) | $MC_{wb} = \dfrac{W_w - W_d}{W_w} \times 100$ | Determines drying requirements | % w.b. |
| 7 | Moisture Content (dry basis) | $MC_{db} = \dfrac{W_w - W_d}{W_d} \times 100$ | Engineering drying calculations | % d.b. |
| 8 | C:N:P Ratio | $\text{C:N:P} = C\% : N\% : P\%$ | Composting / anaerobic digestion design | ratio |
| 9 | Daily Waste Quantity | $Q = P \times G \times C$ | Urban/agricultural planning | tonnes/day |
| 10 | Per Capita Waste | $\text{PCW} = \dfrac{Q}{N_{pop}}$ | Household waste estimation | kg/person/day |

Where:
- $M_{in}, M_p, M_w, M_{by}$ = Input, Product, Waste, By-product mass
- $DO_i, DO_f$ = Initial and final Dissolved Oxygen
- $V_b, V_s, V$ = Volumes of blank, sample, and titrant
- $N$ = Normality of titrant (FAS)
- $W_w, W_d$ = Wet and dry weight
- $P, G, C$ = Population, generation rate, collection efficiency
- $N_{pop}$ = Population served

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-World Engineering Utility:** These equations are foundational in designing **Effluent Treatment Plants (ETP)**, **Composting yards**, **Anaerobic Digesters (CSTR / UASB)**, and **Municipal Solid Waste Management (MSWM) systems** for Kerala municipalities and food processing clusters (e.g., Pala, Kanjirappally, Idukki spice processing).

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->

# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: Waste-to-Product Ratio from Mass Balance

This derivation demonstrates how a KTU 14-mark Part B question is typically structured and solved.

**Problem Statement (Typical KTU Style):**
*A fruit processing unit receives 10,000 kg/day of raw mangoes. It produces 6,000 kg/day of pulp and recovers 1,000 kg/day of by-products (seed kernel, concentrate). Calculate:*
*(a) The daily solid waste generated.*
*(b) The waste-to-product ratio.*
*(c) The percentage of input mass recovered as product.*

### Step 1: Apply the Mass Balance Equation

The general mass balance around any food processing operation states that:

$$M_{in} = M_p + M_w + M_{by}$$

This is a direct statement of **conservation of mass** (Lavoisier's principle) — no mass is created or destroyed during processing; it is merely partitioned.

### Step 2: Substitute Known Values

We are given:
- $M_{in} = 10{,}000 \text{ kg/day}$
- $M_p = 6{,}000 \text{ kg/day}$
- $M_{by} = 1{,}000 \text{ kg/day}$
- $M_w = ?$

Substituting:

$$10{,}000 = 6{,}000 + M_w + 1{,}000$$

### Step 3: Solve for Waste Mass

$$M_w = 10{,}000 - 6{,}000 - 1{,}000$$

$$\boxed{M_w = 3{,}000 \text{ kg/day}}$$

This represents peels, trimmings, and rejected damaged fruits.

### Step 4: Compute the Waste-to-Product Ratio

$$R_{wp} = \frac{M_w}{M_p} = \frac{3{,}000}{6{,}000}$$

$$\boxed{R_{wp} = 0.50}$$

This implies that for every 1 kg of pulp produced, 0.50 kg of solid waste is generated.

### Step 5: Compute Product Recovery Percentage

$$\% \text{ Recovery} = \frac{M_p}{M_{in}} \times 100 = \frac{6{,}000}{10{,}000} \times 100$$

$$\boxed{\% \text{ Recovery} = 60\%}$$

### Step 6: Compute Waste Fraction

$$\% \text{ Waste} = \frac{M_w}{M_{in}} \times 100 = \frac{3{,}000}{10{,}000} \times 100 = 30\%$$

$$\% \text{ By-product} = \frac{M_{by}}{M_{in}} \times 100 = 10\%$$

**Verification:** $60\% + 30\% + 10\% = 100\%$ ✓ (Mass balance check satisfied)

## 3.2 Derivation: BOD₅ of a Dairy Effluent

**Problem:** A dairy plant discharges 500 m³/day of whey-diluted effluent. The initial DO of a diluted sample (1:50) is 8.0 mg/L, and after 5 days of incubation at 20 °C the DO is 3.0 mg/L. Calculate BOD₅ of the undiluted effluent.

**Given:**
- $DO_i = 8.0$ mg/L
- $DO_f = 3.0$ mg/L
- Dilution factor (DF) = 50

### Step 1: Apply the BOD Equation

$$\text{BOD}_5 = (DO_i - DO_f) \times DF$$

### Step 2: Compute the DO Depletion

$$\Delta DO = DO_i - DO_f = 8.0 - 3.0 = 5.0 \text{ mg/L}$$

### Step 3: Multiply by the Dilution Factor

$$\text{BOD}_5 = 5.0 \times 50$$

$$\boxed{\text{BOD}_5 = 250 \text{ mg/L}}$$

### Step 4: Contextual Interpretation

This is a moderately strong organic effluent — well above the Indian CPCB discharge limit of **30 mg/L for BOD** (as per **Environment Protection Rules, 1986**, Schedule VI for food industry effluents discharging to inland surface waters). Therefore, the dairy must install an ETP with primary sedimentation, biological treatment (e.g., UASB or activated sludge), and tertiary polishing.

## 3.3 Analytical Matrix: Waste Sources vs. Pollution Potential

| Source | Waste Type | Typical BOD₅ (mg/L) | Moisture (%) | C:N Ratio | Pollution Severity |
|--------|------------|---------------------|--------------|-----------|-------------------|
| Fruit & Vegetable Processing | Solid + Liquid | 2,000–10,000 | 80–95 | 20:1 – 40:1 | Moderate |
| Dairy | Liquid (whey) | 40,000–60,000 | 93–97 | 25:1 – 30:1 | Very High |
| Meat & Slaughterhouse | Solid + Liquid | 20,000–40,000 | 70–90 | 5:1 – 10:1 | Very High |
| Fisheries | Solid + Liquid | 15,000–25,000 | 80–90 | 8:1 – 12:1 | High |
| Sugar Mill | Liquid (spent wash) | 40,000–50,000 | 90–95 | 20:1 – 25:1 | Very High |
| Distillery (Spent Wash) | Liquid | 50,000–1,00,000 | 92–96 | 18:1 – 22:1 | Extremely High |
| Brewery | Solid (spent grain) | 25,000–30,000 | 80–85 | 15:1 – 20:1 | High |
| Bakery & Confectionery | Solid + Liquid | 8,000–15,000 | 60–80 | 30:1 – 50:1 | Moderate |
| Oil Mill (Solvent Extraction) | Liquid | 15,000–25,000 | 80–90 | 25:1 – 35:1 | High |
| Household / Municipal | Solid | 500–2,000 | 50–70 | 20:1 – 30:1 | Low–Moderate |

# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Block Diagram: Sources of Food Industry Waste Across the Supply Chain

The following Mermaid diagram maps the complete flow of food from production to consumption, with associated waste sources highlighted at each node. This block-level functional flow is the most effective schematic for KTU 14-mark questions on "Sources."

    I[Liquid Effluent Streams]:::waste
    J[Air Emissions and Odour]:::waste

    H --> K

# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 PART A — 3 Mark Questions (Short Answer)

> **KTU 2024 Regulation Note:** Part A consists of short-answer questions of 3 marks each, expected to be answered in 4–6 lines. Focus on precision and use of standard terminology.

### Question 1 `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`

**Q: Differentiate between primary, secondary, and tertiary sources of food industry waste. Give one example for each.**

**Model Answer:**
Primary sources are located at the **agricultural production stage** (e.g., crop residues, farmyard manure, on-field post-harvest losses). Secondary sources arise at the **food processing stage** (e.g., whey from dairies, pomace from fruit juice units, blood from slaughterhouses). Tertiary sources occur at the **distribution, retail, food service, and household consumption stages** (e.g., supermarket culls, canteen plate waste, kitchen vegetable peels). The classification helps in targeting waste minimization and recovery strategies at the appropriate level of the supply chain.

### Question 2 `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`

**Q: List any four sources of food processing industry waste and the type of waste generated from each.**

**Model Answer:**
1. **Dairy industry** → Whey (liquid, high BOD), curd fines (solid).
2. **Fruit and vegetable processing** → Peels, seeds, pomace (solid), process wash water (liquid).
3. **Sugar mills** → Bagasse, molasses (solid), spent wash (liquid, very high BOD).
4. **Meat and poultry** → Blood, bones, feathers, offal (solid), slaughterhouse effluent (liquid).

---

## 5.2 PART B — 14 Mark Questions (ESE Module Internal Choice)

> **KTU 2024 ESE Pattern:** Part B questions carry 14 marks each, with sub-parts of (a) 7 marks and (b) 7 marks. Students must answer ONE of two internal choice questions. The expected answer length is 6–8 pages per question.

---

### Question A (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`

**Q: (a) Classify the various sources of food industry waste with a neat block diagram. Discuss the role of each source in overall waste generation in India. (7 Marks)**

**(b) A fruit processing industry processes 8,000 kg/day of pineapple. It produces 4,500 kg/day of canned slices, 800 kg/day of juice concentrate, and the rest is solid waste. Calculate the daily solid waste generated, the waste-to-product ratio, and the percentage recovery of usable products. Comment on the disposal strategies suitable for the solid waste. (7 Marks)**

#### Model Solution for (a):

Food industry waste sources are classified into three major categories based on the stage of the supply chain at which they originate:

**1. Primary Sources (Agricultural Production):**
These include crop residues (straw, stubble, bagasse), animal husbandry waste (manure, urine), and on-field post-harvest losses. In India, agricultural waste constitutes approximately **350 million tonnes annually**, of which a large portion is burnt in the fields of Punjab, Haryana, and Uttar Pradesh, contributing to severe air pollution.

**2. Secondary Sources (Food Processing Industries):**
These include waste from dairy (whey — 200 million tonnes globally), fruit and vegetable processing (peels, pomace — 30–50% of raw input), sugar mills (bagasse — 3 tonnes per tonne of cane), distilleries (spent wash — 15 L per litre of alcohol), and meat/slaughterhouses (blood, bones).

**3. Tertiary Sources (Distribution & Consumption):**
These include wholesale/retail market waste (aesthetic rejects), food service waste (hotels, restaurants, canteens), and household food waste. India generates approximately **68 million tonnes of food waste annually** at the household level, according to the UN Environment Programme.

A block diagram (refer to Section 4.1) maps all these sources along the supply chain. Each source contributes differently — primary sources contribute by **volume**, secondary by **pollution load (BOD/COD)**, and tertiary by **urban solid waste burden**.

#### Model Solution for (b):

**Given:**
- $M_{in} = 8{,}000$ kg/day (pineapple)
- $M_p = 4{,}500$ kg/day (canned slices)
- $M_{by} = 800$ kg/day (juice concentrate)
- $M_w = ?$

**Step 1: Mass Balance**

$$M_{in} = M_p + M_{by} + M_w$$

$$8{,}000 = 4{,}500 + 800 + M_w$$

$$M_w = 8{,}000 - 5{,}300 = 2{,}700 \text{ kg/day}$$

**Step 2: Waste-to-Product Ratio**

$$R_{wp} = \frac{M_w}{M_p} = \frac{2{,}700}{4{,}500} = 0.60$$

**Step 3: Percentage Recovery**

$$\% \text{ Recovery} = \frac{M_p + M_{by}}{M_{in}} \times 100 = \frac{5{,}300}{8{,}000} \times 100 = 66.25\%$$

$$\% \text{ Waste} = \frac{2{,}700}{8{,}000} \times 100 = 33.75\%$$

**Step 4: Disposal / Valorization Strategies**

Pineapple processing waste (peels, core, crown, trimmings) is rich in **cellulose, hemicellulose, bromelain enzyme, and sugars**. Suitable strategies include:
- **Anaerobic digestion** for biogas production (C:N ratio suitable after co-digestion with cow dung).
- **Bromelain extraction** for pharmaceutical/nutraceutical use.
- **Composting** to produce organic fertilizer.
- **Pectin extraction** from peels for food industry use.
- **Drying and pelletization** for animal feed.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Callout:** Students commonly lose 2–3 marks by: (1) Not showing the full mass balance with all three components (product + by-product + waste); (2) Forgetting to comment on disposal strategies in part (b) — KTU requires a concluding engineering opinion, not just numerical computation; (3) Using inconsistent units (kg vs tonnes) without conversion.

---

### Question B (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`

**Q: (a) Discuss in detail the various sources of food processing industry waste, with specific reference to dairy, fruit & vegetable, and slaughterhouse sectors. Tabulate the typical characteristics (BOD, pH, moisture, C:N ratio) of waste from each. (7 Marks)**

**(b) A sugar mill produces 250 tonnes of sugarcane bagasse per day with a moisture content of 50% (w.b.). It is proposed to combust this bagasse in a boiler with 18% excess air. Calculate: (i) the dry mass of bagasse available per day, (ii) the actual mass of air supplied, and (iii) the flue gas volume at NTP. Assume bagasse composition: C = 45%, H₂ = 6%, O₂ = 43%, ash = 6% (all on dry basis). (7 Marks)**

#### Model Solution for (a):

Food processing industries generate waste in three physical forms — **solid, liquid, and gaseous**. The major sub-sectors contributing to waste generation in India are:

**1. Dairy Industry:**
Waste sources include whey (cheese manufacture), buttermilk (butter processing), curd fines, and CIP (clean-in-place) wash water. Whey has a very high BOD of **40,000–60,000 mg/L**, moisture above **93%**, pH around **5.8–6.4**, and C:N ratio of approximately **25:1 to 30:1**. Disposal of untreated whey causes severe oxygen depletion in receiving waters.

**2. Fruit & Vegetable Processing Industry:**
Waste includes peels, seeds, trimmings, juice pulps, and process wash water. Typical BOD is **2,000–10,000 mg/L**, moisture 80–95%, pH acidic (**3.5–5.0** due to fruit acids), and C:N ratio of **20:1 to 40:1**. High seasonality and heterogeneity are defining features.

**3. Slaughterhouse & Meat Processing:**
Waste includes blood, paunch contents, bones, hides, offal, feathers, and blood-rich wash water. BOD is extremely high at **20,000–40,000 mg/L**, pH is near-neutral to slightly alkaline (**6.8–7.5**), moisture 70–90%, and C:N ratio is low at **5:1 to 10:1** (high nitrogen — rich in protein). Pathogenic contamination is a major concern.

**Comparative Table:**

| Parameter | Dairy | Fruit & Vegetable | Slaughterhouse |
|-----------|-------|-------------------|----------------|
| BOD₅ (mg/L) | 40,000–60,000 | 2,000–10,000 | 20,000–40,000 |
| pH | 5.8–6.4 | 3.5–5.0 | 6.8–7.5 |
| Moisture (%) | 93–97 | 80–95 | 70–90 |
| C:N Ratio | 25:1 – 30:1 | 20:1 – 40:1 | 5:1 – 10:1 |
| Dominant Waste | Whey | Peels, pomace | Blood, bones |

#### Model Solution for (b):

**Given:**
- Wet bagasse mass = 250 tonnes/day
- Moisture content (w.b.) = 50%
- Excess air = 18%
- Bagasse composition (dry basis): C = 45%, H₂ = 6%, O₂ = 43%, ash = 6%

**Step 1: Calculate the Dry Mass of Bagasse**

$$MC_{wb} = \frac{W_w - W_d}{W_w} \times 100$$

$$50 = \frac{250 - W_d}{250} \times 100$$

$$W_d = 250 \times (1 - 0.50) = 125 \text{ tonnes/day}$$

**Step 2: Calculate Component Masses in Dry Bagasse**

$$m_C = 125 \times 0.45 = 56.25 \text{ t/day}$$
$$m_{H_2} = 125 \times 0.06 = 7.50 \text{ t/day}$$
$$m_{O_2} = 125 \times 0.43 = 53.75 \text{ t/day}$$
$$m_{ash} = 125 \times 0.06 = 7.50 \text{ t/day}$$

**Step 3: Stoichiometric Oxygen Requirement**

The combustion reaction for C and H₂ are:

$$C + O_2 \rightarrow CO_2$$
$$H_2 + 0.5 O_2 \rightarrow H_2O$$

The oxygen from the fuel itself partially satisfies the requirement. Available internal O₂ = 53.75 t/day.

Molar basis conversion (tonnes to kmol):
- $n_C = 56.25 / 12 = 4.6875$ kmol/day
- $n_{H_2} = 7.50 / 2 = 3.7500$ kmol/day
- $n_{O_2,\text{fuel}} = 53.75 / 32 = 1.6797$ kmol/day

Oxygen required for C combustion: $4.6875$ kmol/day
Oxygen required for H₂ combustion: $3.75 / 2 = 1.875$ kmol/day
Total O₂ required: $4.6875 + 1.875 = 6.5625$ kmol/day

Net external O₂ required: $6.5625 - 1.6797 = 4.8828$ kmol/day

Convert to mass: $4.8828 \times 32 = 156.25$ tonnes O₂/day

**Step 4: Theoretical and Actual Air**

Mass of air = Mass of O₂ / 0.232 (since air contains 23.2% O₂ by mass)

$$M_{air,theoretical} = \frac{156.25}{0.232} = 673.49 \text{ t/day}$$

Actual air with 18% excess:

$$M_{air,actual} = 673.49 \times 1.18 = 794.72 \text{ tonnes/day}$$

**Step 5: Flue Gas Volume at NTP**

Using ideal gas law at NTP: 1 kmol occupies 22.414 m³.

Moles of CO₂ produced: $4.6875$ kmol/day
Moles of H₂O produced: $3.750$ kmol/day
Moles of N₂ from air: $4.8828 \times (79/21) \times 1.18 = 21.70$ kmol/day
Moles of excess O₂: $4.8828 \times 0.18 = 0.879$ kmol/day

Total flue gas moles $\approx 31.02$ kmol/day

Volume of flue gas at NTP:

$$V_{flue} = 31.02 \times 22.414 = 695.3 \text{ m}^3\text{/day}$$

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Callout:** In combustion problems, students commonly: (1) Forget to subtract the internal oxygen of the fuel — this causes a major error of ~25% in air requirement; (2) Use molecular weight of air as 28.97 vs 29 (negligible, but indicative of rigour); (3) Confuse wet-basis and dry-basis moisture content. Always **re-state the basis** explicitly at the start of your solution.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid-Revision Checklist — Module 1: Sources of Food Industry Waste**

- **Three-Tier Source Classification:** Primary (agricultural), Secondary (processing), Tertiary (distribution & consumption). **Memorize this hierarchy.**

- **Dairy industry waste is the most polluting per unit volume** due to whey (BOD ~50,000 mg/L). 

- **Distillery spent wash is the single highest BOD effluent** in the food sector (~50,000–1,00,000 mg/L).

- **C:N ratio is critical:** Slaughterhouse waste is low C:N (5–10), ideal for direct composting/AD. Fruit waste is high C:N (30–40), requires nitrogen supplementation (urea, poultry litter).

- **Mass balance equation** $M_{in} = M_p + M_w + M_{by}$ is the foundation of every KTU numerical. Always define all variables with units before substituting.

- **Typical waste-to-product ratios to memorize:**
  - Fruits & Vegetables: **0.30 – 0.50**
  - Sugar (cane → sugar): ~3.0 (bagasse-to-sugar ratio)
  - Dairy (milk → cheese): ~9.0 (whey-to-cheese ratio)
  - Oilseeds (seed → oil): ~1.5 (cake-to-oil ratio)

- **Moisture content formulas:** Always specify whether **wet basis (w.b.)** or **dry basis (d.b.)** is being used. The two are related by:

$$MC_{db} = \frac{MC_{wb}}{1 - MC_{wb}}$$

- **Biodegradability index BOD₅/COD:** Ratios above **0.5** indicate highly biodegradable waste (food industry wastes are typically 0.6–0.8).

- **CPCB Discharge Limits (Schedule VI, EPR 1986) — memorize for ETP design questions:**
  - BOD: **30 mg/L** (inland surface water), **100 mg/L** (sewer), **500 mg/L** (land irrigation)
  - SS: **100 mg/L**, **600 mg/L**, **2000 mg/L** respectively
  - pH: **6.5 – 9.0**

- **Kerala Context:** Spice processing (cardamom, pepper, turmeric) clusters in **Idukki, Kanjirappally, Kumily** generate characteristic solid waste (dust, rejected berries) suitable for **essential oil extraction** and **composting**.

- **FAOSTAT & UNEP data points to quote in 14-mark answers:** India generates ~**68 million tonnes** of food waste annually; ~**40%** of food in developing economies is lost between harvest and retail; global food waste contributes ~**8–10%** of greenhouse gas emissions.

- **Diagram requirement:** Always draw a **flow chart or block diagram** of the food chain with waste sources at each node — KTU examiners award 1–2 marks for well-labelled diagrams.

- **Excess air in combustion problems:** Bagasse boilers typically operate at **15–25% excess air**; 18% is a standard assumption. Always state your assumption.

- **C and H₂ combustion stoichiometry** must be written explicitly in solutions. The internal O₂ content of the fuel is a *commonly forgotten* term.

> **End of Module 1 — Sources | PEFTT744 | KTU 2024 Scheme**

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Classification and characterization of food industry waste from different industries - fruit and vegetable processing industry
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PEFTT744 - FOOD INDUSTRY WASTE MANAGEMENT > - **Module:** Module 1 > - **Topic:** Classification and characterization of food industry waste from different industries - fruit and vegetable processing industry # 🍎 Module 1 — Classification & Characterization of Food Industry Waste ## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ### 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU Syllabus Terminology) > **Food Industry Waste** is defined as any residual organic or inorganic matter generated during the receiving, processing, packaging, storage, and distribution stages of food raw materials into finished consumer products. According to **Codex Alimentarius** and **US-EPA (40 CFR 261.2)** frameworks adopted in KTU syllabus, such waste is broadly classified based on **physical state, biological origin, compositional chemistry, and biodegradability index**. **Fruit and Vegetable Processing Industry Waste (FVPW)** specifically refers to the by-product streams arising from unit operations such as sorting, washing, peeling, coring, deseeding, juicing, blanching, pasteurization, and packaging of horticultural produce. This waste stream accounts for approximately **25–60 % of the total raw material weight**, depending on the crop and processing technology deployed. > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU 2024 Highlight:** Fruit & vegetable processing waste is categorized as **"biodegradable, high-moisture, high-organic municipal-analogous industrial waste"** under the KTU Module 1 framework. The characterization follows **APHA Standard Methods (23rd Edition)** and **BIS 10425** protocols for physico-chemical analysis. --- ### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Real-World Intuition Imagine you bring home **10 kg of fresh mangoes** from the market. After your family finishes eating the juicy pulp, what remains? - The **skin (peel)** — outermost fibrous layer - The **stone (seed/kernel)** — hard inner seed - Some **leftover pulp fibers (pomace)** stuck to the peel - A little **sticky juice dripped** on the kitchen counter That entire leftover mass — peel, seed, pomace, wash water, juice spillage — **is exactly what a fruit processing factory produces in tonnes per hour.** Now scale this up: A single **mango juice plant processing 100 tonnes/day of raw mango** generates roughly **35–45 tonnes/day** of solid peel-seed-pomace waste plus **50,000–80,000 litres** of high-strength wash water and pulp wash effluent. This is the waste stream we must classify, characterize, and eventually valorize. > [!NOTE] > **Intuitive Takeaway:** Food industry waste is essentially **"misplaced biomass"** — the same nutritional molecules (sugars, pectin, polyphenols, oils) that exist in the edible portion also exist, sometimes in *higher concentrations*, in the discarded portion. Waste management aims to either **recover, recycle, or responsibly dispose** of this biomass. --- ### 1.3 Master Classification Framework (Hierarchical Tree) ``` FOOD INDUSTRY WASTE (FIW) │ ├── (A) By Physical State │ ├── Solid Waste (peels, seeds, trimmings, pomace, stones) │ ├── Liquid Waste (wash water, juice spillage, condensate) │ └── Semi-Solid / Sludge (clarifier sludge, centrifuge cake) │ ├── (B) By Source Industry │ ├── Fruit & Vegetable Processing Industry ← FOCUS OF THIS TOPIC │ ├── Dairy & Milk Processing Industry │ ├── Meat & Poultry Processing Industry │ ├── Cereal & Grain Milling Industry │ ├── Sugar & Confectionery Industry │ ├── Beverage & Brewing Industry │ └── Seafood Processing Industry │ ├── (C) By Biodegradability │ ├── Biodegradable (>90 % organic) — FVPW falls HERE │ └── Non-Biodegradable (packaging films, metals) │ ├── (D) By Hazard Category │ ├── Hazardous (pesticide-laden, solvent-bearing) │ └── Non-Hazardous (general FVPW biomass) │ └── (E) By Moisture Content ├── High Moisture (>70 % w.b.) — fresh fruit trimmings ├── Medium Moisture (40–70 %) └── Low Moisture (<40 %) — dried pomace, seed cake ``` > [!NOTE] > **Constants & Standards to Memorize:** > - **COD** = Chemical Oxygen Demand (mg O₂/L) > - **BOD** = Biochemical Oxygen Demand (mg O₂/L), typically measured at **5 days, 20 °C (BOD₅)** > - **TS** = Total Solids; **VS** = Volatile Solids > - **BOD/COD ratio** for FVPW generally lies between **0.4 – 0.8**, indicating high biodegradability. # 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ## 2.1 Fruit & Vegetable Processing Industry — Process Flow Mapping The fruit and vegetable processing chain typically follows this sequence: ``` Raw Material Reception → Sorting/Grading → Washing → → Peeling/Coring → Slicing/Dicing/Cutting → → Blanching/Cooking → Juicing/Extraction → → Filtration/Clarification → Pasteurization → → Packaging → Storage ``` Each unit operation generates a **distinct waste stream** with characteristic properties. ### 2.2 Waste Stream Table — Industry-Wise Characterization | **Industry / Process** | **Major Waste Stream** | **Solid Yield (% raw wt.)** | **Moisture (% w.b.)** | **Dominant Component** | |:---|:---|:---:|:---:|:---| | **Mango Juice / Pulp** | Peel, Stone, Pomace | 35–50 % | 60–75 % | Carbohydrates, Pectin | | **Citrus Juice (Orange)** | Peel, Pulp, Seeds, Membrane | 45–60 % | 70–82 % | Pectin, Limonene, Flavonoids | | **Apple Juice / Cider** | Pomace (peel + core + seed) | 25–35 % | 70–80 % | Pectin, Polyphenols, Fiber | | **Tomato Paste / Ketchup** | Skin, Seeds, Pulp, Stem | 10–15 % | 75–85 % | Lycopene, Proteins | | **Potato Processing (Chips/Fries)** | Peel, Trimmings, Slivers | 30–50 % | 75–90 % | Starch, Cellulose | | **Banana Processing** | Peel, Rejected Pulp | 35–40 % | 80–85 % | Starch, Pectin, Tannins | | **Pineapple Canning** | Core, Skin, Trimmings | 50–60 % | 80–85 % | Bromelain, Cellulose | | **Grape Wine / Juice** | Stems, Skin, Seeds (Marc) | 20–30 % | 60–75 % | Polyphenols, Tartaric Acid, Oils | | **Onion / Garlic Dehydration** | Skin, Outer Scales, Trimmings | 15–25 % | 70–80 % | Sulfur Compounds, Quercetin | | **Coconut Processing** | Husk, Shell, Water Effluent | 65–85 % | 50–65 % | Lignin, Coir Fiber, Oil | > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU 2024 — Frequently Tested:** Citrus peel and grape pomace are **highly acidic** (pH 3.0–4.5) and high in bioactive compounds, making them **dual-target wastes** — pollution load + nutraceutical recovery opportunity. --- ## 2.3 Detailed Compositional Analysis The chemical composition of FVPW determines both its **pollution potential** and **valorization potential**. Below is the master composition template. ### 2.3.1 Proximate Composition (% dry basis) | **Component** | **Range in FVPW** | **Significance** | |:---|:---:|:---| | **Total Carbohydrates** | 40–75 % | Main pollution source (high BOD/COD) | | **Dietary Fiber** (Cellulose + Hemicellulose + Lignin + Pectin) | 20–50 % | Bioactive, prebiotic potential | | **Crude Protein** | 2–12 % | Lower than animal waste, but valuable | | **Lipids / Oils** | 1–25 % (high in seeds) | Seed oil extraction opportunity | | **Ash (Minerals)** | 2–10 % | K, Ca, Mg, Fe, P rich | | **Polyphenols / Pigments** | 0.1–5 % | Antioxidant, nutraceutical | | **Pectins** | 5–30 % | Thickener, gelling agent | | **Moisture (fresh basis)** | 60–90 % | High → rapid microbial spoilage | ### 2.3.2 Physico-Chemical Characterization Parameters | **Parameter** | **Symbol** | **Unit** | **Typical FVPW Range** | **Test Method** | |:---|:---|:---:|:---:|:---| | pH | pH | — | 3.5 – 7.0 | APHA 4500-H⁺ | | Moisture Content | M.C. | % w.b. | 60 – 90 | AOAC 934.01 | | Total Solids | TS | mg/L or % | 1.5 – 8.0 % | APHA 2540 B | | Volatile Solids | VS | % of TS | 80 – 95 | APHA 2540 E | | Biological Oxygen Demand | BOD₅ | mg/L | 1,000 – 10,000 | APHA 5210 B | | Chemical Oxygen Demand | COD | mg/L | 2,500 – 25,000 | APHA 5220 D | | Total Suspended Solids | TSS | mg/L | 500 – 5,000 | APHA 2540 D | | Total Kjeldahl Nitrogen | TKN | mg/L | 50 – 500 | APHA 4500-N | | Total Phosphorus | TP | mg/L | 10 – 100 | APHA 4500-P | | Oil & Grease | O&G | mg/L | 50 – 500 | APHA 5520 B | | Total Phenolics | — | mg GAE/L | 20 – 200 | Folin-Ciocalteu | | Conductivity | EC | mS/cm | 2 – 8 | APHA 2510 B | --- ## 2.4 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet | **Formula** | **Equation (LaTeX-safe)** | **Application** | |:---|:---|:---| | **Moisture Content (% w.b.)** | $M.C.\% = \dfrac{W_w - W_d}{W_w} \times 100$ | Fresh wet biomass characterization | | **Dry Matter Yield** | $DM\% = 100 - M.C.\%$ | Estimating dry biomass per tonne raw | | **Volatile Solids %** | $VS\% = \dfrac{W_{ds} - W_{vs}}{W_{ds}} \times 100$ | Organic fraction estimation | | **BOD₅ / COD Ratio (Biodegradability Index)** | $BI = \dfrac{BOD_5}{COD}$ | Predicts biological treatability (BI > 0.5 → easily biodegradable) | | **COD Equivalence of Waste** | $COD_{eq} = \dfrac{m_{substrate} \times \text{Factor}}{V_{effluent}}$ | Convert kg substrate → kg O₂ demand | | **Theoretical Methane Yield (BMP)** | $Y_{CH_4} = \dfrac{0.35 \times (COD_{added} - COD_{effluent})}{V}$ | Anaerobic digester design (m³ CH₄/m³·d) | | **COD of Pure Carbohydrate** | $COD_{CHO} = \dfrac{Mass_{CHO} \times 1.067}{g}$ | Stoichiometric: C₆H₁₂O₆ + 6 O₂ → 6 CO₂ + 6 H₂O | | **Pectin Yield (Citrus Peel)** | $\eta_{pectin} = \dfrac{m_{dry\ pectin}}{m_{dry\ peel}} \times 100$ | Resource recovery efficiency | | **Total Phenolic Content** | $TPC = \dfrac{A_{sample} \times C_{std} \times V_{extract}}{A_{std} \times m_{sample}}$ | mg GAE per g dry weight | | **Heat of Combustion (Approx.)** | $HHV \approx 0.35 \times VS\% + 1.7$ (MJ/kg VS) | Calorific value for combustion/incineration | > [!WARNING] > **Pitfall Alert:** Never write `|x|` inside markdown table cells. Use the LaTeX vertical bar escape `\vert` or `\mid` instead. --- ## 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility - **Effluent Treatment Plant (ETP) Design:** COD and BOD₅ values from characterization directly feed into **activated sludge reactor sizing** using $V = \dfrac{Q \times (S_o - S)}{X \cdot \mu_m/Y}$. - **Anaerobic Digester Sizing:** Volatile Solids loading drives digester volume. - **Composting Process Design:** C:N ratio (computed from TKN & organic carbon) determines bulking agent requirement. - **By-product Recovery Economics:** Pectin yield, polyphenol content, and oil yield determine **payback period** of recovery units. - **Regulatory Compliance:** CPCB/SPCB effluent discharge norms (e.g., **COD ≤ 250 mg/L** for inland discharge) make characterization mandatory. > [!NOTE] > **Engineering Insight:** A 100 TPD mango processing unit discharging 60 m³ effluent/day with **COD = 8,000 mg/L** produces a daily pollution load equivalent to a township of **~15,000 people** in domestic sewage terms — highlighting the disproportionate environmental footprint of FVPW. # 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Worked Numerical Examples ## 3.1 Derivation: Theoretical Oxygen Demand of Glucose The complete biochemical oxidation of **D-glucose (C₆H₁₂O₆)** proceeds stoichiometricically as: $$ C_6H_{12}O_6 + 6\,O_2 \rightarrow 6\,CO_2 + 6\,H_2O $$ **Step 1 — Molecular Weights** $$ M_{glucose} = 6(12) + 12(1) + 6(16) = 180\ \text{g/mol} $$ $$ M_{O_2} = 2 \times 16 = 32\ \text{g/mol} $$ **Step 2 — Stoichiometric Oxygen Demand** $$ \text{O}_2\ \text{required} = 6\ \text{mol} = 6 \times 32 = 192\ \text{g} $$ **Step 3 — Oxygen Demand per gram of Glucose** $$ COD_{glucose} = \frac{192\ \text{g O}_2}{180\ \text{g glucose}} = 1.067\ \text{g O}_2/\text{g glucose} $$ **Step 4 — Generalized Expression for any Carbohydrate (CₐHᵦOᵧ)** $$ COD_{theoretical} = \frac{(2a + 0.5b - \gamma) \times 16}{M_w} \quad \text{[g O}_2/\text{g substrate]} $$ This is the **fundamental basis** for converting waste carbohydrate mass into equivalent oxygen demand — the core unit of pollution measurement. --- ## 3.2 Worked Example: Pollution Load Calculation > **Problem:** A fruit juice plant processes **50 tonnes/day** of mixed fruit (40 % apple + 35 % orange + 25 % pineapple). The process generates **30 tonnes/day solid pomace** (75 % moisture) and **40 m³/day liquid effluent** (avg. COD = 6,500 mg/L). Compute: > (a) Daily dry pomace mass > (b) Daily COD load in effluent (kg O₂/day) > (c) BOD₅ load assuming BOD/COD = 0.6 > (d) Equivalent population load (per capita BOD = 60 g/day) ### Solution **(a) Dry Pomace Mass** $$ M_{dry} = M_{wet} \times \left(1 - \frac{M.C.}{100}\right) $$ $$ M_{dry} = 30{,}000\ \text{kg} \times \left(1 - \frac{75}{100}\right) = 30{,}000 \times 0.25 = 7{,}500\ \text{kg/day} $$ **(b) Daily COD Load** $$ COD_{load} = Q_{effluent} \times COD_{conc} $$ $$ COD_{load} = 40\ \text{m}^3/\text{day} \times 6{,}500\ \text{mg/L} $$ Convert m³ to L: $40\ \text{m}^3 = 40{,}000\ \text{L}$ $$ COD_{load} = 40{,}000\ \text{L} \times 6{,}500\ \text{mg/L} = 2.6 \times 10^{8}\ \text{mg/day} $$ $$ COD_{load} = 260\ \text{kg O}_2/\text{day} $$ **(c) BOD₅ Load** $$ BOD_5 = 0.6 \times COD = 0.6 \times 260 = 156\ \text{kg O}_2/\text{day} $$ **(d) Equivalent Population Load** $$ P_{eq} = \frac{BOD_5\ \text{load}}{\text{per capita BOD}} = \frac{156{,}000\ \text{g/day}}{60\ \text{g/day/person}} $$ $$ P_{eq} = 2{,}600\ \text{persons (population equivalent)} $$ > [!NOTE] > **Interpretation:** A 50 TPD fruit juice plant imposes a **BOD pollution load equivalent to 2,600 people** every single day. This dramatic number is the basis of all FVPW environmental impact assessments. --- ## 3.3 Step-by-Step: Estimation of Anaerobic Biogas Potential (Buswell Equation) The **Buswell equation** gives the theoretical methane yield from any organic substrate of known elemental composition: $$ C_aH_bO_cN_dS_e + \left(a - \tfrac{c}{2} - \tfrac{3d}{4} - \tfrac{e}{2}\right)H_2O \rightarrow $$ $$ \left(\tfrac{a}{2} - \tfrac{c}{8} + \tfrac{d}{4} - \tfrac{e}{4}\right)CH_4 + \left(\tfrac{a}{2} + \tfrac{c}{8} - \tfrac{3d}{8} + \tfrac{e}{4}\right)CO_2 + d\,NH_3 + e\,H_2S $$ **Example — Orange Peel** (approx. elemental formula: C₆H₁₀O₅, basis 162 g/mol): - $a = 6,\ b = 10,\ c = 5,\ d = 0,\ e = 0$ $$ \text{Water moles} = 6 - \tfrac{5}{2} - 0 - 0 = 3.5 $$ $$ CH_4\ \text{moles} = \tfrac{6}{2} - \tfrac{5}{8} = 3 - 0.625 = 2.375 $$ $$ CO_2\ \text{moles} = 3 + 0.625 = 3.625 $$ Per 162 g orange peel organic matter → **2.375 mol CH₄** at STP (22.4 L/mol): $$ V_{CH_4} = 2.375 \times 22.4 = 53.2\ \text{L CH}_4\ \text{per 162 g OM} $$ $$ Y_{CH_4} = \frac{53.2\ \text{L}}{162\ \text{g}} = 0.328\ \text{L CH}_4/\text{g OM} $$ $$ Y_{CH_4} \approx 328\ \text{m}^3\ CH_4/\text{tonne}\ OM\ \text{(theoretical)} $$ > [!IMPORTANT] > **Real digesters achieve only 60–80 % of theoretical yield** due to incomplete hydrolysis, cell wall recalcitrance, and microbial limitations. --- ## 3.4 Analytical Matrix — Waste Category vs. Treatment Pathway | **Waste Sub-type** | **Dominant Compound** | **Recommended Valorization Route** | **KTU Exam Keyword** | |:---|:---|:---|:---| | Citrus Peel | Pectin, Limonene, Flavonoids | Pectin extraction → Limonene recovery | "Pectin-rich substrate" | | Mango Kernel | Starch, Fat (8–10 %) | Starch → Fat → Cosmetics | "Kernel valorization" | | Grape Pomace | Polyphenols, Tartaric acid, Seed oil | Extraction, Nutraceuticals | "Polyphenol-rich waste" | | Apple Pomace | Pectin, Polyphenols, Fiber | Pectin + Dietary fiber | "Pectin + polyphenols" | | Tomato Skin | Lycopene, Cellulose | Lycopene extraction | "Lycopene source" | | Potato Peel | Starch, Phenolics | Biogas / Animal feed | "Starch-rich peel" | | Pineapple Core/Bran | Bromelain, Cellulose | Bromelain enzyme recovery | "Enzyme-rich waste" | | Onion Skin | Quercetin, S-compounds | Quercetin nutraceutical | "Quercetin source" | | Coconut Husk | Lignin, Coir fiber | Coir, Activated carbon | "Lignocellulosic" | | Banana Peel | Starch, Pectin, Tannin | Bioethanol, Pectin | "Cellulosic ethanol" | # 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics ## 4.1 Block Diagram — Fruit Processing Waste Generation Chain ```mermaid flowchart TD A[Raw Fruit Reception] --> B[Sorting & Grading] B --> C[Washing] C --> D[Peeling / Coring / Deseeding] D --> E[Slicing / Pulping / Juicing] E --> F[Pasteurization / Concentration] F --> G[Packaging & Storage] B --> B1[Reject fruit
5-10% raw wt.] C --> C1[Wash water
high BOD/COD effluent] D --> D1[Peel
15-30% raw wt.] D --> D2[Seeds / Stones
5-15% raw wt.] D --> D3[Core / Trimmings
5-10% raw wt.] E --> E1[Pomace / Pulp residue
10-25% raw wt.] F --> F1[Spent water / Condensate
high COD] G --> G1[Packaging rejects
non-biodegradable] B1 --> H[FVPW Collection Hub] C1 --> H D1 --> H D2 --> H D3 --> H E1 --> H F1 --> H H --> I{Valorization Decision} I --> J[Animal Feed] I --> K[Composting] I --> L[Anaerobic Digestion
Biogas] I --> M[Bioactive Extraction
Pectin / Polyphenols / Oil] I --> N[Incineration
with energy recovery] I --> O[Landfill
least preferred] style A fill:#ffd166,stroke:#333 style H fill:#ef476f,stroke:#333,color:#fff style I fill:#06d6a0,stroke:#333 style O fill:#073b4c,stroke:#333,color:#fff ``` --- ## 4.2 Classification Hierarchy — Mermaid Tree ```mermaid flowchart TD Root[Food Industry Waste] Root --> A[By Physical State] Root --> B[By Source Industry] Root --> C[By Biodegradability] A --> A1[Solid
peels, seeds, pomace] A --> A2[Liquid
wash water, spillage] A --> A3[Sludge
clarifier cake] B --> B1[Fruit & Vegetable] B --> B2[Dairy] B --> B3[Meat & Poultry] B --> B4[Cereal Milling] B --> B5[Beverage] B --> B6[Seafood] C --> C1[Biodegradable
BOD/COD greater than 0.4] C --> C2[Non-Biodegradable
plastics, metals] B1 --> B1a[Mango] B1 --> B1b[Citrus] B1 --> B1c[Apple] B1 --> B1d[Tomato] B1 --> B1e[Pineapple] B1 --> B1f[Banana] style Root fill:#118ab2,stroke:#333,color:#fff style B1 fill:#06d6a0,stroke:#333 ``` --- ## 4.3 Characterization Workflow Block Diagram ```mermaid flowchart LR S1[Sample Collection
Composite Sampling] --> S2[Pre-treatment
Drying / Grinding / Sieving] S2 --> S3[Physico-chemical Tests] S3 --> P1[Moisture, TS, VS] S3 --> P2[pH, EC] S3 --> P3[COD, BOD] S3 --> P4[TKN, TP] S3 --> P5[Oil & Grease] S3 --> P6[Total Phenolics] P1 --> Q[Data Analysis
& Comparison] P2 --> Q P3 --> Q P4 --> Q P5 --> Q P6 --> Q Q --> R[Decision Matrix] R --> R1[Composting Feasible
C/N 25-30] R --> R2[AD Feasible
BI greater than 0.5] R --> R3[Extraction Feasible
High Pectin or Polyphenols] R --> R4[Animal Feed
Check Toxins] style S1 fill:#ffd166,stroke:#333 style Q fill:#ef476f,stroke:#333,color:#fff style R fill:#06d6a0,stroke:#333 ``` --- ## 4.4 Waste Management Hierarchy (5R Model) ```mermaid flowchart TD R5[Reduce
Source Minimization] --> R4[Reuse
Direct Reapplication] R4 --> R3[Recycle
Material Recovery] R3 --> R2[Recover
Energy Recovery
Biogas / Incineration] R2 --> R1[Dispose
Landfill / Safe Discharge] style R5 fill:#06d6a0,stroke:#333 style R4 fill:#52b788,stroke:#333 style R3 fill:#95d5b2,stroke:#333 style R2 fill:#ffd166,stroke:#333 style R1 fill:#ef476f,stroke:#333,color:#fff ``` # 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap --- ## 📝 PART A — Short Answer Questions (2 × 3 Marks) ### **Q1.** `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 Style]` **Classify food industry waste. Briefly describe the various categories with at least two examples each.** **Model Answer:** Food industry waste is classified into multiple categories based on different criteria: 1. **By Physical State:** - **Solid waste** — fruit peels, seeds, pulp, trimmings (e.g., apple pomace, orange peel) - **Liquid waste** — wash water, condensate, juice spillage - **Sludge/semi-solid** — clarifier cake, centrifuge residue 2. **By Source Industry:** - Fruit & vegetable processing, dairy, meat & poultry, cereal milling, beverage, seafood 3. **By Biodegradability:** - **Biodegradable** (BOD/COD > 0.4) — peels, pomace - **Non-biodegradable** — plastic packaging, metal cans 4. **By Moisture Content:** High (>70 %), medium (40–70 %), low (<40 %) > *Word limit: 100–120 words. Marks awarded for crisp categorization + 2 examples per category.* --- ### **Q2.** `[KTU University Exam – June 2024 Style]` **List any six physico-chemical parameters used for the characterization of fruit and vegetable processing waste. State the typical range for any three.** **Model Answer:** | **S.No.** | **Parameter** | **Typical Range** | |:---:|:---|:---:| | 1 | pH | 3.5 – 7.0 | | 2 | Moisture Content | 60 – 90 % | | 3 | Total Solids (TS) | 1.5 – 8.0 % | | 4 | BOD₅ | 1,000 – 10,000 mg/L | | 5 | COD | 2,500 – 25,000 mg/L | | 6 | Total Kjeldahl Nitrogen (TKN) | 50 – 500 mg/L | > *Mention APHA standard methods wherever possible for extra marks.* --- ## 📝 PART B — Long Answer Questions (ESE Module Choice: 2 × 14 Marks) > **INTERNAL CHOICE:** Answer **either** Question A **or** Question B in full. --- ### **Question A (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2022 Style]` **(a)** Explain in detail the **classification of food industry waste** based on physical state, source industry, biodegradability, and moisture content. **(7 Marks)** **(b)** Discuss the **characterization of fruit and vegetable processing waste** with reference to proximate composition, physico-chemical parameters, and pollution load estimation. Use **two specific industry examples** (e.g., mango juice, orange processing). **(7 Marks)** #### Model Solution — Part (a) **1. Classification by Physical State (2 marks)** - **Solid waste**: Peels (15–30 % raw wt.), seeds (5–15 %), trimmings, pomace (10–25 %). - **Liquid waste**: Wash water (COD 2,500–10,000 mg/L), evaporator condensate, juice spillage. - **Sludge/Semi-solid**: Clarifier sludge, membrane concentrate, ETP sludge. **2. Classification by Source Industry (2 marks)** - **Fruit & vegetable** — mango, citrus, apple, tomato, pineapple. - **Dairy** — whey, cream residues. - **Meat & poultry** — blood, bone, offal. - **Cereal milling** — bran, husk. - **Beverages** — grape marc, brewery spent grain. **3. Classification by Biodegradability (1.5 marks)** - FVPW is **highly biodegradable** with BOD/COD ratio of 0.4–0.8, making it suitable for biological treatment (composting, AD). - Non-biodegradable: plastics, metals — must be segregated. **4. Classification by Moisture Content (1.5 marks)** - High moisture: Fresh peels, wash water. - Medium moisture: Partially dried pomace. - Low moisture: Dried seed cake, bagasse. #### Model Solution — Part (b) **1. Proximate Composition (3 marks)** - Carbohydrates: 40–75 % of DM - Fiber: 20–50 % (cellulose, hemicellulose, pectin, lignin) - Protein: 2–12 % - Lipid: 1–25 % (high in seeds/kernels) - Ash/Minerals: 2–10 % **2. Physico-chemical Parameters (2 marks)** - pH 3.5–7.0; TS 1.5–8 %; VS 80–95 % of TS - BOD₅: 1,000–10,000 mg/L; COD: 2,500–25,000 mg/L - TKN: 50–500 mg/L; Oil & Grease: 50–500 mg/L **3. Industry Example — Mango Juice (1 mark)** - Solid waste: Peel (15–20 %), Stone (10–15 %), Pomace (10–15 %) = ~40–50 % raw wt. - Moisture: 60–75 % - Pectin in peel: 8–12 % DM - Kernel starch: 30–45 % DM - Effluent COD: 5,000–12,000 mg/L **4. Industry Example — Orange Processing (1 mark)** - Solid waste: Peel (50 %), Pulp (15 %), Seeds (5 %), Membrane (10 %) ≈ 60–65 % raw wt. - Pectin content: 20–30 % DM (highest among FVPW) - Limonene content: 1–2 % of fresh peel - Effluent pH: 3.0–4.5 (acidic) > [!WARNING] > **Examiner's Pitfall Alert — Question A:** > - **Common Mistake:** Confusing "biodegradability" with "moisture content". These are **independent classification axes** — students often mix them up. > - **Mark Loss Area:** Failing to give **industry-specific examples with numbers**. Vague answers ("peel is generated") fetch zero. Always quote **percentages and COD ranges**. > - **Critical:** Do **NOT** skip proximate composition in part (b) — it carries 3 marks. --- ### **Question B (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam – June 2023 Style]` **(a)** Describe the **major unit operations** in a fruit processing plant and identify the **waste streams** generated at each stage. **(7 Marks)** **(b)** For a **citrus processing plant** processing **80 tonnes/day** of oranges, compute the **(i) daily solid waste (peel + pulp + seed)**, **(ii) daily COD load** assuming wastewater generation of 60 m³/day with average COD 8,000 mg/L, and **(iii) equivalent population load** using per capita BOD = 60 g/day and BOD/COD = 0.6. **(7 Marks)** #### Model Solution — Part (a) **1. Unit Operations & Waste Streams Table (7 marks — distribute as 0.5 each)** | **Unit Operation** | **Waste Generated** | **% Raw Wt.** | |:---|:---|:---:| | Receiving & Storage | Spoilage, dust | 2–5 % | | Washing | Wash water (high BOD, soil, pesticide) | High volume, low conc. | | Sorting/Grading | Reject fruit | 5–10 % | | Peeling | Peel (primary solid waste) | 15–30 % | | Coring/Deseeding | Core, seeds | 5–15 % | | Slicing/Dicing | Trimmings | 5–10 % | | Juicing/Extraction | Pomace (pulp, fiber residue) | 10–25 % | | Filtration | Spent filter aid, solids | 1–3 % | | Pasteurization | Condensate, fouling | — | | Packaging | Off-spec product, packaging rejects | <1 % | #### Model Solution — Part (b) **Given:** - Throughput: $80\ \text{T/day}$ - Solid waste yield (citrus): **~60 % of raw wt.** - Effluent: $Q = 60\ \text{m}^3/\text{day}$ - COD $= 8{,}000\ \text{mg/L}$ - BOD/COD $= 0.6$ - Per capita BOD $= 60\ \text{g/day}$ **(i) Daily Solid Waste (2 marks)** $$ M_{solid} = 80{,}000\ \text{kg} \times 0.60 = 48{,}000\ \text{kg/day} = 48\ \text{tonnes/day} $$ **(ii) Daily COD Load (3 marks)** Convert: $60\ \text{m}^3 = 60{,}000\ \text{L}$ $$ COD_{load} = 60{,}000\ \text{L} \times 8{,}000\ \text{mg/L} = 4.8 \times 10^8\ \text{mg/day} $$ $$ COD_{load} = 480\ \text{kg O}_2/\text{day} $$ **(iii) Equivalent Population Load (2 marks)** $$ BOD_5 = 0.6 \times 480 = 288\ \text{kg/day} = 288{,}000\ \text{g/day} $$ $$ P_{eq} = \frac{288{,}000}{60} = 4{,}800\ \text{persons equivalent} $$ > [!WARNING] > **Examiner's Pitfall Alert — Question B:** > - **Common Mistake:** Confusing the **percent of raw weight** that is solid waste vs. percent of dry weight. State clearly whether the figure is on **wet basis (w.b.) or dry basis (d.b.)**. > - **Unit Conversion Trap:** $1\ \text{m}^3 = 1{,}000\ \text{L}$ — students often forget this, leading to 1000× calculation errors. > - **Forgotten Step:** Showing the BOD step before computing population equivalent. Always show $BOD_5 = BI \times COD_{load}$ as an intermediate step. > - **Population Equivalent Interpretation:** Mention what the number means (pollution equivalence of a township) for full marks. --- ## 🎯 TOPIC RECAP & RAPID REVISION CHECKLIST > Use this bulleted list for **last-hour KTU exam revision**: ### ✅ Core Concepts - [ ] Food industry waste = **misplaced biomass** — 25–60 % raw wt. in FVPW - [ ] Classification axes: **physical state, source, biodegradability, moisture, hazard** - [ ] FVPW is **highly biodegradable** (BOD/COD = 0.4–0.8) - [ ] **Citrus peel** = highest pectin source (20–30 % DM) - [ ] **Grape pomace** = richest polyphenol waste - [ ] **Mango kernel** = starch + fat (dual recovery) - [ ] **Pineapple waste** = bromelain enzyme source - [ ] **Banana peel** = starch + pectin → bioethanol precursor ### ✅ Key Numbers to Memorize - [ ] COD of glucose = **1.067 g O₂/g substrate** - [ ] Citrus peel moisture = **70–82 % w.b.** - [ ] Mango juice solid waste = **35–50 % raw wt.** - [ ] Potato processing waste = **30–50 % raw wt.** - [ ] Coconut processing waste = **65–85 % raw wt.** - [ ] Per capita BOD standard = **60 g/day** (Indian CPCB) - [ ] FVPW BOD/COD ratio = **0.4 – 0.8** - [ ] Disposal limit COD (inland) = **≤ 250 mg/L** ### ✅ Formulae (Recall Instantly) - [ ] $M.C.\% = \dfrac{W_w - W_d}{W_w} \times 100$ - [ ] $BI = \dfrac{BOD_5}{COD}$ - [ ] $P_{eq} = \dfrac{BOD_{total}}{60\ \text{g/day}}$ - [ ] Buswell stoichiometric coefficients for CH₄ - [ ] $COD_{glucose} = 1.067\ \text{g O}_2/\text{g}$ ### ✅ Standard Methods to Cite - [ ] APHA 5210 B (BOD), 5220 D (COD), 2540 B/D/E (Solids) - [ ] AOAC 934.01 (Moisture) - [ ] Folin-Ciocalteu (Phenolics) - [ ] CPCB/SPCB effluent norms (Indian context) ### ✅ Keywords for Maximum Marks - "**APHA standard methods**", "**population equivalent**", "**BOD/COD biodegradability index**", "**5R waste hierarchy**", "**Buswell equation**", "**proximate composition**", "**bioactive compounds**", "**dual recovery**", "**circular bioeconomy**" > [!IMPORTANT] > **Final Examiner Tip:** In KTU answers, **always quantify** — never write qualitative statements alone. Every claim must carry a **number, range, or percentage**. This single habit separates 12-mark answers from 14-mark answers.

topics.title: Beverage processing industry
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEFTT744 - FOOD INDUSTRY WASTE MANAGEMENT
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Beverage processing industry

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

The **Beverage Processing Industry** encompasses the segment of the food industry that is involved in the transformation of raw agricultural, horticultural, dairy, or distillery-derived feedstocks into ready-to-consume liquid refreshments. As per the KTU 2024 Scheme syllabus for *Food Industry Waste Management*, this industry is broadly classified into two operational divisions:

1. **Non-Alcoholic Beverages** — Includes soft drinks, fruit juices, bottled water, ready-to-drink (RTD) teas, sports drinks, dairy-based beverages, and energy drinks.
2. **Alcoholic Beverages** — Includes beer, wine, whiskey, rum, vodka, brandy, and other distilled spirits derived from fermentation and distillation of carbohydrate-rich substrates.

From a **waste management perspective**, the beverage industry is considered a *high-volume, low-solid* waste generator — meaning it produces enormous quantities of liquid effluent (typically **3 to 10 m³ of wastewater per m³ of finished product**) with relatively low total suspended solids (TSS usually between **200 to 3000 mg/L**) but extremely high **Biochemical Oxygen Demand (BOD)** in the range of **1000 to 6000 mg/L** and **Chemical Oxygen Demand (COD)** in the range of **2000 to 10000 mg/L**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:** The beverage industry is one of the *largest water-consuming* food processing sectors. The water-to-product ratio is approximately **1.5:1 to 10:1** depending on the beverage category — making wastewater management a critical regulatory and environmental compliance issue under the **Central Pollution Control Board (CPCB)** norms.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — Intuitive Overview

Think of the beverage industry as a **massive kitchen with an extremely high turnover of liquid ingredients**. Imagine a busy restaurant kitchen that prepares thousands of glasses of lemonade, beer, or fruit punch per hour. To make a glass of orange juice, the kitchen must:

- **Wash** dozens of oranges (peel, pulp, seeds → solid waste)
- **Extract** the juice (losses due to spillage → liquid waste)
- **Filter** the juice (filter cake → solid waste)
- **Sterilize** bottles and equipment (cleaning-in-place chemicals → chemical wastewater)
- **Heat** or **pasteurize** the juice (cooling water → thermal wastewater)

Now multiply this single glass by **millions of liters per day** in a real beverage plant. The result is a **continuous stream of three waste categories**:
- **Solid waste:** peels, seeds, pomace, bagasse, spent grains, yeast
- **Liquid waste:** process effluents, wash waters, condensate
- **Energy waste:** thermal energy in cooling and heating streams

> [!NOTE]
> **Plain-English Takeaway:** Every glass of beverage you drink represents approximately **1.5 to 10 liters of water** used somewhere along the supply chain. A significant portion of this water eventually becomes wastewater that must be treated before discharge. This is why the beverage industry is a focal point of food waste management studies in KTU's B.Tech Food Technology curriculum.

## 1.3 Industry Scale Metrics (Standard Benchmarks)

| Parameter | Typical Value | Unit |
|---|---|---|
| Global beverage market size | > **850 billion USD** (2024 estimate) | USD/year |
| Average water consumption | **1.5 – 10** | m³ water / m³ product |
| Wastewater generation | **3 – 10** | m³ effluent / m³ product |
| Effluent BOD | **1000 – 6000** | mg/L |
| Effluent COD | **2000 – 10000** | mg/L |
| Effluent pH | **3.0 – 12.0** | pH units |
| Total Suspended Solids (TSS) | **200 – 3000** | mg/L |
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Classification of Beverages — Structured Logic

The beverage industry is operationally segmented based on the **raw material source** and the **principal preservation method**. The following hierarchical breakdown reflects the KTU 2024 Scheme classification framework:

### A. Non-Alcoholic Beverages
- **Carbonated Soft Drinks (CSDs):** Cola, lemon-lime, flavored sodas. *Method:* Carbonation with CO₂ (typically **2.5 – 4.0 volumes** of CO₂ at 4°C).
- **Fruit Juices & Nectars:** Orange, apple, mango, grape. *Method:* Extraction + pasteurization + aseptic packaging.
- **Bottled Water:** Still or sparkling mineral water. *Method:* Filtration (RO/UF) + UV/ozone sterilization.
- **Dairy Beverages:** Flavored milk, buttermilk, lassi. *Method:* Homogenization + UHT/pasteurization.
- **RTD Teas & Coffees:** Ready-to-drink iced tea, cold brew. *Method:* Brewing + sterilization + aseptic filling.
- **Energy & Sports Drinks:** Isotonic formulations. *Method:* Mixing + carbonation + fortification.

### B. Alcoholic Beverages
- **Beer:** Malted barley + hops + yeast. *Method:* Mashing → boiling → fermentation → maturation → packaging.
- **Wine:** Grape must. *Method:* Crushing → fermentation → aging → bottling.
- **Distilled Spirits (Whiskey, Rum, Vodka, Gin):** *Method:* Fermentation → distillation → aging (optional) → bottling.
- **Other Fermented Beverages:** Cider, mead, toddy.

## 2.2 Process Flow — Generalized Beverage Manufacturing

The core process flow for a generic beverage plant is presented in structured step-wise logic:

### **Step 1: Raw Material Reception & Storage**
- Reception of fruits, grains, sugar, water, and adjuncts.
- Quality inspection (Brix, pH, acidity, microbial load).
- Cold storage or silo storage for grains.

> [!NOTE]
> **Why this matters for waste management:** Damaged or rejected raw materials (typically **2–8% of intake**) become the *first solid waste stream* in the process chain.

### **Step 2: Pre-processing (Washing, Sorting, Peeling)**
- Water-intensive step. Typical water usage: **0.5 – 2.0 m³ per ton of raw material**.
- Generates **high-organic-load wash water** with soil, pesticides, and microbial contaminants.
- Peels, seeds, and cores constitute **30–50% of fruit mass** → major solid by-product stream.

### **Step 3: Extraction / Milling / Mashing**
- **Juices:** Pressing, decanter centrifugation, or enzymatic extraction.
- **Beer:** Milling of malt + mashing at **65–70°C** for starch conversion.
- **Wine:** Crushing and destemming of grapes.
- **Distilled spirits:** Cooking of grains with enzymes.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Key Concept:** The extraction step is the **single largest contributor to organic waste load** (BOD) in beverage effluent. Press cakes, spent grains, and pomace have moisture content of **60–80%** and are highly biodegradable.

### **Step 4: Clarification / Filtration**
- Removal of suspended solids, colloids, and haze-forming compounds.
- Uses diatomaceous earth (DE), bentonite, or membrane filters.
- **Filter cake** is a solid waste stream; spent DE is often classified as a *hazardous waste* due to crystalline silica content.

### **Step 5: Formulation & Standardization**
- Blending of sugar syrup, acids (citric, phosphoric), colors, flavors, and preservatives.
- **Cleaning-In-Place (CIP)** of mixing tanks generates alkaline (NaOH, **1–3%**) and acidic (HNO₃, **1–2%**) waste streams.

### **Step 6: Pasteurization / Sterilization**
- **HTST pasteurization:** **72°C for 15 seconds** for juices.
- **UHT treatment:** **135–150°C for 2–5 seconds** for milk-based beverages.
- **Flash pasteurization** for beer: **71–74°C for 30 seconds**.
- **Condensate** from heating becomes a thermal wastewater stream.

### **Step 7: Packaging**
- Bottle washing, filling, capping, labeling, and shrink-wrapping.
- Glass bottle washing consumes **0.3 – 0.8 liters of water per bottle**.
- Effluent contains label adhesives, detergent residues, and broken glass.

### **Step 8: Storage & Distribution**
- Cold storage of finished products.
- Minimal direct waste generation, but **rejected/expired products** contribute to downstream waste.

## 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet (Cheat Sheet)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **CRITICAL FORMATTING NOTE:** Vertical bars in all mathematical expressions below use `\vert` (LaTeX absolute value) or `\mid` (LaTeX mid-line) to avoid breaking markdown table syntax.

| # | Formula / Parameter | Expression | Description | Unit |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | BOD Loading | $BOD_L = Q \times BOD_5$ | Volumetric organic load on wastewater | kg/day |
| 2 | COD:BOD Ratio | $R_{CB} = \dfrac{COD}{BOD_5}$ | Biodegradability indicator; **R_CB < 3** = biodegradable | dimensionless |
| 3 | Wastewater Volume | $V_{ww} = F \times W_p$ | F = water-to-product factor (1.5–10), W_p = product mass | m³/day |
| 4 | Solid Waste Generation | $M_s = M_{in} \times Y_s$ | Y_s = solid yield coefficient (e.g., 0.30–0.50 for fruit peel) | kg/day |
| 5 | Mass Balance (Input − Output = Accumulation) | $\sum m_{in} = \sum m_{out} + m_{accum}$ | Conservation of mass | kg |
| 6 | BOD Removal Efficiency | $\eta_{BOD} = \dfrac{BOD_{in} - BOD_{out}}{BOD_{in}} \times 100$ | Aerobic treatment efficiency | % |
| 7 | Solid Recovery (e.g., pomace) | $R_{solid} = \dfrac{M_{recovered}}{M_{generated}} \times 100$ | Waste valorization metric | % |
| 8 | Water Reuse Ratio | $WRR = \dfrac{V_{reused}}{V_{total}} \times 100$ | Sustainability indicator | % |
| 9 | BOD of Substrate (Theoretical) | $BOD_{th} = \dfrac{C_x \times O_2}{MW_{substrate}}$ | Stoichiometric oxygen demand | mg O₂/mg |
| 10 | Specific Wastewater Volume | $q = \dfrac{V_{ww}}{P}$ | P = product output | m³/m³ product |

### Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Design of Effluent Treatment Plants (ETP):** Equations 1, 2, and 3 are used to size equalization tanks, aeration basins, and clarifiers.
- **Regulatory Compliance:** Equation 6 is used to demonstrate compliance with **CPCB discharge norms** (BOD < 30 mg/L for inland discharge).
- **Zero-Liquid-Discharge (ZLD) Feasibility:** Equation 8 quantifies the percentage of water that can be recycled back into the process.

## 2.4 Quantitative Snapshot of Solid Waste Streams (Per Beverage Type)

| Beverage Type | Major Solid Waste | Yield (% of raw material) | Moisture Content (%) | Disposal / Valorization Route |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| Orange Juice | Peel, pulp, seeds | **45 – 55** | 75 – 85 | Pectin extraction, cattle feed |
| Apple Juice | Pomace | **25 – 35** | 70 – 80 | Pectin, fiber, biogas |
| Sugarcane Juice / Soft Drink | Bagasse | **30 – 35** | 45 – 55 | Boiler fuel, paper |
| Beer | Spent grains, spent hops, yeast | **85 (grains alone)** | 75 – 80 | Animal feed, biogas |
| Wine | Grape pomace, lees, stems | **20 – 30** | 60 – 70 | Distillation (grappa), compost |
| Distillery (Whiskey, Rum) | Spent wash (liquid), dregs | **> 90 (liquid vinasse)** | > 90 | Biogas, fertigation |
| Dairy Beverages | Whey | **80 – 90** of milk volume | 93 | Whey protein, lactose recovery |
| Bottled Water | RO reject | **25 – 50** of feed water | 100 | Recovery or evaporation |
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: Mass Balance Across a Beverage Plant

The mass balance is the **fundamental engineering equation** used to quantify waste generation in a beverage plant. It is derived from the **Law of Conservation of Mass** (Lavoisier's Law, 1789) and is a high-yield KTU exam topic.

### **Derivation: General Mass Balance Equation**

Consider a beverage process as a *control volume* with multiple input and output streams. For steady-state operation (no accumulation), the law of conservation of mass states:

$$
\text{Mass In} = \text{Mass Out}
$$

For a multi-stream system with $n$ input streams and $m$ output streams:

$$
\sum_{i=1}^{n} \dot{m}_{in,i} = \sum_{j=1}^{m} \dot{m}_{out,j}
$$

Where $\dot{m}$ denotes mass flow rate in **kg/h** or **ton/day**.

### **Expanded Form for a Beverage Plant:**

$$
M_{raw} + M_{water,in} + M_{chemicals} = M_{product} + M_{waste,solid} + M_{waste,liquid} + M_{evaporation,loss}
$$

### **Step-by-Step Worked Example: Orange Juice Plant**

**Given Data (per 1 hour of operation):**
- Orange fruit input: $M_{raw}$ = **1000 kg/h**
- Water input (washing + dilution): $M_{water,in}$ = **1500 kg/h**
- Sugar/acid/additives: $M_{chem}$ = **100 kg/h**
- Juice extraction yield: **55%** (mass of juice per mass of fruit)
- Solid waste generation: **45%** of fruit input
- Evaporation losses (pasteurization): **50 kg/h**

**Step 1: Calculate the product output**

The juice yield is 55% of the raw fruit mass. The product includes extracted juice plus added water and chemicals that are retained in the final product.

$$
M_{product} = (M_{raw} \times Y_{juice}) + (M_{water,retained} \times R_w) + M_{chem}
$$

We assume that 80% of the input water is retained in the product (the rest is lost as wastewater or evaporation):

$$
M_{water,retained} = 1500 \times 0.80 = 1200 \text{ kg/h}
$$

$$
M_{product} = (1000 \times 0.55) + 1200 + 100
$$

$$
M_{product} = 550 + 1200 + 100 = 1850 \text{ kg/h}
$$

**Step 2: Calculate solid waste**

$$
M_{waste,solid} = M_{raw} \times Y_{solid} = 1000 \times 0.45 = 450 \text{ kg/h}
$$

**Step 3: Calculate liquid waste (wastewater)**

Total input mass:

$$
M_{in,total} = 1000 + 1500 + 100 = 2600 \text{ kg/h}
$$

Total output (excluding wastewater):

$$
M_{out,total} = 1850 + 450 + 50 = 2350 \text{ kg/h}
$$

By mass balance:

$$
M_{waste,liquid} = M_{in,total} - M_{out,total} = 2600 - 2350 = 250 \text{ kg/h}
$$

**Step 4: Validate the mass balance**

$$
M_{in} = M_{out}
$$

$$
2600 \text{ kg/h} = 1850 + 450 + 250 + 50 = 2600 \text{ kg/h} \quad \checkmark
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Insight:** The liquid waste of **250 kg/h** represents wash water overflow, condensate, and CIP effluent. For a plant operating **16 hours/day**, the daily liquid waste is **4000 kg/day = 4 m³/day** of high-BOD effluent that must be treated before discharge.

## 3.2 Derivation: BOD Loading on Effluent Treatment Plant (ETP)

The BOD loading determines the size of the aeration tank in the ETP.

**Given:**
- Daily wastewater volume: $Q$ = **100 m³/day**
- Influent BOD₅: $BOD_{in}$ = **3000 mg/L**
- Target effluent BOD: $BOD_{out}$ = **30 mg/L** (CPCB norm)
- F/M ratio for activated sludge: **0.2 kg BOD/kg MLVSS/day**

**Step 1: Calculate BOD loading**

$$
BOD_L = Q \times BOD_{in}
$$

$$
BOD_L = 100 \text{ m}^3/\text{day} \times 3000 \text{ g/m}^3
$$

$$
BOD_L = 100 \times 3000 = 300{,}000 \text{ g/day} = 300 \text{ kg BOD/day}
$$

**Step 2: Calculate required MLVSS in aeration tank**

$$
MLVSS = \dfrac{BOD_L}{F/M} = \dfrac{300}{0.2} = 1500 \text{ kg}
$$

**Step 3: Calculate aeration tank volume** (assuming MLVSS concentration of **3000 mg/L**)

$$
V_{tank} = \dfrac{MLVSS}{X_{MLVSS}} = \dfrac{1500 \text{ kg}}{3 \text{ kg/m}^3} = 500 \text{ m}^3
$$

**Step 4: Calculate removal efficiency**

$$
\eta_{BOD} = \dfrac{BOD_{in} - BOD_{out}}{BOD_{in}} \times 100
$$

$$
\eta_{BOD} = \dfrac{3000 - 30}{3000} \times 100 = 99.0\%
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Exam Tip:** The BOD removal efficiency of **99%** demonstrates that beverage industry wastewater, despite its high organic load, is **highly amenable to biological treatment** because the R_CB (COD/BOD ratio) is typically between **1.5 and 2.5** — well within the biodegradable range.

## 3.3 Analytical Matrix: Waste Characterization by Beverage Sub-sector

| Sub-sector | Water Use (L/L product) | Effluent BOD (mg/L) | Solid Waste (kg/kL product) | Hazardous Component? |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| Carbonated Soft Drinks | **1.5 – 2.5** | 500 – 1500 | 5 – 15 (label waste) | No |
| Fruit Juices | **3 – 7** | 2000 – 6000 | 200 – 400 (pomace) | No (biodegradable) |
| Bottled Water | **1.2 – 1.8** | < 50 | None | No (clean effluent) |
| Beer | **4 – 8** | 1500 – 4000 | 150 – 250 (spent grain) | No |
| Wine | **2 – 4** | 1000 – 3000 | 100 – 200 (pomace) | No |
| Distillery (Sugarcane) | **10 – 25** | 40,000 – 60,000 (vinasse) | 800 – 1200 (spent wash) | Yes (high TDS, dark color) |
| Dairy Beverages | **2 – 5** | 800 – 2500 | 50 – 100 (whey) | No |
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture — Generic Beverage Processing Plant

The following Mermaid block diagram maps the interactions between unit operations, material flows, and waste generation points in a typical beverage plant.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A1[RAW MATERIAL RECEPTION] --> A2[QUALITY INSPECTION]
    A2 --> A3[STORAGE SILOS OR COLD ROOM]
    A3 --> B1[WASHING AND SORTING]
    B1 --> B2[PEELING DESTONING OR MILLING]
    B2 --> C1[EXTRACTION OR MASHING]
    C1 --> C2[CLARIFICATION AND FILTRATION]
    C2 --> D1[FORMULATION AND BLENDING]
    D1 --> D2[PASTEURIZATION OR UHT]
    D2 --> E1[ASEPTIC FILLING]
    E1 --> E2[LABELING AND PACKAGING]
    E2 --> F1[COLD STORAGE]
    F1 --> G1[DISTRIBUTION]

    W1[SOLID WASTE PEELS POMACE SPENT GRAINS] -.-> B1
    W2[HIGH BOD WASH WATER] -.-> B1
    W3[FILTER CAKE SPENT DE] -.-> C2
    W4[CIP WASTE ALKALINE AND ACIDIC] -.-> D1
    W5[CONDENSATE THERMAL WASTE] -.-> D2
    W6[BROKEN GLASS LABEL ADHESIVE] -.-> E2
    W7[EXPIRED PRODUCT REJECT] -.-> F1
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Diagram Interpretation:** The solid arrows represent the **main process flow** (raw material to finished product). The dashed arrows represent **waste streams** that exit the process at specific unit operations. Each waste stream has unique characteristics and requires a dedicated treatment or valorization strategy.

## 4.2 Block Diagram — Waste Management Hierarchy in Beverage Industry

This diagram illustrates the **5R waste management hierarchy** applied to the beverage industry.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    H1[REFUSE] --> H2[REDUCE]
    H2 --> H3[REUSE]
    H3 --> H4[RECYCLE]
    H4 --> H5[RECOVER ENERGY]
    H5 --> H6[DISPOSAL LANDFILL]

    H1A[Eliminate single use plastics] -.-> H1
    H2A[Water reduction closed loop CIP] -.-> H2
    H3A[Glass bottle return and refill] -.-> H3
    H4A[Pomace to pectin extraction] -.-> H4
    H5A[Biogas from vinasse and spent wash] -.-> H5
    H6A[Last resort only after treatment] -.-> H6
```

## 4.3 Process Flow — Distillery (Highest Waste Intensity)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    P1[SUGARCANE MOLASSES OR GRAIN] --> P2[MILLING OR COOKING]
    P2 --> P3[FERMENTATION 48 TO 72 HOURS]
    P3 --> P4[DISTILLATION]
    P4 --> P5[ALCOHOL PRODUCT]
    P4 --> P6[SPENT WASH VINASSE]
    P5 --> P7[AGING OPTIONAL]
    P7 --> P8[BLENDING AND BOTTLING]

    P6 --> P9[BIODIGESTER FOR BIOGAS]
    P6 --> P10[CONCENTRATION EVAPORATION]
    P10 --> P11[DRY SPENT WASH FERTILIZER]
```

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Insight:** The distillery sector is the **most waste-intensive** sub-sector of the beverage industry. For every liter of alcohol produced, **8 to 15 liters of spent wash (vinasse)** are generated. This spent wash has an **extremely high BOD of 40,000 to 60,000 mg/L** and a dark brown color due to melanoidin compounds, making it the most challenging wastewater stream in the entire food processing industry.
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

## 5.1 PART A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### **Question 1** `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]`

**Define the term "Beverage Processing Industry" and classify beverages into major categories with one example each.**

**Model Answer:**

The beverage processing industry is the segment of the food industry that converts raw agricultural, horticultural, dairy, or fermentable substrates into liquid refreshments for human consumption. It is classified into two major categories:

1. **Non-Alcoholic Beverages:** Beverages with ethanol content less than 0.5% ABV. *Example:* Carbonated soft drinks (Coca-Cola, Pepsi), fruit juices (orange, apple), bottled water, RTD tea, and dairy-based beverages.
2. **Alcoholic Beverages:** Beverages produced by fermentation of sugar-rich substrates, with ethanol content typically between 3% and 50% ABV. *Example:* Beer (4–8% ABV), wine (10–14% ABV), and distilled spirits such as whiskey, rum, vodka, and brandy (35–50% ABV).

---

### **Question 2** `[KTU University Exam – May 2024]`

**List the major categories of solid waste generated by the beverage processing industry.**

**Model Answer:**

The major categories of solid waste from beverage processing are:

1. **Raw material rejects** — Damaged fruits, spoiled grains, and off-specification ingredients.
2. **Process-derived solids** — Peels, pulp, seeds, cores, bagasse, pomace, spent grains, and spent hops.
3. **Filtration residues** — Filter cake, spent diatomaceous earth (DE), and bentonite sludge.
4. **Packaging waste** — Broken glass, plastic containers, label adhesives, and metal caps.
5. **By-products** — Yeast biomass, distillation dregs, and lees from wine.
6. **Expired/returned products** — Rejected finished products from quality control or distribution returns.

---

## 5.2 PART B Questions (14 Marks Each)

### **INTERNAL CHOICE — Select Either Question A OR Question B**

---

### **Question A (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]`

**(a) [7 Marks]** Describe in detail the **general unit operations** involved in a fruit juice processing plant, highlighting the **type and quantity of waste generated at each stage**.

**(b) [7 Marks]** A fruit juice plant processes **5000 kg/day** of mangoes. The juice yield is **60%**, the solid waste (peel + stone) is **38%**, and the remaining **2%** is lost as evaporation. The plant uses **8000 liters of water per day** for washing and processing. Calculate the **(i) finished juice output, (ii) solid waste generated, and (iii) wastewater generated** per day.

**Model Solution:**

#### Part (a) — Unit Operations and Waste Streams

A fruit juice processing plant involves the following unit operations, each with distinct waste generation:

| Unit Operation | Process Description | Waste Generated |
|---|---|---|
| 1. **Reception & Sorting** | Unloading, weighing, visual inspection | Reject fruits (2–5%) |
| 2. **Washing** | Spray washing, soak washing | High-BOD wash water (soil, pesticide residues) |
| 3. **Peeling & Destoning** | Mechanical or enzymatic peeling | Peel, stone (35–45% of raw material) |
| 4. **Pulping/Extraction** | Pulper, finisher, decanter centrifuge | Pulp residues, press cake (15–25%) |
| 5. **Clarification** | Filtration, centrifugation | Filter cake, sludge |
| 6. **Pasteurization** | HTST at 72°C / 15 s | Condensate, cooling water |
| 7. **Packaging** | Bottle washing, filling, sealing | Detergent effluent, broken glass |

#### Part (b) — Numerical Solution

**Given:**
- Mango input: $M_{raw}$ = 5000 kg/day
- Juice yield: 60%
- Solid waste: 38%
- Evaporation loss: 2%
- Water input: 8000 L/day

**Step 1: Finished juice output (including added water retained in product)**

Assuming 90% of input water is retained in the product:

$$
