M_{juice} = M_{raw} \times 0.60 = 5000 \times 0.60 = 3000 \text{ kg/day}
$$

$$
M_{water,retained} = 8000 \times 0.90 = 7200 \text{ kg/day}
$$

$$
M_{product} = 3000 + 7200 = 10{,}200 \text{ kg/day}
$$

**Step 2: Solid waste generated**

$$
M_{solid} = M_{raw} \times 0.38 = 5000 \times 0.38 = 1900 \text{ kg/day}
$$

**Step 3: Evaporation loss**

$$
M_{evap} = M_{raw} \times 0.02 = 5000 \times 0.02 = 100 \text{ kg/day}
$$

**Step 4: Wastewater generated (by mass balance)**

$$
M_{wastewater} = M_{in} - M_{out}
$$

$$
M_{in} = 5000 + 8000 = 13{,}000 \text{ kg/day}
$$

$$
M_{out} = 10{,}200 + 1900 + 100 = 12{,}200 \text{ kg/day}
$$

$$
M_{wastewater} = 13{,}000 - 12{,}200 = 800 \text{ kg/day} = 800 \text{ L/day}
$$

**Final Answer:**
- **(i) Finished juice output** = 10,200 kg/day (including retained water)
- **(ii) Solid waste** = 1900 kg/day
- **(iii) Wastewater** = 800 L/day

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall:** Students commonly make two mistakes here. **(1)** They forget to include the **retained water** in the finished product mass, leading to an underestimation of output. **(2)** They treat the **evaporation loss** as wastewater — it is NOT, because water lost by evaporation exits the liquid phase and enters the atmosphere. Only the *unaccounted* liquid is wastewater. Marks are deducted for confusing these streams.

---

### **Question B (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam – May 2024]`

**(a) [7 Marks]** Discuss the **sources and characteristics of wastewater** generated by the **distillery industry**. Why is distillery spent wash considered the most polluting industrial effluent in the food processing sector?

**(b) [7 Marks]** A brewery produces **50,000 liters of beer per day**. The brewing process generates **20 kg of spent grains per hectoliter (hL) of beer**, **0.5 kg of spent yeast per hL**, and **0.2 kg of spent hops per hL**. Calculate the **(i) total solid waste generated per day, (ii) solid waste per liter of beer, and (iii) annual solid waste if the plant operates 300 days/year.**

**Model Solution:**

#### Part (a) — Distillery Spent Wash

Distilleries produce **spent wash (vinasse)** — a dark brown, highly acidic, and extremely high-BOD liquid by-product of the distillation step. Key characteristics include:

| Parameter | Spent Wash Value |
|---|---|
| Volume | **8 – 15 L per liter of alcohol** |
| pH | **3.5 – 5.0** (highly acidic) |
| BOD₅ | **40,000 – 60,000 mg/L** |
| COD | **80,000 – 120,000 mg/L** |
| TDS | **30,000 – 60,000 mg/L** |
| Color | Dark brown (melanoidin pigments) |

**Why it is the most polluting:** The BOD and COD of distillery spent wash are **10 to 20 times higher** than typical domestic sewage. The presence of recalcitrant melanoidin pigments (from Maillard reactions during cooking of molasses/grains) makes it resistant to conventional biological treatment. Discharge into water bodies causes **severe oxygen depletion, fish kills, and aesthetic damage** due to the dark color.

#### Part (b) — Numerical Solution

**Given:**
- Beer production: 50,000 L/day = 500 hL/day
- Spent grains: 20 kg/hL
- Spent yeast: 0.5 kg/hL
- Spent hops: 0.2 kg/hL
- Operating days: 300 days/year

**Step 1: Total solid waste per day**

$$
M_{grains} = 500 \times 20 = 10{,}000 \text{ kg/day}
$$

$$
M_{yeast} = 500 \times 0.5 = 250 \text{ kg/day}
$$

$$
M_{hops} = 500 \times 0.2 = 100 \text{ kg/day}
$$

$$
M_{total,solid} = 10{,}000 + 250 + 100 = 10{,}350 \text{ kg/day}
$$

**Step 2: Solid waste per liter of beer**

$$
q_{solid} = \dfrac{10{,}350}{50{,}000} = 0.207 \text{ kg/L}
$$

**Step 3: Annual solid waste**

$$
M_{annual} = 10{,}350 \times 300 = 3{,}105{,}000 \text{ kg/year} = 3105 \text{ tonnes/year}
$$

**Final Answer:**
- **(i) Total solid waste per day** = 10,350 kg/day
- **(ii) Solid waste per liter** = 0.207 kg/L
- **(iii) Annual solid waste** = 3,105 tonnes/year

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall:** Two common errors in this problem: **(1)** Unit conversion mistakes — students forget to convert **liters to hectoliters** (1 hL = 100 L), leading to a 100× error in the final answer. **(2)** Students calculate only spent grains and ignore yeast and hops, losing **2 out of 7 marks**. Always account for **all** solid waste streams in a mass balance problem.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid-Revision Checklist for KTU Exam Day:**

- ✅ The beverage industry is the **largest water-consuming** food processing sector, with a water-to-product ratio of **1.5:1 to 10:1**.
- ✅ Beverages are classified as **Non-Alcoholic** (< 0.5% ABV) and **Alcoholic** (3–50% ABV).
- ✅ Major solid wastes are: **peels, pomace, bagasse, spent grains, spent hops, yeast, filter cake, whey, and vinasse**.
- ✅ Wastewater BOD ranges from **1000 to 6000 mg/L** for most beverages; distillery spent wash is the **exception** (40,000–60,000 mg/L).
- ✅ COD/BOD ratio < **3** indicates **biodegradable** effluent — applicable to most beverage effluents.
- ✅ **HTST pasteurization** standard: **72°C for 15 seconds** (for fruit juices).
- ✅ **Distillery spent wash** = **8–15 L per liter of alcohol produced** — highest waste intensity in the industry.
- ✅ **Beer** generates ~**20 kg spent grains per hL** of finished beer — the single largest solid waste stream in brewing.
- ✅ **Orange juice** peel waste = **45–55%** of raw fruit mass — a major source of pectin, essential oils, and cattle feed.
- ✅ **5R Hierarchy** for beverage waste: **Refuse → Reduce → Reuse → Recycle → Recover Energy → Dispose**.
- ✅ **CIP (Cleaning-In-Place)** generates **alkaline (NaOH) and acidic (HNO₃)** waste streams that require pH neutralization before biological treatment.
- ✅ Mass balance equation: $\sum M_{in} = \sum M_{out}$ — always validate your numerical answers using this conservation law.
- ✅ The **CPCB discharge norm** for BOD in food industry effluent is **< 30 mg/L** for inland surface water discharge.
- ✅ **Spent wash valorization** through anaerobic biogas digesters is the most widely adopted treatment strategy in Indian distilleries.
- ✅ **Whey** from dairy beverage plants = **80–90%** of milk volume — a major resource for whey protein and lactose recovery.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Sugar industry
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEFTT744 - FOOD INDUSTRY WASTE MANAGEMENT
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Sugar industry

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: CORE TECHNICAL DEFINITION & INTUITIVE OVERVIEW

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

The **sugar industry** is a major agro-processing sector that extracts crystalline sucrose (C₁₂H₂₂O₁₁) from plant-based raw materials—primarily **sugarcane** (*Saccharum officinarum*) and **sugar beet** (*Beta vulgaris*)—through a sequence of physical, thermal, and chemical operations such as milling, juice extraction, clarification, evaporation, crystallization, and centrifugation. The industry is classified as a **seasonal, solid-waste-intensive, and high-water-consumption** food processing industry, generating substantial quantities of **bagasse, molasses, press mud (filter cake), boiler fly ash, and high-strength liquid effluent (spent wash / condenser water)**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:** The sugar industry is treated as a *model case study* for solid–liquid waste management because it simultaneously produces three distinct waste streams (solid, liquid, and gaseous), making it ideal for understanding integrated waste minimization and resource recovery strategies.

## 1.2 Key Industry Metrics (KTU-Standard Values)

| Parameter | Typical Value |
|---|---|
| Sugarcane crushed per day (Indian mill average) | **2,500 – 5,000 TCD** (Tonnes Crushed per Day) |
| Sugar recovery | **10 – 12 %** of cane weight |
| Bagasse generation | **28 – 32 %** of cane weight |
| Molasses generation | **4 – 5 %** of cane weight |
| Press mud (filter cake) | **3 – 5 %** of cane weight |
| Water consumption | **1,500 – 2,500 litres per tonne of cane** |
| Wastewater (spent wash) | **500 – 1,000 litres per tonne of cane** |
| COD of spent wash | **80,000 – 1,20,000 mg/L** |
| BOD of spent wash | **40,000 – 60,000 mg/L** |
| Calorific value of bagasse | **1,800 – 2,200 kcal/kg** |

## 1.3 Conceptual Analogy — The "Squeezed Orange" Model

Imagine cutting an orange in half and squeezing it. What you get is:
- **The juice** → analogous to **sugarcane juice** (the desired extract).
- **The pulp and seeds** → analogous to **bagasse and press mud** (the fibrous solid residue).
- **The sticky syrup left on the peel** → analogous to **molasses** (the thick, uncrystallizable by-product).
- **The rinse water that turns brown** → analogous to **spent wash / effluent** (the high-strength wastewater).

Just as nothing from the orange is truly "waste" if you have the right technology (the peel can be candied, the pulp made into marmalade, the seeds pressed for oil), the sugar industry transforms what looks like waste into **energy, ethanol, fertilizer, and building materials**—a perfect illustration of the **circular bioeconomy**.

> [!NOTE]
> **Why sugar industry is the "flagship case study" of food waste management:**
> 1. Every 100 tonnes of cane generates **~46 tonnes of solid by-products** and **~50,000 litres of high-strength effluent**.
> 2. The industry is a **net-zero-energy operation** when bagasse is burnt in boilers.
> 3. It demonstrates the **waste-to-wealth hierarchy** (Reduce → Reuse → Recycle → Recover → Dispose) better than any other food sector.

## 1.4 Classification of Sugar Industry Wastes

| Waste Type | Specific Waste | Physical State | Primary Origin |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Solid** | Bagasse | Fibrous, ~50 % moisture | Milling station |
| **Solid** | Press mud / Filter cake | Semi-solid, dark brown | Juice clarification |
| **Solid** | Boiler fly ash | Fine powder | Boiler house |
| **Liquid** | Spent wash (distillery effluent) | Dark, acidic, high BOD | Fermentation (if attached distillery) |
| **Liquid** | Condenser water | Warm, low-pollution | Evaporation & crystallization |
| **Liquid** | Floor wash & imbibition water | Muddy, sugar-bearing | Mill house |
| **Gaseous** | Particulate emissions & NOₓ | Particulate + gases | Boiler stack |

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: DEEP THEORETICAL ANALYSIS & KTU HIGH-YIELD FORMULA SHEET

## 2.1 Process Flow of Sugar Manufacturing

The sugar manufacturing process can be decomposed into the following **seven sequential unit operations**:

### Step 1 — Receiving and Washing of Cane
- Cane is unloaded, weighed, and washed to remove soil, trash, and extraneous matter.
- Trash content: **3 – 8 %** of cane weight.

### Step 2 — Milling / Juice Extraction
- Cane is shredded (via *fibrizer* / *shredder*) and passed through a **multi-roller mill train** (typically 4–6 mills in series).
- **Imbibition water** (hot water, 70–80 °C) is sprayed on the bagasse between mills to leach out residual sucrose.
- **Mill extraction efficiency (η_mill)** is governed by:

$$\eta_{mill} = \frac{\text{Sucrose in mixed juice}}{\text{Sucrose in cane}} \times 100\%$$

- Typical target: **92 – 95 %** in modern Indian mills.

### Step 3 — Juice Clarification
- Mixed juice is heated to **70 – 75 °C** and treated with **lime (CaO)** and **sulphur dioxide (SO₂)** in *phosphoric acid–flocculation* or *carbonatation* process.
- Purpose: precipitate colloids, neutralize organic acids, and bleach the juice.
- The sludge formed is the **press mud / filter cake**.

### Step 4 — Evaporation (Multi-Effect Evaporators)
- Clarified juice (~15 % sucrose) is concentrated to **thick juice / syrup (~55 – 65 % brix)** in a **multiple-effect evaporator (MEE)**, typically **5-effect with vapour bleeding**.
- The vapour bled is used to heat the **pan station** (energy integration).

### Step 5 — Crystallization (Pan Boiling)
- Syrup is further concentrated under **vacuum pans** to form **massecuite** (a mixture of sugar crystals and mother liquor).
- A *three-boiling scheme* (A, B, C massecuites) is common.

### Step 6 — Centrifugation & Drying
- Massecuite is centrifuged to separate **A-sugar** (marketable white sugar) from **A-molasses**.
- Sugar is dried in a **rotary / fluidized bed dryer** to **< 0.05 % moisture**.

### Step 7 — Effluent Treatment
- All wastewater streams are segregated, treated in **anaerobic lagoons / Upflow Anaerobic Sludge Blanket (UASB)** reactors, and polished via **facultative ponds** before discharge.

## 2.2 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> The following equations are **frequently asked in KTU ESE numericals** for Module 1.

| # | Formula | Description | Units |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | **Mill Extraction Efficiency** $\eta_{mill} = \dfrac{J \times P_j}{C \times P_c} \times 100$ | Where J = juice weight, C = cane weight, P_j = pol % juice, P_c = pol % cane | % |
| 2 | **Bagasse Generation Index** $B = C \times f_b$ | f_b = bagasse fraction (0.28 – 0.32) | tonnes/day |
| 3 | **Molasses Generation** $M = C \times f_m$ | f_m = molasses fraction (0.04 – 0.05) | tonnes/day |
| 4 | **Press Mud Generation** $P_m = C \times f_p$ | f_p = press mud fraction (0.03 – 0.05) | tonnes/day |
| 5 | **BOD Load (Spent Wash)** $L_{BOD} = Q \times C_{BOD}$ | Q = effluent flow, C_BOD = BOD concentration | kg/day |
| 6 | **COD/BOD Ratio Indicator** $\rho = \dfrac{COD}{BOD}$ | Typical for spent wash: **2.0 – 2.5** | dimensionless |
| 7 | **Bagasse Calorific Energy** $E_b = B \times CV_b \times \eta_{boiler}$ | CV_b = calorific value, η_boiler = thermal efficiency (0.65 – 0.75) | kcal/day or MJ/day |
| 8 | **Anaerobic Biogas Yield** $V_{CH_4} = L_{BOD\,removed} \times 0.35$ | Theoretical: **0.35 m³ CH₄ per kg BOD removed** at STP | m³/day |
| 9 | **Mass Balance (Cane)** $C = S + B + M + P_m + L_e + \text{losses}$ | Where L_e = evaporative + unaccounted losses | tonnes/day |
| 10 | **Water Footprint** $W_f = Q_{imbibition} + Q_{process} + Q_{boiler} + Q_{cooling}$ | Total freshwater input | m³/day |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **CRITICAL FORMATTING NOTE FOR KTU EXAMS:** When writing the COD/BOD ratio or any mathematical expression with absolute value in your answer sheet, always use the symbol $\vert$ or write "absolute value of" in words—do NOT use vertical bars inside table cells, as examiners may mark it ambiguously.

## 2.3 Real-World Engineering Utility

- The **mill extraction efficiency equation** is used by process engineers to benchmark plant performance against the **Cocker–Kreiss–Dedekind (CKD) formula** and cane payment policy of the Indian Sugar Mills Association (ISMA).
- The **BOD load equation** is fundamental in designing the **capacity (m³/day) of anaerobic digesters** in distillery effluent treatment plants (ETPs).
- The **bagasse energy equation** justifies the **cogeneration (steam + electricity) model** that has made Indian sugar mills *export electricity to the grid* under the **Sugar Industry Promotion Policy, 2014**.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: STEP-BY-STEP DERIVATIONS & ANALYTICAL MATRIX

## 3.1 Worked Numerical — Material Balance Across a Sugar Mill

> [!NOTE]
> This is a **classic KTU 14-mark style problem** combining a material balance with a downstream waste-utilization calculation.

### Problem Statement

A sugar mill crushes **3,000 tonnes of cane per day (TCD)**. Given the following operating data, calculate:
1. Daily production of **bagasse, molasses, and press mud**.
2. **Energy generated** from complete combustion of bagasse in the boiler.
3. **BOD load** discharged per day in spent wash.

**Operating Data:**
- Bagasse fraction, $f_b = 0.30$
- Molasses fraction, $f_m = 0.045$
- Press mud fraction, $f_p = 0.035$
- Calorific value of bagasse, $CV_b = 2{,}000\ \text{kcal/kg}$
- Boiler thermal efficiency, $\eta_{boiler} = 0.70$
- Spent wash flow, $Q = 0.8\ \text{m}^3/\text{tonne of cane}$
- BOD concentration in spent wash, $C_{BOD} = 50{,}000\ \text{mg/L}$

### Step 1 — Daily Mass Generation of Solid By-products

**Bagasse generation:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
B &= C \times f_b \\
  &= 3{,}000 \times 0.30 \\
  &= 900\ \text{tonnes/day}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Text logic:** Multiply total cane crushed by the bagasse fraction to obtain daily bagasse output. Indian mills average a bagasse-to-cane ratio of 28–32 %; using 0.30 is statistically reasonable.

**Molasses generation:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
M &= C \times f_m \\
  &= 3{,}000 \times 0.045 \\
  &= 135\ \text{tonnes/day}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Text logic:** Molasses is the residual syrup after the final crystallization stage. The standard yield is 4–5 % of cane weight.

**Press mud generation:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
P_m &= C \times f_p \\
    &= 3{,}000 \times 0.035 \\
    &= 105\ \text{tonnes/day}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Text logic:** Press mud arises from the rotary vacuum filter that removes suspended solids during juice clarification. Typical yield: 3–5 % of cane.

### Step 2 — Energy Generated from Bagasse Combustion

**Total calorific energy in bagasse:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
E_{gross} &= B \times CV_b \\
          &= 900 \times 1{,}000 \times 2{,}000\ \text{kcal/day} \\
          &= 1.8 \times 10^9\ \text{kcal/day}
\end{aligned}
$$

> Note: $B$ is in tonnes; convert to kg by multiplying by 1,000.

**Useful (recoverable) steam energy:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
E_{useful} &= E_{gross} \times \eta_{boiler} \\
           &= 1.8 \times 10^9 \times 0.70 \\
           &= 1.26 \times 10^9\ \text{kcal/day}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Convert to electrical equivalent (1 kWh = 860 kcal):**

$$
\begin{aligned}
E_{electrical} &= \frac{1.26 \times 10^9}{860} \\
               &\approx 1.465 \times 10^6\ \text{kWh/day} \\
               &\approx 1{,}465\ \text{MWh/day}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Text logic:** Indian sugar mills with 60–70 % boiler efficiency typically export 20–40 MW to the grid. A 3,000 TCD mill producing ~1,465 MWh/day aligns with real-world *bagasse cogeneration* plants (e.g., those promoted under the Ministry of New & Renewable Energy scheme).

### Step 3 — BOD Load of Spent Wash

**Convert concentration to kg/m³:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
C_{BOD} &= 50{,}000\ \text{mg/L} = 50{,}000 \times 10^{-6}\ \text{kg/mL} \times 10^3\ \text{mL/L} \\
        &= 50\ \text{kg/m}^3
\end{aligned}
$$

> Quick conversion rule: divide mg/L by 1,000 to obtain kg/m³.

**Total daily effluent flow:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
Q_{daily} &= 0.8 \times 3{,}000 = 2{,}400\ \text{m}^3/\text{day}
\end{aligned}
$$

**BOD load:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
L_{BOD} &= Q_{daily} \times C_{BOD} \\
       &= 2{,}400 \times 50 \\
       &= 1.2 \times 10^5\ \text{kg/day} \\
       &= 120\ \text{tonnes\ of\ BOD/day}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Equivalent population (per capita BOD ≈ 60 g/day):**

$$
\begin{aligned}
P_{eq} &= \frac{1.2 \times 10^5 \times 10^3\ \text{g/day}}{60\ \text{g/day/person}} \\
       &= 2 \times 10^6\ \text{persons}
\end{aligned}
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Engineering Insight:** A 3,000 TCD sugar mill with an attached distillery pollutes as much as a **city of 2 million people** in BOD terms. This is why distillery effluent is among the most polluting liquid wastes regulated by the **Central Pollution Control Board (CPCB)** under the **MoEFCC Red Category**.

## 3.2 Analytical Matrix — Waste-to-Wealth Mapping

| Waste Stream | Quantity (tonnes/day) | Treatment / Valorization Route | End Product | Revenue Potential (₹/tonne) |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| Bagasse | 900 | Boiler combustion → Cogeneration | Steam + Electricity | 4 – 6 (electricity) |
| Bagasse (surplus) | 200 – 300 | Pulp & paper / Particle board | Paper, board | 8,000 – 12,000 |
| Molasses | 135 | Fermentation → Distillation | Ethanol / Rectified spirit | 35,000 – 45,000 |
| Press mud | 105 | Composting / Vermicomposting | Bio-fertilizer (KMB) | 3,000 – 5,000 |
| Fly ash | 15 – 25 | Brick manufacturing / Cement | Building material | 1,500 – 2,500 |
| Spent wash | 2,400 m³/day | UASB + Biomethanation | Biogas (CH₄) | 25,000 (per 1,000 m³) |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->
