<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1
**[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, PEFTT744]**
**Q: List and briefly explain the six elements (6R principles) important for efficient management of food industry waste.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

The 6R principles, arranged in order of decreasing priority, are:

1. **Rethink** — Redesigning the process to inherently avoid waste generation. (0.5 mark)
2. **Refuse** — Avoiding the use of unnecessary materials or inputs. (0.5 mark)
3. **Reduce** — Minimizing waste generation at the source through process optimization. (0.5 mark)
4. **Reuse** — Using waste materials again for the same or different purpose without major processing. (0.5 mark)
5. **Recycle** — Reprocessing waste into new raw materials or products. (0.5 mark)
6. **Recover** — Extracting energy from waste through incineration, biogas, or pyrolysis. (0.5 mark)

The hierarchy ensures that prevention is always preferred over treatment. *(Final 0.5 mark for hierarchy statement)*

### Question 2
**[KTU University Exam – May 2024, PEFTT744]**
**Q: Define BOD/COD ratio. What does a ratio of 0.65 indicate about the effluent from a food processing unit?**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

**Definition (1.5 marks):** The BOD/COD ratio, also called the **Biodegradability Index (BI)**, is the ratio of 5-day Biochemical Oxygen Demand to Chemical Oxygen Demand of a wastewater sample. It indicates the fraction of organic matter that is **biodegradable** by microbial action.

$$BI = \frac{BOD_5}{COD}$$

**Interpretation of 0.65 (1.5 marks):** A value of 0.65 indicates that **65% of the organic load is biodegradable**, which classifies the effluent as **highly biodegradable**. Such effluents are amenable to **conventional aerobic biological treatment** (e.g., activated sludge process) without requiring extensive physico-chemical pre-treatment. A value > 0.5 is considered ideal for biological treatment systems.

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each)

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall — Module 2 Specific:**
> Students frequently lose 2–3 marks in the following ways:
> 1. **Confusing the 3R and 6R principles** — they list only 3Rs when the question asks for 6Rs (or vice versa).
> 2. **Forgetting units** in numerical problems — always write mg/L, kg/day, m³, etc.
> 3. **Not mentioning specific Indian regulations** (FSSAI, CPCB) when discussing compliance.
> 4. **Skipping the mass balance step** in energy recovery problems — mass balance is worth 2–3 marks on its own.
> 5. **Writing generic answers** without sector-specific examples (dairy, fruit, brewery) — examiners reward contextualization.

### Question A (14 Marks)

**[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, PEFTT744, Module 2, Modified]**

**(a)** Discuss in detail the **eight key elements** that are important for the efficient management of wastes generated from food industries. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A fruit processing industry generates **8,000 kg/day** of organic solid waste with moisture content **75%** and volatile solids (VS) fraction of **0.85** of total solids. The waste is treated in an anaerobic digester with **80% VS destruction efficiency** and the methane yield is **0.35 m³ CH₄ per kg VS destroyed**. Calculate:
  (i) The mass of volatile solids generated per day.
  (ii) The volume of methane produced per day.
  (iii) The total energy content of the methane in MJ/day (CV of CH₄ = 35.8 MJ/m³).
  (iv) The electrical energy equivalent in kWh/day (assume 35% conversion efficiency). **(7 marks)**

---

**Solution to Question A:**

**(a) The Eight Key Elements (7 marks):**

1. **Waste Minimization and Source Reduction (1 mark):** This is the most preferred element. It involves process modification, GMP, inventory control (FIFO), trimming optimization, and yield improvement. The cost of waste is reduced by avoiding waste generation altogether.

2. **Waste Characterization and Quantification (1 mark):** Physical, chemical, and biological analysis of waste streams to determine pH, BOD, COD, moisture, oil & grease, nutrients, and microbial load. This data drives the choice of treatment technology.

3. **Source Segregation (0.5 mark):** Separation of different waste streams at the point of generation to maintain their individual recovery value and prevent cross-contamination that complicates treatment.

4. **Collection, Storage and Handling (0.5 mark):** Design of appropriate containers (SS-304/316 for acidic waste), temperature control, and sizing using $V = Q \cdot t / f$ to prevent putrefaction and odor.

5. **Treatment and Disposal (1 mark):** Multi-stage treatment train comprising primary (screening, equalization), secondary (biological), and tertiary (disinfection) stages. Anaerobic digestion is particularly favored for high-strength food effluents.

6. **Recovery and Recycling (1 mark):** Application of the 6R hierarchy — Rethink, Refuse, Reduce, Reuse, Recycle, Recover. Recovery includes both material recovery (e.g., whey protein from dairy) and energy recovery (e.g., biogas from anaerobic digestion).

7. **Regulatory and Legal Compliance (1 mark):** Compliance with the Water Act 1974, Air Act 1981, Hazardous Waste Management Rules 2016, FSSAI Act 2006, and SWM Rules 2016. Discharge standards of BOD ≤ 100 mg/L, COD ≤ 250 mg/L, TSS ≤ 100 mg/L must be met.

8. **Economic and Environmental Sustainability (1 mark):** Evaluation using NPV, payback period, IRR, and environmental metrics like carbon footprint, water footprint, and Life Cycle Assessment (LCA).

---

**(b) Numerical Solution (7 marks):**

**Given:**
- $M_{wet} = 8000 \text{ kg/day}$
- $MC = 75\% = 0.75$
- $VS_{fraction} = 0.85$
- $\eta_{destruction} = 80\% = 0.80$
- $Y_{CH_4} = 0.35 \text{ m}^3\text{/kg VS destroyed}$
- $CV_{CH_4} = 35.8 \text{ MJ/m}^3$
- $\eta_{generator} = 35\% = 0.35$

**Step 1 (i): Mass of volatile solids per day.** *(2 marks)*

$$M_{dry} = M_{wet} \times (1 - MC) = 8000 \times (1 - 0.75) = 8000 \times 0.25 = 2000 \text{ kg/day}$$

$$M_{VS} = M_{dry} \times VS_{fraction} = 2000 \times 0.85 = 1700 \text{ kg VS/day}$$

**Step 2 (ii): Volume of methane produced per day.** *(2 marks)*

$$V_{CH_4} = M_{VS} \times Y_{CH_4} \times \eta_{destruction}$$

$$V_{CH_4} = 1700 \times 0.35 \times 0.80 = 476 \text{ m}^3\text{ CH}_4\text{/day}$$

**Step 3 (iii): Energy content of methane in MJ/day.** *(1.5 marks)*

$$E_{biogas} = V_{CH_4} \times CV_{CH_4} = 476 \times 35.8 = 17040.8 \text{ MJ/day}$$

**Step 4 (iv): Electrical energy equivalent in kWh/day.** *(1.5 marks)*

$$E_{electrical} = E_{biogas} \times \eta_{generator} = 17040.8 \times 0.35 = 5964.28 \text{ MJ/day}$$

$$E_{kWh} = 5964.28 / 3.6 = 1656.7 \text{ kWh/day}$$

**Final Answer:** The plant produces **1700 kg VS/day**, **476 m³ CH₄/day**, **17040.8 MJ/day**, equivalent to **1656.7 kWh/day** of electrical energy.

---

### Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice

**[KTU University Exam – May 2024, PEFTT744, Module 2, Modified]**

**(a)** Explain in detail the **importance of waste characterization** in the food industry. Discuss the physical, chemical, and biological parameters used for characterization, and explain how the BOD/COD ratio is used in selecting the appropriate treatment technology. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A dairy industry discharges **1000 m³/day** of wastewater with a COD concentration of **20,000 mg/L**. The wastewater is to be treated in a **UASB (Upflow Anaerobic Sludge Blanket) reactor** with a design OLR of **5 kg COD/m³·day**. The expected COD removal efficiency is **80%** and the methane yield is **0.35 m³ CH₄ per kg COD removed**.
  (i) Calculate the volume of the UASB reactor.
  (ii) Calculate the Hydraulic Retention Time (HRT).
  (iii) Calculate the volume of methane produced per day.
  (iv) Comment on whether the design is appropriate for a dairy effluent. **(7 marks)**

---

**Solution to Question B:**

**(a) Importance of Waste Characterization (7 marks):**

**Introduction (1 mark):** Waste characterization is the **systematic determination of physical, chemical, and biological properties** of waste streams. It is the foundation upon which all subsequent decisions in waste management — collection, treatment, recovery, and disposal — are based. Without characterization, the treatment plant is designed "blind."

**Physical Characterization (1.5 marks):** Includes measurement of:
- **Moisture content** (typically 60–95% for food waste) — affects calorific value and choice of dewatering.
- **Density and particle size** — affects pump and conveyor selection.
- **Total Solids (TS), Total Suspended Solids (TSS), Total Dissolved Solids (TDS)** — affect settling and filtration.

**Chemical Characterization (2 marks):** Includes:
- **pH** (typically 4.5–9.0) — affects biological activity and chemical precipitation.
- **BOD₅** (1000–30,000 mg/L for food effluent) — measures biodegradable organic load.
- **COD** (2000–50,000 mg/L) — measures total organic load.
- **Oil and Grease** (100–1000 mg/L) — causes interference in biological treatment.
- **Nitrogen and Phosphorus** — required for nutrient balance in biological treatment.

**Biological Characterization (1 mark):** Includes microbial counts, coliform indicators, and pathogen detection — especially important for meat, dairy, and seafood waste due to public health risk.

**BOD/COD Ratio and Treatment Selection (1.5 marks):** The Biodegradability Index (BI) is calculated as $BI = BOD_5 / COD$. It is interpreted as:
- BI > 0.5 → Highly biodegradable → **Aerobic biological treatment** is sufficient.
- BI between 0.3 and 0.5 → Moderately biodegradable → **Anaerobic + Aerobic** combination.
- BI < 0.3 → Poorly biodegradable → **Physico-chemical treatment** with advanced oxidation.

For example, a fruit processing effluent with BOD = 6000 mg/L and COD = 10,000 mg/L gives BI = 0.6, indicating suitability for direct aerobic treatment.

---

**(b) Numerical Solution (7 marks):**

**Given:**
- $Q = 1000 \text{ m}^3\text{/day}$
- $S_0 = 20{,}000 \text{ mg/L} = 20 \text{ kg/m}^3$
- $OLR_{design} = 5 \text{ kg COD/m}^3\text{·day}$
- $\eta_{removal} = 80\% = 0.80$
- $Y_{CH_4} = 0.35 \text{ m}^3\text{/kg COD removed}$

**Step 1 (i): Volume of UASB reactor.** *(2 marks)*

$$V = \frac{Q \times S_0}{OLR_{design}} = \frac{1000 \times 20}{5} = 4000 \text{ m}^3$$

**Step 2 (ii): Hydraulic Retention Time (HRT).** *(1.5 marks)*

$$HRT = \frac{V}{Q} = \frac{4000}{1000} = 4.0 \text{ days}$$

**Step 3 (iii): Volume of methane produced per day.** *(2 marks)*

$$V_{CH_4} = Q \times S_0 \times \eta_{removal} \times Y_{CH_4}$$

$$V_{CH_4} = 1000 \times 20 \times 0.80 \times 0.35 = 5600 \text{ m}^3\text{ CH}_4\text{/day}$$

**Step 4 (iv): Comment on design appropriateness.** *(1.5 marks)*

The HRT of **4.0 days** falls within the **recommended range of 2–5 days** for UASB reactors treating high-strength food industry effluents. The design OLR of **5 kg COD/m³·day** is also within the typical design range of **3–10 kg COD/m³·day** for such applications. Therefore, the UASB design is **appropriate** for the dairy effluent. The methane production of **5600 m³/day** represents a significant energy recovery opportunity (worth ~₹30,000–50,000/day in energy equivalent at current industrial natural gas prices).

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — Module 2**

- ☐ **Efficient waste management** is built on **eight pillars**: minimization, characterization, segregation, storage, treatment, recovery, compliance, and sustainability.
- ☐ The **6R hierarchy** is **Rethink → Refuse → Reduce → Reuse → Recycle → Recover**, with disposal as the last resort.
- ☐ **Waste characterization** is the first step — know BOD, COD, TS, VS, pH, oil & grease, and moisture.
- ☐ **Biodegradability Index (BOD/COD)** dictates treatment choice: > 0.5 aerobic; 0.3–0.5 anaerobic + aerobic; < 0.3 physico-chemical.
- ☐ **Anaerobic digestion** is the **workhorse technology** for high-strength food industry waste due to energy recovery and low sludge production.
- ☐ The **standard methane yield** for food waste is **0.35 m³ CH₄ per kg VS destroyed** (or per kg COD removed, both are used interchangeably in literature for design estimates).
- ☐ **Discharge standards** (India): BOD ≤ 100 mg/L, COD ≤ 250 mg/L, TSS ≤ 100 mg/L, Oil & Grease ≤ 10 mg/L.
- ☐ **Key Indian regulations**: Water Act 1974, Air Act 1981, FSSAI Act 2006, SWM Rules 2016, Hazardous Waste Management Rules 2016.
- ☐ **Mass balance** at steady state: $\sum M_{in} = \sum M_{out}$ — this is the **fundamental equation** for any waste audit.
- ☐ **Storage volume** calculation: $V = Q \cdot t / f_{packing}$, with $f_{packing}$ typically 0.6–0.8.
- ☐ **Energy recovery formula**: $E = m_{waste} \times CV \times \eta$ — memorize and apply.
- ☐ For KTU problems, always **state the units explicitly** and **show all calculation steps** to secure full marks.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Standards for emission or discharge of environmental pollutants from food processing industries
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEFTT744 - FOOD INDUSTRY WASTE MANAGEMENT
> - **Module:** Module Module 2
> - **Topic:** Standards for emission or discharge of environmental pollutants from food processing industries

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Standards for emission or discharge of environmental pollutants** refer to the legally binding maximum permissible limits prescribed by regulatory authorities (primarily the **Central Pollution Control Board - CPCB** under the **Environment (Protection) Act, 1986** and the **Water/Air Acts of 1974/1981**) for the concentration of physical, chemical, and biological contaminants that food processing industries are allowed to release into receiving water bodies, the atmosphere, or land.

These standards are categorized into:
- **Effluent Discharge Standards (Water)** – Governed under Schedule VI of the **Environment (Protection) Rules, 1986** and CPCB's industry-specific norms.
- **Emission Standards (Air)** – Governed for boiler stacks, incinerators, and process vents.
- **Ambient Air Quality Standards (AAQS)** – Prescribed under the **National Ambient Air Quality Standards (NAAQS), 2009**.
- **Noise Standards** – As per the **Noise Pollution (Regulation and Control) Rules, 2000**.

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:** The module specifically emphasizes that food industries are categorized as **"Orange/Red" category** industries under the **revised CPCB classification (2016)**, mandating consent to establish and operate under the **Water Act, 1974** and **Air Act, 1981**.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuitive Overview

Imagine your food factory as a household kitchen. After cooking, you cannot simply throw the oily, greasy, leftover water onto the street — it would clog drains, breed pests, and create foul smells. **Similarly, an industry cannot release untreated wastewater into rivers or the atmosphere.**

The government has therefore set **"rules of the road"** — just like traffic signals regulate vehicles to keep the city safe — **pollution standards regulate industrial discharges** to keep the environment safe. These are not just suggestions; they are **legal obligations** with heavy penalties (imprisonment up to 7 years and fines up to ₹1 crore) under the **Environment (Protection) Act, 1986**.

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Insight:** The standards are **concentration-based (mg/L)** for water, **mass-based (kg/day) or concentration-based (mg/Nm³)** for air, and **decibel-based (dB(A))** for noise.

## 1.3 Important Physical Constants & Standard Metrics

| Parameter | Standard Metric | Reference |
|-----------|----------------|-----------|
| **Biochemical Oxygen Demand (BOD)** | mg/L (3 days, **27 °C**) | IS 3025 (Part 44) |
| **Chemical Oxygen Demand (COD)** | mg/L | IS 3025 (Part 58) |
| **Total Suspended Solids (TSS)** | mg/L | IS 3025 (Part 17) |
| **pH** | Dimensionless (6.5–8.5) | IS 3025 (Part 11) |
| **Noise** | dB(A) | Weighting A network |
| **Stack Emissions** | mg/Nm³ (dry basis, **6% O₂ for solid fuel**, **15% O₂ for liquid/gas**) | CPCB norms |
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Regulatory Framework Governing Food Industry Discharges

The standards flow through a hierarchical regulatory pyramid:

**Step 1 — Apex Body:** **Ministry of Environment, Forest and Climate Change (MoEFCC)**
Issues the **Environment (Protection) Act, 1986** and subordinate rules.

**Step 2 — National Level:** **Central Pollution Control Board (CPCB)**
Sets nationwide standards, classifies industries, and supervises SPCBs.

**Step 3 — State Level:** **State Pollution Control Boards (SPCBs)**
Implement standards, grant **Consent to Establish (CTE)** and **Consent to Operate (CTO)**, conduct monitoring.

**Step 4 — Industry Level:** **Food Processing Units**
Must install **Effluent Treatment Plant (ETP)**, **Sewage Treatment Plant (STP)**, and **Air Pollution Control Devices (APCD)**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The **Consent Mechanism (Sec. 25/26 of Water Act & Sec. 21/22 of Air Act)** is the operational tool. Without valid CTE → CTO, the industry is **illegal**.

## 2.2 Classification of Food Industries by Pollution Load

| Category | Pollution Index | Examples | Mandatory Treatment |
|----------|----------------|----------|---------------------|
| **Red** | ≥ 60 | Distillery, Slaughterhouse, Tanneries | Primary + Secondary + Tertiary ETP |
| **Orange** | 41–59 | Dairy, Sugar, Brewery, Edible Oil, Fish Processing | Primary + Secondary ETP |
| **Green** | 21–40 | Bakery, Flour Mill, Confectionery | Basic treatment |
| **White** | ≤ 20 | Packaged water, Cold storage (non-processing) | Minimal |

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Yield Numerical Formulas for KTU Board Examination:**

| Formula / Parameter | Expression | Units | Application |
|---------------------|-----------|-------|-------------|
| **BOD Ultimate (BOD₅ → BODₗ)** | $BOD_L = \dfrac{BOD_5}{1 - e^{-k \cdot 5}}$ | mg/L | Waste characterization |
| **BOD₅ at temperature T** | $BOD_5(T) = BOD_5(20) \cdot (1.047)^{(T-20)}$ | mg/L | Temperature correction |
| **Dilution Equation** | $BOD_5(D) = \dfrac{\left(D_1 \cdot BOD_1 + D_2 \cdot BOD_2\right)}{D_1 + D_2}$ | mg/L | Mixing calculations |
| **Removal Efficiency (η)** | $\eta = \dfrac{\left(C_{in} - C_{out}\right)}{C_{in}} \times 100$ | % | ETP performance |
| **Population Equivalent (PE)** | $PE = \dfrac{\text{Total BOD per day (kg)}}{\text{Per capita BOD (0.08 kg/day)}}$ | Persons | Capacity design |
| **Stack Height Formula (CPCB)** | $H = 74 \cdot (Q)^{0.27}$ where Q is emission rate (t/h) OR $H = 14 \cdot Q^{0.27}$ | m | Boiler design |
| **Specific Air Consumption (SAC)** | $SAC = \dfrac{Q_{air}}{MW}$ | Nm³/kg fuel | Combustion efficiency |
| **Sound Pressure Level (SPL)** | $L_p = 20 \cdot \log_{10}\left(\dfrac{p}{p_0}\right)$ where $p_0 = 20 \, \mu Pa$ | dB | Noise measurement |
| **Combined Sound Level** | $L_{total} = 10 \cdot \log_{10} \left( \sum 10^{L_i/10} \right)$ | dB(A) | Multi-source noise |
| **CO₂ Generation from Fuel** | $CO_2 = C \cdot \dfrac{44}{12}$ where C = carbon content | kg | Emission inventory |
| **SS to VSS ratio** | $f_v = \dfrac{VSS}{TSS}$ (typically 0.7–0.85) | Dimensionless | Sludge design |
| **F/M Ratio (Food/Microorganism)** | $\dfrac{F}{M} = \dfrac{S_0 \cdot Q}{V \cdot X}$ | day⁻¹ | Activated sludge design |
| **Hydraulic Retention Time (HRT)** | $HRT = \dfrac{V}{Q}$ | hours/days | Reactor sizing |
| **Organic Loading Rate (OLR)** | $OLR = \dfrac{Q \cdot S_0}{V}$ | kg BOD/m³·day | Anaerobic digester |

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Utility:** These formulas are universally applied in **ETP design, stack sizing for boilers (a key auxiliary in food processing), EIA reports, and consent application dossiers** submitted to SPCBs.

## 2.4 Standards Philosophy — The "Why" Behind the Limits

- **pH (6.5–8.5):** Protects aquatic life; extreme pH destroys fish gills and disrupts biological treatment.
- **BOD (≤100 mg/L for most food industries):** High BOD → dissolved oxygen depletion → fish death → **eutrophication**.
- **TSS (≤100 mg/L):** Suspended solids cause siltation, reduce photosynthesis, clog aquatic organism respiratory systems.
- **Oil & Grease (≤10 mg/L):** Forms films on water surface, prevents oxygen transfer, fouls treatment systems.
- **COD (≤250 mg/L):** Indicates total oxidizable load (including non-biodegradable organics).
- **Total Nitrogen (≤10 mg/L) & Phosphorus (≤2 mg/L):** Limits algal blooms.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Pitfall:** Students often confuse **BOD₅ (5-day)** with **BODₗ (ultimate)**. By default, all food industry effluent standards in India refer to **BOD₃ at 27 °C** (a 3-day test at Indian ambient temperature) — NOT the international standard of BOD₅/20 °C.

# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Industry-Specific Effluent Discharge Standards (CPCB Norms)

### 3.1.1 Dairy Industry (Milk Processing, Cheese, Ghee, Ice Cream)

| Parameter | Inlet to ETP | Discharge Limit | Unit |
|-----------|--------------|-----------------|------|
| pH | Variable (lactic acid) | **6.5 – 8.5** | — |
| BOD₃ at 27 °C | 1500–3000 | **≤ 100** | mg/L |
| COD | 3000–6000 | **≤ 250** | mg/L |
| TSS | 800–1500 | **≤ 100** | mg/L |
| Oil & Grease | 200–500 | **≤ 10** | mg/L |
| Total Nitrogen | — | **≤ 10** | mg/L |
| Temperature | — | **≤ 40** | °C |

### 3.1.2 Sugar Industry (Cane Sugar, Refined Sugar)

| Parameter | Condenser Cooling + Process | Discharge Limit |
|-----------|----------------------------|-----------------|
| pH | 5.5–7.5 | **6.5 – 8.5** |
| BOD₃ | 1000–2000 | **≤ 100** |
| COD | 2000–4000 | **≤ 250** |
| TSS | 500–1000 | **≤ 100** |
| Sulphate | — | **≤ 1000** mg/L |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **For molasses-based distilleries attached to sugar mills**, the standard is **ZERO DISCHARGE**. Spent wash (BOD ≈ 30,000–50,000 mg/L) must be concentrated via **Multiple Effect Evaporator (MEE)** to produce **bio-compost** for land application.

### 3.1.3 Distillery Industry (Standalone)

**Three-Tier Standard System:**

**Tier 1 — Spent Wash (Raw):**
- BOD: **30,000 – 50,000 mg/L**
- COD: **80,000 – 1,00,000 mg/L**
- Total Dissolved Solids (TDS): **40,000 – 60,000 mg/L**

**Tier 2 — After Biomethanation (Post-anaerobic treatment):**
- BOD: **≤ 5,000 mg/L** (for land application as bio-compost)
- COD: **≤ 15,000 mg/L**
- pH: **7.0 – 8.5**

**Tier 3 — After Concentration (MEE → Bio-compost):**
- Concentrated spent wash solids: **≥ 50% moisture**
- Used as **organic fertilizer** in agricultural fields (consent-required)

> [!NOTE]
> The **"Zero Liquid Discharge (ZLD)"** concept is **mandatory** for distilleries per CPCB's 2005 guidelines.

### 3.1.4 Slaughterhouse & Meat Processing

| Parameter | Discharge Limit |
|-----------|----------------|
| pH | 6.5 – 8.5 |
| BOD₃ at 27 °C | **≤ 100 mg/L** |
| COD | **≤ 250 mg/L** |
| TSS | **≤ 100 mg/L** |
| Oil & Grease | **≤ 10 mg/L** |
| Total Nitrogen | **≤ 10 mg/L** |
| Fecal Coliform | **≤ 10³ MPN/100 mL** |

### 3.1.5 Fish & Sea-food Processing

| Parameter | Limit |
|-----------|-------|
| BOD₃ at 27 °C | **≤ 50 mg/L** (stricter than other food industries) |
| TSS | **≤ 50 mg/L** |
| Free Ammonia | **≤ 5 mg/L** |
| pH | 6.5 – 8.5 |
| Chlorine Residual | **≤ 1 mg/L** |

### 3.1.6 Vegetable Oil Processing (Refining, Hydrogenation)

| Parameter | Limit |
|-----------|-------|
| pH | 6.5 – 8.5 |
| BOD₃ | **≤ 100 mg/L** |
| COD | **≤ 250 mg/L** |
| Oil & Grease | **≤ 10 mg/L** (critical for refiners) |
| Total Suspended Solids | **≤ 100 mg/L** |

### 3.1.7 Fruit & Vegetable Processing (Juice, Pickle, Jam, Sauce)

| Parameter | Limit |
|-----------|-------|
| pH | 6.5 – 8.5 |
| BOD₃ | **≤ 100 mg/L** |
| COD | **≤ 250 mg/L** |
| TSS | **≤ 100 mg/L** |
| Total Nitrogen | **≤ 10 mg/L** |

## 3.2 Air Emission Standards for Food Industry Boilers

> [!NOTE]
> Most food processing units use **package boilers (oil-fired or coal-fired)** for steam generation — a key auxiliary process. The CPCB emission standards (per **MOEF notification GSR 593(E), 2017**) are:

| Pollutant | Oil/Gas Fired (mg/Nm³ at **15% excess air**) | Coal Fired (mg/Nm³ at **6% excess air**) |
|-----------|---------------------------------------------|------------------------------------------|
| **Particulate Matter (PM)** | ≤ 75 | ≤ 115 |
| **SO₂** | ≤ 1,600 (oil only) | **Plant-specific (based on coal S content)** |
| **NOx** | ≤ 110 (gas) / ≤ 170 (oil) | ≤ 300 |
| **CO** | ≤ 80 | ≤ 80 |
| **Mercury (Hg)** | — | ≤ 0.04 |

**Stack Height Determination (CPCB Formula):**

$$H = 14 \cdot Q^{0.27}$$

where $Q$ = fuel consumption rate in **tonnes per hour (t/h)**.

> [!NOTE]
> This formula gives minimum stack height in **metres**. For coal-fired boilers, **online continuous emission monitoring systems (OCEMS)** are mandatory for units >2 TPH.

## 3.3 Ambient Air Quality Standards (NAAQS, 2009)

| Pollutant | Industrial Area (24-hr avg) | Sensitive Area (24-hr avg) |
|-----------|----------------------------|---------------------------|
| **PM₂.₅** | 60 µg/m³ | 30 µg/m³ |
| **PM₁₀** | 100 µg/m³ | 60 µg/m³ |
| **SO₂** | 80 µg/m³ | 30 µg/m³ |
| **NOx** | 80 µg/m³ | 30 µg/m³ |
| **CO** | 4,000 µg/m³ (8-hr) | 2,000 µg/m³ |
| **NH₃** | 400 µg/m³ | 100 µg/m³ |

## 3.4 Noise Standards (Ambient, dB(A))

| Zone Type | Day (6 AM – 10 PM) | Night (10 PM – 6 AM) |
|-----------|-------------------|----------------------|
| **Industrial** | **75 dB(A)** | **70 dB(A)** |
| **Commercial** | 65 dB(A) | 55 dB(A) |
| **Residential** | 55 dB(A) | 45 dB(A) |
| **Silence Zone** (hospitals, schools, courts) | 50 dB(A) | 40 dB(A) |

## 3.5 Step-by-Step Derivation — BOD Temperature Correction

A common KTU question: *"Effluent has BOD₅ at 20 °C = 250 mg/L. Compute BOD₃ at 27 °C."*

**Step 1 — Convert 20 °C BOD₅ to 20 °C BOD₃:**

$$BOD_3^{20} = BOD_5^{20} \times \left(1 - e^{-k \cdot 3}\right) \Big/ \left(1 - e^{-k \cdot 5}\right)$$

**Step 2 — Temperature-corrected rate constant at 27 °C:**

$$k_{27} = k_{20} \times (1.047)^{(27-20)} = k_{20} \times (1.047)^7$$

This step applies the **van't Hoff-Arrhenius approximation**, doubling the reaction rate for every **10 °C rise**.

**Step 3 — Convert BOD₃ at 20 °C to BOD₃ at 27 °C:**

$$BOD_3^{27} = BOD_3^{20} \times (1.047)^{(27-20)} = BOD_3^{20} \times (1.047)^7$$

**Numerical Example (Assume k₂₀ = 0.23 day⁻¹):**

$$BOD_3^{20} = 250 \times \dfrac{1 - e^{-0.23 \times 3}}{1 - e^{-0.23 \times 5}} = 250 \times \dfrac{1 - 0.5024}{1 - 0.3166} = 250 \times 0.729$$

This yields $BOD_3^{20} \approx 182.2 \, \text{mg/L}$.

$$BOD_3^{27} = 182.2 \times (1.047)^7 = 182.2 \times 1.379 = 251.3 \, \text{mg/L}$$

This shows BOD value increases by **~38%** when measured at 27 °C instead of 20 °C — a critical consideration for food industry effluents which are discharged warm.

## 3.6 Stack Height Numerical Example

**Problem:** A dairy plant operates a 1.5 t/h coal-fired boiler. Find minimum stack height.

$$H = 14 \cdot Q^{0.27} = 14 \times (1.5)^{0.27}$$

$$H = 14 \times 1.120 = 15.68 \, \text{m} \approx 16 \, \text{m}$$

This is the **absolute minimum** to maintain ground-level concentrations below NAAQS.

## 3.7 Compliance Verification — ETP Removal Efficiency

**Problem:** A fruit processing ETP receives wastewater with BOD = 1200 mg/L. Discharge limit is 100 mg/L. What is the **minimum required removal efficiency**?

$$\eta = \dfrac{C_{in} - C_{out}}{C_{in}} \times 100 = \dfrac{1200 - 100}{1200} \times 100$$

$$\eta = \dfrac{1100}{1200} \times 100 = 91.67\%$$

A **two-stage ETP** (primary clarification + activated sludge process) is therefore mandatory. Simple primary treatment alone gives only **30–50% BOD removal** — insufficient.
