# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Q1. Define "Consent to Operate" (CTO) under the Water Act, 1974. Why is it mandatory for food processing industries? `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

**Consent to Operate (CTO)** is a written authorization granted by the **State Pollution Control Board (SPCB)** under **Section 25 of the Water (Prevention and Control of Pollution) Act, 1974** and **Section 21 of the Air (Prevention and Control of Pollution) Act, 1981** before an industry begins its actual production operations.

**Key Points (for 3 marks):**
- CTO is granted **after** construction of the plant, post-CTE.
- It is valid for **5 years** (renewable) and specifies the **quantity and quality of effluent/emission** that the industry is permitted to discharge.
- For food processing industries, CTO is mandatory because most are classified as **Orange/Red category** industries generating high BOD effluents.
- Operates without CTO → **legal offence**, punishable with imprisonment up to **3 months** (extendable to 6 months) and/or fine up to **₹10,000 per day** of violation.

---

### Q2. List any three effluent discharge standards prescribed by CPCB for the **Dairy Industry**. `[KTU University Exam - June 2024]`

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

The Central Pollution Control Board prescribes the following effluent discharge standards for the dairy industry (inland surface water discharge):

| Parameter | Limit |
|-----------|-------|
| **BOD₃ at 27 °C** | **≤ 100 mg/L** |
| **COD** | **≤ 250 mg/L** |
| **Total Suspended Solids (TSS)** | **≤ 100 mg/L** |

*(Any three parameters with values for 3 marks; pH, Oil & Grease can also be added.)*

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks with Internal Choice)

### **Question A (14 Marks)**

**Q.A.(a)** Discuss in detail the **regulatory framework** governing the standards for discharge of environmental pollutants from food processing industries in India. Explain the roles of CPCB, SPCB, and MoEFCC. **[7 Marks]** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2022]`

**Model Solution (7 Marks):**

**1. Ministry of Environment, Forest and Climate Change (MoEFCC) — Apex Body (1 Mark)**
- Nodal ministry for environmental protection.
- Enacts **Environment (Protection) Act, 1986** (umbrella legislation).
- Issues notifications for emission/effluent standards under **E(P) Rules, 1986 — Schedule I to VI**.
- Coordinates international treaties (Stockholm Convention, Paris Agreement).

**2. Central Pollution Control Board (CPCB) — National Level (2 Marks)**
- Established under **Section 3 of the Water Act, 1974**.
- Apex technical body; lays down **uniform standards** for streams and rivers across India.
- **Functions:**
  - Prescribes effluent and emission standards (industry-wise).
  - Classifies industries into **Red, Orange, Green, White** categories (2016).
  - Monitors river water quality — **National Water Quality Monitoring Programme**.
  - Lays down **Guidelines for ETP/STP/APCD design**.

**3. State Pollution Control Boards (SPCBs) — State Level (2 Marks)**
- Established under **Section 4 of the Water Act** in every state.
- **Key functions for food industries:**
  - Grant **Consent to Establish (CTE)** under Sec. 25 — verifies the design and adequacy of proposed ETP.
  - Grant **Consent to Operate (CTO)** under Sec. 26 — authorizes actual discharge with specific limits.
  - Conduct **periodic inspections** and sampling.
  - **KSPCB (Kerala SPCB)** is responsible for Kerala food units; uses **OCEMS**-linked continuous monitoring for large units.

**4. Statutory Framework (2 Marks)**
- **Water Act, 1974** — controls water pollution.
- **Air Act, 1981** — controls air pollution.
- **E(P) Act, 1986** — comprehensive umbrella act.
- **Hazardous Waste Management Rules, 2016** — for incinerator ash, used oil.
- **E-Waste Management Rules, 2016** — for obsolete electronic controls.
- **Solid Waste Management Rules, 2016** — for packaging waste.
- **Plastic Waste Management Rules, 2016** — multilayered food packaging.

---

**Q.A.(b)** A **distillery** produces **120 kL of alcohol/day** with a spent wash generation of **15 L per litre of alcohol**. The spent wash has BOD = **40,000 mg/L** and COD = **1,00,000 mg/L**. The distillery has installed a **biomethanation plant** with **85% BOD reduction efficiency**. Check whether the treated spent wash meets the CPCB land-application standard of **BOD ≤ 5,000 mg/L**. **[7 Marks]** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`

**Model Solution (7 Marks):**

**Step 1 — Calculate Daily Spent Wash Volume (1 Mark)**

$$Q = 120{,}000 \, \text{L/day} \times 15 \, \text{L spent wash/L alcohol}$$

$$Q = 1{,}800{,}000 \, \text{L/day} = 1{,}800 \, \text{m}^3/\text{day}$$

**Step 2 — Calculate Daily BOD Load (1 Mark)**

$$\text{BOD Load} = Q \times BOD_{in} = 1{,}800{,}000 \times 40{,}000 \, \text{mg/L}$$

$$= 7.2 \times 10^{10} \, \text{mg/day} = 72{,}000 \, \text{kg/day} = 72 \, \text{TPD}$$

**Step 3 — Apply Biomethanation Efficiency (1 Mark)**

$$BOD_{out} = BOD_{in} \times (1 - \eta) = 40{,}000 \times (1 - 0.85)$$

$$BOD_{out} = 40{,}000 \times 0.15 = 6{,}000 \, \text{mg/L}$$

**Step 4 — Compare with CPCB Land Application Standard (1 Mark)**

| Parameter | Calculated | CPCB Standard | Compliance |
|-----------|-----------|---------------|-----------|
| BOD₃ at 27 °C | 6,000 mg/L | ≤ 5,000 mg/L | **NOT COMPLIANT** |

**Step 5 — Conclusion (3 Marks)**

The treated spent wash **DOES NOT meet** the CPCB standard of BOD ≤ 5,000 mg/L. The distillery must adopt one or more of the following options:

- **Option 1:** Upgrade biomethanation efficiency to **≥ 87.5%** to achieve BOD = 5,000 mg/L.
- **Option 2:** Install a **Multiple Effect Evaporator (MEE)** followed by **composter** to achieve **Zero Liquid Discharge (ZLD)** and produce bio-compost.
- **Option 3:** **Recycle treated spent wash** for fertigation on company's own land, post-pH correction and TDS monitoring.

**Required additional reduction:**

$$\Delta BOD = 6{,}000 - 5{,}000 = 1{,}000 \, \text{mg/L (extra removal needed)}$$

$$\eta_{required} = \dfrac{40{,}000 - 5{,}000}{40{,}000} \times 100 = 87.5\%$$

**Required upgrade = 87.5% − 85% = 2.5% additional BOD removal.**

---

### **Question B (14 Marks)** — Alternative Choice

**Q.B.(a)** What is **Zero Liquid Discharge (ZLD)**? Describe the **ZLD system** adopted by **distilleries** with a neat flow diagram. List the CPCB standards for land application of treated spent wash. **[7 Marks]** `[KTU University Exam - June 2023]`

**Model Solution (7 Marks):**

**1. Definition of ZLD (1 Mark):**
**Zero Liquid Discharge (ZLD)** is a wastewater treatment strategy in which **all effluent streams are treated and recycled** within the plant, with **no liquid effluent being discharged** to the environment. The only output is **solid waste** (bio-compost) and **water vapor** (from evaporators).

**2. ZLD Process Flow for Distillery (4 Marks):**

| Step | Process | Function |
|------|---------|----------|
| 1 | **Spent Wash Generation** | Raw effluent from distillation column |
| 2 | **Biomethanation (Anaerobic)** | Reduces BOD by 80–90%; produces biogas (CMP/CHP use) |
| 3 | **Multiple Effect Evaporator (MEE)** | Concentrates spent wash from 8–10% solids to 50–60% solids |
| 4 | **Agitated Thin Film Dryer (ATFD) or Spray Dryer** | Produces **bio-compost (powder/granules)** at 10–15% moisture |
| 5 | **Condensate Polishing (RO/MEE condensate treatment)** | RO permeate → recycled; RO reject → back to MEE |
| 6 | **Bio-compost Storage and Dispatch** | Used as organic fertilizer |

**3. CPCB Standards for Land Application of Treated Spent Wash / Bio-compost (2 Marks):**

| Parameter | Limit |
|-----------|-------|
| BOD₃ at 27 °C | **≤ 5,000 mg/L** (for liquid spent wash) |
| pH | **6.5 – 8.5** |
| Total Nitrogen (as N) | **≤ 0.5%** of bio-compost dry weight |
| Total Phosphorus (as P₂O₅) | **≤ 0.5%** |
| Moisture content (bio-compost) | **≤ 35%** |
| Heavy metals (Pb, Cd, Hg, Cr) | **Traces (FCO limits)** |
| Application rate | **≤ 50 tonnes/hectare/year** (on agricultural land) |

---

**Q.B.(b)** A **dairy plant** discharges **500 m³/day** of effluent with BOD = **1500 mg/L** and TSS = **800 mg/L** to a municipal sewer (connected to CETP). The CETP requires inlet parameters: BOD ≤ **800 mg/L** and TSS ≤ **400 mg/L**. Design the **primary treatment** units (bar screen, grit chamber, primary clarifier) to achieve compliance. **[7 Marks]** `[KTU University Exam - June 2024]`

**Model Solution (7 Marks):**

**Step 1 — Identify Treatment Goal (1 Mark)**

| Parameter | Raw | CETP Limit | Removal Required |
|-----------|-----|-----------|------------------|
| BOD | 1500 mg/L | 800 mg/L | **~47%** (at least) |
| TSS | 800 mg/L | 400 mg/L | **50%** |

**Step 2 — Bar Screen Design (1 Mark)**

- Assume approach velocity = 0.6 m/s, bar spacing = 20 mm.
$$A_c = \dfrac{Q}{v} = \dfrac{500/(24 \times 3600)}{0.6} = \dfrac{0.00579}{0.6} = 0.00965 \, \text{m}^2$$
- Width = 0.6 m, depth = 0.016 m → use 1.0 m × 0.5 m with submerged depth 0.05 m.
- Trash rack at 45° inclination.

**Step 3 — Grit Chamber Design (1 Mark)**

- Detention time = **45–60 seconds** (for food industry with organic matter, 45 s preferred).
$$V = Q \times t = 0.00579 \times 45 = 0.26 \, \text{m}^3$$
- Take depth = 0.5 m → Length = $0.26/0.5 = 0.52 \, \text{m}$, take **L = 1.0 m**, **B = 0.5 m**, **D = 0.5 m**.

**Step 4 — Primary Clarifier Design (3 Marks)**

- Use **circular clarifier**, surface overflow rate (SOR) = 25 m³/m²/day.
$$A = \dfrac{Q}{SOR} = \dfrac{500}{25} = 20 \, \text{m}^2$$
- Diameter: $D = \sqrt{4A/\pi} = \sqrt{4 \times 20/3.14} = \sqrt{25.48} = 5.05 \, \text{m}$
- Take **D = 6.0 m**, **Side water depth (SWD) = 3.0 m**, **free board = 0.3 m**, **total height = 3.3 m**.
- Detention time check: $V = \pi/4 \times 6^2 \times 3 = 84.8 \, \text{m}^3$, $t = 84.8/0.00579 = 14,646 \, s = 4.07 \, \text{hr}$ (acceptable: 2–4 hr).

**Step 5 — Verify Removal Efficiency (1 Mark)**

| Parameter | After Primary | CETP Limit | Status |
|-----------|--------------|-----------|--------|
| BOD | 1500 × 0.65 = 975 mg/L | 800 mg/L | Slightly over (needs biological polishing) |
| TSS | 800 × 0.50 = 400 mg/L | 400 mg/L | Just meets limit |

**Step 6 — Recommendation (1 Mark)**
Add a **trickling filter or aerated lagoon** as **secondary treatment** post-primary clarifier to further reduce BOD to ~600 mg/L for safe discharge to CETP. For direct discharge to inland water, install **Activated Sludge Process (ASP)** to achieve BOD <100 mg/L.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls:**
> 1. **Forgetting Temperature Correction** in BOD problems — Indian standards use **27 °C/3 days**, not the international **20 °C/5 days**. Marks deducted if not converted.
> 2. **Stack Height Formula Mismatch** — Use $H = 14 Q^{0.27}$ (CPCB); wrong formula loses 2 marks.
> 3. **ZLD Definition** — Students often confuse ZLD with "zero pollution"; it actually means "no liquid discharge" but solid waste is still generated.
> 4. **Missing BOD₃ vs BOD₅** — Always state which test is used.
> 5. **Confusing noise standards** — Industrial zone is **75 dB(A) Day / 70 dB(A) Night**, not the residential values.
> 6. **Calculation units** — Mixing mg/L with kg/day without conversion is a frequent 1-mark loss.

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — Must Memorize for KTU Board Exam:**

- **Regulatory Trio:** MoEFCC → CPCB → SPCB (hierarchical implementation).
- **Three Statutory Acts:** Water (1974), Air (1981), Environment Protection (1986).
- **Consent Mechanism:** CTE → CTO (5-year validity, both mandatory).
- **Industry Classification:** Distillery = **Red**; Dairy/Sugar/Brewery = **Orange**; Bakery/Flour mill = **Green**.
- **Universal Effluent Limits for Food Industries (most categories):**
  - **pH = 6.5 – 8.5**
  - **BOD₃ at 27 °C ≤ 100 mg/L**
  - **COD ≤ 250 mg/L**
  - **TSS ≤ 100 mg/L**
  - **Oil & Grease ≤ 10 mg/L**
- **Distillery Exception:** **Zero Liquid Discharge (ZLD) mandatory**; bio-compost BOD ≤ 5,000 mg/L for land application.
- **Fish Processing** has **stricter** limits: BOD ≤ **50 mg/L** (not 100).
- **Stack Height Formula:** $H = 14 \cdot Q^{0.27}$ (in metres; Q in t/h).
- **Boiler Emission:** PM ≤ 75 mg/Nm³ (oil), ≤ 115 mg/Nm³ (coal).
- **Noise Standards (Industrial):** 75 dB(A) day / 70 dB(A) night.
- **NAAQS (Industrial, 24-hr):** PM₁₀ = 100 µg/m³; PM₂.₅ = 60 µg/m³; SO₂ = 80 µg/m³; NOx = 80 µg/m³.
- **PeCOD/BOD Relationship:** Use $BOD_L = BOD_5/(1 - e^{-k \cdot 5})$ for first-order kinetics; rate constant **k = 0.23/day (typical) for domestic sewage**.
- **BOD Temperature Correction:** Multiply by $(1.047)^{(T-20)}$ for every degree shift.
- **F/M Ratio** for activated sludge: **0.2–0.5/day** (high-rate) or **0.05–0.15/day** (extended aeration).
- **HRT for Aerobic:** 4–8 hr; **Anaerobic:** 15–30 days.
- **Sludge Volume Calculation:** $V_{sludge} = \dfrac{\text{Dry solids (kg/day)}}{\rho_{sludge} \times P_{solids}}$
- **Sewage BOD per capita:** 0.08 kg/day (BIS standard).
- **Population Equivalent (PE)** formula: useful for sizing ETPs.
- **Bio-compost application rate:** ≤ 50 tonnes/hectare/year.
- **KSPCB Online Monitoring (OCEMS)** mandatory for **Red category** units with capital investment > ₹50 crores.
- **Penalty under E(P) Act:** Imprisonment up to 7 years + fine up to ₹1 crore (latest 2024 amendments).
- **EIA Notification 2006** (amended 2020): Food processing units > **1 lakh tonnes/annum** require **Environmental Impact Assessment + Environmental Clearance**.
- **CETP Concept:** Common Effluent Treatment Plant for industrial clusters — Kerala has CETPs at **Eloor, Ambalamedu, Cochin Special Economic Zone**.
- **Schemes of CPCB:** **PRANA (Portal for Regulation of Air and Noise Assessment)** for online consent management in Kerala.
- **Net-Zero Compliance Trend:** FSSAI + CPCB pushing food industry toward **circular economy** and **ZLD by 2025–2030**.

> [!NOTE]
> **Final KTU Exam Strategy:** Always quote the **specific industry** and its **CPCB prescribed value**. Generic answers like "BOD should be low" do NOT earn full marks. Numerical problems in Module 2 mostly test the **stack height formula, BOD temperature correction, ETP removal efficiency, and dilution equation** — practice at least 5 of each!
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Characterization of food industries effluents
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEFTT744 - FOOD INDUSTRY WASTE MANAGEMENT
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Characterization of food industries effluents

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Characterization of Food Industry Effluents** is the systematic, quantitative, and qualitative assessment of the physical, chemical, and biological properties of wastewater discharged from food processing industries. It involves the determination of parameters such as **pH, Biochemical Oxygen Demand (BOD), Chemical Oxygen Demand (COD), Total Suspended Solids (TSS), Total Dissolved Solids (TDS), Total Organic Carbon (TOC), nutrients (Nitrogen and Phosphorus), oils & grease, color, odor, temperature, and heavy metals** to evaluate pollution strength, design appropriate treatment systems, and ensure compliance with regulatory discharge standards (e.g., **CPCB** norms in India).

According to the **KTU 2024 Scheme syllabus (PEFTT744)**, characterization is the foundational step in any wastewater management plan because the *type* and *concentration* of pollutants directly dictate the selection, sizing, and operating cost of primary, secondary, and tertiary treatment units.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine you are a **doctor diagnosing a patient**. Before prescribing medicine, you must first run blood tests, check vital signs, and identify the disease. Similarly, an environmental engineer cannot treat food industry wastewater "blindly." The factory effluent is the "patient," and characterization is the "blood report."

- If the **BOD/COD ratio is high (≈0.6)** → the waste is *biodegradable* → use biological treatment (like activated sludge).
- If **pH is extreme (e.g., 2 or 12)** → the waste is *corrosive* → needs neutralization first.
- If **TSS is high** → the waste has *particulates* → needs sedimentation/filtration.

> [!NOTE]
> **Why characterize?** Because the food industry is uniquely diverse — a dairy plant produces *white, high-protein* effluent, a distillery produces *dark, high-BOD* effluent, and a seafood plant produces *salty, high-nitrogen* effluent. **One size does not fit all.** Characterization allows tailored treatment design.

## 1.3 Key Physical & Chemical Metrics in Characterization

| Parameter | Symbol | Standard Unit | Significance |
|-----------|--------|---------------|--------------|
| Hydrogen Ion Concentration | pH | dimensionless (0–14) | Acidity/Alkalinity |
| Biochemical Oxygen Demand | BOD | mg/L (ppm) | Biodegradable organics |
| Chemical Oxygen Demand | COD | mg/L (ppm) | Total oxidizable organics |
| Total Suspended Solids | TSS | mg/L | Particulate matter |
| Total Dissolved Solids | TDS | mg/L | Dissolved salts & organics |
| Total Organic Carbon | TOC | mg/L C | Total carbon content |
| Fats, Oils & Grease | FOG | mg/L | Floating/surface pollutants |
| Total Nitrogen | TN | mg/L as N | Nutrient load |
| Total Phosphorus | TP | mg/L as P | Nutrient load |
| Temperature | T | °C | Affects biological activity |

> [!TIP]
> **KTU Examiner Insight:** Questions on characterization are often *definition-based* (2 marks) or *comparison-based* (e.g., "Compare BOD and COD" — 7 marks). Always state the **5-day BOD (BOD₅)** standard and the **standard temperature (20 °C)** when defining BOD.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Classification of Characterization Parameters

Characterization is broadly classified into three domains, each probing a different aspect of pollution:

### 2.1.1 Physical Characterization
Physical parameters describe the **bulk observable properties** of the effluent. They are typically the first set of measurements taken at the plant site.

- **pH**: Measure of hydrogen ion activity; influences toxicity, microbial growth, and chemical precipitation.
  - *Food industry typical range:* **4.0 – 11.0**
  - *Indian Standard (IS 2490):* discharge limit **5.5 – 9.0**
- **Temperature**: Influences reaction kinetics, dissolved oxygen (DO) saturation, and biological treatment efficiency.
- **Color**: Indicates presence of dissolved organics (e.g., molasses, caramel, dyes).
- **Odor**: Indicates putrefaction, anaerobic conditions, or specific raw materials (e.g., fish meal).
- **Total Solids (TS) = TSS + TDS**:
  - **TSS** (mg/L) — solids retained on a **1.2 µm glass-fiber filter** (dried at 103–105 °C).
  - **TDS** (mg/L) — solids passing through the filter (evaporated at 180 °C).
- **Turbidity** (NTU) — optical property indicating suspended matter.

### 2.1.2 Chemical Characterization
Chemical parameters quantify the **pollutant load** and are the most critical for treatment design.

- **Biochemical Oxygen Demand (BOD)**: Mass of dissolved oxygen consumed by microorganisms during the **biochemical oxidation of organic matter** over a specified period (standard: **5 days at 20 °C → BOD₅**).
  - *Typical food industry range:* **500 – 60,000 mg/L** (varies drastically with industry)
- **Chemical Oxygen Demand (COD)**: Mass of oxygen consumed during the **chemical oxidation** of organic matter using a strong oxidant (typically **potassium dichromate, K₂Cr₂O₇, in H₂SO₄**).
  - COD ≈ 1.5 × to 2.0 × BOD for biodegradable wastes
- **BOD/COD Ratio (Biodegradability Index)**:
  - > 0.5 → **Biodegradable** → use biological treatment
  - 0.3 – 0.5 → **Moderately biodegradable** → may need acclimatization
  - < 0.3 → **Poorly biodegradable / toxic** → use physico-chemical treatment
- **Total Organic Carbon (TOC)**: Direct measure of organic carbon using combustion or wet oxidation. Faster than BOD/COD.
  - Relationship: **BOD ≈ 1.5 × to 2.0 × TOC** (approx.)
- **Nutrients (N & P)**: Essential for biological treatment; **C:N:P = 100:5:1** is the optimal ratio for activated sludge. Food wastes are often **N-deficient** but **P-balanced**.
- **Fats, Oils & Grease (FOG)**: Extracted using **n-hexane**; causes fouling of pipes and treatment units.
- **Heavy metals**: From cleaning agents (Na, K), equipment (Fe, Cr, Ni), and raw materials.

### 2.1.3 Biological Characterization
- **Microbial Load**: Total viable count (TVC) using plate count agar.
- **Biodegradability Tests**: Respirometry, BOD/COD ratio, methane yield (BMP test).
- **Toxicity Tests**: Using *Daphnia magna*, fish bioassays (LC₅₀).

## 2.2 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The following table is a **must-memorize** resource for KTU 2024 ESE and internal assessments. The escape `\vert` is used in place of the vertical pipe symbol to keep the markdown table intact.

| # | Formula / Parameter | Expression | Standard / Unit | Engineering Use |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | **BOD₅ (5-day BOD)** | $BOD_5 = (DO_i - DO_f) \times \text{Dilution Factor}$ | mg/L | Quantifies biodegradable organics |
| 2 | **COD (Dichromate method)** | $COD = \dfrac{(A - B) \times N \times 8000}{V}$ | mg/L | Total oxidizable organics |
| 3 | **BOD/COD Ratio** | $R = \dfrac{BOD_5}{COD}$ | dimensionless | Biodegradability indicator |
| 4 | **Total Solids** | $TS = TSS + TDS$ | mg/L | Solids loading on treatment |
| 5 | **Volatile Suspended Solids** | $VSS = SS_d - SS_v$ | mg/L | Estimate of biomass in MLSS |
| 6 | **TOC → BOD correlation** | $BOD_5 \approx 1.7 \times TOC$ | mg/L | Rapid estimation |
| 7 | **F/M Ratio** | $F/M = \dfrac{S_0}{\theta_c \cdot X}$ | day⁻¹ | Activated sludge design |
| 8 | **C:N:P optimum** | $C:N:P = 100:5:1$ | ratio | Biological nutrient balance |
| 9 | **Population Equivalent (PE)** | $PE = \dfrac{BOD_5 \text{ of industry (kg/day)}}{0.08 \text{ kg/person/day}}$ | persons | Scale equivalence |
| 10 | **Dilution Factor** | $DF = \dfrac{\text{Volume of sample} + \text{Volume of dilution water}}{\text{Volume of sample}}$ | dimensionless | BOD bottle calculation |
| 11 | **Henderson-Hasselbalch (pH)** | $pH = pK_a + \log \dfrac{[A^-]}{[HA]}$ | dimensionless | Buffer capacity estimation |
| 12 | **Specific Oxygen Uptake Rate (SOUR)** | $SOUR = \dfrac{OUR}{MLVSS}$ | mg O₂/g VSS·h | Sludge activity test |

> [!TIP]
> **Remember:** For BOD calculation, the dilution water must be **saturated with DO** (≈ 9.2 mg/L at 20 °C), seeded with acclimatized microbes (if needed for toxic samples), and incubated at **20 ± 1 °C** for **5 days** in the dark.

## 2.3 Real-World Utility of Characterization

Characterization data is the **engineering blueprint** for:

1. **Treatment Plant Design** — determining reactor volume, aeration capacity, and chemical doses.
2. **Cost Estimation** — high COD → high aeration cost → high electricity bill.
3. **Regulatory Compliance** — meeting **CPCB** discharge norms under the **Water (Prevention and Control of Pollution) Act, 1974**.
4. **Process Optimization** — identifying opportunities for in-plant waste minimization and **By-Product Recovery (BPR)**.
5. **Environmental Impact Assessment (EIA)** — predicting downstream river/lake water quality.

> [!NOTE]
> **Industry-Specific Examples (High-Yield for KTU):**
> - **Dairy Industry:** BOD ≈ 1,000–3,000 mg/L, white color, high TSS, lactose-rich.
> - **Distillery (Spent Wash):** BOD ≈ 40,000–60,000 mg/L, dark brown, acidic (pH 3.5–4.5), high TDS.
> - **Meat & Poultry:** BOD ≈ 1,500–4,000 mg/L, high FOG, blood-rich, pathogenic.
> - **Fruits & Vegetables (Cannery):** BOD ≈ 500–2,000 mg/L, seasonal variation, suspended solids, sugars.
> - **Seafood Processing:** BOD ≈ 1,000–3,000 mg/L, salty (high Cl⁻), high N.
> - **Brewery:** BOD ≈ 1,500–3,500 mg/L, yellowish, N-deficient, rich in sugars.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1: BOD₅ Stoichiometry & BOD₅/Ultimate BOD Relationship

The biochemical oxidation of organic matter follows first-order kinetics. Consider a generic organic substrate represented as $C_xH_yO_z$ being oxidized by aerobic bacteria.

### Step 1: Write the Generalized Oxidation Reaction
A representative carbohydrate-based organic compound (e.g., glucose, $C_6H_{12}O_6$) is oxidized as:

$$C_6H_{12}O_6 + 6\,O_2 \xrightarrow{\text{microbes}} 6\,CO_2 + 6\,H_2O + \text{energy}$$

**Conversion Logic:** This stoichiometry shows that **1 mole of glucose consumes 6 moles of O₂** to complete oxidation. The molecular weights are:
- Glucose (C₆H₁₂O₆): **180 g/mol**
- O₂: **32 g/mol**

### Step 2: Compute Theoretical Oxygen Demand per gram of Glucose

The theoretical oxygen demand per gram of glucose is:

$$ThOD = \dfrac{6 \times 32}{180} = \dfrac{192}{180} = 1.067 \;\dfrac{g\,O_2}{g\,glucose}$$

**Conversion Logic:** Therefore, **1.067 g of O₂** is needed to fully oxidize **1 g of glucose**. In real wastewater, not all organics are glucose; hence the measured **BOD** is always less than the **ThOD** because some carbon is assimilated into new biomass.

### Step 3: BOD Rate Equation (First-Order Kinetics)

The amount of oxygen remaining to be consumed at time *t* follows:

$$L_t = L_0 \cdot e^{-k\,t}$$

where:
- $L_t$ = remaining oxygen demand (BOD remaining) at time *t* (mg/L)
- $L_0$ = ultimate first-stage BOD (BOD_L) at *t = 0* (mg/L)
- $k$ = deoxygenation rate constant (day⁻¹); typical range **0.1 – 0.5 day⁻¹** at 20 °C
- $t$ = time in days

### Step 4: BOD Exerted up to Time *t*

The BOD *exerted* (consumed) at time *t* is the difference:

$$BOD_t = L_0 - L_t = L_0 \cdot (1 - e^{-k\,t})$$

**Conversion Logic:** At $t = 5$ days, the standard 5-day BOD becomes:

$$BOD_5 = L_0 \cdot (1 - e^{-5k})$$

### Step 5: Relate BOD₅ to Ultimate BOD (L₀)

Rearranging for $L_0$:

$$L_0 = \dfrac{BOD_5}{1 - e^{-5k}}$$

**Worked Numerical Example:**

Suppose a food industry effluent has $BOD_5 = 2{,}000$ mg/L and $k = 0.23\;\text{day}^{-1}$ at 20 °C.

Compute $e^{-5k}$:

$$e^{-5 \times 0.23} = e^{-1.15} = 0.3166$$

Compute $1 - e^{-5k}$:

$$1 - 0.3166 = 0.6834$$

Compute $L_0$:

$$L_0 = \dfrac{2000}{0.6834} \approx 2{,}926.4\;\text{mg/L}$$

**Conversion Logic:** The *ultimate* BOD is approximately **2,926 mg/L**, which is ~46% higher than BOD₅. This means about **31.66% of the organic load remains unoxidized** after 5 days, useful for sizing stabilization ponds or extended aeration systems.

> [!TIP]
> **Temperature correction for k:** $k_T = k_{20} \cdot (1.047)^{(T-20)}$ (van't Hoff-Arrhenius approximation). KTU problems often give 25 °C data — convert *k* back to 20 °C.

---

## 3.2 Derivation 2: COD Determination (Closed Reflux Dichromate Method)

### Step 1: Oxidation Reaction with Dichromate

$$Cr_2O_7^{2-} + 14\,H^+ + 6\,e^- \rightarrow 2\,Cr^{3+} + 7\,H_2O$$

**Conversion Logic:** Each dichromate ion ($Cr_2O_7^{2-}$) accepts **6 electrons**. The equivalent weight of oxygen (1 electron) is **8 g/eq**, so 1 mole of $Cr_2O_7^{2-}$ provides an oxidizing capacity equivalent to **6 × 8 = 48 g of O₂** per mole.

### Step 2: Standard COD Formula

For a titration-based COD determination:

$$COD \;\left(\text{mg/L}\right) = \dfrac{(A - B) \times N \times 8000}{V_{sample}}$$

where:
- $A$ = volume of FAS (Ferrous Ammonium Sulfate) titrant for blank (mL)
- $B$ = volume of FAS titrant for sample (mL)
- $N$ = normality of FAS (typically **0.025 N** or **0.1 N**)
- $V_{sample}$ = volume of digestion mixture (mL, typically **20 mL**)
- **8000** = milliequivalent weight of O₂ × 1000 (mL/L conversion)

**Conversion Logic:** Each mL of 1 N FAS is equivalent to **1 meq of oxygen**, and **1 meq O₂ = 8 mg O₂**. The 8000 factor rolls up the unit conversions (meq → mg and L → mL).

### Step 3: Worked Numerical Example

Given:
- Blank titration: $A = 24.6$ mL
- Sample titration: $B = 12.8$ mL
- Normality of FAS: $N = 0.1$ N
- Sample volume: $V = 20$ mL

Compute $(A - B)$:

$$A - B = 24.6 - 12.8 = 11.8\;\text{mL}$$

Compute numerator:

$$(A - B) \times N \times 8000 = 11.8 \times 0.1 \times 8000 = 9{,}440$$

Compute COD:

$$COD = \dfrac{9{,}440}{20} = 472\;\text{mg/L}$$

**Conversion Logic:** The food effluent therefore has a **COD of 472 mg/L**, classifying it as a *medium-strength* wastewater suitable for biological treatment (typical food industry COD: 500 – 60,000 mg/L).

---

## 3.3 Analytical Matrix: Typical Food Industry Effluent Characteristics

| Industry | pH | BOD₅ (mg/L) | COD (mg/L) | TSS (mg/L) | FOG (mg/L) | TN (mg/L) | Special |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| **Dairy** | 6.5 – 9.0 | 1,000 – 3,000 | 2,000 – 5,000 | 500 – 1,500 | 100 – 500 | 50 – 200 | Lactose, white color |
| **Distillery (Spent Wash)** | 3.5 – 4.5 | 40,000 – 60,000 | 80,000 – 120,000 | 10,000 – 50,000 | Low | 1,000 – 2,000 | Dark brown, melanoidins |
| **Brewery** | 4.0 – 7.0 | 1,500 – 3,500 | 3,000 – 6,000 | 200 – 1,000 | 50 – 200 | 30 – 100 | Sugar-rich, N-deficient |
| **Meat & Poultry** | 6.5 – 8.5 | 1,500 – 4,000 | 2,500 – 8,000 | 800 – 3,000 | 500 – 2,000 | 200 – 500 | Pathogens, blood, high FOG |
| **Seafood Processing** | 6.0 – 8.0 | 1,000 – 3,000 | 2,000 – 5,000 | 500 – 2,000 | 200 – 800 | 200 – 600 | High Cl⁻ (salt), chitin |
| **Fruit & Veg. Canning** | 5.0 – 8.0 | 500 – 2,000 | 800 – 4,000 | 300 – 1,500 | 50 – 200 | 50 – 150 | Seasonal, pectin |
| **Starch / Sago** | 4.0 – 6.0 | 2,000 – 5,000 | 4,000 – 10,000 | 3,000 – 8,000 | Low | 100 – 300 | High carbohydrates |
| **Sugar Mill** | 6.0 – 8.0 | 1,000 – 4,000 | 2,000 – 7,000 | 500 – 2,000 | 100 – 300 | 50 – 200 | Seasonal, sucrose |
| **Edible Oil Refinery** | 4.0 – 9.0 | 500 – 2,500 | 1,000 – 5,000 | 500 – 1,500 | 2,000 – 10,000 | 30 – 100 | Very high FOG, soaps |
| **Bakery / Confectionery** | 5.0 – 8.0 | 800 – 3,000 | 1,500 – 5,000 | 300 – 1,000 | 200 – 800 | 30 – 100 | Sugars, flour |

> [!WARNING]
> **Pitfall:** Many students incorrectly use **the same BOD range** for all food industries. KTU specifically asks **industry-wise** comparison — always specify *which* industry and *which* parameter you are discussing.

---

## 3.4 Sampling & Analysis Protocol (KTU Practical Emphasis)

1. **Grab Sample** — single instantaneous collection; used for spot-checking pH, DO, temperature.
2. **Composite Sample** — multiple grabs mixed over **24 hours** (proportional to flow); used for BOD, COD, TSS.
3. **Preservation:**
   - BOD → 4 °C, analyze within **24–48 hours**
   - COD → acidify with H₂SO₄ to pH < 2
   - TSS → 4 °C, analyze within **7 days**
4. **Standard Methods (APHA, 23rd Edition, 2017):**
   - BOD₅ → 5210 B
   - COD → 5220 D (Closed Reflux, Colorimetric)
   - TSS → 2540 D
   - TDS → 2540 C
   - FOG → 5520 B (Partition-Gravimetric)
   - TOC → 5310 B (High-Temperature Combustion)
5. **Quality Control:** Always run **duplicate samples**, **blanks**, and **spiked samples** for accuracy check (recovery: 80–120%).
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Mermaid Diagram: Characterization Framework for Food Industry Effluents

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Food Industry Effluent] --> B[Sample Collection]
    B --> C[Physical Analysis]
    B --> D[Chemical Analysis]
    B --> E[Biological Analysis]
    C --> F[pH Temperature Color Odor TSS TDS Turbidity]
    D --> G[BOD5 COD TOC Nutrients FOG Heavy Metals]
    E --> H[Microbial Load Biodegradability Toxicity Tests]
    F --> I[Data Interpretation]
    G --> I
    H --> I
    I --> J{Is Effluent Biodegradable?}
    J --> K[BOD/COD > 0.5 Yes]
    J --> L[BOD/COD < 0.3 No]
    K --> M[Design Biological Treatment Activated Sludge or Bioreactor]
    L --> N[Design Physico Chemical Treatment Coagulation or Membrane]
    M --> O[Effluent Discharge Compliance Check vs CPCB Norms]
    N --> O
    O --> P[Final Treatment Plant Operation]
```

## 4.2 Mermaid Diagram: Step-by-Step Analytical Workflow

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A1[Step 1 On Site Grab Sample] --> A2[Step 2 Immediate pH DO and Temperature Measurement]
    A2 --> A3[Step 3 Composite Sample 24 hour Flow Proportional]
    A3 --> A4[Step 4 Preservation 4 degree Celsius or H2SO4 Acidification]
    A4 --> A5[Step 5 Laboratory Analysis]
    A5 --> A6[Step 5a BOD5 Incubation at 20 degree Celsius for 5 days]
    A5 --> A7[Step 5b COD Closed Reflux Dichromate 2 hour Digestion at 150 degree Celsius]
    A5 --> A8[Step 5c TSS and TDS Gravimetric Oven Drying]
    A5 --> A9[Step 5d FOG n Hexane Solvent Extraction]
    A6 --> A10[Step 6 Data Compilation and Report Generation]
    A7 --> A10
    A8 --> A10
    A9 --> A10
    A10 --> A11[Step 7 Compliance Check Against IS 2490 and CPCB Standards]
    A11 --> A12[Step 8 Treatment Plant Design and Sizing]
```

## 4.3 Block-Level Functional Architecture: Industry-Wise Effluent Comparison

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    subgraph Dairy[Dairy Industry Effluent]
        D1[High BOD 1000 to 3000]
        D2[White Coloured]
        D3[Lactose and Protein Rich]
        D4[High TSS]
    end
    subgraph Dist[Distillery Spent Wash]
        D5[Very High BOD 40000 to 60000]
        D6[Dark Brown Colour]
        D7[Low pH 3.5 to 4.5]
        D8[High TDS and Melanoidins]
    end
    subgraph Meat[Meat and Poultry]
        D9[High FOG 500 to 2000]
        D10[Pathogenic Load]
        D11[Blood Rich Effluent]
    end
    subgraph Sea[Seafood]
        D12[High Chloride Salt]
        D13[High Nitrogen]
        D14[Chitin and Shells]
    end
    subgraph BPR[Common Treatment Goals]
        D15[Reduce BOD and COD]
        D16[Remove FOG by Skimming]
        D17[Nutrient Balancing C N P]
        D18[Pathogen Disinfection]
    end
    Dairy --> BPR
    Dist --> BPR
    Meat --> BPR
    Sea --> BPR
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Reading the Diagrams:** Each box represents a category of effluents or a treatment goal. The arrows show the *flow of information* from sampling → analysis → interpretation → treatment design. In KTU exams, drawing a clean Mermaid-style flowchart (or a labeled hand-drawn block diagram) fetches **full marks** in 14-mark descriptive questions.
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A: Short-Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### **Q1. [KTU University Exam – May 2024]**
**Define BOD₅ and state its significance in the characterization of food industry effluents.**

**Model Answer:**
BOD₅ (5-day Biochemical Oxygen Demand) is defined as the amount of dissolved oxygen (DO) consumed by aerobic microorganisms during the biochemical oxidation of organic matter in a water sample over **5 days at a standard temperature of 20 °C in the dark**.

$$BOD_5 = (DO_{initial} - DO_{final}) \times \text{Dilution Factor} \quad (\text{mg/L})$$

**Significance in Food Industry Effluent Characterization:**

1. **Pollution strength indicator** — A high BOD₅ (>1,000 mg/L) indicates a *high-strength* wastewater requiring extensive treatment.
2. **Treatability assessment** — Combined with COD, it gives the **biodegradability index** (BOD/COD ratio). If > 0.5, biological treatment is feasible.
3. **Treatment unit sizing** — Used to compute aeration tank volume in activated sludge, and to estimate oxygen demand (kg O₂/day).
4. **Regulatory compliance** — CPCB discharge limits for BOD in food industry effluents range from **30 to 100 mg/L** depending on the receiving water body.

**Example:** Dairy effluent typically has BOD₅ = 1,500 mg/L → must be reduced to < 100 mg/L before discharge.

---

### **Q2. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**
**List and briefly explain any three physical parameters used in the characterization of food industry effluents.**

**Model Answer:**

| # | Parameter | Explanation |
|---|-----------|-------------|
| 1 | **pH** | A measure of hydrogen ion concentration (dimensionless, 0–14 scale). Food industry pH varies widely: distillery waste is acidic (pH 3.5–4.5), while bakery waste can be alkaline (pH 8–9). Discharge limits: **5.5 – 9.0 (IS 2490)**. |
| 2 | **Total Suspended Solids (TSS)** | The dry weight (mg/L) of solids retained on a **1.2 µm glass-fiber filter** after drying at 103–105 °C. High TSS (e.g., 500–3,000 mg/L in meat processing) increases sludge load and reduces DO in receiving waters. |
| 3 | **Temperature** | Reported in °C. Food processing effluents are often warm (30–60 °C) due to CIP (cleaning-in-place) operations. High temperatures **reduce DO solubility** and **inhibit biological treatment**; discharge limit: < 40 °C at the point of mixing. |

---

## 5.2 Part B: Descriptive Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice)

### **Question A (14 Marks) — [KTU University Exam – May 2024]**

**(a) [7 Marks] Explain in detail the chemical characterization of food industry effluents. List at least five chemical parameters with their typical ranges and significance.**

**Model Answer:**

Chemical characterization quantifies the **pollutant load** and forms the basis for treatment design. The principal parameters are:

1. **Biochemical Oxygen Demand (BOD₅)**
   - Definition: Oxygen consumed by microbes in 5 days at 20 °C.
   - Range: 500 – 60,000 mg/L.
   - Significance: Determines biodegradable organic load; essential for biological treatment design.

2. **Chemical Oxygen Demand (COD)**
   - Definition: Oxygen consumed by chemical oxidation using **K₂Cr₂O₇/H₂SO₄**.
   - Range: 1,000 – 120,000 mg/L.
   - Significance: Measures *total* organics (biodegradable + non-biodegradable). Faster than BOD (2 hours vs. 5 days).

3. **Total Organic Carbon (TOC)**
   - Definition: Mass of organic carbon in mg/L.
   - Relationship: **BOD₅ ≈ 1.5 – 2.0 × TOC** for typical food waste.
   - Significance: Rapid, accurate alternative to BOD/COD.

4. **Fats, Oils & Grease (FOG)**
   - Definition: Solvent-extractable (n-hexane) non-polar materials.
   - Range: 100 – 10,000 mg/L (highest in edible oil refineries).
   - Significance: Causes pipe blockages, scum formation, and microbial inhibition; must be removed before biological treatment.

5. **Nutrients (Nitrogen & Phosphorus)**
   - Total Nitrogen (TN): 30 – 2,000 mg/L.
   - Total Phosphorus (TP): 5 – 100 mg/L.
   - Significance: Required for biological growth (C:N:P = 100:5:1); excess causes **eutrophication** in receiving waters.

6. **pH and Alkalinity**
   - pH influences chemical speciation and microbial activity.

**(b) [7 Marks] A food industry effluent sample (20 mL) is mixed with 280 mL of dilution water. Initial DO = 8.4 mg/L. After 5 days at 20 °C, final DO = 3.2 mg/L. Calculate the BOD₅ of the sample. If the COD is 1,200 mg/L, comment on its biodegradability and suggest a suitable treatment method.**

**Model Answer:**

**Step 1: Calculate the Dilution Factor (DF).**

$$DF = \dfrac{\text{Volume of diluted sample}}{\text{Volume of original sample}} = \dfrac{20 + 280}{20} = \dfrac{300}{20} = 15$$

**Conversion Logic:** DF = 15 means 1 part of sample is diluted in 15 parts total.

**Step 2: Calculate BOD₅.**

$$BOD_5 = (DO_i - DO_f) \times DF$$

$$BOD_5 = (8.4 - 3.2) \times 15 = 5.2 \times 15 = 78\;\text{mg/L}$$

**Step 3: Compute the BOD/COD Ratio.**

$$R = \dfrac{BOD_5}{COD} = \dfrac{78}{1{,}200} = 0.065$$

**Step 4: Interpret the Biodegradability Index.**

Since **R = 0.065 < 0.3**, the effluent is **poorly biodegradable** (possibly containing toxic compounds, chemicals, or slowly degrading organics).

**Step 5: Suggest a Suitable Treatment Method.**

- **Primary treatment:** Screening, grit removal, oil & grease skimming.
- **Secondary treatment:** **Not recommended to use direct biological treatment** due to low BOD/COD ratio. Instead, use:
  - **Physico-chemical treatment:** Coagulation–flocculation, Fenton oxidation, or ozonation to break down refractory organics.
  - **Advanced oxidation processes (AOPs):** To improve biodegradability, then send to biological treatment.
- **Tertiary treatment:** Activated carbon adsorption, reverse osmosis, or UV disinfection.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall / Common Mistakes:**
> 1. **Forgetting the Dilution Factor** — If you report $BOD_5 = 5.2$ mg/L instead of 78 mg/L, you lose **3 marks** immediately.
> 2. **Using wrong units** — Always report in **mg/L** (or ppm).
> 3. **Not commenting on biodegradability** — The examiner expects the *interpretation* of BOD/COD ratio, not just numerical calculation. Skipping the *treatment suggestion* costs another 2 marks.
> 4. **Mis-stating standard conditions** — BOD is at **20 °C for 5 days**, not 25 °C or 7 days. Standard dilution water must be seeded if the sample is toxic.

---

### **Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**

**(a) [7 Marks] Discuss the physical, chemical, and biological characterization of food industry effluents with suitable examples. Highlight the importance of BOD/COD ratio in selecting a treatment method.**

**Model Answer:**

Effluent characterization is a **three-pronged assessment** performed before any treatment design:

### (i) Physical Characterization
Physical parameters describe bulk, observable properties. They are simple, fast, and serve as preliminary screening.

- **pH** — dairy effluent pH ≈ 6.5–9.0; distillery pH ≈ 3.5–4.5.
- **Temperature** — Canning waste up to 60 °C; must be cooled before biological treatment.
- **Color & Odor** — Distillery spent wash is dark brown (due to melanoidins); seafood waste has a strong fishy odor (amines).
- **TSS/TDS** — Meat processing: TSS 800–3,000 mg/L; dairy: TSS 500–1,500 mg/L.
- **Turbidity** — Fruit canning waste has high turbidity from suspended fruit fibers.

### (ii) Chemical Characterization
Chemical parameters quantify pollutant loads.

- **BOD₅** — Brewery 1,500–3,500; Distillery 40,000–60,000.
- **COD** — Always higher than BOD; ratio indicates biodegradability.
- **TOC, FOG, N, P** — Detailed in Q-A above.
- **Heavy metals** — Chromium (from stainless-steel equipment), Iron (corrosion), Copper (pesticide residues in fruits).

### (iii) Biological Characterization
- **Microbial count (CFU/mL)** — Meat and seafood wastes have 10⁶ – 10⁸ CFU/mL total plate count.
- **Pathogen screening** — *E. coli*, *Salmonella*, *Listeria* in meat/dairy.
- **Biodegradability tests** — Respirometry, BMP (Biochemical Methane Potential).
- **Toxicity bioassays** — LC₅₀ for *Daphnia magna*, fish.

### Importance of BOD/COD Ratio

The **BOD/COD ratio** is the **single most important parameter** for selecting a treatment method.

- **BOD/COD > 0.5** → Highly biodegradable → **Biological treatment** (Activated Sludge, UASB, Trickling Filter).
- **BOD/COD = 0.3 – 0.5** → Moderately biodegradable → May need **acclimatized biomass** or **two-stage** biological treatment.
- **BOD/COD < 0.3** → Poorly biodegradable → **Physico-chemical treatment** (Coagulation, Fenton, Adsorption, Membrane).

**Example:**
- Distillery spent wash: BOD/COD ≈ 0.4 → UASB + aerobic post-treatment.
- Distillery residue after biomethanation: BOD/COD < 0.2 → needs **RO or adsorption**.

---

**(b) [7 Marks] A composite sample from a dairy plant is analyzed. TSS = 800 mg/L, BOD₅ = 1,800 mg/L, COD = 3,200 mg/L, FOG = 250 mg/L, TN = 80 mg/L, TP = 15 mg/L, pH = 7.2, flow = 50 m³/day. Compute the Population Equivalent (PE) and discuss whether biological treatment is feasible.**

**Model Answer:**

**Step 1: Compute Daily BOD Load.**

Daily BOD load = $BOD_5 \times Q$

$$\text{Daily BOD} = 1{,}800\;\text{mg/L} \times 50\;\text{m}^3/\text{day}$$

Converting m³ to L: $50\;\text{m}^3 = 50{,}000\;\text{L}$

$$\text{Daily BOD} = 1{,}800 \times 50{,}000 = 90 \times 10^6\;\text{mg/day} = 90\;\text{kg/day}$$

**Step 2: Compute Population Equivalent (PE).**

Standard per-capita BOD contribution = **0.08 kg/person/day** (WHO standard).

$$PE = \dfrac{\text{Daily BOD load (kg/day)}}{0.08\;\text{kg/person/day}} = \dfrac{90}{0.08} = 1{,}125\;\text{persons}$$

**Step 3: Compute the BOD/COD Ratio.**

$$R = \dfrac{BOD_5}{COD} = \dfrac{1{,}800}{3{,}200} = 0.5625$$

**Step 4: Check C:N:P Ratio.**

BOD-based C: $C = 1{,}800$ mg/L (proxy)
TN = 80 mg/L
TP = 15 mg/L
C:N = 1800:80 = 22.5:1 (much higher than 100:5 = 20:1 ✓ reasonable)
C:P = 1800:15 = 120:1 (lower than ideal 100:1, so P is borderline sufficient)
N:P = 80:15 = 5.3:1 (✓ close to ideal 5:1)

**Step 5: Comment on Treatment Feasibility.**

Since **BOD/COD = 0.5625 > 0.5** and the **C:N:P ratio is near optimal**, the dairy effluent is:

- ✅ **Highly biodegradable**
- ✅ **Nitrogen is sufficient**; **phosphorus is slightly low** (may need minor P supplementation if high-rate biological treatment is used).
- ✅ **Recommended Treatment:** **Anaerobic (UASB) + Aerobic (Activated Sludge) + Nutrient removal** sequence.
- ⚠️ **FOG at 250 mg/L** — must be removed via **DAF (Dissolved Air Flotation)** or **grease trap** *before* biological treatment to prevent scum formation.

**Conclusion:** The dairy plant's effluent load is equivalent to a town of **1,125 persons** and is **readily treatable** by conventional biological methods after primary FOG removal.

> [!WARNING]
> **Examiner's Pitfall — Question B (b):**
> 1. **Wrong PE constant** — Indian (CPCB) per-capita BOD = 0.08 kg/day. Using 0.17 (Western) is incorrect for KTU questions.
> 2. **Not converting m³/day to L/day** — A common error leads to a PE off by a factor of 1,000.
> 3. **Forgetting FOG pre-treatment** — Markers specifically look for the FOG discussion in dairy questions (4–5 marks worth).
> 4. **Ignoring nutrient ratio** — Always check C:N:P — it's a free 2-mark item.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!TIP]
> **Rapid-Revision Checklist (Last-Minute KTU Prep)**

- ✅ Characterization = Physical + Chemical + Biological assessment of effluent.
- ✅ **BOD₅** is measured at **20 °C, 5 days, in the dark**; units **mg/L**.
- ✅ **COD** uses **K₂Cr₂O₇/H₂SO₄**; faster (2 hours) than BOD.
- ✅ **BOD/COD Ratio:** > 0.5 → biodegradable → biological treatment; < 0.3 → physico-chemical.
- ✅ **TSS** = retained on 1.2 µm filter; **TDS** = pass-through; both dried at 103–105 °C.
- ✅ **TOC** correlates with BOD: $BOD_5 \approx 1.7 \times TOC$ approximately.
- ✅ **FOG** extracted with **n-hexane**; high in edible oil & meat industries.
- ✅ **C:N:P optimal** for biological treatment = **100:5:1**.
- ✅ **Population Equivalent (PE)** = Daily BOD (kg/d) / **0.08 kg/person/day** (CPCB/Indian standard).
- ✅ Food industry BOD ranges: Dairy 1,000–3,000; Brewery 1,500–3,500; **Distillery 40,000–60,000** (highest).
- ✅ pH discharge limit: **5.5 – 9.0** (IS 2490).
- ✅ Temperature discharge limit: < **40 °C** at point of mixing.
- ✅ Sampling: **Composite (24-hr, flow-proportional)** for BOD/COD; **Grab** for pH/temperature.
- ✅ Preservation: BOD at **4 °C, analyze within 24–48 h**; COD → **acidify with H₂SO₄**.
- ✅ Standard methods: **APHA 23rd Edition (2017)** — reference for all KTU practicals.
- ✅ First-order BOD kinetics: $BOD_t = L_0 (1 - e^{-kt})$; **k ≈ 0.23 day⁻¹** at 20 °C (typical food waste).
- ✅ **Distillery spent wash** is the **darkest, strongest** food effluent (dark brown, acidic, very high BOD).
- ✅ **Dairy effluent** = white, lactose-rich, requires **FOG + lactose removal** before biological treatment.
- ✅ **Meat/Poultry** = high FOG, pathogens, blood → **DAF + chlorination** essential.
- ✅ **Seafood** = high salt (Cl⁻), high N → use **marine-adapted biomass** in biological treatment.
- ✅ Always compute **PE** in numerical problems to assess scale-equivalent load.
- ✅ BOD calculation **always** needs the **Dilution Factor (DF)**; do not forget.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: In terms of parameters of importance
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEFTT744 - FOOD INDUSTRY WASTE MANAGEMENT
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** In terms of parameters of importance

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

In the context of **Food Industry Waste Management**, the phrase *"In terms of parameters of importance"* refers to the systematic identification, measurement, and interpretation of key **physical, chemical, biological, and biochemical parameters** that govern the characterization, handling, treatment, and valorization of waste streams generated across the food processing sector. These parameters serve as the **decision-making matrix** for engineers to determine whether a particular waste is hazardous, biodegradable, recyclable, or suitable for energy/resource recovery.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition (Verbatim Significance):**
> *Parameters of importance* are quantitative and qualitative descriptors that define the pollution strength, resource potential, treatability, and regulatory compliance status of a given food industry waste. They form the foundation for designing **Effluent Treatment Plants (ETP)**, **Sewage Treatment Plants (STP)**, **Composting units**, **Biogas reactors**, and **Circular Economy frameworks** in food processing industries.

## 1.2 Categories of Parameters of Importance

The parameters of importance in food industry waste are broadly classified into **four primary categories**:

| Sl. No. | Category | Examples of Parameters |
|:-------:|----------|------------------------|
| 1 | **Physical Parameters** | Colour, Odour, Temperature, Total Solids (TS), Total Suspended Solids (TSS), Total Dissolved Solids (TDS), Moisture Content, Particle Size, Density |
| 2 | **Chemical Parameters** | pH, Chemical Oxygen Demand (COD), Biochemical Oxygen Demand (BOD), Total Organic Carbon (TOC), Total Kjeldahl Nitrogen (TKN), Phosphates, Sulphates, Oils & Grease, Heavy Metals |
| 3 | **Biological Parameters** | Total Plate Count (TPC), Coliform count, Pathogen load, Microbial activity index |
| 4 | **Biochemical/Process Parameters** | C/N ratio, Biogas yield, Methane content, Compost maturity index, Volatile Fatty Acids (VFA) |

## 1.3 Conceptual Analogy / Intuitive Understanding

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-World Analogy — "The Patient's Diagnostic Report":**
> Imagine a patient visiting a doctor. The doctor doesn't prescribe medicine based on a guess — they order a **diagnostic report** (blood test, sugar level, cholesterol, BP, etc.). Similarly, a waste management engineer cannot design a treatment plant without first obtaining the "diagnostic report" of the waste, which is precisely the **parameters of importance**.
> - **pH** is like the "body temperature" — tells you if the system is acidic, neutral, or alkaline.
> - **BOD/COD** is the "cholesterol level" — tells you how much "organic load" the waste carries.
> - **TSS/TDS** is the "weight" — tells you how much solid matter is suspended or dissolved.
> - **C/N ratio** is the "diet balance" — tells microbes whether the feed is right for them to thrive.

Without these parameters, any treatment design is essentially a **blind prescription** — which is unacceptable in KTU board examinations and in actual plant engineering.

## 1.4 Key Standard Metrics (Bold Highlights)

- **BOD₅ (5-day Biochemical Oxygen Demand)** is the most widely used parameter — measured in **mg/L**.
- **COD (Chemical Oxygen Demand)** is the **gold standard** for measuring total oxidizable organic matter.
- **pH** is a **logarithmic scale (0–14)**.
- **Standard BOD/COD ratio for food waste** lies between **0.4 – 0.8** (indicating high biodegradability).
- **C/N ratio for optimal composting** = **25:1 to 30:1**.

## 1.5 Why Are These Parameters Important? (KTU Exam Favourite)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Three Primary Reasons (Memorize for KTU Board Exams):**
> 1. **Regulatory Compliance** — CPCB / KSPCB discharge norms mandate specific parameter limits.
> 2. **Treatment Selection** — Parameters dictate whether biological, chemical, or physical treatment is required.
> 3. **Resource Recovery Potential** — Parameters quantify the viability of biogas, compost, or byproduct recovery.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Step-by-Step Theoretical Breakdown of Major Parameters

### 2.1.1 pH (Potential of Hydrogen)

- **Definition:** pH is the negative logarithm (base 10) of hydrogen ion concentration in the waste stream.
- **Mathematical Expression:**

$$\text{pH} = -\log_{10}[\text{H}^+]$$

- **Logic Step 1:** A pH of **7** indicates a neutral state.
- **Logic Step 2:** pH < 7 → **acidic** (common in dairy, fruit processing waste due to organic acid production).
- **Logic Step 3:** pH > 7 → **alkaline** (common in bakery, egg processing waste due to ammonia and protein breakdown).
- **Why it matters:** Microbial communities in biological treatment systems function optimally between **pH 6.5 – 7.5**. Extreme pH inhibits methanogens and causes treatment failure.

### 2.1.2 Biochemical Oxygen Demand (BOD)

- **Definition:** BOD is the amount of dissolved oxygen (DO) required by aerobic microorganisms to biologically oxidize the organic matter in a waste sample over a **standard period of 5 days at 20°C** in the dark.
- **Why 5 days?** Because 5 days corresponds to the maximum biological oxidation of carbonaceous matter in standard conditions.

$$\text{BOD}_5 = (D_1 - D_2) - \frac{(B_1 - B_2)(1 - f)}{f}$$

Where:
- $D_1$ = DO of diluted sample immediately after preparation (mg/L)
- $D_2$ = DO of diluted sample after 5 days incubation (mg/L)
- $B_1$ = DO of dilution water before seeding (mg/L)
- $B_2$ = DO of dilution water after 5 days incubation (mg/L)
- $f$ = Fraction of dilution water in the sample

### 2.1.3 Chemical Oxygen Demand (COD)

- **Definition:** COD is the amount of oxygen required to chemically oxidize both biodegradable and non-biodegradable organic matter using a strong oxidizing agent (typically **potassium dichromate, K₂Cr₂O₇**) in acidic medium.

$$\text{COD (mg/L)} = \frac{(V_b - V_s) \times N \times 8 \times 1000}{V_{\text{sample}}}$$

Where:
- $V_b$ = Volume of FAS (Ferrous Ammonium Sulphate) used for blank titration (mL)
- $V_s$ = Volume of FAS used for sample titration (mL)
- $N$ = Normality of FAS
- 8 = Equivalent weight of oxygen

> [!NOTE]
> **COD/BOD Relationship (KTU Important):**
> For **food industry waste**, COD/BOD ratio is typically between **1.3 – 2.5**, indicating the waste is **highly biodegradable**. Industrial chemical waste shows COD/BOD > 5, indicating non-biodegradable nature.

### 2.1.4 Total Suspended Solids (TSS) and Total Dissolved Solids (TDS)

$$\text{Total Solids (TS)} = \text{TSS} + \text{TDS}$$

$$\text{TSS (mg/L)} = \frac{(W_{\text{filter+residue}} - W_{\text{filter}}) \times 1000}{V_{\text{sample}}}$$

- **TSS** is measured by filtering the sample through a pre-weighed **glass fibre filter (1.2 µm pore size)** and drying at **103–105°C**.
- **TDS** is measured by evaporating the filtrate and drying at **180°C**.

### 2.1.5 C/N Ratio (Carbon to Nitrogen Ratio)

- **Definition:** Ratio of total carbon content to total nitrogen content in the waste.
- **Optimal Range for Composting:** **25:1 to 30:1**.
- **Optimal Range for Anaerobic Digestion:** **20:1 to 30:1**.

$$\text{C/N Ratio} = \frac{\text{Total Carbon (\%)}}{\text{Total Nitrogen (\%)}}$$

### 2.1.6 Moisture Content (MC)

$$\text{MC (\%)} = \frac{(W_{\text{wet}} - W_{\text{dry}})}{W_{\text{wet}}} \times 100$$

- Food industry waste (especially fruit & vegetable processing) often has **moisture > 80%**, making it unsuitable for direct composting without **bulking agents** (sawdust, rice husk, dry leaves).

## 2.2 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **The following table is EXAM-CRITICAL. Memorize before KTU Board Exam.**

| Parameter | Formula / Method | Unit | Standard Range (Food Waste) | Significance |
|-----------|------------------|------|----------------------------|--------------|
| pH | $\text{pH} = -\log_{10}[\text{H}^+]$ | — | 4.0 – 9.0 (varies) | Treatability indicator |
| BOD₅ | Winkler / Respirometric method | mg/L | 1,000 – 100,000 | Organic pollution strength |
| COD | Dichromate reflux method | mg/L | 2,000 – 150,000 | Total oxidation load |
| BOD/COD | Ratio | — | 0.4 – 0.8 | Biodegradability index |
| TSS | Gravimetric (105°C) | mg/L | 500 – 20,000 | Suspended load |
| TDS | Gravimetric (180°C) | mg/L | 1,000 – 30,000 | Dissolved load |
| TS | TSS + TDS | mg/L | Sum of above | Total solid load |
| Moisture Content | $(W_{\text{wet}} - W_{\text{dry}})/W_{\text{wet}} \times 100$ | % | 60 – 95 | Process selection |
| C/N Ratio | C% / N% | — | 25:1 – 30:1 | Composting/AD feasibility |
| TKN | Kjeldahl digestion | mg/L | 100 – 5,000 | Nitrogen content |
| Oils & Grease | Soxhlet / Partition-Gravimetric | mg/L | 50 – 10,000 | Floating layer / blockages |
| Volatile Solids (VS) | Loss on ignition at 550°C | mg/L | 60 – 90% of TS | Organic fraction indicator |
| Colour | Pt-Co scale / ADMI | Hazen | < 100 (after treatment) | Aesthetic / regulatory |
| Odour | Threshold Odour Number (TON) | — | Variable | Public nuisance |

## 2.3 Real-World Utility in Food Industry

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Applications of Parameters of Importance:**
> - **Dairy Industry (Milk Processing):** High BOD (1,000–3,000 mg/L), high oil & grease → requires **anaerobic + aerobic hybrid system**.
> - **Brewery Industry:** Very high BOD (15,000–25,000 mg/L), acidic pH (4–5), high C/N ratio → suitable for **biogas generation via UASB reactor**.
> - **Fruit & Vegetable Processing:** High moisture, high TDS, seasonal variability → requires **equalization tank + activated sludge process**.
> - **Meat & Poultry Industry:** High protein, high TKN, pathogen risk → requires **thermal pre-treatment + biological nitrification-denitrification**.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: BOD₅ First-Stage Reaction Kinetics

The standard KTU-expected derivation of BOD follows the **first-order reaction kinetics** of carbonaceous organic matter oxidation.

### Step 1: Basic First-Order Differential Equation

The rate of oxygen consumption by microorganisms is directly proportional to the remaining BOD at any time $t$:

$$\frac{dL_t}{dt} = -k \cdot L_t$$

Where:
- $L_t$ = BOD remaining (unoxidized) at time $t$ (mg/L)
- $k$ = BOD rate constant (per day), typically $k = 0.23$ /day at 20°C for food waste

### Step 2: Separation of Variables

Rearranging and integrating from $t = 0$ (where $L_0$ is the ultimate BOD) to time $t$:

$$\int_{L_0}^{L_t} \frac{dL_t}{L_t} = -k \int_{0}^{t} dt$$

### Step 3: Integration

$$\ln\left(\frac{L_t}{L_0}\right) = -k \cdot t$$

$$L_t = L_0 \cdot e^{-kt}$$

### Step 4: Relating Remaining BOD to BOD Exerted

The BOD exerted up to time $t$ is:

$$y_t = L_0 - L_t = L_0 \left(1 - e^{-kt}\right)$$

### Step 5: At $t = 5$ days (Standard Condition)

$$\text{BOD}_5 = L_0 \left(1 - e^{-k \times 5}\right)$$

For $k = 0.23$ /day:

$$\text{BOD}_5 = L_0 \left(1 - e^{-1.15}\right) = L_0 (1 - 0.3166) = 0.683 \cdot L_0$$

Therefore:

$$L_0 = \frac{\text{BOD}_5}{0.683} = 1.46 \times \text{BOD}_5$$

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Board Exam Result Interpretation:** For a typical food industry waste sample, if the measured BOD₅ = 4,000 mg/L, the **ultimate BOD ($L_0$) = 1.46 × 4,000 = 5,840 mg/L**. This value is used for designing aeration tanks and biological reactors.

## 3.2 Analytical Matrix: Correlation of Parameters with Industry Type

The following table is a **high-yield KTU exam table** that directly links parameters with food industry types.

| Food Industry | pH | BOD (mg/L) | COD (mg/L) | TSS (mg/L) | Special Parameter | Recommended Treatment |
|---------------|-----|------------|------------|------------|-------------------|----------------------|
| **Dairy** | 6.0 – 8.0 | 1,000 – 3,000 | 2,000 – 6,000 | 500 – 3,000 | High O&G (200–1000 mg/L) | Anaerobic + Aerobic |
| **Brewery** | 4.0 – 6.5 | 15,000 – 25,000 | 25,000 – 50,000 | 1,000 – 4,000 | High C/N (>30) | UASB Reactor |
| **Distillery (Spent Wash)** | 3.5 – 5.0 | 40,000 – 60,000 | 80,000 – 120,000 | 10,000 – 20,000 | Very high K, dark colour | Biomethanation + RO |
| **Fruit & Vegetable** | 5.0 – 7.0 | 1,500 – 4,000 | 2,500 – 7,000 | 800 – 2,500 | High suspended solids, seasonal | Activated Sludge |
| **Meat & Poultry** | 6.5 – 7.5 | 2,000 – 8,000 | 4,000 – 15,000 | 1,000 – 5,000 | High TKN, pathogens | Nitrification-Denitrification |
| **Sugar Mill** | 5.5 – 7.0 | 3,000 – 8,000 | 5,000 – 12,000 | 2,000 – 6,000 | High sulphate | Biomethanation |
| **Fish Processing** | 6.0 – 7.5 | 4,000 – 10,000 | 7,000 – 20,000 | 1,500 – 4,000 | High protein, salt | Anaerobic + MBR |

## 3.3 Worked Numerical Example (KTU Board Style)

**Problem:** A food industry wastewater sample was diluted by a factor of 1:50. The initial DO of the diluted sample was 8.4 mg/L. After 5 days of incubation at 20°C, the DO dropped to 3.6 mg/L. Calculate the BOD₅ of the original sample.

**Solution (Step-by-Step):**

Given:
- Dilution factor $(f) = 1/50 = 0.02$
- $D_1$ = 8.4 mg/L
- $D_2$ = 3.6 mg/L
- Assume seeded dilution water correction is negligible

Using the simplified BOD formula:

$$\text{BOD}_5 = \frac{(D_1 - D_2)}{f}$$

Substituting values:

$$\text{BOD}_5 = \frac{(8.4 - 3.6)}{0.02} = \frac{4.8}{0.02}$$

$$\boxed{\text{BOD}_5 = 240 \text{ mg/L}}$$

**Verification:** This value lies in the typical range of fruit and vegetable processing waste, confirming consistency with the analytical matrix above.

## 3.4 Derivation: C/N Ratio Adjustments for Composting

### Step 1: Identify Substrate C and N Values

For a food waste mix containing 60% fruit waste (C/N = 35) and 40% vegetable waste (C/N = 25):

$$\text{Weighted C/N} = \frac{(0.6 \times 35) + (0.4 \times 25)}{(0.6 \times 1) + (0.4 \times 1)} = \frac{21 + 10}{1} = 31$$

### Step 2: Compare with Optimal Range

The calculated C/N of **31** is slightly above the optimal range (25–30). To bring it down to 28, we need to **add a nitrogen-rich bulking agent** (e.g., poultry manure with C/N = 10).

### Step 3: Mass Balance for Bulking Agent Addition

Let $x$ be the fraction of poultry manure to be added:

$$0.95 \times 31 + 0.05 \times 10 = 28$$

$$29.45 + 0.5 = 28 \rightarrow \text{Infeasible; recompute}$$

After re-iteration (let $y$ be mass of manure added per 100 kg waste):

$$\frac{95 \times 31 + y \times 10}{95 + y} = 28$$

$$2945 + 10y = 28(95 + y) = 2660 + 28y$$

$$2945 - 2660 = 28y - 10y$$

$$285 = 18y \rightarrow y = 15.83 \text{ kg}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Result:** To achieve the optimal C/N ratio of 28, add **~15.83 kg of poultry manure per 100 kg** of food waste mix.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Block Diagram: Hierarchical Classification of Parameters of Importance in Food Industry Waste

The following **Mermaid block diagram** illustrates the hierarchical categorization of all critical parameters governing food industry waste management decisions.

    B1 --> C4[Total Solids]

    B2 --> D1[pH]

## 4.2 Block Diagram: Decision-Making Flowchart Based on Parameters of Importance

The following Mermaid flowchart shows **how engineers use parameters to decide treatment options** — a **high-yield KTU exam diagram**.

```mermaid
flowchart TD

The second shows **how to interpret** those measurements into treatment actions. The third gives a **strategic quadrant** for treatment selection based on combined BOD/COD and waste volume parameters.
