# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

## PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1
**[KTU University Exam – Model Question 2024 Scheme]**
*"List any six important physical and chemical parameters used for characterization of food industry waste."*

**Model Answer (3 Marks – Each parameter = 0.5 mark):**

The important parameters are:

1. **pH** – Determines acidic or alkaline nature; affects microbial treatment.
2. **Biochemical Oxygen Demand (BOD₅)** – Indicates biodegradable organic load (mg/L).
3. **Chemical Oxygen Demand (COD)** – Indicates total organic load including non-biodegradable (mg/L).
4. **Total Suspended Solids (TSS)** – Indicates suspended particulate load.
5. **Total Dissolved Solids (TDS)** – Indicates dissolved salt/mineral content.
6. **Moisture Content** – Critical for selecting composting vs. drying processes.

> [!WARNING]
> **Examiner's Pitfall:** Students often forget the **units (mg/L, %)**. Always specify units in KTU board answers.

### Question 2
**[KTU University Exam – Model Question 2024 Scheme]**
*"Explain the significance of the BOD/COD ratio in determining the biodegradability of food industry waste."*

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

- The BOD/COD ratio is the **biodegradability index** (1 mark).
- A ratio of **0.4 – 0.8** indicates **highly biodegradable** waste (typical for food industry waste) (1 mark).
- A ratio **< 0.3** indicates **non-biodegradable** or **toxic** waste, requiring physico-chemical treatment (1 mark).
- For example, brewery waste has a BOD/COD ratio of ~0.6, making it ideal for anaerobic digestion.

---

## PART B — 14-Mark Questions (ESE Module Choice Format)

### 🔹 Question A (14 Marks) — Choice 1

**[KTU University Exam – Model Question 2024 Scheme]**

**(a)** Discuss in detail the major **physical, chemical, and biological parameters of importance** in food industry waste characterization. Mention the standard methods and units for each parameter. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A dairy industry discharges 500 m³/day of wastewater with BOD₅ = 2,500 mg/L. Calculate the total daily BOD load in kg/day. If the discharge standard mandates BOD < 100 mg/L, what percentage of BOD must be removed? **(7 marks)**

---

#### Model Solution to Question A (a) — 7 Marks

**Physical Parameters (2 marks):**
- **Colour & Odour** – measured by Pt-Co scale and TON respectively.
- **Total Solids (TS)** – sum of TSS and TDS; gravimetric method at 105°C.
- **Temperature** – affects microbial kinetics in treatment.
- **Moisture Content** – critical for solid waste from food processing.

**Chemical Parameters (3 marks):**
- **pH** – electrometric method; food waste varies from 4.0 to 9.0.
- **BOD₅** – Winkler method; standard 5-day test at 20°C.
- **COD** – closed reflux dichromate method; oxidizes both biodegradable and non-biodegradable organics.
- **TKN, Phosphates** – Kjeldahl method for nitrogen; stannous chloride method for phosphate.
- **Oils & Grease** – Soxhlet extraction with petroleum ether.

**Biological Parameters (2 marks):**
- **Total Plate Count (TPC)** – serial dilution and plate count method.
- **Coliforms / Pathogen Load** – MPN (Most Probable Number) technique; critical for meat and poultry waste.
- **Microbial Activity Index** – used to assess compost stability and biogas digester health.

---

#### Model Solution to Question A (b) — 7 Marks

**Given:**
- Discharge flow $Q = 500$ m³/day $= 500 \times 10^3$ L/day
- Influent BOD₅ $= 2,500$ mg/L
- Standard BOD limit $= 100$ mg/L

**Step 1: Calculate Total BOD Load (kg/day) (3 marks)**

$$\text{BOD Load} = Q \times \text{BOD}_5$$

$$\text{BOD Load} = 500 \times 10^3 \text{ L/day} \times 2500 \text{ mg/L}$$

$$\text{BOD Load} = 1.25 \times 10^9 \text{ mg/day}$$

Converting to kg:

$$\text{BOD Load} = \frac{1.25 \times 10^9}{10^6} = 1,250 \text{ kg/day}$$

**Step 2: Calculate Required BOD Removal Percentage (4 marks)**

$$\text{BOD removed} = 2500 - 100 = 2,400 \text{ mg/L}$$

$$\text{Removal Efficiency} = \frac{2400}{2500} \times 100 = 96\%$$

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Mistakes:**
> 1. **Unit conversion errors** — Failing to convert mg/L to kg/day is the single biggest mistake. Always: mg/L × L/day ÷ 10⁶ = kg/day.
> 2. **Confusing BOD with COD** — Don't substitute COD for BOD in calculations.
> 3. **Forgetting to mention methods** — Examiners give at least 1 mark for naming the analytical method.

---

### 🔹 Question B (14 Marks) — Choice 2 (Alternative)

**[KTU University Exam – Model Question 2024 Scheme]**

**(a)** Explain the **significance of C/N ratio, moisture content, and pH** as parameters of importance in food waste composting. State the optimal ranges for each. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A composite food waste sample contains 70% fruit waste (C/N = 35:1) and 30% vegetable waste (C/N = 20:1). Calculate the weighted C/N ratio. Is it suitable for composting? If not, suggest a corrective measure. **(7 marks)**

---

#### Model Solution to Question B (a) — 7 Marks

**C/N Ratio (2.5 marks):**
- The C/N ratio determines whether the carbon and nitrogen balance is conducive for microbial growth during composting.
- **Optimal range: 25:1 to 30:1**.
- If C/N is too high (e.g., >35), nitrogen becomes the limiting nutrient → composting slows down.
- If C/N is too low (e.g., <15), excess nitrogen is lost as **ammonia (NH₃)** → odour problems and nutrient loss.

**Moisture Content (2.5 marks):**
- Moisture content affects microbial mobility, oxygen diffusion, and temperature in the compost pile.
- **Optimal range: 50% to 60%**.
- Food industry waste (especially fruit and vegetable) often has MC > 80%, requiring **bulking agents** like sawdust, dry leaves, or rice husk to bring down moisture.

**pH (2 marks):**
- pH influences microbial community succession during composting.
- **Initial optimal pH: 5.5 to 8.0**.
- Mesophilic bacteria prefer slightly alkaline; fungi prefer slightly acidic.
- A mature compost reaches **pH 7.0–8.5** indicating humification.

---

#### Model Solution to Question B (b) — 7 Marks

**Given:**
- 70% fruit waste with C/N = 35
- 30% vegetable waste with C/N = 20

**Step 1: Weighted C/N Calculation (4 marks)**

$$\text{C/N}_{\text{mix}} = \frac{(0.7 \times 35) + (0.3 \times 20)}{(0.7 \times 1) + (0.3 \times 1)}$$

$$\text{C/N}_{\text{mix}} = \frac{24.5 + 6.0}{1.0} = \frac{30.5}{1.0}$$

$$\boxed{\text{C/N}_{\text{mix}} = 30.5:1}$$

**Step 2: Suitability Assessment (1 mark)**

The calculated C/N = 30.5 is **slightly above** the optimal range of 25–30. Composting will proceed but may be slow.

**Step 3: Corrective Measure (2 marks)**

To bring C/N to 28 (mid-range), add a **nitrogen-rich co-substrate** such as:
- Poultry manure (C/N ≈ 10)
- Urea (N ≈ 46%)
- Green leafy waste (C/N ≈ 15)

Adding approximately **8–10% poultry manure by weight** will bring the ratio to the optimal range.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Mistakes:**
> 1. **Don't add C/N values directly** (35 + 20 = 55 is WRONG). The mix is **mass-weighted**, not simple sum.
> 2. **Always mention the optimal range** for full marks.
> 3. **Provide at least one specific corrective measure** — not just "add nitrogen."

---

## 📌 TOPIC RECAP & IMPORTANT THINGS TO REMEMBER (Rapid-Revision Checklist)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Print This Section Before Walking Into the Exam Hall!**

- ✅ **pH is logarithmic** — a change of 1 unit = 10× change in [H⁺].
- ✅ **BOD₅** uses 5 days at 20°C; COD uses dichromate reflux.
- ✅ **BOD/COD ratio** > 0.4 = **biodegradable**; < 0.3 = **non-biodegradable**.
- ✅ **Food industry waste** typically has BOD/COD = **0.4 – 0.8**.
- ✅ **Optimal C/N** = **25:1 to 30:1** (composting) and **20:1 to 30:1** (anaerobic digestion).
- ✅ **Optimal moisture** for composting = **50–60%**.
- ✅ **TSS** is measured at 103–105°C; **TDS** is measured at 180°C.
- ✅ **Volatile Solids (VS)** = loss on ignition at **550°C** for 1 hour.
- ✅ **Ultimate BOD ($L_0$)** = **1.46 × BOD₅** (for $k = 0.23$ /day).
- ✅ **COD is always higher than BOD** for the same sample.
- ✅ **Dairy waste** has high oils & grease → pre-treatment via **DAF (Dissolved Air Flotation)** is mandatory.
- ✅ **Brewery/distillery waste** is the **best substrate for biogas** due to high BOD and biodegradability.
- ✅ **TS = TSS + TDS** — memorize this identity.
- ✅ **For KTU numerical problems**, always:
  1. Write given data
  2. State the formula
  3. Substitute with units
  4. Provide final answer with **units + interpretation**
- ✅ **CPCB discharge norm** for BOD in food industry effluent = **< 100 mg/L** (general standard).
- ✅ **Compost maturity** is indicated by **C/N < 15** and **germination index > 80%**.

> [!NOTE]
> **Final KTU Board Exam Tip:** The topic *"In terms of parameters of importance"* is most likely to appear as either a **Part A 3-mark question** (list & define parameters) or a **Part B 14-mark numerical + theory** combination. Always pair theory with a numerical example for guaranteed full marks.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Screening
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEFTT744 - FOOD INDUSTRY WASTE MANAGEMENT
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Screening

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SCREENING – Module 3 Comprehensive Study Notes

## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Screening** is a *primary physical unit operation* in solid–liquid separation where a **screen medium** (bars, plates, perforated plates, wire mesh, or wedge wires) is used to **retain suspended or floating solids larger than the screen aperture** while allowing the liquid fraction and fine particles to pass through. In the context of the **Food Industry Waste Management (PEFTT744)** syllabus, screening is the *first line of defence* in the treatment train of food processing effluents (dairy, brewery, meat, fruit & vegetable, seafood) to protect downstream equipment (pumps, heat exchangers, biological reactors) from fouling and clogging.

The retained solids are called **screenings** (or *rackings* for coarse screens), and they are routinely removed and disposed of by **landfilling, incineration, or composting** depending on their organic content.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **"The Kitchen Sieve Analogy"** 🧺
> Imagine washing rice in your kitchen. When you pour the milky water through a **sieve**, the rice grains stay behind while the cloudy water passes through. That is exactly what a *bar screen* does — but on an industrial scale, using metal bars instead of wire mesh, and handling **tonnes of wastewater per hour** instead of a glass of water.
>
> *Coarse screens* (large gaps) catch big debris (bones, fruit peels, packaging).
> *Fine screens* (small gaps) catch small particles (seeds, pulp fragments).
> *Microscreens* (tiny holes) are used for polishing the effluent.

### 1.3 Standard Screening Metrics

| Metric | Symbol | Typical Value |
|---|---|---|
| **Bar spacing (aperture)** | *b* | **6–150 mm** |
| **Velocity through screen** | *v* | **0.6–1.2 m/s** |
| **Approach velocity** | *v<sub>a</sub>* | **0.3–0.6 m/s** |
| **Headloss** | *h* | **0.15–0.75 m** |
| **Screen angle** | *θ* | **30°–60°** from horizontal |
| **Open area ratio** | *A<sub>o</sub>* | **30%–70%** |

### 1.4 Classification of Screens (KTU Syllabus Highlight)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Two broad categories** are tested repeatedly in KTU exams:
>
> 1. **Coarse Screens (Bar Racks)** – aperture > **6 mm**, used as the first unit operation.
>    - *Trash racks / Grizzly*
>    - *Bar screens* (hand-cleaned or mechanically cleaned)
>
> 2. **Fine Screens** – aperture **0.2–6 mm**.
>    - *Drum screens*
>    - *Travelling band screens*
>    - *Vibrating screens*
>    - *Microstrainers / Microscreens* (aperture < 0.2 mm)

The Food Industry waste streams, being rich in **biodegradable organics (BOD/COD)**, must be screened *as fresh as possible* to prevent **souring, fermentation, and acidification** of the wastewater.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Operational Theory of Bar Screens

A bar screen consists of a series of **parallel bars** placed across the flow channel. Wastewater flows through the screen, and **particles larger than the bar spacing** are caught on the upstream face. The accumulation of solids causes:

- A **rise in upstream water level** → measurable **headloss** (*h*)
- A **reduction in effective open area** → increased velocity → danger of **screen blinding**

The screen must therefore be **cleaned periodically** to maintain the design headloss within limits.

### 2.2 Step-by-Step Operational Logic

> [!NOTE]
> **Mechanism of Separation:**
> 1. Wastewater approaches the screen with approach velocity *v<sub>a</sub>*.
> 2. It is forced through the **bar openings** (aperture *b*) between adjacent bars of width *W*.
> 3. Particles of size *d > b* are physically intercepted and retained.
> 4. The screened water (effluent) continues to the next treatment unit.
> 5. Screenings accumulate → headloss increases → automatic cleaning cycle is triggered.
> 6. Cleaned screenings are conveyed to a compaction/dewatering unit.

### 2.3 Key Design Parameters (Stepwise)

**Step 1 — Bar Spacing Selection**
- *Coarse* (manual cleaning): 25–75 mm
- *Medium* (mechanical cleaning): 10–25 mm
- *Fine*: 1.5–10 mm

**Step 2 — Channel Dimensions**
The submerged open area is:
$$A = Q \, / \, v_s$$
where *Q* is the design flow (m³/s) and *v<sub>s</sub>* is the velocity *through the screen opening* (typically **0.6–1.2 m/s**).

**Step 3 — Number of Bars**
$$n = \frac{B_{\text{channel}}}{W + b}$$
where *B<sub>channel</sub>* is the channel width, *W* is the bar width, and *b* is the clear spacing.

**Step 4 — Headloss Computation (Kirschmer's Equation)** — *the most tested formula in KTU*:
$$h = \beta \cdot \left(\frac{W}{b}\right)^{4/3} \cdot h_v \cdot \sin\theta$$
where:
- *h* = headloss (m)
- *β* = **bar shape factor** (1.67 for rectangular, **2.42 for sharp-edged**, 1.83 for circular, 1.79 for teardrop)
- *W* = maximum bar width perpendicular to flow (m)
- *b* = minimum bar spacing (m)
- *h<sub>v</sub>* = velocity head = *v² / (2g)*
- *θ* = angle of bar screen with horizontal

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Maximum permissible headloss** at average flow = **0.15 m**; at peak flow = **0.75 m**. Beyond this, the screen *blinds* and the upstream channel may overflow.

**Step 5 — Velocity Profile**
- **Approach velocity (*v<sub>a</sub>*)** = velocity in the channel *upstream* of the screen.
- **Velocity through screen (*v<sub>s</sub>*)** = velocity *between the bars*.
- For **self-cleaning screens**, *v<sub>s</sub>* must be > **0.9 m/s** to prevent deposition in the bars.

### 2.4 KTU High-Yield Formula Cheat Sheet

| # | Formula | Description | Standard Unit |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $A = Q/v_s$ | Effective open submerged area | m² |
| 2 | $n = B_{\text{ch}}/(W+b)$ | Number of bars in screen | — |
| 3 | $h = \beta (W/b)^{4/3} h_v \sin\theta$ | **Kirschmer headloss equation** | m |
| 4 | $h_v = v^2/(2g)$ | Velocity head | m |
| 5 | $A_o = b/(b+W) \times 100$ | Open area ratio | % |
| 6 | $E = (Q_{in} - Q_{through, large})/Q_{in}$ | Screening efficiency | % |
| 7 | $v_a = Q/A_{\text{channel}}$ | Approach velocity | m/s |
| 8 | $L = h_{\text{total}}/\sin\theta$ | Channel slope length | m |

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Tip:** When using **Kirschmer's equation**, students frequently confuse *b* (spacing) and *W* (bar width). Remember: **b is the *gap*, W is the *bar***. Swapping them gives a *wildly* wrong headloss!

### 2.5 Engineering Real-World Utility

In **food industry waste management**, screening serves three critical functions:

1. **Equipment Protection** – prevents fibrous matter (sugarcane bagasse, fruit skins) from destroying pumps and centrifuges.
2. **Biological Load Reduction** – removes settleable/floatable organics, reducing downstream BOD by **10–25%**.
3. **Sludge Reduction** – screenings are *dewatered separately*, reducing the main sludge volume.

For example, in a **dairy plant**, fine screening removes curd particles, fat globules, and casein fines that would otherwise *foul* the anaerobic digester.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation of the Kirschmer Headloss Equation

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The **Kirschmer Equation** is empirically derived (not from first principles) but the following *physical reasoning* is the standard answer expected in KTU 14-mark questions.

### Starting Point: D'Alembert's Resistance Principle
The headloss across an obstruction is proportional to the **velocity head** and a **shape resistance coefficient** (C<sub>d</sub>).

$$h \propto C_d \cdot h_v$$

For a *screen made of multiple parallel bars*, the velocity at the bar face is *amplified* because the flow contracts through the openings. The ratio of **bar width to spacing** controls this contraction:

$$\text{Contraction factor} \propto \left(\frac{W}{b}\right)^{4/3}$$

The fourth-thirds power is empirically fitted and accounts for the **vena contracta** formed downstream of each bar.

Additionally, the headloss is *reduced* when the bars are **inclined** (because the effective flow path increases). The trigonometric correction is **sin θ** where *θ* is measured from horizontal.

Combining all three effects:

$$\boxed{\,h = \beta \cdot \left(\frac{W}{b}\right)^{4/3} \cdot \frac{v^2}{2g} \cdot \sin\theta\,}$$

### Step-by-Step Worked Numerical Example (KTU Standard)

> **Problem:** A bar screen is installed at **θ = 60°** from horizontal. Bar shape is *rectangular*, with **W = 10 mm** width and **b = 20 mm** clear spacing. The velocity through the screen is **0.9 m/s**. Compute the **headloss** using Kirschmer's equation. Take *β* = 1.67 and *g* = 9.81 m/s².

**Step 1 — Identify the bar shape factor**

For rectangular bars: **β = 1.67** (given).

**Step 2 — Compute the ratio W/b**

$$\frac{W}{b} = \frac{10 \text{ mm}}{20 \text{ mm}} = 0.5$$

**Step 3 — Raise to the 4/3 power**

$$\left(\frac{W}{b}\right)^{4/3} = (0.5)^{4/3}$$

$$\begin{aligned}
\ln(0.5^{4/3}) &= \frac{4}{3} \ln(0.5) \\
&= \frac{4}{3} \times (-0.6931) \\
&= -0.9241
\end{aligned}$$

$$(0.5)^{4/3} = e^{-0.9241} = 0.3969$$

**Step 4 — Compute the velocity head**

$$h_v = \frac{v^2}{2g} = \frac{(0.9)^2}{2 \times 9.81} = \frac{0.81}{19.62} = 0.0413 \text{ m}$$

**Step 5 — Compute sin θ**

$$\sin(60°) = 0.8660$$

**Step 6 — Combine all terms**

$$\begin{aligned}
h &= 1.67 \times 0.3969 \times 0.0413 \times 0.8660 \\
h &= 1.67 \times 0.3969 \times 0.0358 \\
h &= 0.0237 \text{ m} \\
h &\approx 23.7 \text{ mm}
\end{aligned}$$

> **Result:** Headloss *h* ≈ **23.7 mm** (well within the **150 mm** design limit at average flow). ✅

## 3.2 Derivation of the Open Area Ratio

The open area of a screen is the **fraction of the cross-section not occupied by bars**.

For a single bar-spacing pair, open area = *b*, total area = *(W + b)*.

For *n* identical pairs, the ratio remains the same:

$$A_o = \frac{n \cdot b}{n \cdot (W+b)} = \frac{b}{W+b}$$

In percentage:

$$\boxed{\,A_o \,(\%) = \frac{b}{W+b} \times 100\,}$$

**Numerical Check:**
For *W* = 10 mm, *b* = 20 mm:
$$A_o = \frac{20}{10+20} \times 100 = 66.67\%$$

## 3.3 Analytical Decision Matrix — Screen Selection

| Wastewater Source | Recommended Screen | Aperture | Cleaning Mode |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Dairy effluent** | Fine drum screen | 1–2 mm | Continuous backwash |
| **Brewery wastewater** | Rotating bar screen | 6–10 mm | Mechanical scraper |
| **Fish/meat processing** | Coarse bar rack + fine | 10 mm + 1.5 mm | Manual + auto |
| **Fruit & vegetable** | Travelling band screen | 3–6 mm | Spray wash |
| **Sugar mill** | Trash rack + fine | 25 mm + 2 mm | Hand + mechanical |
| **Edible oil refinery** | Microscreen (final polish) | 0.1–0.3 mm | Backwash |

## 3.4 Screening Efficiency Derivation

Screening efficiency is defined as the **mass removal rate** of particles above the aperture size:

$$E = \frac{M_{\text{screened}}}{M_{\text{in}}} \times 100 \, (\%) $$

For a continuous flow process:
$$E = \frac{Q \times (C_{\text{in}} - C_{\text{out}})}{Q \times C_{\text{in}}} \times 100 = \frac{C_{\text{in}} - C_{\text{out}}}{C_{\text{in}}} \times 100$$

where *C<sub>in</sub>* and *C<sub>out</sub>* are the **concentrations of large particles** (size > *b*) upstream and downstream of the screen, respectively.

A well-designed bar screen achieves **E = 70–95%** for particles within its size range.
