# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

> **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 Model]**
> **Q1. Define screening. How are screens classified based on aperture size?**
>
> **Model Answer (3 marks):**
> Screening is a *primary physical unit operation* used to separate *suspended or floating solids* from a liquid stream by passing it through a perforated or slotted surface (3 marks for definition).
> Based on aperture size, screens are classified as:
> - **Coarse screens** (aperture > 6 mm) – e.g., bar racks
> - **Fine screens** (aperture 0.2–6 mm) – e.g., drum, band screens
> - **Microscreens** (aperture < 0.2 mm) – e.g., microstrainers
>
> *(Full 3 marks require both definition and classification.)*

> **[KTU University Exam – May 2024 Model]**
> **Q2. What is the significance of the bar shape factor *β* in Kirschmer's equation?**
>
> **Model Answer (3 marks):**
> The bar shape factor *β* is an *empirical coefficient* that accounts for the **hydraulic resistance** offered by the cross-sectional shape of the bar to the flowing wastewater. Common values are:
> - Sharp-edged rectangular: **2.42**
> - Rectangular (streamlined): **1.67**
> - Circular: **1.83**
> - Teardrop: **1.79**
> A *higher β* indicates *more turbulence* and *greater headloss*. The shape is selected to **minimize headloss** while maintaining mechanical strength.

---

## 5.2 PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each)

> **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2024 Model — ESE Module 3 Internal Choice]**

### ⭐ Question A (14 Marks)

**Q(A). (a)** With a neat sketch, explain the working principle of a **mechanically cleaned bar screen**. List the design parameters that govern its selection. **(7 marks)**

**Q(A). (b)** A bar screen has the following specifications:
- Bar shape: rectangular, *W* = 12 mm, *b* = 25 mm
- Screen angle: *θ* = 45° from horizontal
- Velocity through screen: *v* = 1.0 m/s
- Channel width: 1.2 m
- Design flow: 0.4 m³/s

**Compute:**
(i) Number of bars required
(ii) Headloss using Kirschmer's equation
(iii) Whether the design is safe (permissible headloss = 0.15 m at average flow) **(7 marks)**

#### 📘 Model Solution for Q(A)(a) — 7 marks

**Working Principle:**
- A mechanically cleaned bar screen consists of *inclined parallel bars* fixed in a channel.
- Wastewater flows through the gaps; large solids are retained on the upstream face.
- A *motor-driven rake* travels along the bars at fixed intervals (or when a level sensor triggers).
- The rake lifts the screenings to the top of the screen, drops them into a *compactor or disposal bin*.
- Cleaning is automatic → suitable for **high-flow food industry effluents**.

**Key Design Parameters (list with brief explanation):**
1. **Bar spacing (*b*)** — determines the *cut size*.
2. **Bar width (*W*)** — affects *headloss* and *open area ratio*.
3. **Bar shape factor (*β*)** — controls hydraulic resistance.
4. **Screen angle (*θ*)** — usually 30°–60°; shallower angles → lower headloss.
5. **Approach velocity (*v<sub>a</sub>*)** — must be < 0.6 m/s to prevent sand settling.
6. **Velocity through screen (*v<sub>s</sub>*)** — must be > 0.6 m/s for self-cleaning.
7. **Channel dimensions** — must accommodate peak wet-weather flow.
8. **Headloss limit** — ≤ 0.15 m at average flow; ≤ 0.75 m at peak.

*(Award full 7 marks for a clear sketch description + complete parameter list.)*

#### 📘 Model Solution for Q(A)(b) — 7 marks

**Given:** *W* = 12 mm = 0.012 m, *b* = 25 mm = 0.025 m, *θ* = 45°, *v* = 1.0 m/s, *B*<sub>ch</sub> = 1.2 m, *Q* = 0.4 m³/s, *β* = 1.67 (rectangular).

**(i) Number of bars (2 marks):**

$$n = \frac{B_{\text{ch}}}{W+b} = \frac{1.2}{0.012 + 0.025} = \frac{1.2}{0.037} = 32.43$$

$$\boxed{\,n \approx 33 \text{ bars}\,}$$

**(ii) Headloss by Kirschmer (3 marks):**

Velocity head:
$$h_v = \frac{v^2}{2g} = \frac{(1.0)^2}{2 \times 9.81} = 0.0510 \text{ m}$$

Ratio:
$$\frac{W}{b} = \frac{0.012}{0.025} = 0.48$$

Power:
$$(0.48)^{4/3}$$

$$\begin{aligned}
\ln(0.48^{4/3}) &= \frac{4}{3} \ln(0.48) \\
&= \frac{4}{3} \times (-0.7340) \\
&= -0.9787
\end{aligned}$$

$$(0.48)^{4/3} = e^{-0.9787} = 0.3758$$

Sine:
$$\sin(45°) = 0.7071$$

Combining:
$$\begin{aligned}
h &= 1.67 \times 0.3758 \times 0.0510 \times 0.7071 \\
h &= 1.67 \times 0.3758 \times 0.0361 \\
h &= 0.0226 \text{ m} \\
h &\approx 22.6 \text{ mm}
\end{aligned}$$

$$\boxed{\,h \approx 22.6 \text{ mm}\,}$$

**(iii) Design check (2 marks):**
Permissible headloss = 0.15 m = 150 mm.
Calculated *h* = 22.6 mm ≪ 150 mm.
**Design is SAFE** ✅ at average flow with a large safety margin.

---

### ⭐ Question B (14 Marks)

**Q(B). (a)** Compare **coarse screens and fine screens** with respect to aperture, cleaning mode, applications in food industry, and removal efficiency. **(7 marks)**

**Q(B). (b)** A food industry wastewater treatment plant handles **Q = 0.5 m³/s** of effluent through a bar screen inclined at **60°** with bar width **15 mm** and clear spacing **30 mm**. The approach velocity is **0.4 m/s**. Compute:
(i) The submerged open area of the screen.
(ii) The velocity through the screen.
(iii) The headloss (use *β* = 2.42 for sharp-edged rectangular bars). **(7 marks)**

#### 📘 Model Solution for Q(B)(a) — 7 marks

**Comparison Table (write in exam):**

| Feature | Coarse Screens | Fine Screens |
|---|---|---|
| Aperture | 6–150 mm | 0.2–6 mm |
| Cleaning mode | Manual / mechanical rake | Continuous backwash / spray |
| Typical use | First-stage protection | Secondary polishing |
| Food industry application | Debris removal (bones, peels) | Pulp, curd, fibre removal |
| BOD removal | 5–15% | 15–25% |
| Headloss | Low (50–150 mm) | Moderate (100–300 mm) |
| Capital cost | Lower | Higher |

**Examples (1 mark):**
- Coarse: *trash rack, bar rack* in meat processing plants.
- Fine: *drum screen* in dairy industry, *band screen* in brewery.

*(Award full 7 marks for a detailed comparison with food industry examples.)*

#### 📘 Model Solution for Q(B)(b) — 7 marks

**Given:** *Q* = 0.5 m³/s, *θ* = 60°, *W* = 0.015 m, *b* = 0.030 m, *v<sub>a</sub>* = 0.4 m/s, *β* = 2.42.

**(i) Submerged open area (2 marks):**
$$A = \frac{Q}{v_a} = \frac{0.5}{0.4} = 1.25 \text{ m}^2$$

$$\boxed{\,A = 1.25 \text{ m}^2\,}$$

**(ii) Velocity through screen (2 marks):**
The velocity through the screen is the velocity in the *openings* between bars, not the approach velocity. Since the problem does not specify otherwise, we interpret *v* as the approach velocity used to compute area, and the through-screen velocity is computed using the **continuity** of flow *Q*.

If the channel is rectangular and the approach velocity *v<sub>a</sub>* = 0.4 m/s is the channel velocity, then:
$$v_s = \frac{Q}{A_{\text{open}}}$$
where *A<sub>open</sub>* is computed from the open area ratio:
$$A_o = \frac{b}{W+b} = \frac{0.030}{0.015+0.030} = \frac{0.030}{0.045} = 0.667$$

If the effective submerged area is 1.25 m², then *A<sub>open</sub>* = 0.667 × 1.25 = 0.834 m².

$$v_s = \frac{0.5}{0.834} = 0.60 \text{ m/s}$$

$$\boxed{\,v_s \approx 0.60 \text{ m/s}\,}$$

> *Note: If the examiner assumes v = v<sub>a</sub>* = 0.4 m/s, the answer for v<sub>s</sub> would be 0.4 m/s. Both interpretations accepted; *state assumption clearly*.

**(iii) Headloss (3 marks):**
Using *v* = 0.6 m/s (from part ii):
$$h_v = \frac{(0.6)^2}{2 \times 9.81} = \frac{0.36}{19.62} = 0.01835 \text{ m}$$

Ratio:
$$\frac{W}{b} = 0.5$$

Power:
$$(0.5)^{4/3} = 0.3969$$

Sine:
$$\sin(60°) = 0.8660$$

Combining:
$$h = 2.42 \times 0.3969 \times 0.01835 \times 0.8660$$

$$\begin{aligned}
h &= 2.42 \times 0.3969 \times 0.01589 \\
h &= 0.01526 \text{ m} \\
h &\approx 15.3 \text{ mm}
\end{aligned}$$

$$\boxed{\,h \approx 15.3 \text{ mm}\,}$$

**Design check:** *h* = 15.3 mm < 150 mm → **Design is SAFE** ✅.

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mark-Loss Zones in Screening Questions:**
>
> 1. **Confusing *b* and *W*** — A staggering 60% of KTU scripts swap the bar spacing and bar width. Always re-state: *b = gap, W = bar*.
> 2. **Forgetting the *sin θ* term** — Students treat the screen as if it were vertical. The inclination correction can change the answer by 30–40%.
> 3. **Wrong *β* value** — Don't assume *β* = 1 always. The value depends on the **cross-section of the bar** (rectangular, circular, teardrop, etc.).
> 4. **Unit inconsistency** — Convert all dimensions to **metres** before substituting. Mixing mm and m is a *silent killer* for marks.
> 5. **Not stating the design-check conclusion** — Always end with "Design is SAFE" or "Design FAILS — headloss exceeds limit." Examiners reward this closure.
> 6. **Skipping the sketch** — In 7-mark theory questions, a labelled sketch (even a hand-drawn one) carries **2–3 marks**.
> 7. **Confusing approach velocity with through-screen velocity** — These are *different*; v<sub>a</sub> is in the channel, v<sub>s</sub> is between the bars.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist)

> 📌 **Quick-Reference Summary:**

- ✅ **Screening** = primary solid–liquid separation using a *perforated/barred surface*.
- ✅ **Three classes**: Coarse (> 6 mm), Fine (0.2–6 mm), Micro (< 0.2 mm).
- ✅ **Open area ratio** *A<sub>o</sub>* = *b / (W + b) × 100%*.
- ✅ **Number of bars** *n* = *B<sub>ch</sub> / (W + b)*.
- ✅ **Kirschmer equation** is the *single most important* formula:
  $$h = \beta (W/b)^{4/3} h_v \sin\theta$$
- ✅ **Bar shape factor (β)**: 2.42 (sharp), 1.67 (rectangular), 1.83 (circular), 1.79 (teardrop).
- ✅ **Permissible headloss** ≤ 0.15 m (average flow), ≤ 0.75 m (peak).
- ✅ **Approach velocity** < 0.6 m/s; **through-screen velocity** > 0.6 m/s.
- ✅ **Screen angle** typically 30°–60° from horizontal.
- ✅ **Food industry relevance**: protects pumps, reduces BOD, separates recyclables (peels, bones, curd).
- ✅ **Cleaning modes**: manual rake, mechanical reciprocating rake, rotating drum, travelling band, microscreen backwash.
- ✅ **Efficiency** of a bar screen = 70–95% for particles above its cut size.
- ✅ Always state the *assumption* (e.g., v<sub>s</sub> = v<sub>a</sub> or v<sub>s</sub> = Q/A<sub>open</sub>) before solving.
- ✅ Always **close with a design-check conclusion**.

> 🎯 **Mnemonic to remember β values:** **"Sharp Rectangles Cut Tissues"** → 2.42, 1.67, 1.83, 1.79 (Sharp / Rectangular / Circular / Teardrop).
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Sedimentation
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PEFTT744 - FOOD INDUSTRY WASTE MANAGEMENT > - **Module:** Module 3 > - **Topic:** Sedimentation # SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Terminology) > [!IMPORTANT] > **Sedimentation** is a **gravity-driven physical separation process** in which suspended solid particles that are heavier (denser) than the surrounding liquid medium settle to the bottom of a vessel under the influence of gravitational force, forming a concentrated sludge layer, while the clarified supernatant liquid is decanted from the top. In the context of **Food Industry Waste Management**, sedimentation is the **primary (preliminary) unit operation** used to remove settleable suspended solids (SS), grit, organic slurries, and food-processing particulates from wastewater streams emanating from dairy plants, breweries, fruit/vegetable canneries, slaughterhouses, sugar mills, and seafood processing industries. The driving force is the **buoyant weight** of the particle: $$F_{net} = (\rho_s - \rho_l) \cdot V_p \cdot g$$ where $\rho_s$ = particle density, $\rho_l$ = liquid density, $V_p$ = particle volume, and $g$ = gravitational acceleration (**9.81 m/s²**). ## 1.2 Intuitive Overview — The "Glass of Muddy Water" Analogy > [!NOTE] > **Conceptual Analogy:** Imagine you collect a glass of muddy river water. If you leave it on a table undisturbed for an hour, you will see a thin layer of clear water at the top and a thick brown sediment layer at the bottom. **That is sedimentation in its purest form.** The dirt particles were momentarily suspended by turbulence, but once motion stopped, gravity pulled each particle downward at a speed dependent on its size, shape, and weight. In a **food industry setting**, the "muddy water" is replaced by **effluent** — a mixture of water, food particles, oils, fats, and dissolved organic matter. The same physics applies, but on an industrial scale inside **clarifiers, settling tanks, or thickeners**. ### Why is Sedimentation Crucial in Food Waste Management? - **Removes 50–70 %** of total suspended solids (TSS) before biological treatment. - **Reduces BOD/COD load** entering downstream activated sludge units. - **Recovers valuable solids** (e.g., brewery spent grain, dairy sludge) for **by-product valorization**. - **Prevents clogging** of pipes, pumps, and aeration diffusers. - **Lower capital cost** compared to advanced filtration or membrane systems. ## 1.3 Classification of Particles — Why Each Behaves Differently | Particle Type | Size Range | Behavior During Settling | Typical Food Industry Example | |---|---|---|---| | **Discrete (Type 1)** | > 100 µm | Settles as individual particles, no interaction | Sand, grit, fruit pulp chunks | | **Flocculent (Type 2)** | 10–100 µm | Aggregates while settling, accelerating | Colloidal proteins, yeast cells | | **Hindered / Zone (Type 3)** | Bulk suspension | Mass settles as a blanket, clear interface forms | Activated sludge, biological flocs | | **Compression (Type 4)** | Compact layer | Very slow consolidation under own weight | Thickened bottom sludge | > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU 2024 Highlight:** Module 3 explicitly focuses on **Type 1 (Discrete)**, **Type 2 (Flocculent)**, and **Type 4 (Compression) sedimentation**, as these are the four settling regimes that govern clarifier design in food industry effluent treatment plants (ETPs). --- # SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ## 2.1 The Four Regimes of Sedimentation — Detailed Breakdown ### 2.1.1 Type 1 — Discrete (Free) Settling - Each particle settles **independently**, without contact with neighbors. - Settling velocity remains **constant** throughout the descent. - Particles retain their identity, shape, and size. - **Governing Principle:** **Stokes' Law** (for laminar, $Re < 0.3$) and **Newton's Law** (for turbulent, $Re > 1000$). - **Typical Application:** Pre-sedimentation to remove **grit, sand, and coarse food debris** from canning industry wastewater. **Why is it called "discrete"?** Because each particle behaves as an isolated entity — like a single raindrop falling through still air. ### 2.1.2 Type 2 — Flocculent Settling - Particles **coalesce (flocculate)** during descent, forming larger aggregates. - Settling velocity **increases with depth and time**. - Predominant in **colloidal suspensions** common in food waste (milk proteins, brewer's yeast). - Analyzed using **column settling tests** and **Talmadge–Fitch method**. ### 2.1.3 Type 3 — Hindered (Zone) Settling - High concentration of particles causes **inter-particle interference**. - Particles settle as a **uniform blanket**, with a **distinct solid–liquid interface**. - Settling velocity decreases with increasing solids concentration. - **Typical in:** Secondary clarifiers of biological treatment systems. ### 2.1.4 Type 4 — Compression Settling - Occurs at the **bottom of the sludge blanket** where particles physically touch. - Further consolidation occurs by **squeezing out water** under the weight of overlying solids. - Governed by **solids flux theory** and **Dick– Ewing equation**. ## 2.2 Stokes' Law — The Foundation of Sedimentation Theory For a spherical particle settling in a quiescent Newtonian fluid under **laminar flow conditions** ($Re < 0.3$): $$v_s = \frac{g \cdot (\rho_s - \rho_l) \cdot d_p^2}{18 \cdot \mu}$$ where: - $v_s$ = terminal settling velocity (m/s) - $g$ = gravitational acceleration (**9.81 m/s²**) - $\rho_s$ = particle density (kg/m³) - $\rho_l$ = liquid density (kg/m³) - $d_p$ = particle diameter (m) - $\mu$ = dynamic viscosity of liquid (Pa·s or N·s/m²) ## 2.3 Newton's Law — For Turbulent Settling For larger particles where $Re > 1000$, drag is dominated by inertial forces: $$v_s = \sqrt{\frac{4 \cdot g \cdot (\rho_s - \rho_l) \cdot d_p}{3 \cdot C_D \cdot \rho_l}}$$ where $C_D$ = drag coefficient (function of particle Reynolds number). ## 2.4 Transition (Allied) Regime — $0.3 < Re < 1000$ For intermediate Reynolds numbers, $C_D$ is empirically determined: $$C_D = \frac{24}{Re} + \frac{3}{\sqrt{Re}} + 0.34$$ ## 2.5 Hazen's Simplified Velocity Equation > [!NOTE] > **KTU Favorite Equation:** Hazen observed that for **discrete particles in dilute suspensions**, settling velocity is approximately: > $$v_s \approx \frac{g \cdot (\rho_s - \rho_l) \cdot d_p^2}{18 \cdot \mu}$$ > This is the **engineering shorthand** used in textbook problems. ## 2.6 Solids Flux Theory (For Thickener Design) $$G_i = C_i \cdot v_i$$ Total solids flux: $$G = G_i + G_u = C_i \cdot v_i + \frac{Q_c \cdot C_i}{A}$$ where: - $G_i$ = flux due to gravity settling (kg/m²·h) - $G_u$ = flux due to underflow pumping (kg/m²·h) - $Q_c$ = underflow recycle rate (m³/h) - $A$ = cross-sectional area (m²) - $C_i$ = solids concentration at depth $i$ (kg/m³) ## 2.7 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet | # | Formula Name | Equation | Application | Units | |---|---|---|---|---| | 1 | **Stokes' Law** | $v_s = \dfrac{g(\rho_s - \rho_l) d_p^2}{18 \mu}$ | Discrete settling, $Re < 0.3$ | m/s | | 2 | **Newton's Law** | $v_s = \sqrt{\dfrac{4 g(\rho_s - \rho_l) d_p}{3 C_D \rho_l}}$ | Turbulent settling, $Re > 1000$ | m/s | | 3 | **Drag Coefficient** | $C_D = \dfrac{24}{Re} + \dfrac{3}{\sqrt{Re}} + 0.34$ | Intermediate regime | dimensionless | | 4 | **Reynolds Number** | $Re = \dfrac{\rho_l \cdot v_s \cdot d_p}{\mu}$ | Flow regime identification | dimensionless | | 5 | **Detention Time** | $t_d = \dfrac{V}{Q}$ | Tank sizing | h or s | | 6 | **Overflow Rate** | $v_o = \dfrac{Q}{A_s}$ | Critical settling velocity | m/h | | 7 | **Hazen's Velocity** | $v_H = \dfrac{d_p^2 (\rho_s - \rho_l) g}{18 \mu}$ | Design simplification | m/s | | 8 | **Solids Flux** | $G = C_i v_i + \dfrac{Q_c C_i}{A}$ | Thickener design | kg/m²·h | | 9 | **Weir Loading** | $WL = \dfrac{Q}{L_w}$ | Effluent weir design | m³/m·d | | 10 | **Mass Balance** | $Q C_o = Q C_e + Q_u C_u$ | Solids accounting | kg/d | ## 2.8 Real-World Utility in Food Industry - **Dairy Industry:** Sedimentation removes milk-fat globules and casein fines before anaerobic digestion. - **Breweries:** Spent grain and yeast are recovered from fermentation broth by gravity settling. - **Canneries:** Tomato peel, seed, and pulp are removed from process water. - **Sugar Industry:** Mud settling (clarification) using multi-tray thickeners. - **Meat Processing:** Blood and bone particles separated in primary clarifiers. > [!IMPORTANT] > **Engineering Insight:** The choice of sedimentation regime directly determines the **clarifier geometry**. Discrete settling requires **horizontal flow tanks** with low surface loading rates, while flocculent settling benefits from **lamella plates** to enhance effective settling area. --- # SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ## 3.1 Complete Derivation of Stokes' Law ### Step 1: Force Balance on a Falling Sphere A spherical particle falling under gravity experiences **three forces**: 1. **Downward Gravitational Force:** $F_g = m_p \cdot g = \rho_s \cdot V_p \cdot g$ 2. **Upward Buoyant Force:** $F_b = \rho_l \cdot V_p \cdot g$ 3. **Upward Drag Force:** $F_d$ (opposes motion) For a sphere: $V_p = \dfrac{\pi}{6} d_p^3$ ### Step 2: Net Driving Force (Downward) $$F_{net} = F_g - F_b = (\rho_s - \rho_l) \cdot \frac{\pi}{6} d_p^3 \cdot g$$ > *This is the effective weight of the particle submerged in the fluid.* ### Step 3: Drag Force Expression (Stokes' Assumption) For **laminar flow past a sphere** ($Re < 0.3$), Stokes analytically solved the Navier–Stokes equations and obtained: $$F_d = 3 \pi \mu d_p v_s$$ ### Step 4: Terminal Velocity Condition At **terminal velocity**, acceleration is zero, so net force = drag force: $$F_{net} = F_d$$ $$(\rho_s - \rho_l) \cdot \frac{\pi}{6} d_p^3 \cdot g = 3 \pi \mu d_p v_s$$ ### Step 5: Algebraic Simplification Divide both sides by $\pi d_p$: $$(\rho_s - \rho_l) \cdot \frac{1}{6} d_p^2 \cdot g = 3 \mu v_s$$ Solve for $v_s$: $$v_s = \frac{g (\rho_s - \rho_l) d_p^2}{18 \mu}$$ This is the **canonical Stokes' settling velocity equation**. ## 3.2 Worked Example Problem (KTU Style) ### **Problem Statement** A food processing industry discharges wastewater containing spherical organic particles of density $\rho_s = 1050$ kg/m³ and diameter $d_p = 50$ µm. The wastewater has density $\rho_l = 1000$ kg/m³ and viscosity $\mu = 1.0 \times 10^{-3}$ Pa·s. Calculate the terminal settling velocity and verify the regime using Reynolds number. ### **Step-by-Step Solution** **Step 1:** Identify given values $$\rho_s = 1050 \; \text{kg/m}^3, \quad \rho_l = 1000 \; \text{kg/m}^3, \quad d_p = 50 \; \mu\text{m} = 50 \times 10^{-6} \; \text{m}$$ $$\mu = 1.0 \times 10^{-3} \; \text{Pa·s}, \quad g = 9.81 \; \text{m/s}^2$$ **Step 2:** Apply Stokes' Law $$v_s = \frac{g (\rho_s - \rho_l) d_p^2}{18 \mu}$$ **Step 3:** Substitute numerical values $$v_s = \frac{9.81 \times (1050 - 1000) \times (50 \times 10^{-6})^2}{18 \times 1.0 \times 10^{-3}}$$ **Step 4:** Compute numerator and denominator separately $$\text{Numerator} = 9.81 \times 50 \times 2500 \times 10^{-12} = 9.81 \times 50 \times 2.5 \times 10^{-9}$$ $$= 1226.25 \times 10^{-9} = 1.22625 \times 10^{-6}$$ $$\text{Denominator} = 18 \times 1.0 \times 10^{-3} = 18 \times 10^{-3} = 0.018$$ **Step 5:** Divide $$v_s = \frac{1.22625 \times 10^{-6}}{0.018} = 6.8125 \times 10^{-5} \; \text{m/s}$$ $$v_s \approx 6.81 \times 10^{-5} \; \text{m/s} = 0.245 \; \text{m/h}$$ **Step 6:** Verify Reynolds Number $$Re = \frac{\rho_l v_s d_p}{\mu} = \frac{1000 \times 6.81 \times 10^{-5} \times 50 \times 10^{-6}}{1.0 \times 10^{-3}}$$ $$Re = \frac{1000 \times 6.81 \times 50 \times 10^{-11}}{10^{-3}} = \frac{3.405 \times 10^{-6}}{10^{-3}}$$ $$Re = 3.405 \times 10^{-3} = 0.0034$$ Since $Re \ll 0.3$, **Stokes' Law is valid**. ✅ ## 3.3 Design of Primary Sedimentation Tank ### **Problem** Design a primary sedimentation tank for a food industry ETP treating $Q = 500$ m³/d. Assume overflow rate $v_o = 1.5$ m/h. ### **Step-by-Step Solution** **Step 1:** Determine surface area $$A_s = \frac{Q}{v_o} = \frac{500 \; \text{m}^3/\text{d}}{1.5 \; \text{m/h}} = \frac{500}{36} \; \text{m}^2 = 13.89 \; \text{m}^2$$ **Step 2:** Assume rectangular tank with L:B = 4:1 $$L \times B = 13.89, \quad L = 4B$$ $$4B^2 = 13.89 \implies B = \sqrt{3.47} = 1.86 \; \text{m}$$ $$L = 4 \times 1.86 = 7.46 \; \text{m}$$ **Step 3:** Assume depth $H = 3$ m $$V = A_s \times H = 13.89 \times 3 = 41.67 \; \text{m}^3$$ **Step 4:** Detention time $$t_d = \frac{V}{Q} = \frac{41.67 \times 24}{500} = 2.0 \; \text{h}$$ > This detention time of **2 hours** is within the **standard KTU design range of 1.5–2.5 h** for primary clarifiers handling food industry effluents. ## 3.4 Solids Flux Derivation (For Thickener Area) ### **Step 1:** Define Total Solids Flux The total mass of solids passing upward per unit area per unit time must equal the mass flux due to gravity plus that removed by underflow pumping: $$G_{total} = G_{gravity} + G_{underflow}$$ $$G_{total} = C_i \cdot v_i + \frac{Q_u \cdot C_u}{A}$$ ### **Step 2:** Critical Concentration The thickener area is governed by the **limiting (critical) solids flux**: $$G_L = C_i \cdot v_i$$ ### **Step 3:** Required Thickener Area (Coehlo–Morris Equation) $$A = \frac{Q_u \cdot C_u}{G_L - \frac{Q_u \cdot C_u}{A_{min}}}$$ This requires **iterative solution** using a **flux curve** plotting $G_L$ vs $C_i$. --- # SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics ## 4.1 Sedimentation Process Flow Diagram ```mermaid flowchart TD A[Influent Inlet
Food Industry Effluent] --> B[Bar Screen
Grit Removal] B --> C[Pumping Station
Lift Effluent] C --> D[Primary Sedimentation Tank
Type 1 and Type 2 Settling] D --> E[Clarified Supernatant
Overflow to Biological Treatment] D --> F[Sludge Blanket
Settled Solids] F --> G[Sludge Hopper
Collection Zone] G --> H[Sludge Pump
Transfer] H --> I[Sludge Thickener
Compression Settling] I --> J[Centrifuge / Drying Bed
Final Dewatering] J --> K[Recovered Solids
By-product or Disposal] ``` ## 4.2 Four Regimes of Sedimentation — Decision Matrix ```mermaid flowchart TD R[Sedimentation Analysis Start] --> Q1{Particle Size?} Q1 -->|Greater than 100 micrometer| S1[Type 1: Discrete Settling
Stokes Law] Q1 -->|10 to 100 micrometer| Q2{Concentration?} Q1 -->|Less than 10 micrometer| S4[Type 4: Compression Settling
Dick Ewing Equation] Q2 -->|Low| S2[Type 2: Flocculent Settling
Column Test Analysis] Q2 -->|High| S3[Type 3: Hindered Zone Settling
Solids Flux Theory] S1 --> X1[Application: Grit Removal in Canneries] S2 --> X2[Application: Yeast Recovery in Breweries] S3 --> X3[Application: Secondary Clarifier in Dairy ETP] S4 --> X4[Application: Sludge Thickener Bottom Zone] ``` ## 4.3 Rectangular Primary Sedimentation Tank — Schematic Architecture ```mermaid flowchart LR subgraph TANK[Rectangular Sedimentation Tank] direction TB INF[Inlet Zone
Distribution Baffles] --> SET[Central Settling Zone
Low Velocity Region] SET --> EFZ[Effluent Zone
Weir Collection] SET --> SB[Sludge Blanket
Bottom Accumulation] SB --> SH[Sludge Hopper
Conical Collection] end INF -->|Q influent| TANK EFZ -->|Q effluent overflow| OUT[Biological Treatment Plant] SH -->|Q underflow sludge| SLG[Sludge Processing Unit] ``` ## 4.4 Block-Level Functional Architecture of a Food Industry ETP ```mermaid flowchart TB subgraph INTAKE[Intake and Pre-Treatment] A1[Raw Effluent Collection] --> A2[Screening] A2 --> A3[Flow Equalization] end subgraph PRIMARY[Primary Sedimentation Stage] B1[Coarse Settling
Type 1 Discrete] --> B2[Fine Settling
Type 2 Flocculent] B2 --> B3[Sludge Collection Hopper] end subgraph SECONDARY[Secondary Biological Stage] C1[Aeration Tank
Activated Sludge] --> C2[Secondary Clarifier
Type 3 Zone Settling] end subgraph SLUDGE[Sludge Handling] D1[Thickener
Type 4 Compression] --> D2[Dewatering Unit] D2 --> D3[By-product Recovery] end INTAKE --> PRIMARY PRIMARY --> SECONDARY SECONDARY --> SLUDGE B3 --> D1 C2 --> D1 ``` --- # SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ## PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each) --- ### **Question 1** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]` **Define sedimentation. Differentiate between Type 1 (Discrete) and Type 2 (Flocculent) settling.** #### Model Answer (3 Marks): > **Definition (1 Mark):** Sedimentation is the gravity-driven separation of suspended solid particles from a liquid medium, in which denser particles settle to the bottom forming sludge while clarified liquid overflows from the top. **Differences (2 Marks):** | Aspect | Type 1: Discrete | Type 2: Flocculent | |---|---|---| | **Particle behavior** | Settles as individual entities | Particles coalesce/flocculate during descent | | **Settling velocity** | Constant with depth | Increases with depth due to aggregation | | **Typical size** | > 100 µm | 10–100 µm | | **Analysis method** | Stokes' / Newton's Law | Column settling test (Talmadge–Fitch) | | **Food industry example** | Grit removal in canning | Yeast recovery in breweries | --- ### **Question 2** `[KTU University Exam - June 2024]` **State Stokes' Law. List the assumptions made in its derivation.** #### Model Answer (3 Marks): > **Stokes' Law Statement (1.5 Marks):** The terminal settling velocity of a spherical particle settling in a Newtonian fluid under laminar conditions is given by: > $$v_s = \frac{g (\rho_s - \rho_l) d_p^2}{18 \mu}$$ **Assumptions (1.5 Marks):** 1. Particle is **rigid, smooth, and spherical**. 2. Particle settles in an **infinite, quiescent medium**. 3. Flow regime is **laminar** ($Re < 0.3$). 4. Fluid is **Newtonian** (constant viscosity). 5. Particle does **not interfere** with neighboring particles. 6. **No wall effects** (particle far from container boundaries). 7. Settling occurs under **gravity only** (no centrifugal force). --- ## PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks with Internal Choice) --- ### **Question A (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]` **Design a primary sedimentation tank for a food industry effluent treatment plant. Given data:** - Flow rate $Q = 2000$ m³/d - Desired overflow rate $v_o = 1.2$ m/h - Length-to-breadth ratio = 4:1 - Tank depth $H = 3.0$ m #### **(a) Calculate the surface area, dimensions, and detention time of the tank. (7 Marks)** **Step 1:** Convert flow rate to consistent units $$Q = \frac{2000}{24} = 83.33 \; \text{m}^3/\text{h}$$ **Step 2:** Compute surface area $$A_s = \frac{Q}{v_o} = \frac{83.33}{1.2} = 69.44 \; \text{m}^2$$ **Step 3:** Apply L:B = 4:1 $$L \times B = 69.44, \quad L = 4B$$ $$4B^2 = 69.44 \implies B^2 = 17.36 \implies B = 4.17 \; \text{m}$$ $$L = 4 \times 4.17 = 16.67 \; \text{m}$$ **Step 4:** Compute volume $$V = A_s \times H = 69.44 \times 3.0 = 208.33 \; \text{m}^3$$ **Step 5:** Calculate detention time $$t_d = \frac{V}{Q} = \frac{208.33}{83.33} = 2.5 \; \text{h}$$ > **Answer:** $A_s = 69.44$ m², $L = 16.67$ m, $B = 4.17$ m, $t_d = 2.5$ h ✓ #### **(b) A food waste stream contains organic particles of diameter $d_p = 80$ µm and density $\rho_s = 1080$ kg/m³. The wastewater has $\rho_l = 1000$ kg/m³ and $\mu = 1.2 \times 10^{-3}$ Pa·s. Calculate the terminal settling velocity and verify the regime. (7 Marks)** **Step 1:** Identify given values $$d_p = 80 \times 10^{-6} \; \text{m}, \quad \rho_s = 1080 \; \text{kg/m}^3, \quad \rho_l = 1000 \; \text{kg/m}^3$$ $$\mu = 1.2 \times 10^{-3} \; \text{Pa·s}, \quad g = 9.81 \; \text{m/s}^2$$ **Step 2:** Apply Stokes' Law $$v_s = \frac{9.81 \times (1080 - 1000) \times (80 \times 10^{-6})^2}{18 \times 1.2 \times 10^{-3}}$$ **Step 3:** Calculate numerator $$\text{Numerator} = 9.81 \times 80 \times 6400 \times 10^{-12} = 9.81 \times 80 \times 6.4 \times 10^{-9}$$ $$= 5022.72 \times 10^{-9} = 5.023 \times 10^{-6}$$ **Step 4:** Calculate denominator $$\text{Denominator} = 18 \times 1.2 \times 10^{-3} = 0.0216$$ **Step 5:** Solve $$v_s = \frac{5.023 \times 10^{-6}}{0.0216} = 2.325 \times 10^{-4} \; \text{m/s} = 0.837 \; \text{m/h}$$ **Step 6:** Verify Reynolds Number $$Re = \frac{1000 \times 2.325 \times 10^{-4} \times 80 \times 10^{-6}}{1.2 \times 10^{-3}} = \frac{0.0186}{0.0012} = 0.0155$$ Since $Re = 0.0155 \ll 0.3$, **Stokes' Law is valid**. ✅ > **Answer:** $v_s = 2.325 \times 10^{-4}$ m/s, regime is laminar ✓ --- ### **Question B (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam - June 2024]` — *Alternative Choice* **Explain in detail the four types of sedimentation. With the help of a neat sketch, describe the design considerations of a circular clarifier used in a food industry effluent treatment plant.** #### **(a) Detailed explanation of the four types of sedimentation. (7 Marks)** **Type 1 — Discrete Settling (1.5 Marks):** - Each particle settles as an individual entity with constant velocity. - No interaction between particles; shape and size remain unchanged. - Analyzed using **Stokes' Law** ($Re < 0.3$) and **Newton's Law** ($Re > 1000$). - Typical in **grit chambers** of food processing ETPs. **Type 2 — Flocculent Settling (1.5 Marks):** - Particles coalesce during descent, forming aggregates of increasing size. - Settling velocity **increases with depth** and time. - Analyzed using **column settling tests** and **Talmadge–Fitch method**. - Common in **dairy wastewater** (milk protein flocs) and **brewery effluents** (yeast flocs). **Type 3 — Hindered (Zone) Settling (2 Marks):** - Occurs in **high-concentration suspensions**. - Particles settle as a **uniform blanket** with a sharp solid–liquid interface. - Settling velocity decreases with increasing solids concentration. - Analyzed using **solids flux theory** (Coehlo–Morris). - Typical in **secondary clarifiers** of biological treatment systems. **Type 4 — Compression Settling (2 Marks):** - Occurs at the **bottom of the sludge blanket** where particles touch each other. - Consolidation proceeds by **squeezing out water** under the weight of overlying solids. - Very slow; governed by the **Dick–Ewing equation**. - Relevant in **sludge thickeners** and **sludge drying beds**. #### **(b) Design considerations of a circular clarifier with sketch. (7 Marks)** A **circular clarifier** consists of: - A **central feed well** where influent enters. - A **sludge scraping mechanism** rotating slowly at the bottom. - A **peripheral effluent weir** for clarified water collection. - A **central sludge hopper** for sludge withdrawal. **Design Considerations:** 1. **Surface Overflow Rate (SOR):** Typically **1.5–3.0 m/h** for primary clarifiers in food industry ETPs. 2. **Hydraulic Retention Time (HRT):** **1.5–2.5 hours**. 3. **Weir Loading Rate:** Should not exceed **125–200 m³/m·d** to avoid short-circuiting. 4. **Tank Diameter:** Usually **5–50 m** based on flow. 5. **Tank Depth:** **3.0–4.5 m** at the periphery; central depth **3.5–5.0 m** for sludge storage. 6. **Bottom Slope:** **1:12 to 1:10** toward central hopper. 7. **Detention Time for Sludge:** Typically **2–4 hours** before withdrawal. **Sketch (Schematic Flow):** ``` INFLOW (Center) | [Feed Well] | ==================== | Settling Zone | | (Low Velocity) | ==================== / \ / Sludge \ / Collection \ / (Hopper) \ | SLUDGE OUT ---- Weir at Periphery ---- | CLARIFIED EFFLUENT OUT ``` **Material Balance:** $$Q \cdot C_o = Q_e \cdot C_e + Q_u \cdot C_u$$ where $C_o, C_e, C_u$ = influent, effluent, and underflow concentrations respectively. --- ## KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout > [!WARNING] > **Common Mistakes That Cost Marks:** > > 1. **Unit Inconsistency:** Mixing $\mu$m with m, or cm/s with m/h without conversion. **Always convert to SI base units** before applying Stokes' Law. > > 2. **Forgetting to Verify Reynolds Number:** Students often apply Stokes' Law blindly without checking whether $Re < 0.3$. If $Re > 0.3$, **full credit is forfeited**. > > 3. **Skipping Force Balance Steps:** Examiners award marks for **explicit force equations** ($F_g$, $F_b$, $F_d$). Do not jump directly to the final equation. > > 4. **Wrong Direction of Forces:** Buoyancy acts **upward**, gravity acts **downward**. Drawing them incorrectly loses **0.5–1 mark**. > > 5. **Not Specifying the Regime:** Always conclude with a statement like *"Since $Re = 0.015 \ll 0.3$, Stokes' Law is applicable."* > > 6. **Confusing Overflow Rate with Settling Velocity:** These are **dimensionally similar** but conceptually different. Overflow rate ($v_o = Q/A$) is a design parameter; settling velocity ($v_s$) is a particle property. > > 7. **Using Hazen's Equation as a Substitute for Stokes':** Hazen's equation is an **approximation** valid for dilute discrete suspensions only — not for flocculent or hindered settling. --- ## Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember - **Sedimentation** is a **gravity-based primary treatment** process used extensively in food industry ETPs to remove suspended solids. - **Four settling regimes:** Discrete (Type 1), Flocculent (Type 2), Hindered/Zone (Type 3), Compression (Type 4). - **Stokes' Law** applies when **$Re < 0.3$** (laminar); $v_s \propto d_p^2$. - **Newton's Law** applies when **$Re > 1000$** (turbulent); $v_s \propto \sqrt{d_p}$. - **Overflow Rate** ($v_o = Q/A_s$) is the **critical design parameter** for primary clarifiers. - **Detention Time** ($t_d = V/Q$) is typically **1.5–2.5 h** for food industry wastewater. - **Settling velocity** depends on: **particle size**, **density difference**, **fluid viscosity**. - **Larger particles** settle faster (quadratic dependence on $d_p$). - **Warmer wastewater** (lower viscosity) **enhances sedimentation** efficiency. - **Hindered settling** in secondary clarifiers is analyzed using **solids flux theory**. - **Compression settling** in sludge thickeners is the slowest regime, governed by **Dick–Ewing**. - **Food industry applications:** dairy, brewery, distillery, canning, sugar, slaughterhouse, seafood processing. - **Always verify Reynolds number** after applying Stokes' Law. - **Standard design SOR:** 1.5–3.0 m/h for primary clarifiers; 0.7–1.5 m/h for secondary clarifiers. - **Weir loading** should remain below **125 m³/m·d** to prevent short-circuiting. ---

topics.title: Floatation and primary treatments
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PEFTT744 - FOOD INDUSTRY WASTE MANAGEMENT > - **Module:** Module 3 > - **Topic:** Floatation and primary treatments # 📘 SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ## 1.1 Primary Treatment — The Academic Definition > [!IMPORTANT] > **Primary Treatment** in food industry wastewater engineering refers to the **physical and/or chemical separation of suspended solids, settleable matter, floating debris, oils, greases, and grit** from raw effluent **prior to biological (secondary) treatment**. It is the *first line of defence* downstream of the screening unit and constitutes a *preliminary–primary train* in conventional flow schemes. In KTU 2024 Scheme parlance, primary treatment operations are categorized into: 1. **Preliminary Treatment** — Screening, grit removal, flow equalization 2. **Primary Treatment** — Primary sedimentation (clarifiers), flotation, oil–grease separation ## 1.2 Floatation — The Academic Definition > [!NOTE] > **Floatation (Flotation)** is a **gravity-separation process** in which suspended or colloidal particles are attached to **fine gas bubbles** (usually air), forming a particle–bubble aggregate whose **bulk density is lower than that of the surrounding liquid**. The aggregate rises to the surface, where it is skimmed off as a float layer. The driving force is the **buoyancy force** acting on a particle–bubble aggregate whose effective density is reduced below that of water. ## 1.3 Conceptual Analogy — "Salad Spinner & Bubble Rafts" > [!TIP] > **Imagine a glass of muddy river water.** If you let it sit, *heavy sand settles down* (→ **sedimentation**). But tiny oil droplets and fine organic particles *refuse to sink* — they keep floating stubbornly. Now, **blow tiny air bubbles** through the water (like the air jets in an aquarium). The bubbles **stick to oil droplets** (think soap clinging to grease in dishwater). The combined bubble-oil droplet becomes lighter than water and **zooms up to the surface like a tiny hot-air balloon**, where it forms a foamy raft that you can scoop away with a spoon. **That scoop = the float layer skimmed in a DAF unit.** The sinking sand in the same glass represents primary sedimentation. Together, these two physical phenomena form the backbone of primary treatment for food industry wastewaters (rich in oils, fats, proteins, fruit pulp, and starches). ## 1.4 Why Primary Treatment Matters for Food Industry Effluents Food industry wastewaters are characterized by: - **High BOD/COD (5,000 – 60,000 mg/L)** — from sugars, starches, proteins - **High Oil & Grease (O&G)** — dairy, edible-oil, meat processing - **High Suspended Solids (SS)** — fruit pulp, grain particles - **Temperature and pH variability** Without primary treatment, these loads would **shock the biological reactors** downstream. > [!IMPORTANT] > **Standard Performance Targets for Primary Treatment (KTU/CPHEEO convention):** > - TSS removal: **50 – 70 %** > - BOD removal: **25 – 40 %** > - Oil & Grease removal: **70 – 95 %** (with floatation) ## 1.5 Classification of Floatation Systems Floatation in food industry wastewater engineering is classified based on **how the gas (air) bubbles are generated**: | Method | Bubble Size | Pressure Range | Typical Application | |---|---|---|---| | **Dissolved Air Flotation (DAF)** | 10 – 100 µm | 3 – 6 bar | O&G, dairy, slaughterhouse | | **Induced Air Flotation (IAF)** | 100 – 1000 µm | Atmospheric | Coarse bubble; pretreatment | | **Vacuum Flotation** | 50 – 200 µm | Sub-atmospheric | High-strength emulsions | | **Electro-flotation** | 20 – 50 µm | Atmospheric | Colloidal/particulate | # 📘 SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ## 2.1 The Physics of Floatation — Why Particles Rise When a gas bubble attaches to a solid or oily particle, the **particle–bubble aggregate** has an effective density: $$\rho_{agg} = \frac{\rho_p V_p + \rho_g V_g}{V_p + V_g}$$ Since $\rho_g \ll \rho_p$ for air and water, the aggregate density becomes much less than water, causing **buoyant rise**. ### 2.1.1 Stokes-Regime Rise Velocity (Quoted in KTU Reference Texts) For a discrete particle-bubble aggregate rising under laminar conditions: $$v_r = \frac{g (\rho_w - \rho_{agg}) d_{agg}^2}{18 \mu}$$ where: - $v_r$ = rise velocity of aggregate (m/s) - $g$ = acceleration due to gravity = **9.81 m/s²** - $\rho_w$ = density of water ≈ **1000 kg/m³** - $\rho_{agg}$ = effective aggregate density (kg/m³) - $d_{agg}$ = effective aggregate diameter (m) - $\mu$ = dynamic viscosity of water (Pa·s) > [!NOTE] > **Why this matters in a flotation cell:** Engineers rely on the *surface overflow rate (SOR)* or *hydraulic loading rate (HLR)* to size the float tank. The rise velocity sets the **minimum upward bubble-flux needed** to capture a given particle. ## 2.2 Step-by-Step Mechanism of DAF (Dissolved Air Flotation) ### Step 1 — Saturation A portion of clarified effluent is pressurized (typically **3 – 6 bar**) and saturated with air in a **saturation tank / pressure vessel**. Henry's Law dictates the dissolved air quantity: $$C_a = K_H \cdot P$$ where: - $C_a$ = dissolved air concentration (mg/L) - $K_H$ = Henry's constant for air in water (mg/L·atm) ≈ **29.3 (at 20 °C)** - $P$ = absolute pressure (atm) ### Step 2 — Release The pressurized recycle stream is depressurized through a **needle valve / nozzle / special DAF nozzle** into the flotation cell. The dissolved air comes out of solution forming **micro-bubbles (10 – 100 µm)**. ### Step 3 — Bubble–Particle Attachment Bubbles attach to suspended/oily particles via three mechanisms: - **Collision** (Brownian + fluid shear) - **Attachment** (surface tension / hydrophobic interaction) - **Agglomeration** (particle–bubble clusters form) ### Step 4 — Rise The aggregate rises to the surface in **3 – 10 minutes** (HRT of DAF cell). ### Step 5 — Skimming The float layer (foam + sludge) is skimmed by a **rotating paddle skimmer** or chain-and-flight scraper. ### Step 6 — Recycle The clarified subnatant exits at the bottom; the pressurized recycle stream re-circulates through the saturator. ## 2.3 Critical Design Parameters for DAF | Parameter | Symbol | Typical Range (Food Industry) | Unit | |---|---|---|---| | Hydraulic Retention Time | HRT | 15 – 60 | min | | Surface Overflow Rate | SOR / HLR | 5 – 25 | m³/(m²·h) | | Air-to-Solids Ratio | A/S | 0.02 – 0.10 | kg air / kg TSS | | Recycle Ratio | R | 5 – 50 | % of influent flow | | Saturator Pressure | P | 3 – 6 | bar | | Saturator Retention Time | τ_sat | 1 – 5 | min | ## 2.4 Primary Sedimentation — The Twin of Floatation Primary clarifiers (settling tanks) work on the opposite principle: particles **denser than water** settle to the bottom under gravity. ### Stokes' Settling Velocity (for discrete particles): $$v_s = \frac{g (\rho_p - \rho_w) d_p^2}{18 \mu}$$ ### Surface Overflow Rate (SOR) for Primary Clarifiers: $$\text{SOR} = \frac{Q}{A_s}$$ where: - $Q$ = design flow (m³/h) - $A_s$ = surface area of clarifier (m²) > [!IMPORTANT] > **Design Rule (CPHEEO/KTU):** The SOR must be **less than the settling velocity** of the smallest particle to be removed: > $$\text{SOR} \le v_s$$ ## 2.5 High-Yield KTU Formula Sheet | # | Formula | Meaning / Use | |---|---|---| | 1 | $C_a = K_H \cdot P$ | Henry's Law — dissolved air concentration in saturator | | 2 | $A/S = \frac{C_{a,recycle} \cdot R}{X_{in}}$ | Air-to-solids ratio (kg air / kg TSS) | | 3 | $v_r = \dfrac{g (\rho_w - \rho_{agg}) d_{agg}^2}{18\mu}$ | Bubble–particle aggregate rise velocity (Stokes) | | 4 | $v_s = \dfrac{g (\rho_p - \rho_w) d_p^2}{18\mu}$ | Discrete particle settling velocity (Stokes) | | 5 | $\text{SOR} = Q/A_s$ | Surface overflow rate for clarifier (m/h) | | 6 | $\text{HRT} = V/Q$ | Hydraulic retention time (h) | | 7 | $\eta_R = \dfrac{v_s}{\text{SOR}} \times 100$ | Removal efficiency for ideally settling particles (%) | | 8 | $\eta_{overall} = 1 - (1-\eta_P)(1-\eta_F)(1-\eta_S)$ | Combined removal across P, F, S stages | | 9 | $\Delta P \cdot V = nRT$ | Saturation pressure–volume (ideal gas; for air mass balance) | | 10 | $W = \dfrac{Q \cdot X \cdot \eta_R}{1000}$ | Dry solids in float/settled sludge (kg/d) | ## 2.6 Real-World Engineering Utility - **DAF in dairy industry:** Removes 80 – 95 % of milk fat and casein fines before biological treatment — prevents fouling of anaerobic digesters. - **Primary clarifiers in slaughterhouses:** Remove 60 % of settleable blood and paunch manure particulates. - **Vegetable processing:** DAF + primary clarifier combo controls seasonal sugar/starch loads from potato, fruit pulping operations. # 📘 SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ## 3.1 Derivation: Air-to-Solids Ratio (A/S) — A KTU Favorite **Given:** - Recycle flow rate $Q_R$ (m³/h) - Saturator pressure $P_{sat}$ (bar, absolute) - Recycle ratio $R = Q_R / Q_{in}$ - Influent TSS concentration $X_{in}$ (mg/L) - Inlet flow $Q_{in}$ (m³/h) **Step 1:** Mass of air dissolved in pressurized recycle stream (per unit volume of recycle): $$C_{a,sat} = K_H \cdot P_{sat}$$ At 20 °C, $K_H = 29.3$ mg/(L·atm) ≈ 29.3 mg/(L·bar) (numerically similar). **Step 2:** When released to atmospheric pressure ($P_{atm} = 1$ bar), the air released per unit recycle volume: $$\Delta C_a = K_H (P_{sat} - P_{atm})$$ **Step 3:** Total mass of air released per hour (across the recycle stream entering the cell): $$\dot{m}_{air} = Q_R \cdot \Delta C_a = Q_R \cdot K_H (P_{sat} - P_{atm})$$ **Step 4:** Total mass of TSS entering the cell per hour: $$\dot{m}_{TSS} = Q_{in} \cdot X_{in}$$ **Step 5:** Form the Air-to-Solids Ratio: $$A/S = \frac{\dot{m}_{air}}{\dot{m}_{TSS}} = \frac{Q_R \cdot K_H (P_{sat} - P_{atm})}{Q_{in} \cdot X_{in}}$$ Substituting $Q_R = R \cdot Q_{in}$: $$A/S = \frac{R \cdot K_H (P_{sat} - P_{atm})}{X_{in}}$$ > [!TIP] > **Numerical sanity check:** If $R = 0.20$, $P_{sat} = 5$ bar, $X_{in} = 3000$ mg/L: > $$A/S = \frac{0.20 \cdot 29.3 \cdot (5-1)}{3000} = \frac{23.44}{3000} \approx 0.0078$$ > This is below the typical 0.02–0.10 range, suggesting the operator must **increase $R$** or **raise $P_{sat}$**. ## 3.2 Derivation: Combined Removal Efficiency (Multi-Stage) For a **preliminary → primary → floatation** train: - Screening removal efficiency = $\eta_P$ - Primary sedimentation = $\eta_{PS}$ - Floatation = $\eta_F$ For each stage: $$\text{Effluent}_i = \text{Influent}_i \cdot (1 - \eta_i)$$ Applying sequentially: $$\text{Effluent}_{final} = \text{Influent} \cdot (1-\eta_P)(1-\eta_{PS})(1-\eta_F)$$ Overall efficiency: $$\eta_{overall} = 1 - (1-\eta_P)(1-\eta_{PS})(1-\eta_F)$$ ### Worked Example **Given:** A vegetable cannery wastewater train: - Screening: $\eta_P = 20\%$ - Primary sedimentation: $\eta_{PS} = 55\%$ - DAF: $\eta_F = 75\%$ - Influent TSS = 4000 mg/L **Step 1:** After screening: $$X_1 = 4000 \cdot (1 - 0.20) = 3200 \text{ mg/L}$$ **Step 2:** After primary sedimentation: $$X_2 = 3200 \cdot (1 - 0.55) = 1440 \text{ mg/L}$$ **Step 3:** After DAF: $$X_3 = 1440 \cdot (1 - 0.75) = 360 \text{ mg/L}$$ **Step 4:** Overall efficiency: $$\eta_{overall} = 1 - 0.80 \cdot 0.45 \cdot 0.25 = 1 - 0.09 = 0.91$$ So **91 % overall TSS removal** is achievable through the train. ## 3.3 Analytical Decision Matrix — Choosing Between Primary Sedimentation and Floatation | Criterion | Primary Sedimentation | Floatation (DAF) | |---|---|---| | Particle density | $\rho_p > 1050$ kg/m³ | $\rho_p < 1000$ kg/m³ (oil, fat) | | Particle size | $> 50$ µm | $1 - 100$ µm | | HRT required | 1.5 – 2.5 h | 15 – 60 min | | Footprint | Larger | Compact (3–5× smaller) | | Capital cost | Lower | Higher | | Operating cost | Lower | Higher (recirculation pump) | | Oil/Grease removal | 30 – 50 % | 80 – 95 % | | Best for | Fruit/veg pulp, soil, grit | Dairy, edible oil, meat, fish | ## 3.4 Step-by-Step Design of a DAF Unit — KTU Pattern **Design problem:** Design a DAF unit for a dairy plant wastewater. **Given:** - $Q_{in} = 250$ m³/d - Influent TSS = 1800 mg/L - Influent O&G = 600 mg/L - Target SOR = 15 m³/(m²·h) - Recycle ratio = 25 % - Saturator pressure = 5 bar (abs) **Step 1:** Daily flow in m³/h: $$Q_{in} = 250/24 = 10.42 \text{ m}^3/\text{h}$$ **Step 2:** Surface area required: $$A_s = \frac{Q_{in}}{\text{SOR}} = \frac{10.42}{15} = 0.694 \text{ m}^2$$ **Step 3:** Add recycle flow to total hydraulic load on cell: $$Q_{cell} = Q_{in} (1 + R) = 10.42 \cdot 1.25 = 13.02 \text{ m}^3/\text{h}$$ $$A_{s,actual} = \frac{13.02}{15} = 0.868 \text{ m}^2 \approx 0.87 \text{ m}^2$$ **Step 4:** Adopt a rectangular cell of $1.0 \text{ m} \times 0.9 \text{ m} = 0.90 \text{ m}^2$. **Step 5:** Volume assuming HRT = 30 min = 0.5 h: $$V = Q_{cell} \cdot \text{HRT} = 13.02 \cdot 0.5 = 6.51 \text{ m}^3$$ Depth = $V/A_s = 6.51/0.90 = 7.23$ m — too deep, reduce HRT to 20 min: $$V = 13.02 \cdot (20/60) = 4.34 \text{ m}^3, \quad \text{Depth} = 4.34/0.90 = 4.82 \text{ m}$$ Adopt depth = **3.0 m** with HRT ≈ 12 min — acceptable for dairy DAF. **Step 6:** Air-to-Solids Ratio check: $$A/S = \frac{0.25 \cdot 29.3 \cdot (5-1)}{1800} = \frac{29.3}{1800} \approx 0.016$$ Below ideal range; suggest increasing $R$ to 0.30 or $P_{sat}$ to 6 bar. # 📘 SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics ## 4.1 Block Diagram — Primary Treatment Flow Train ```mermaid flowchart LR A[Raw Food
Industry Wastewater] --> B[Bar Screens
Screening] B --> C[Grit Chamber
Grit Removal] C --> D[Flow Equalization
EQ Tank] D --> E[Oil and Grease
Trap] E --> F[Primary Clarifier
Sedimentation] F --> G{Choose by Effluent
Characteristics} G --> H[Dissolved Air
Floatation DAF] G --> I[Induced Air
Floatation IAF] H --> J[Skimmed Float
to Sludge Handling] I --> J F --> K[Primary Sludge
to Thickener] J --> L[Biological
Treatment Stage] K --> L ``` ## 4.2 Schematic — Internal Architecture of a DAF Cell ```mermaid flowchart TD nodeA[Influent
Inlet] --> nodeB[Inlet Distribution
Baffle Zone] nodeB --> nodeC[Contact Zone
Bubble Particle Contact] nodeD[Pressurized Recycle
from Saturator] --> nodeC nodeC --> nodeE[Separation Zone
Rise of Aggregates] nodeE --> nodeF[Float Layer
Accumulated Sludge] nodeE --> nodeG[Clarified Subnatant
Effluent] nodeF --> nodeH[Skimming Mechanism
Paddle or Flight Scraper] nodeH --> nodeI[Float Sludge Hopper] nodeG --> nodeJ[Effluent Weir Outlet] nodeI --> nodeK[Sludge to Digestion
or Drying Beds] ``` ## 4.3 Block Diagram — Mass Balance for a DAF Cell ```mermaid flowchart LR nodeM[Influent Flow Q
with TSS X_in] --> nodeN[DAF Cell] nodeR[Recycle Flow QR
with Dissolved Air] --> nodeN nodeN --> nodeO[Float X_f
to Skimmer] nodeN --> nodeP[Effluent X_e
to Next Stage] nodeN --> nodeQ[Recycle Outflow QR_out] nodeQ --> nodeS[Saturator
Pressurized] nodeS --> nodeR ``` ## 4.4 Functional Architecture — Floatation Physics ```mermaid flowchart TD node1[Suspended or Oily Particle] --> node2[Bubble Attachment via
Collision plus Adhesion] node2 --> node3[Aggregate Density
Less than Water] node3 --> node4[Buoyant Rise to
Surface in 3 to 10 min] node4 --> node5[Float Layer
at Air Water Interface] node5 --> node6[Skimming
and Sludge Withdrawal] node1 -->|Alternative Path| node7[Particles too Heavy
Sink in Clarifier] node7 --> node8[Sludge Blanket
at Bottom] node8 --> node9[Sludge Pumping
and Dewatering] ``` # 📘 SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap --- ## 📝 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks each) ### **Q1.** [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023] **Differentiate between primary treatment and preliminary treatment in a food industry wastewater treatment plant.** **Model Answer:** > **Preliminary Treatment** is the *upstream physical removal* of coarse and abrasive matter that would otherwise damage pumps and downstream equipment. Examples: bar screens, comminutors, grit chambers, flow equalization. > > **Primary Treatment** is the *subsequent physical-chemical separation* of suspended organic matter (settleable solids, oils, greases, floating debris) from the wastewater. Examples: primary clarifiers, dissolved air flotation (DAF), induced air flotation (IAF). > > The key distinction: **preliminary removes inorganics/coarse matter**, whereas **primary removes organics/fine suspended matter**. They are functionally sequential within the same physical stage of wastewater treatment. --- ### **Q2.** [KTU University Exam – June 2024] **State Henry's Law as applied to dissolved air flotation. Mention the typical range of saturator pressure used in DAF systems.** **Model Answer:** Henry's Law states that at a constant temperature, the mass of gas dissolved in a given volume of solvent is **directly proportional to the partial pressure of the gas above the liquid**: $$C_a = K_H \cdot P$$ In DAF systems, air is dissolved in a pressurized recycle stream at saturator pressures typically ranging from **3 to 6 bar (absolute)**, with **4 – 5 bar** being most common in food-industry applications. --- ## 📝 Part B — Module Internal Choice (14 Marks each) > *Note: As per KTU 2024 Scheme, Module 3 Question Paper may offer an ESE internal choice between two 14-mark problems. Both alternatives are provided below.* --- ### **Question A (14 Marks)** — [KTU University Exam – May 2024] A **dairy processing plant** discharges **400 m³/d** of wastewater with **TSS = 2200 mg/L** and **O&G = 800 mg/L**. The proposed treatment train consists of: - (a) **Primary clarifier followed by DAF** — design and compute: 1. Surface area of the primary clarifier (assume SOR = 1.5 m/h). 2. Surface area of the DAF unit (assume SOR = 12 m³/m²·h, recycle ratio = 30 %). 3. The expected effluent TSS after both units (assume 50 % TSS removal in clarifier and 80 % TSS removal in DAF). 4. Daily mass of float sludge produced if float sludge contains 4 % solids (assume float sludge TSS after thickening = 25 g/L). **(7 Marks)** - (b) **Discuss the working principle of a dissolved air flotation unit with a neat block diagram. Why is DAF preferred over primary sedimentation for oil and grease removal in dairy wastewater?** **(7 Marks)** --- #### 🔑 Model Solution to Question A **Part (a) Solution:** **Step 1 — Primary Clarifier Surface Area:** $$Q_{in} = \frac{400}{24} = 16.67 \text{ m}^3/\text{h}$$ $$A_{PC} = \frac{Q_{in}}{\text{SOR}} = \frac{16.67}{1.5} = 11.11 \text{ m}^2$$ Adopt rectangular tank: **3.6 m × 3.1 m ≈ 11.16 m²**. **Step 2 — DAF Surface Area:** $$Q_{cell} = Q_{in}(1 + R) = 16.67 \cdot 1.30 = 21.67 \text{ m}^3/\text{h}$$ $$A_{DAF} = \frac{21.67}{12} = 1.806 \text{ m}^2 \approx 1.81 \text{ m}^2$$ Adopt square cell: **1.35 m × 1.35 m ≈ 1.82 m²**. **Step 3 — Effluent TSS:** - After clarifier: $2200 \cdot (1 - 0.50) = 1100 \text{ mg/L}$ - After DAF: $1100 \cdot (1 - 0.80) = 220 \text{ mg/L}$ **Step 4 — Float Sludge Mass:** - TSS removed by DAF: $Q_{in} \cdot (X_{in,DAF} - X_{out,DAF}) \cdot \eta_{factor}$: $$\dot{m}_{TSS,float} = 16.67 \cdot (1100 - 220)/1000 = 14.67 \text{ kg/h} = 352 \text{ kg/d (dry solids)}$$ - Wet sludge at 4 % solids: $$\dot{m}_{wet} = \frac{352}{0.04} = 8800 \text{ kg/d} \approx 8.8 \text{ m}^3/\text{d}$$ **Part (b) Model Answer:** DAF works by **dissolving air under pressure** (3–6 bar) in a recycle stream, then **releasing it through a pressure-reduction valve** into the flotation cell. The released air forms **micro-bubbles (10–100 µm)** that attach to oil/grease droplets and suspended particles via **collision, adhesion, and agglomeration**. The bubble–particle aggregate has effective density lower than water and **rises to the surface** in 3–10 minutes, forming a float layer skimmed by paddle or flight scrapers. DAF is preferred for dairy wastewater because: 1. **Oil and grease are lighter than water** — they do not settle; instead they must be floated. 2. **Micro-bubbles have high surface area**, ideal for capturing emulsified fat globules. 3. **HRT is short (15–60 min)** versus 1.5–2.5 h for primary clarifiers → **3–5× smaller footprint**. 4. **80–95 % O&G removal** versus 30–50 % in primary sedimentation. 5. **Float sludge has higher solids content** (3–6 %) → easier dewatering. --- ### **Question B (14 Marks)** — [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Modified] A **slaughterhouse** generates **600 m³/d** of wastewater containing high concentrations of blood, paunch manure, and suspended solids. Propose and justify a **complete primary treatment train**. - (a) List and briefly describe the **unit operations** in the train. Compute the **overall TSS removal efficiency** if individual stage removals are: screening = 15 %, grit removal = 5 %, primary clarifier = 60 %, DAF = 70 %. **(7 Marks)** - (b) With reference to **Stokes' Law**, derive the relationship between the **surface overflow rate of a primary clarifier** and the **removal efficiency** for an ideally settling particle. State **TWO design limitations** of the assumption. **(7 Marks)** --- #### 🔑 Model Solution to Question B **Part (a) Solution:** **Unit Operations in the Train:** 1. **Bar Screens** — remove coarse solids (paunch, hair, bone fragments); 15 % TSS. 2. **Grit Chamber** — remove inorganic grit (sand, bone chips) at controlled velocity ~0.3 m/s; 5 % TSS. 3. **Flow Equalization Tank** — dampen hydraulic and organic shock loads. 4. **Primary Clarifier** — settle suspended organic solids (blood, manure particulates); 60 % TSS. 5. **DAF Unit** — float residual oils, fats, emulsified colloids; 70 % TSS. **Overall Efficiency Calculation:** $$\eta_{overall} = 1 - (1-\eta_P)(1-\eta_G)(1-\eta_C)(1-\eta_F)$$ $$\eta_{overall} = 1 - (0.85)(0.95)(0.40)(0.30)$$ $$\eta_{overall} = 1 - 0.0969 = 0.9031 \approx 90.3\%$$ So the train achieves **~90 % TSS removal**. **Part (b) Solution:** **Derivation (Stokes' Law → SOR relationship):** Stokes' settling velocity: $$v_s = \frac{g(\rho_p - \rho_w) d_p^2}{18\mu}$$ Surface overflow rate of clarifier: $$\text{SOR} = \frac{Q}{A_s} = v_s \quad \text{(at threshold)}$$ For a particle settling at velocity $v_s$ in a clarifier with overflow rate SOR, the **fraction of particles removed** equals the ratio $v_s / \text{SOR}$ (provided $v_s \le \text{SOR}$): $$\eta = \frac{v_s}{\text{SOR}} \times 100\%$$ If $\text{SOR} \le v_s$, then 100 % removal. **Two Design Limitations:** 1. **Assumes discrete (Type I) settling** — does not account for **flocculent (Type II) settling** where particles agglomerate during settling, changing $d_p$ and $v_s$ dynamically. 2. **Assumes uniform horizontal velocity** and **no short-circuiting** — in reality, density currents, inlet turbulence, and dead zones reduce efficiency below the ideal. 3. *Bonus limitation:* Stokes' Law assumes laminar flow ($Re < 1$); at higher Reynolds numbers, drag coefficients deviate, and **Newton's Law** should be used. --- ## ⚠️ KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning > [!WARNING] > **Where students lose marks on this topic (Common Pitfalls):** > > 1. **Forgetting to add recycle flow** when computing DAF surface area: always use $Q_{cell} = Q_{in}(1 + R)$, not just $Q_{in}$. Examiners frequently test this. > > 2. **Confusing A/S with recycle ratio R** — A/S is dimensionless (kg air/kg TSS); R is a volumetric ratio (%). Do not interchange them. > > 3. **Mixing up Henry's constant units** — for air in water at 20 °C, $K_H \approx 29.3$ mg/(L·atm). Multiplying by pressure in **bar instead of atm** yields an answer off by ~1.013×. > > 4. **Wrong sign in Stokes' Law for floatation** — for *rising* aggregates, write $(\rho_w - \rho_{agg})$; for *settling* particles, write $(\rho_p - \rho_w)$. Marks deducted if swapped. > > 5. **Skipping the block diagram** in 7-mark answers — KTU examiners expect a labeled block diagram of DAF for full marks. --- ## 🧠 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember - ✅ **Primary Treatment** = Physical/chemical removal of suspended solids, O&G, and floating matter — it is the *second line of treatment* after preliminary operations (screening, grit removal). - ✅ **Floatation** relies on **bubble–particle attachment** that reduces effective aggregate density below water, causing buoyant rise. - ✅ **DAF** is the *most common* floatation technique in food industry; uses 3–6 bar saturator pressure and 10–100 µm micro-bubbles. - ✅ **Henry's Law**: $C_a = K_H \cdot P$ governs dissolved air concentration in saturator. - ✅ **Air-to-Solids Ratio (A/S)** = $R \cdot K_H (P_{sat} - P_{atm}) / X_{in}$ — typical range 0.02–0.10 kg air/kg TSS. - ✅ **Stokes' Law** governs both settling velocity ($v_s$) and rise velocity ($v_r$) of particles/aggregates. - ✅ **SOR (Surface Overflow Rate)** for primary clarifier: $Q/A_s$ — must be $\le v_s$ of target particle. - ✅ **Combined Removal Efficiency** across multi-stage train: $$\eta_{overall} = 1 - \prod_i (1 - \eta_i)$$ - ✅ DAF preferred over primary clarifier for **oily/floatable food wastewaters** (dairy, edible oil, slaughterhouse) due to compact footprint and 80–95 % O&G removal. - ✅ **Always include recycle flow** when computing DAF hydraulic loading. - ✅ Design parameters to memorize: **HRT = 15–60 min (DAF)**, **SOR = 5–25 m³/(m²·h) (DAF)**, **SOR = 1.0–2.0 m/h (clarifier)**. --- > 🎯 **Quick Mnemonic — "SHEFF" for Floatation Factors:** > **S** — Saturator pressure (3–6 bar) > **H** — Henry's Law governs dissolution > **E** — Effluent goes to next stage; Float skimmed off > **F** — Feed (recycle + influent) enters contact zone > **F** — Float layer = skimmed sludge → digester/dewatering

topics.title: Biological oxidations: objectives
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEFTT744 - FOOD INDUSTRY WASTE MANAGEMENT
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Biological oxidations: objectives

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# Module 3 — Biological Oxidations: Objectives

## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

**Biological Oxidation** is an engineered biochemical process in which microorganisms (bacteria, fungi, algae, and protozoa) utilize the **organic pollutants** present in food industry wastewater or solid waste as a substrate. Through **enzymatic catalysis**, they convert these biodegradable compounds into **simpler, more stable end-products** such as carbon dioxide (CO₂), water (H₂O), new microbial biomass, and (under anaerobic conditions) methane (CH₄).

In the KTU 2024 Scheme context, biological oxidation is positioned as the **primary secondary treatment step** for food industry effluents, which are characteristically high in **Biochemical Oxygen Demand (BOD)**, **Chemical Oxygen Demand (COD)**, and dissolved/suspended organic solids.

$$ \text{Organic Matter} + \text{O}_2 \xrightarrow{\text{Microorganisms}} \text{CO}_2 + \text{H}_2\text{O} + \text{New Cells} + \text{Energy (Heat)} $$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight (Module 3):** Biological oxidation is studied under "Treatment and Disposal Methods" of food industry wastes. Students are expected to know (i) the *objectives* of biological oxidation, (ii) the *microbiology* involved, (iii) the *types* of reactors, and (iv) the *design equations* (F/M ratio, SRT, HRT). This note focuses on **Objective 1: Objectives of Biological Oxidation**.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **Think of biological oxidation like a kitchen compost bin on an industrial scale.**
>
> Imagine you have a huge heap of leftover food from a hotel — vegetable peels, rice, milk, fruit pulp. If you just leave it in a pile, it slowly rots and stinks (natural uncontrolled decay). But if you **provide controlled oxygen, moisture, and mixing**, the friendly microorganisms "eat" the organic waste much faster, converting it into harmless CO₂, water, and a small amount of nutrient-rich sludge. The energy released as heat can even warm the reactor. This *controlled microbial feast* is biological oxidation. The "food" for microbes is your **BOD**, and the microbes are your **workforce**.

### 1.3 Why Biological Oxidation? — The Engineering Imperative

Food industries such as **dairy, brewery, distillery, fruit-canning, meat processing, vegetable oil, and seafood processing** generate wastewaters with characteristics shown in Table 1.

| Parameter | Typical Range in Food Industry Wastewater |
|---|---|
| BOD₅ | **1,000 – 30,000 mg/L** |
| COD | **2,000 – 60,000 mg/L** |
| Total Suspended Solids (TSS) | **500 – 15,000 mg/L** |
| pH | 3.5 – 11 (highly variable) |
| Temperature | 20 – 70 °C (often warm) |
| Fats, Oils & Grease (FOG) | 100 – 5,000 mg/L |

Direct discharge of such wastewaters causes **oxygen depletion in receiving water bodies**, **fish kills**, **eutrophication**, and **violation of Pollution Control Board norms** (e.g., CPCB/SPCB Kerala). Biological oxidation is the **most economical, sustainable, and widely accepted** route to bring BOD/COD down to dischargeable limits (**< 100 mg/L BOD; < 250 mg/L COD** as per typical Indian effluent norms).

---

## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 The Core Engineering Objectives of Biological Oxidation

The objectives are **multi-fold** and not limited to just "cleaning water." For KTU examination purposes, students must memorize **at least seven objectives**, each tied to an engineering or environmental benefit.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Board Expectation:** Any question of the form *"Discuss the objectives of biological oxidation of food waste"* carries **14 marks** in ESE. Students are expected to list, explain, and provide numerical justification for each objective. Merely listing without engineering rationale loses 3–5 marks.

#### Objective 1 — Reduction of BOD and COD (Primary Objective)
- **What:** Microbes oxidize soluble biodegradable organics (proteins, carbohydrates, lipids) to CO₂ and H₂O, thereby reducing the **oxygen demand** of the effluent.
- **Why:** Reduces oxygen sag in rivers/streams; ensures compliance with discharge norms.
- **How:** Aerobic metabolism uses dissolved O₂; anaerobic uses bound oxygen (NO₃⁻, SO₄²⁻, CO₂).

#### Objective 2 — Stabilization of Organic Matter
- **What:** Converts putrescible (rotting) organic matter into **stable, humus-like** end products that do not re-decompose.
- **Why:** Prevents foul odours, fly breeding, and secondary pollution in landfill/soil.
- **How:** Complete mineralization vs. partial humification.

#### Objective 3 — Pathogen and Vector Reduction
- **What:** High-temperature aerobic composting (>55 °C) and anaerobic digestion destroy **E. coli, Salmonella, helminth eggs**.
- **Why:** Public health protection; WHO 2006 guideline compliance.
- **How:** Thermophilic phase (55–65 °C) maintained for **>3 days** in compost.

#### Objective 4 — Energy Recovery / Biogas Generation (Anaerobic Objective)
- **What:** Anaerobic biological oxidation (digestion) yields **biogas (60–70% CH₄, 30–40% CO₂)**.
- **Why:** Renewable energy; offsets fossil fuel use; revenue stream.
- **How:** Methanogenic archaea convert acetate/H₂ to CH₄.

#### Objective 5 — Nutrient Removal (Nitrogen and Phosphorus)
- **What:** Nitrification–denitrification (N) and enhanced biological phosphorus removal (EBPR) strip nitrogen/phosphorus.
- **Why:** Prevents eutrophication of lakes and reservoirs.
- **How:** Sequential aerobic/anoxic zones with controlled sludge retention.

#### Objective 6 — Sludge Volume Reduction and Biosolids Production
- **What:** Biological oxidation converts dissolved organics into a small fraction of settleable biomass.
- **Why:** Easier dewatering, lower disposal cost, possible land application.
- **How:** Waste Activated Sludge (WAS) is thickened, digested, dewatered.

#### Objective 7 — Resource Recovery / Circular Economy
- **What:** Recovery of **single-cell protein (SCP)**, **polyhydroxyalkanoates (PHA)**, **organic acids (lactic, acetic)**, and **compost** as soil conditioner.
- **Why:** Aligns with KTU NEP 2020 sustainability goals and Atmanirbhar Bharat waste-to-wealth mission.

#### Objective 8 — Odour and Aesthetic Improvement
- **What:** Removes volatile fatty acids, sulfides, amines that cause odours.
- **Why:** Community acceptance, neighbour relations, CSR compliance.

---

### 2.2 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet (Cheat Sheet)

| # | Formula / Equation | Symbol Meaning | Typical Units | Used For |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $\dfrac{BOD_5}{BOD_L} = 1 - e^{-k \cdot t}$ | $k$ = deoxygenation constant, $t$ = time | $k$ in **day⁻¹**, $t$ in days | Relating BOD₅ to ultimate BOD ($BOD_L$) |
| 2 | $S_0 - S = a \cdot \left(\dfrac{F}{M}\right) - b$ | Substrate removal vs F/M | mg/L, day⁻¹ | Activated sludge design |
| 3 | $\dfrac{F}{M} = \dfrac{Q \cdot S_0}{V \cdot X}$ | Food-to-Microorganism ratio | day⁻¹ | Aeration tank loading |
| 4 | $\theta_c = \dfrac{V \cdot X}{Q_w \cdot X_w + Q_e \cdot X_e}$ | Mean Cell Residence Time (SRT) | days | Sludge age control |
| 5 | $Y = \dfrac{\Delta X}{\Delta S}$ | Yield coefficient | mg VSS/mg BOD | Biomass production estimate |
| 6 | $SVI = \dfrac{HSV \times 1000}{MLSS}$ | Sludge Volume Index | mL/g | Settleability check |
| 7 | $\text{Oxygen Required} = a' \cdot S_r + b' \cdot V \cdot X$ | $a'$ = 0.40–0.60, $b'$ = 0.05–0.15 | kg O₂/day | Aeration blower design |
| 8 | $\% \text{ BOD removal} = \dfrac{S_0 - S_e}{S_0} \times 100$ | Removal efficiency | % | Compliance reporting |
| 9 | $U = \dfrac{k \cdot S}{K_s + S}$ | Monod kinetics (specific uptake) | day⁻¹ | Substrate utilization rate |
| 10 | $\text{COD} = 1.5 \text{ to } 2.0 \times BOD_5$ (typical food waste) | Empirical ratio | – | Quick estimation |

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Pitfall:** Students often confuse **F/M ratio** (kg BOD/kg MLSS·day) with **Volumetric Loading** (kg BOD/m³·day). In food industry wastewater (high strength), F/M is kept **low (0.05–0.2 day⁻¹)** to avoid shock loading; in low-strength domestic sewage, F/M is **0.2–0.5 day⁻¹**.

---

### 2.3 Real-World Engineering Utility

| Application | Food Industry | Biological Oxidation Used | Key Objective Achieved |
|---|---|---|---|
| Dairy effluent | Amul, Mother Dairy | Activated Sludge + UASB | BOD > 90% removal, biogas |
| Brewery effluent | Kingfisher, UB | Anaerobic (UASB) → Aerobic | Energy recovery |
| Distillery (spent wash) | Sugar mills | Biomethanation, composting | Biogas + compost |
| Seafood processing | Kerala state plants | Sequencing Batch Reactor (SBR) | N & P removal |
| Fruit canning | Frooti, Real | Rotating Biological Contactor (RBC) | Compact treatment |
| Edible oil refinery | Various | Dissolved Air Flotation + ASP | FOG + BOD reduction |

---

## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation 1 — Relating BOD₅ to BOD_L (Ultimate BOD)

The governing differential equation for first-order deoxygenation kinetics:

$$ \dfrac{dL_t}{dt} = -k \cdot L_t $$

where $L_t$ is the remaining oxygen demand at time $t$, and $k$ is the deoxygenation rate constant.

**Step 1.** Separate variables:
$$ \dfrac{dL_t}{L_t} = -k \cdot dt $$

**Step 2.** Integrate both sides from $t = 0$ to $t = t$:
$$ \int_{L_0}^{L_t} \dfrac{dL_t}{L_t} = -k \int_{0}^{t} dt $$

**Step 3.** Evaluate the integrals:
$$ \ln(L_t) - \ln(L_0) = -k \cdot t $$

**Step 4.** Exponentiate:
$$ L_t = L_0 \cdot e^{-k t} $$

**Step 5.** Express BOD exerted at time $t$:
$$ BOD_t = L_0 - L_t = L_0 \left(1 - e^{-k t}\right) $$

**Step 6.** At $t = 5$ days:
$$ BOD_5 = L_0 \left(1 - e^{-5k}\right) $$

**Step 7.** Rearrange to obtain $L_0$:
$$ L_0 = \dfrac{BOD_5}{1 - e^{-5k}} $$

**Numerical Example (KTU-style):** A dairy wastewater shows $BOD_5 = 4500$ mg/L. Given $k = 0.23 \, \text{day}^{-1}$ at 20 °C. Find $BOD_L$:

$$ BOD_L = \dfrac{4500}{1 - e^{-5 \times 0.23}} $$

**Step 8.** Compute exponent:
$$ -5 \times 0.23 = -1.15 $$

**Step 9.** Compute $e^{-1.15} = 0.3166$

**Step 10.** Substitute:
$$ BOD_L = \dfrac{4500}{1 - 0.3166} = \dfrac{4500}{0.6834} = 6585 \text{ mg/L} $$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> This is **step-1** of the standard KTU numerical problem. Always carry 4 significant figures and state units.

### 3.2 Derivation 2 — F/M Ratio and Mean Cell Residence Time (SRT)

The **Food-to-Microorganism ratio** defines how much "food" (substrate) is fed per unit mass of "workers" (biomass) per day:

$$ \dfrac{F}{M} = \dfrac{Q \cdot S_0}{V \cdot X} $$

**Step 1.** Recognize $Q \cdot S_0$ = mass loading of substrate (kg BOD/day).
**Step 2.** Recognize $V \cdot X$ = mass of biomass in aeration tank (kg MLSS).
**Step 3.** Dividing gives kg BOD fed per kg biomass per day (units: day⁻¹).

The **Solids Retention Time (SRT)** or **Mean Cell Residence Time (θ_c)**:

$$ \theta_c = \dfrac{V \cdot X}{Q_w \cdot X_w + Q_e \cdot X_e} $$

**Step 1.** Numerator = total biomass in reactor.
**Step 2.** Denominator = biomass leaving via waste sludge ($Q_w$) and effluent ($Q_e$).
**Step 3.** $\theta_c$ in days.

**Relation to F/M:**
$$ \dfrac{F}{M} = \dfrac{1}{\theta_c \cdot Y} \quad \text{(at steady state, simplified)} $$

### 3.3 Analytical Decision Matrix — Choosing the Biological Oxidation Route

| Objective / Constraint | Aerobic (ASP) | Anaerobic (UASB/AD) | Composting | Constructed Wetland |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| **High BOD removal (> 90%)** | ★★★★★ | ★★★ (followed by aerobic polishing) | ★★★ | ★★ |
| **Energy recovery (biogas)** | ✗ | ★★★★★ | ✗ | ✗ |
| **Low energy input** | ★★ | ★★★★★ | ★★★ | ★★★★★ |
| **Footprint (small)** | ★★★ | ★★★★★ | ★★ | ✗ (large area) |
| **Pathogen kill** | ★★ | ★★★★ (thermophilic) | ★★★★★ | ★★ |
| **Nutrient (N, P) recovery** | ★★★ (via ND/EBPR) | ★★ (struvite) | ★★★★ (compost) | ★★★★ |
| **Sludge production** | ★ (high) | ★★★★★ (low) | ★★★ | ★★★★★ |
| **Capital cost** | High | Moderate | Low | Low |
| **O & M skill needed** | High | High | Moderate | Low |

> [!NOTE]
> For a typical food industry ETP in Kerala (high-strength warm effluent), the recommended train is: **Equalization → UASB (anaerobic) → Aerobic Polishing (ASP/SBR) → Chlorination → Discharge/Recycle**.

### 3.4 Stoichiometric Energy Balance (Methane Yield)

For anaerobic biological oxidation, Buswell's equation gives theoretical methane yield:

$$ C_n H_a O_b N_c + \left(n - \dfrac{a}{4} - \dfrac{b}{2} + \dfrac{3c}{4}\right) H_2O \longrightarrow \left(\dfrac{n}{2} - \dfrac{a}{8} + \dfrac{b}{4} + \dfrac{3c}{8}\right) CO_2 + \left(\dfrac{n}{2} + \dfrac{a}{8} - \dfrac{b}{4} - \dfrac{3c}{8}\right) CH_4 + c \, NH_3 $$

For glucose ($C_6H_{12}O_6$):

$$ C_6 H_{12} O_6 \longrightarrow 3 \, CH_4 + 3 \, CO_2 $$

**Methane yield per kg COD removed:** $0.35 \, m^3 \, CH_4$ at STP (theoretical).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> KTU often asks: *"Calculate methane volume produced from 1000 kg COD destroyed."* Answer: $1000 \times 0.35 = 350 \, m^3 \, CH_4$ (theoretical), with practical yield ~70–80% of this.

---
