## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->

### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

#### Q1. [KTU University Exam — May 2024, Model]
**"List any three objectives of biological oxidation of food industry waste."**

**Model Answer (3 marks — break-up shown):**
1. **Reduction of BOD/COD** to comply with discharge norms (1 mark).
2. **Stabilization of putrescible organic matter** into stable humic substances (1 mark).
3. **Recovery of energy in the form of biogas** through anaerobic digestion (1 mark).

#### Q2. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023, Model]
**"State the role of biological oxidation in nutrient removal from food industry effluent."**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
Biological oxidation facilitates **nitrification** (conversion of NH₄⁺ → NO₂⁻ → NO₃⁻ by *Nitrosomonas* and *Nitrobacter*) under aerobic conditions, followed by **denitrification** (NO₃⁻ → N₂ gas) under anoxic conditions using heterotrophic bacteria. Phosphorus is removed via **Enhanced Biological Phosphorus Removal (EBPR)** employing Polyphosphate Accumulating Organisms (PAOs) under alternating anaerobic–aerobic conditions. This addresses **eutrophication** control.

---

### 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Internal Choice)

---

#### **Question A — 14 Marks** (Internal Choice Option)

**[KTU University Exam — June 2024, Adapted]**

**(a) Discuss in detail the **primary, secondary, and tertiary objectives** of biological oxidation of food industry wastes, with specific reference to BOD/COD reduction, stabilization, and biogas recovery. (7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Introduction (1 mark):**
Biological oxidation is the cornerstone of secondary treatment for food industry wastewaters, leveraging microbial metabolism to transform organic pollutants into stable end-products. The objectives span environmental protection, public health, energy recovery, and resource circularity.

**Primary Objective — BOD/COD Reduction (2 marks):**
Microorganisms (heterotrophic bacteria) oxidize soluble organic compounds — carbohydrates ($C_6H_{12}O_6$), proteins (amino acids), and lipids (fatty acids) — to CO₂ and H₂O. Typical food-industry ASP achieves **90–95% BOD₅ removal**, bringing effluent from 5,000 mg/L down to 50–250 mg/L, meeting CPCB norms.

**Secondary Objective — Stabilization (2 marks):**
The process converts putrescible organics into **stable humified solids** (in composting) or **mineralized end-products** (in aerobic/anaerobic digestion). This eliminates foul odours, vector attraction, and long-term secondary pollution. Stabilization is measured by:
$$ \text{Oxygen Uptake Rate (OUR)} < 1.5 \, mg \, O_2/g \, VS \cdot h $$

**Tertiary Objective — Resource Recovery (2 marks):**
Anaerobic digestion recovers **0.35 m³ CH₄/kg COD destroyed (theoretical)**, equivalent to ~10 kWh of thermal energy. Aerobic biomass can be valorized as **compost** (N–P–K fertilizer), **single-cell protein (SCP)**, or **polyhydroxyalkanoate (PHA) bioplastic** precursors.

**(b) With the help of a flow diagram, describe the working of an Upflow Anaerobic Sludge Blanket (UASB) reactor for treating food industry wastewater. (7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Principle (1 mark):**
UASB relies on the formation of a **granular sludge blanket** at the bottom of the reactor. Wastewater flows upward through this blanket; anaerobic bacteria convert organics to biogas (CH₄ + CO₂).

**Construction (2 marks):**
1. **Inlet distribution system** at the base for uniform upflow.
2. **Sludge blanket zone** where granules (~1–3 mm diameter) settle.
3. **Sludge bed** with high biomass concentration (**30,000–80,000 mg VSS/L**).
4. **Three-phase separator (GSS)** at the top separating biogas, effluent, and sludge.
5. **Effluent launder** and **biogas collection hood**.

**Working (2 marks):**
- Wastewater enters at **0.5–1.0 m/h upflow velocity**.
- **Hydraulic Retention Time (HRT):** 4–24 hours depending on strength.
- **Organic Loading Rate (OLR):** 5–15 kg COD/m³·day for food waste.
- Biogas bubbles rise, providing mixing; granules retain biomass despite washout.

**Performance (1 mark):**
- **COD removal: 70–85%** for dairy and brewery.
- **Methane yield: 0.30–0.35 m³ CH₄/kg COD removed**.
- Effluent polishing needed downstream (aerobic step).

**Advantages (1 mark):**
- No mechanical mixing; low energy (~0.1 kWh/m³); high biomass retention; biogas generation.

---

#### **Question B — 14 Marks** (Internal Choice Alternative)

**[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023, Adapted]**

**(a) Explain the **objectives and significance** of biological oxidation in the context of food industry waste management. Highlight at least **six objectives** with engineering justification. (7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Definition (1 mark):** Biological oxidation is the microbial degradation of organic waste under controlled conditions (aerobic, anaerobic, or anoxic) for pollution mitigation and resource recovery.

**Six Key Objectives (each ~1 mark):**

1. **BOD/COD Reduction:** Reduces oxygen-depleting loadings; ensures compliance with CPCB/SPCB Kerala discharge norms.
2. **Stabilization:** Converts putrescible organics to stable humus or mineralized products, eliminating secondary pollution.
3. **Pathogen Reduction:** Thermophilic composting (55–65 °C) achieves 99.9% reduction of *E. coli* and helminth ova.
4. **Energy Recovery:** Anaerobic digestion yields ~3,500 kcal/m³ of methane — usable in boilers, CHP, or as CNG.
5. **Nutrient Management:** Sequential nitrification–denitrification removes nitrogen (80–95%); EBPR removes phosphorus (70–90%).
6. **Resource Circularity:** Recovery of compost, PHA, organic acids supports circular economy, aligned with Swachh Bharat and Atmanirbhar Bharat missions.

**(b) A dairy industry generates 500 m³/day of wastewater with BOD₅ = 4,000 mg/L. The activated sludge plant operates at F/M = 0.20 day⁻¹, MLSS = 3,000 mg/L, and yield coefficient Y = 0.5. Calculate (i) aeration tank volume, (ii) excess sludge production per day, (iii) oxygen requirement assuming a′ = 0.5 and b′ = 0.08. (7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Given:**
- $Q = 500 \, m^3/\text{day}$
- $S_0 = 4{,}000 \, mg/L = 4 \, kg/m^3$
- $F/M = 0.20 \, \text{day}^{-1}$
- $X = 3{,}000 \, mg/L = 3 \, kg/m^3$
- $Y = 0.5$
- $a' = 0.5$, $b' = 0.08$

**(i) Aeration Tank Volume (2 marks):**

Using:
$$ \dfrac{F}{M} = \dfrac{Q \cdot S_0}{V \cdot X} $$

Rearrange:
$$ V = \dfrac{Q \cdot S_0}{(F/M) \cdot X} $$

$$ V = \dfrac{500 \times 4}{0.20 \times 3} = \dfrac{2000}{0.6} = 3333.33 \, m^3 $$

**Answer:** $V \approx 3333 \, m^3$.

**(ii) Excess Sludge Production (2 marks):**

Substrate removed per day:
$$ S_r \approx S_0 - S_e \approx 4{,}000 \, mg/L \times 80\% = 3{,}200 \, mg/L = 3.2 \, kg/m^3 $$

Total substrate removed:
$$ \Delta S = Q \cdot S_r = 500 \times 3.2 = 1600 \, kg/\text{day} $$

Excess sludge:
$$ \Delta X = Y \cdot \Delta S = 0.5 \times 1600 = 800 \, kg \, VSS/\text{day} $$

**Answer:** $800 \, kg \, VSS/\text{day}$.

**(iii) Oxygen Requirement (3 marks):**

Formula:
$$ O_2 = a' \cdot \Delta S + b' \cdot V \cdot X $$

$$ O_2 = (0.5 \times 1600) + (0.08 \times 3333.33 \times 3) $$

$$ O_2 = 800 + 800 = 1600 \, kg \, O_2/\text{day} $$

**Answer:** $1{,}600 \, kg \, O_2/\text{day}$ (i.e., 1.6 tonnes of oxygen demand daily — significant; requires ~6,400 m³ of air at 21% O₂ and 10% transfer efficiency).

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls:**
> 1. **Unit mismatch:** Students frequently forget to convert mg/L to kg/m³ (divide by 1000). Always: $1 \, mg/L = 1 \, g/m^3 = 10^{-3} \, kg/m^3$.
> 2. **Wrong F/M units:** $F/M$ is dimensionless but represents day⁻¹ (i.e., $\text{kg BOD}/\text{kg MLSS} \cdot \text{day}$).
> 3. **Ignoring effluent BOD:** If $S_e \neq 0$, use $S_r = S_0 - S_e$ explicitly.
> 4. **Forgetting to state assumptions:** Always mention "Assuming $S_e \approx 0$ for high-strength food wastewater" or "Assuming 80% BOD removal."
> 5. **Confusing VSS with TSS:** VSS ≈ 0.7 × TSS for activated sludge. Examiners deduct 1 mark if not clarified.

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — KTU Module 3, Biological Oxidations: Objectives**

- ✅ **Biological oxidation = microbial conversion of organics** to CO₂, H₂O (aerobic) or CH₄ + CO₂ (anaerobic).
- ✅ **Primary objective: BOD/COD reduction** (>90% in well-designed ASP).
- ✅ **Secondary objective: stabilization** of putrescible organics (OUR < 1.5 mg O₂/g VS·h).
- ✅ **Tertiary objective: biogas/energy recovery** (0.35 m³ CH₄/kg COD theoretical).
- ✅ **Pathogen destruction** achieved in thermophilic composting (>55 °C, >3 days).
- ✅ **Nutrient removal** via nitrification–denitrification and EBPR.
- ✅ **F/M ratio:** Low (0.05–0.2) for food industry; typical $\theta_c = 5\text{–}20$ days.
- ✅ **MLSS:** 2,000–4,000 mg/L for conventional ASP.
- ✅ **Buswell equation:** $C_nH_aO_bN_c \to CO_2 + CH_4 + NH_3$ — used in theoretical methane yield.
- ✅ **$SVI$** should be **50–150 mL/g** for good settleability.
- ✅ **CPCB/SPCB Kerala discharge norm:** BOD < 30 mg/L (inland surface water); COD < 250 mg/L.
- ✅ **High-strength food wastewater** → use **UASB + ASP** train (most energy efficient).
- ✅ **Energy in biogas:** 1 m³ CH₄ ≈ 35.8 MJ ≈ 9.9 kWh thermal.
- ✅ **Compost value:** N–P–K ≈ 1–1–1.5%; water holding capacity improved.
- ✅ **Monod kinetics:** $U = U_{max} \cdot \dfrac{S}{K_s + S}$ — substrate utilization rate.
- ✅ **KTU favourite numerical:** Calculate $V$, $\Delta X$, $O_2$ from given $Q$, $S_0$, $F/M$, $X$, $Y$, $a'$, $b'$.

> **End of Module 3 Notes — Biological Oxidations: Objectives**

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEFTT744 - FOOD INDUSTRY WASTE MANAGEMENT
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Organisms

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

In the context of **Food Industry Waste Management**, the term **"Organisms"** refers to the diverse group of **biological agents (microorganisms, macroorganisms, and higher life forms)** that actively participate in the **bioconversion, degradation, stabilization, and valorization** of food processing wastes. These organisms drive key biochemical unit operations such as **composting, anaerobic digestion (biogas production), vermicomposting, bioaugmentation, enzymatic hydrolysis, fermentation, and wastewater treatment**.

Under the KTU 2024 Scheme, "Organisms" is studied under the broader umbrella of **Biological Treatment Systems for Food Waste** and is mapped to the following Course Outcomes (COs):

- **CO3:** Apply the principles of biological treatment for the management of food industry wastes.
- **CO4:** Identify suitable organisms and biochemical pathways for value-added product recovery from food waste.

### Formal Classification of Organisms Used in Food Waste Management

| **Class** | **Examples** | **Primary Role in Waste Management** |
|-----------|--------------|-------------------------------------|
| **Bacteria** | *Bacillus subtilis*, *Pseudomonas aeruginosa*, *Lactobacillus acidophilus*, *Clostridium butyricum*, *Acetobacter aceti*, *Streptomyces* spp. | Aerobic/anaerobic digestion, lactic acid fermentation, protein recovery, enzyme production |
| **Fungi** | *Aspergillus niger*, *Penicillium notatum*, *Trichoderma viride*, *Rhizopus oligosporus*, *Phanerochaete chrysosporium* | Solid-state fermentation (SSF), cellulase/lipase production, lignin degradation, bioethanol |
| **Yeasts** | *Saccharomyces cerevisiae*, *Candida utilis*, *Pichia stipitis* | Ethanol fermentation, SCP (Single Cell Protein) production |
| **Algae** | *Chlorella vulgaris*, *Spirulina platensis*, *Scenedesmus* | Phycoremediation of nutrient-rich food wastewater, CO₂ sequestration |
| **Actinomycetes** | *Streptomyces griseus*, *Nocardia* | Degradation of complex carbohydrates, biopolymer breakdown |
| **Earthworms** | *Eisenia fetida*, *Lumbricus rubellus* | Vermicomposting of organic kitchen/food waste |
| **Black Soldier Fly Larvae (BSFL)** | *Hermetia illucens* | Bioconversion of food waste into high-protein larval biomass |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight:** The 2024 Scheme emphasizes not just the *identification* of organisms but also their **metabolic pathways, growth kinetics, oxygen requirements, substrate specificity, and the engineering parameters** that govern their activity in real food-waste bioreactors.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuitive Overview

Imagine a city garbage dump. If you just leave the waste there, it will rot slowly, smell terribly, and attract pests. But what if you hire a team of **specialized "natural cleanup workers"** who:
1. **Eat the waste as food**,
2. **Convert it into useful products** (compost, biogas, protein, enzymes), and
3. **Reproduce themselves** so the workforce grows as long as waste is available?

That is exactly what **microorganisms and certain macroorganisms do in food waste management**.

**Real-world analogy — The Restaurant Kitchen Crew:**
- **Bacteria** are like the *prep cooks* — they chop, soften, and pre-digest complex waste material very fast.
- **Fungi** are the *slow-cook chefs* — they take their time, prefer drier, solid food waste, and produce powerful enzymes.
- **Yeasts** are the *specialty bakers* — they ferment sugars in liquid waste into alcohol and CO₂.
- **Algae** are the *gardeners* — they soak up nutrients (N, P) from food industry wastewater and grow into useful biomass.
- **Earthworms** are the *cleaning staff* — they ingest solid organic waste and excrete nutrient-rich vermicompost.
- **BSFL larvae** are the *protein miners* — they convert tonnes of food waste into animal feed protein in just days.

> [!NOTE]
> **Plain-English Summary for First-Time Learners:**
> Organisms in food waste management are living "factories" that treat waste biologically. They are classified by what they need (oxygen vs. no oxygen), what they eat (sugars, proteins, fats, cellulose), and what useful product they generate (compost, methane, ethanol, enzymes, or biomass itself). The food industry engineer must select the **right organism for the right waste stream** to achieve efficient treatment and resource recovery.

## 1.3 Key Quantitative Anchors (KTU 2024 Standard Metrics)

> [!NOTE]
> The following constants and metrics are **must-know** for KTU board examinations:
> - **Doubling time of *E. coli* under optimal conditions:** ≈ **20 minutes**
> - **Optimal pH for most waste-degrading bacteria:** **6.5 – 7.5**
> - **Optimal pH for fungi (e.g., *Aspergillus*):** **3.5 – 5.5**
> - **Thermophilic composting temperature range:** **45 °C – 65 °C**
> - **Mesophilic anaerobic digestion range:** **30 °C – 40 °C**
> - **Standard yield coefficient (Y) for aerobic heterotrophs:** **0.4 – 0.6 g VSS/g COD**
> - **C:N ratio ideal for composting:** **25:1 – 30:1** (by mass)
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Reference Sheet

## 2.1 Structured Theoretical Framework

The organisms involved in food waste management can be systematically understood along the following **six analytical axes**:

### Axis 1: Oxygen Requirement (Redox Classification)

- **Obligate Aerobes** — Require molecular O₂ for respiration (e.g., *Bacillus subtilis*, *Pseudomonas*).
  - *Use case:* Aerobic composting, activated sludge for food processing effluents.
- **Obligate Anaerobes** — Oxygen is toxic; use alternative electron acceptors (e.g., *Clostridium* spp., methanogens like *Methanobacterium*).
  - *Use case:* Biogas digesters treating dairy, distillery, or brewery waste.
- **Facultative Anaerobes** — Can switch between aerobic and anaerobic metabolism (e.g., *Escherichia coli*, *Saccharomyces cerevisiae*).
  - *Use case:* Versatile in fluctuating dissolved oxygen conditions of food wastewater.
- **Microaerophiles** — Require low O₂ (2–10%) (e.g., *Lactobacillus*).
  - *Use case:* Lactic acid fermentation of vegetable and fruit waste.

### Axis 2: Temperature Preference

| **Class** | **Optimum Range** | **Example Organisms** | **Engineering Application** |
|-----------|------------------|----------------------|------------------------------|
| **Psychrophiles** | < 15 °C | *Psychrobacter* | Cold-storage waste streams |
| **Mesophiles** | 20 °C – 45 °C | Most bacteria, yeasts | Mesophilic AD, ambient composting |
| **Thermophiles** | 45 °C – 80 °C | *Thermus*, *Geobacillus*, *Methanococcus jannaschii* | Thermophilic composting, pathogen destruction |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Engineering Insight:** Thermophilic processes (55–65 °C) are 5–10× faster than mesophilic but require more energy input. KTU questions often ask students to **justify the choice** of temperature regime for a given waste.

### Axis 3: Substrate (Carbon Source) Specificity

- **Cellulolytic organisms** — *Trichoderma reesei*, *Aspergillus niger*, *Cellulomonas* — degrade cellulose/hemicellulose abundant in fruit/vegetable peels.
- **Lipolytic organisms** — *Candida rugosa*, *Rhizopus oryzae*, *Pseudomonas* spp. — degrade fats/oils in dairy/meat waste.
- **Proteolytic organisms** — *Bacillus licheniformis*, *Streptomyces griseus* — break down proteins in fish/meat waste.
- **Amylolytic organisms** — *Aspergillus oryzae*, *Bacillus amyloliquefaciens* — hydrolyze starch in bakery/cereal waste.
- **Ligninolytic organisms** — *Phanerochaete chrysosporium*, *Trametes versicolor* — degrade lignocellulosic complexes in spent grain/husk waste.

### Axis 4: Metabolic End-Product (What They Make)

- **Methanogens** → CH₄ + CO₂
- **Lactic acid bacteria** → Lactic acid
- **Acetic acid bacteria** → Acetic acid
- **Ethanol-fermenting yeasts** → Ethanol + CO₂
- **SCO-producing microbes** → Single-cell protein (SCP)
- **Enzyme-producing fungi** → Cellulase, amylase, protease, lipase

### Axis 5: Ecological Role in Natural Cycles

| **Biogeochemical Cycle** | **Key Organism Group** | **Role in Food Waste Context** |
|--------------------------|----------------------|-------------------------------|
| **Carbon Cycle** | Decomposers (bacteria + fungi) | Mineralize organic C → CO₂ / CH₄ |
| **Nitrogen Cycle** | Nitrifiers (*Nitrosomonas*), Denitrifiers (*Pseudomonas*), N-fixers (*Azotobacter*) | Convert proteins/urea → NH₄⁺ → NO₃⁻ / N₂ |
| **Phosphorus Cycle** | Phosphate-solubilizing bacteria (*Bacillus megaterium*) | Release PO₄³⁻ from organic complexes |
| **Sulfur Cycle** | *Desulfovibrio*, *Thiobacillus* | Reduce sulfate from dairy waste → H₂S (in AD) |

### Axis 6: Engineering Form (Pure Culture vs. Consortia)

- **Pure culture** — Single species (used in industrial fermentation, e.g., *Saccharomyces* for ethanol).
- **Defined co-culture** — Two or more known species (e.g., co-culture of *Aspergillus* + yeast for simultaneous saccharification and fermentation).
- **Mixed microbial consortium (MMC)** — Undefined natural community from soil, compost, rumen, or anaerobic digester sludge — **most commonly used in real-world food waste treatment** for robustness and resilience.

## 2.2 The 'Why' and 'How' Behind Each Step

> **Why classify organisms?** — Because the food industry generates **highly heterogeneous wastes** (dairy ≠ bakery ≠ brewery ≠ fruit-canning). No single organism can handle all. Classification helps the engineer **match organism to waste stream**.

> **How are these organisms deployed in practice?**
> 1. **Inoculation** of the waste stream with a pre-grown seed culture (or natural consortium from compost/AD sludge).
> 2. **Process control** of pH, temperature, moisture, C:N, and mixing to maintain the **optimum growth window** of the selected organism.
> 3. **Monitoring** of growth via OD (Optical Density), CFU/mL, MLVSS, or molecular methods (16S rRNA / ITS sequencing for community profiling).
> 4. **Harvesting** of the desired end-product (biogas, compost, ethanol, SCP, enzyme).

## 2.3 KTU High-Yield Reference Sheet (Formula & Quick-Recall Table)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Master these equations for any numerical question on microbial growth in food waste systems.**

| **Parameter** | **Formula / Definition** | **Typical Unit** | **Use in Food Waste Engineering** |
|---------------|--------------------------|------------------|-----------------------------------|
| **Specific Growth Rate (μ)** | $\mu = \dfrac{1}{X}\dfrac{dX}{dt}$ | h⁻¹ | Measures how fast the organism is growing on the waste |
| **Doubling Time (t_d)** | $t_d = \dfrac{\ln 2}{\mu}$ | h | Time for biomass to double |
| **Monod Kinetic Equation** | $\mu = \mu_{max}\dfrac{S}{K_s + S}$ | h⁻¹ | Relates growth rate to limiting substrate (e.g., COD) concentration |
| **Half-Saturation Constant (K_s)** | Substrate concentration at μ = μ_max/2 | mg/L | Affinity indicator — lower K_s = higher affinity |
| **Biomass Yield Coefficient (Y)** | $Y = \dfrac{\Delta X}{\Delta S}$ | g VSS / g COD | Biomass produced per gram of substrate consumed |
| **Endogenous Decay Coefficient (k_d)** | $k_d = -\dfrac{1}{X}\dfrac{dX_{decay}}{dt}$ | day⁻¹ | Models cell lysis/maintenance energy loss |
| **Substrate Removal Rate** | $q = \dfrac{\mu}{Y}$ | g substrate / g biomass · h | Engineering design of bioreactor size |
| **Observed Yield (Y_obs)** | $Y_{obs} = \dfrac{Y}{1 + k_d \cdot SRT}$ | g VSS / g COD | True net yield accounting for endogenous decay (SRT = Solids Retention Time) |
| **C:N Ratio** | $\dfrac{\text{Carbon mass}}{\text{Nitrogen mass}}$ | dimensionless | Must be 25–30 for optimal composting |
| **Oxygen Uptake Rate (OUR)** | $\dfrac{dO_2}{dt}$ per unit volume | mg O₂ / L · h | Indicator of aerobic microbial activity in food wastewater |

> [!NOTE]
> **Kerala Board Tip:** A common 7-mark question is to *"Apply the Monod equation to a food waste biotreatment system"* — make sure you can derive, plot (μ vs S), and explain the two asymptotes: μ → 0 when S → 0, and μ → μ_max when S >> K_s.

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Dairy industry** (cheese whey, milk processing): Anaerobic digesters with **thermophilic methanogens** convert lactose-rich effluent to biogas (≈ 0.35 m³ CH₄ / kg COD removed).
- **Brewery industry** (spent grain, yeast slurry): Solid-state fermentation with *Aspergillus oryzae* + *Saccharomyces cerevisiae* → bioethanol.
- **Fruit & vegetable processing** (peels, pulp waste): Vermicomposting with *Eisenia fetida* OR fungal composting with *Trichoderma* → high-value organic compost.
- **Meat & fish processing** (blood, bone, offal): Anaerobic digestion + proteolytic bacterial cultures → biogas + liquid biofertilizer.
- **Edible oil industry** (spent oil, oil cake): Lipase-producing fungi (*Rhizopus*) + biodiesel transesterification of recovered oil.
- **Bakery waste** (stale bread, dough): Amylolytic fungi (*A. oryzae*) → fermentable sugar syrup → ethanol via yeast.

# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1 — Monod Growth Kinetics for a Food Waste Bioreactor

The Monod equation is the **cornerstone equation** for modeling microbial growth on a limiting substrate. In a food waste context, the substrate is typically **Chemical Oxygen Demand (COD), Biological Oxygen Demand (BOD), Total Organic Carbon (TOC), or a specific sugar/protein concentration**.

### 3.1.1 Setting Up the Mass Balance

Consider a **continuously stirred tank reactor (CSTR)** of volume V receiving food industry wastewater with:
- Influent substrate concentration = $S_0$ (mg/L)
- Effluent substrate concentration = $S$ (mg/L)
- Influent biomass concentration = $X_0$ (mg VSS/L)
- Effluent biomass concentration = $X$ (mg VSS/L)
- Volumetric flow rate = Q (L/h)
- Hydraulic residence time $\theta = V/Q$ (h)

### 3.1.2 Step-by-Step Substrate Mass Balance

The substrate is **consumed** by microbial growth and is **lost** in the effluent.

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Accumulation} &= \text{Input} - \text{Output} - \text{Consumption by growth} \\
V \dfrac{dS}{dt} &= Q S_0 - Q S - V \left(\dfrac{\mu X}{Y}\right) \\
\dfrac{dS}{dt} &= \dfrac{S_0 - S}{\theta} - \dfrac{\mu X}{Y}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Explanation of each term:**
- $\dfrac{S_0 - S}{\theta}$ → net change due to flow (input – output) divided by residence time.
- $\dfrac{\mu X}{Y}$ → rate of substrate removal by biomass (μ is specific growth rate, X is biomass, Y is yield coefficient).

### 3.1.3 Step-by-Step Biomass Mass Balance

$$
\begin{aligned}
V \dfrac{dX}{dt} &= Q X_0 - Q X + V (\mu X - k_d X) \\
\dfrac{dX}{dt} &= \dfrac{X_0 - X}{\theta} + (\mu - k_d) X
\end{aligned}
$$

**Explanation of each term:**
- $\mu X$ → biomass generation by growth.
- $k_d X$ → biomass loss by endogenous decay.
- $\dfrac{X_0 - X}{\theta}$ → biomass change due to flow (assuming sterile feed, $X_0 = 0$, this simplifies).

### 3.1.4 Steady-State Assumption

At steady state, all derivatives = 0. With $X_0 = 0$ (sterile influent, common assumption in food waste biotreatment design):

$$
\begin{aligned}
0 &= -X/\theta + (\mu - k_d)X \\
\mu &= \dfrac{1}{\theta} + k_d \\
\boxed{\mu &= \dfrac{1}{\theta} + k_d}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Interpretation:** At steady state, the specific growth rate exactly balances the washout rate (1/θ) plus the decay rate (k_d). This sets the **critical residence time** for the reactor.

### 3.1.5 Substituting the Monod Equation

Recall: $\mu = \mu_{max}\dfrac{S}{K_s + S}$

$$
\begin{aligned}
\dfrac{1}{\theta} + k_d &= \mu_{max}\dfrac{S}{K_s + S} \\
\dfrac{S}{K_s + S} &= \dfrac{1/\theta + k_d}{\mu_{max}} \\
K_s + S &= S \cdot \dfrac{\mu_{max}}{1/\theta + k_d} \\
S \left( 1 - \dfrac{\mu_{max}}{1/\theta + k_d} \right) &= -K_s \\
S &= \dfrac{K_s \cdot (1/\theta + k_d)}{\mu_{max} - (1/\theta + k_d)} \\
\boxed{S &= \dfrac{K_s \, (1 + k_d \theta)}{\mu_{max} \theta - (1 + k_d \theta)}}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Engineering meaning:** This is the **design equation** — given a food waste with known K_s and a chosen residence time θ, we can predict the effluent substrate concentration S (i.e., how clean the water will be).

### 3.1.6 Critical Washout Residence Time

If we set S → S₀ (meaning no treatment occurred), we get the washout condition:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\dfrac{1}{\theta_c} + k_d &= \mu_{max}\dfrac{S_0}{K_s + S_0} \\
\theta_c &= \dfrac{1}{\mu_{max}\dfrac{S_0}{K_s + S_0} - k_d}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Physical meaning:** If θ < θ_c, the organisms wash out of the bioreactor faster than they can grow — the system fails. This is a **classic KTU design question** — given μ_max, K_s, S₀, and k_d, find the safe minimum HRT.

## 3.2 Derivation 2 — Observed Yield Coefficient (Y_obs) for a Food Waste Aerobic Digester

The **observed yield** accounts for biomass loss by endogenous decay over the Solids Retention Time (SRT = θ_c, designer's choice).

### Step-by-Step

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Net biomass production rate} &= \text{Growth rate} - \text{Decay rate} \\
\dfrac{dX}{dt}\bigg|_{net} &= Y \dfrac{dS}{dt} - k_d X
\end{aligned}
$$

At steady state, the **observed yield** (Y_obs) is defined as the net biomass produced per unit substrate consumed:

$$
\begin{aligned}
Y_{obs} &= \dfrac{\text{Net biomass produced}}{\text{Substrate consumed}} \\
Y_{obs} &= \dfrac{Y \cdot \Delta S - k_d X \cdot t}{\Delta S} \\
\text{(For a CSTR at steady state with SRT} = \theta_c) & \\
\boxed{Y_{obs} &= \dfrac{Y}{1 + k_d \cdot \theta_c}}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Engineering interpretation:**
- For a **low SRT** (e.g., θ_c = 2 days, like in a high-rate food wastewater plant), Y_obs ≈ Y (decay negligible).
- For a **high SRT** (e.g., θ_c = 20 days, like in a fully aerobic sludge digester), Y_obs is much smaller — most growth is offset by decay → less sludge to dispose of.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Numerical Tip:** If the question gives you Y = 0.5, k_d = 0.05 d⁻¹, and SRT = 10 d, then $Y_{obs} = 0.5 / (1 + 0.05 \times 10) = 0.5 / 1.5 = 0.333$ g VSS / g COD.

## 3.3 Worked Numerical Example (KTU Board Style)

### Problem

A food industry biotreatment CSTR is treating dairy effluent with the following parameters:
- Influent COD, $S_0 = 1000$ mg/L
- Maximum specific growth rate, $\mu_{max} = 0.30$ h⁻¹
- Half-saturation constant, $K_s = 200$ mg/L
- Yield coefficient, $Y = 0.45$ mg VSS / mg COD
- Endogenous decay, $k_d = 0.02$ h⁻¹
- Reactor volume, $V = 500$ L
- Flow rate, $Q = 50$ L/h

**Find:**
(a) The hydraulic residence time θ.
(b) The critical washout residence time θ_c.
(c) The effluent substrate concentration S.
(d) The biomass concentration X in the reactor.

### Solution

**(a) Hydraulic Residence Time**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\theta = \dfrac{V}{Q} = \dfrac{500 \text{ L}}{50 \text{ L/h}} = 10 \text{ h}
\end{aligned}
$$

**(b) Critical Washout Residence Time**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\theta_c &= \dfrac{1}{\mu_{max}\dfrac{S_0}{K_s + S_0} - k_d} \\
\dfrac{S_0}{K_s + S_0} &= \dfrac{1000}{200 + 1000} = \dfrac{1000}{1200} = 0.833 \\
\mu_{max} \cdot 0.833 &= 0.30 \times 0.833 = 0.250 \text{ h}^{-1} \\
\mu_{max} \cdot 0.833 - k_d &= 0.250 - 0.02 = 0.230 \text{ h}^{-1} \\
\theta_c &= \dfrac{1}{0.230} = 4.348 \text{ h}
\end{aligned}
$$

Since θ = 10 h > θ_c = 4.348 h, the reactor is **operationally safe** (no washout).

**(c) Effluent Substrate Concentration**

$$
\begin{aligned}
S &= \dfrac{K_s (1 + k_d \theta)}{\mu_{max} \theta - (1 + k_d \theta)} \\
k_d \theta &= 0.02 \times 10 = 0.20 \\
\mu_{max} \theta &= 0.30 \times 10 = 3.0 \\
\text{Numerator} &= 200 \times (1 + 0.20) = 200 \times 1.20 = 240 \\
\text{Denominator} &= 3.0 - 1.20 = 1.80 \\
S &= \dfrac{240}{1.80} = 133.33 \text{ mg/L}
\end{aligned}
$$

**(d) Biomass Concentration**

Using the steady-state substrate mass balance:

$$
\begin{aligned}
X &= \dfrac{Y (S_0 - S)}{1 + k_d \theta} \\
X &= \dfrac{0.45 \times (1000 - 133.33)}{1 + 0.20} \\
X &= \dfrac{0.45 \times 866.67}{1.20} \\
X &= \dfrac{390.0}{1.20} = 325.0 \text{ mg VSS/L}
\end{aligned}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Check:** Substrate removal efficiency = $\dfrac{S_0 - S}{S_0} \times 100\% = \dfrac{866.67}{1000} \times 100\% = 86.67\%$ — this is realistic for a well-designed food wastewater CSTR.

## 3.4 Analytical Comparison Matrix — Organisms vs. Food Waste Streams

| **Food Waste Stream** | **Key Organism (Generic Group)** | **Process** | **Key End-Product** | **Critical Operating Parameter** |
|----------------------|----------------------------------|-------------|---------------------|----------------------------------|
| **Dairy whey** | *Lactobacillus helveticus* + methanogens | Lactic acid fermentation → AD | Lactic acid + CH₄ | pH 6.0, 37 °C |
| **Bakery waste** | *Saccharomyces cerevisiae* + *Aspergillus oryzae* | SSF → SSF | Bioethanol | Moisture 70%, 30 °C |
| **Fruit peels** | *Trichoderma viride* + *Eisenia fetida* | Fungal pre-treatment → Vermicomposting | Vermicompost + cellulase | C:N 30, 25 °C |
| **Brewery spent grain** | *Phanerochaete chrysosporium* + yeast | Lignin degradation → fermentation | Bioethanol | 28 °C, pH 5.0 |
| **Meat/blood waste** | *Bacillus licheniformis* + methanogens | Hydrolysis → AD | Biogas + blood-meal | 55 °C, HRT 20 d |
| **Vegetable oil waste** | *Rhizopus oryzae* | SSF for lipase → biodiesel | Biodiesel | 30 °C, moisture 60% |
| **Cassava peels** | *Aspergillus niger* + *Saccharomyces* | SSF → ethanol fermentation | Ethanol | pH 4.5, 30 °C |
| **Fish waste** | *Streptomyces griseus* + proteolytic bacilli | Ensiling → AD | Fish silage + biogas | pH 4.0, 25 °C |

> [!TIP]
> **Exam Shortcut:** When asked to "**justify the choice of organism**," always answer using the four-criterion template: **(i) Substrate affinity → matches waste composition, (ii) Oxygen requirement → matches reactor design, (iii) Temperature optimum → matches local climate/energy budget, (iv) End-product profile → matches recovery objective.**
