# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Q1. [KTU University Exam — May 2024 Style]
**"List and briefly explain the three main classes of microorganisms used in food industry waste management, giving one industrial example for each."**

**Model Answer:**

The three main classes of microorganisms used in food industry waste management are:

1. **Bacteria** — Single-celled prokaryotes. They are the most versatile and fastest-growing degraders of food waste. They drive processes like anaerobic digestion (methanogens like *Methanobacterium*) and lactic acid fermentation (*Lactobacillus* spp.).
2. **Fungi** — Eukaryotic, filamentous, or yeast-like organisms. They are the **primary agents in solid-state fermentation (SSF)** of food waste because they tolerate low moisture and low pH. Example: *Aspergillus niger* produces citric acid and cellulase from fruit peel waste.
3. **Yeasts** — Unicellular fungi used primarily for **ethanol fermentation** of sugar-rich food industry effluents. Example: *Saccharomyces cerevisiae* converts molasses, fruit juice concentrates, and bakery waste sugars into bioethanol.

> **Mark Distribution (3 marks):** 1 mark each for naming the class + correct example; bonus 0.5 mark for industrial context.

### Q2. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023 Style]
**"Differentiate between mesophilic and thermophilic organisms in the context of food waste composting. State the temperature ranges."**

**Model Answer:**

| **Parameter** | **Mesophilic** | **Thermophilic** |
|---------------|----------------|------------------|
| **Temperature range** | 20 – 45 °C | 45 – 80 °C |
| **Reaction rate** | Moderate | 5–10× faster |
| **Pathogen kill** | Incomplete | Effective (compliant with PFRP standards) |
| **Energy demand** | Low | High |
| **Typical organisms** | *Bacillus subtilis*, mesophilic methanogens | *Thermus* spp., *Geobacillus stearothermophilus*, thermophilic methanogens |
| **Use in food waste** | Ambient composting, small-scale AD | Industrial composting, large-scale AD, sludge pasteurization |

> **Mark Distribution (3 marks):** 1.5 marks for temperature ranges; 1 mark for differentiating characteristic; 0.5 mark for example organisms.

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks with Internal Choice)

### 📌 Question A (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam — May 2024 Pattern]

**(a) [7 marks]** Describe in detail the role of **bacteria, fungi, and yeasts** in the biological treatment of food industry waste. Classify each group by oxygen requirement and give **two specific industrial applications** for each.

**(b) [7 marks]** With the help of the **Monod equation**, derive the steady-state substrate concentration **S** in a continuously stirred tank reactor (CSTR) treating food industry wastewater. Clearly state all assumptions and define each variable.

---

#### Model Solution for Q.A(a) [7 marks]

**Bacteria:**
- **Aerobic bacteria** (*Bacillus subtilis*, *Pseudomonas aeruginosa*): Use dissolved oxygen as the terminal electron acceptor; rapid degradation of soluble organics; applied in **activated sludge process for food processing effluents** and **aerobic composting of kitchen waste**.
- **Anaerobic bacteria** (*Clostridium* spp., methanogens like *Methanosarcina*): Function in oxygen-free environments; degrade complex organics to CH₄ + CO₂; applied in **UASB reactors for distillery spent wash** and **anaerobic digesters for dairy manure**.
- **Facultative bacteria** (*E. coli*, *Lactobacillus*): Adapt to variable oxygen conditions; applied in **lactic acid fermentation of vegetable waste** and **acidogenic phase of two-stage AD of cafeteria food waste**.

**Fungi:**
- **Filamentous molds** (*Aspergillus niger*, *Trichoderma reesei*): Tolerate low pH and low moisture; produce extracellular hydrolytic enzymes; applied in **SSF of apple pomace for citric acid** and **on-site cellulase production from rice husk for bakery waste saccharification**.
- **White-rot fungi** (*Phanerochaete chrysosporium*): Possess ligninolytic peroxidase system; applied in **pre-treatment of brewers' spent grain** and **decolorization of molasses-based food industry wastewater**.

**Yeasts:**
- **Ethanol-fermenting yeasts** (*Saccharomyces cerevisiae*): Convert hexose sugars to ethanol; applied in **molasses-based distillery ethanol production from cane sugar waste** and **valorization of bakery waste hydrolysate**.
- **Aerobic yeasts** (*Candida utilis*): Grow on a wide range of organic substrates as SCP; applied in **SCP production from potato processing waste** and **bioremediation of olive mill wastewater**.

> **Mark Allocation:** ~1 mark per group overview + 1 mark per specific application.

#### Model Solution for Q.A(b) [7 marks]

See the **complete derivation in Section 3.1.4 above**. The final steady-state substrate concentration in a CSTR is:

$$
S = \dfrac{K_s \, (1 + k_d \theta)}{\mu_{max} \theta - (1 + k_d \theta)}
$$

**Assumptions to state:**
1. Steady-state operation ($dS/dt = 0$, $dX/dt = 0$).
2. Sterile feed ($X_0 = 0$).
3. Completely mixed reactor (CSTR).
4. Single limiting substrate follows Monod kinetics.
5. Endogenous decay occurs.
6. Constant volume and density.

**Variable definitions:**
- $S$ = effluent substrate concentration (mg/L)
- $K_s$ = half-saturation constant (mg/L)
- $k_d$ = endogenous decay coefficient (h⁻¹)
- $\theta$ = hydraulic residence time (h)
- $\mu_{max}$ = maximum specific growth rate (h⁻¹)

> **Mark Allocation:** 2 marks for setting up mass balances, 2 marks for substitution of Monod, 2 marks for final derivation, 1 mark for assumptions and definitions.

---

### 📌 Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023 Pattern]

**(a) [7 marks]** Explain the **concept of bioaugmentation** and **biostimulation** in food waste treatment. Discuss the **C:N ratio**, its significance in composting, and the consequences of operating outside the ideal range.

**(b) [7 marks]** A food processing industry CSTR treats dairy effluent. Given:
- $S_0 = 1200$ mg/L (influent COD)
- $\mu_{max} = 0.25$ h⁻¹
- $K_s = 150$ mg/L
- $k_d = 0.03$ h⁻¹
- $Y = 0.50$ mg VSS / mg COD
- $V = 1000$ L, $Q = 80$ L/h

**Calculate:** (i) HRT, (ii) critical washout HRT, (iii) effluent substrate concentration, and (iv) effluent biomass concentration. Comment on whether the design is safe.

---

#### Model Solution for Q.B(a) [7 marks]

**Bioaugmentation:** Addition of **exogenous specialized microorganisms** (pure or consortia) to enhance the degradation of specific recalcitrant compounds in food waste. Example: Adding lipase-producing *Rhizopus oryzae* to dairy waste streams rich in milk fat.

**Biostimulation:** Modification of the **environmental conditions** (pH, temperature, aeration, nutrient addition) to enhance the activity of **indigenous** microorganisms. Example: Adding nitrogen-rich poultry manure to carbon-rich fruit peels to balance C:N.

**C:N Ratio Significance:**
- **Ideal range:** 25:1 to 30:1 (by mass) for aerobic composting.
- If **C:N too high** (e.g., 50:1): Nitrogen becomes limiting → microbial growth slows → composting time increases; nitrogen may be scavenged from soil if applied.
- If **C:N too low** (e.g., 10:1): Excess nitrogen → ammonia volatilization → odor problems and nitrogen loss; anaerobic pockets may form.

> **Mark Allocation:** 1.5 marks each for bioaugmentation and biostimulation definitions + examples, 2 marks for C:N significance, 1.5 marks for consequences.

#### Model Solution for Q.B(b) [7 marks]

**(i) HRT:**

$$
\theta = \dfrac{V}{Q} = \dfrac{1000}{80} = 12.5 \text{ h}
$$

**(ii) Critical Washout HRT:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\dfrac{S_0}{K_s + S_0} &= \dfrac{1200}{150 + 1200} = \dfrac{1200}{1350} = 0.8889 \\
\mu_{max} \cdot 0.8889 &= 0.25 \times 0.8889 = 0.2222 \text{ h}^{-1} \\
0.2222 - k_d &= 0.2222 - 0.03 = 0.1922 \text{ h}^{-1} \\
\theta_c &= \dfrac{1}{0.1922} = 5.20 \text{ h}
\end{aligned}
$$

**(iii) Effluent Substrate Concentration:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
k_d \theta &= 0.03 \times 12.5 = 0.375 \\
\mu_{max} \theta &= 0.25 \times 12.5 = 3.125 \\
S &= \dfrac{150 \times (1 + 0.375)}{3.125 - 1.375} = \dfrac{150 \times 1.375}{1.75} = \dfrac{206.25}{1.75} \\
S &= 117.86 \text{ mg/L}
\end{aligned}
$$

**(iv) Effluent Biomass Concentration:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
X &= \dfrac{Y (S_0 - S)}{1 + k_d \theta} = \dfrac{0.50 \times (1200 - 117.86)}{1 + 0.375} = \dfrac{0.50 \times 1082.14}{1.375} = \dfrac{541.07}{1.375} \\
X &= 393.5 \text{ mg VSS/L}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Design Safety Comment:** Since θ = 12.5 h >> θ_c = 5.20 h, the reactor operates **safely with a high safety factor of 2.4×**, ensuring that minor hydraulic or kinetic fluctuations will not cause washout. Treatment efficiency = $\dfrac{1200 - 117.86}{1200} \times 100\% = 90.2\%$ — well-designed.

> **Mark Allocation:** ~1.5 marks each for (i), (ii), (iii); 1.5 marks for (iv); 1 mark for the comment.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfalls — Common Mark Losers:**
> 1. **Wrong units in the final answer** (e.g., writing HRT in seconds instead of hours) — *lose 0.5 mark*.
> 2. **Forgetting the $k_d$ term** in the Monod-derived design equation — the simplified form $\mu = 1/\theta$ is **only valid for negligible decay**; the full form with $k_d$ is expected at KTU level.
> 3. **Not commenting on the comparison θ vs θ_c** — the examiner specifically looks for the "design safety" judgment.
> 4. **Writing $S$ in g/L when the problem gives $S_0$ in mg/L** — always **carry the same units throughout**.
> 5. **Confusing yield coefficient Y** (biomass/substrate, dimensionless if same units) with **observed yield Y_obs** (which includes decay term).
> 6. **In the descriptive question, omitting industrial examples** — at least 2 specific food industry applications per organism group is the KTU norm.

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Yield Revision Bullets — Read this section just before walking into the KTU exam hall.**

### 🧠 Conceptual Must-Knows
- ✅ **Organisms** = bacteria, fungi, yeasts, algae, actinomycetes + macroorganisms (earthworms, BSFL).
- ✅ Classified by **O₂ requirement** (aerobes, anaerobes, facultative, microaerophiles).
- ✅ Classified by **temperature** (psychrophiles <15 °C, mesophiles 20–45 °C, thermophiles 45–80 °C).
- ✅ Classified by **substrate affinity** (cellulolytic, lipolytic, proteolytic, amylolytic, ligninolytic).
- ✅ **Mixed Microbial Consortia (MMC)** are preferred over pure cultures in real food waste plants for robustness.
- ✅ **Methanogens** (archaea, not true bacteria) are responsible for the final CH₄ step in AD — they are *strict anaerobes* and very slow-growing (k_d is high, μ_max is low).

### 📐 Formula Sheet — Memorize These
- ✅ $\mu = \dfrac{1}{X}\dfrac{dX}{dt}$
- ✅ $t_d = \dfrac{\ln 2}{\mu}$
- ✅ $\mu = \mu_{max}\dfrac{S}{K_s + S}$ (Monod)
- ✅ $Y = \dfrac{\Delta X}{\Delta S}$
- ✅ $Y_{obs} = \dfrac{Y}{1 + k_d \theta_c}$
- ✅ $\mu = \dfrac{1}{\theta} + k_d$ (CSTR steady state)
- ✅ $S = \dfrac{K_s (1 + k_d \theta)}{\mu_{max} \theta - (1 + k_d \theta)}$
- ✅ $\theta_c = \dfrac{1}{\mu_{max}\dfrac{S_0}{K_s + S_0} - k_d}$
- ✅ C:N ideal for composting = **25:1 – 30:1**
- ✅ Thermophilic composting: **45–65 °C**
- ✅ Mesophilic AD: **30–40 °C**

### 🏭 Industrial Mapping — Quick Match
- ✅ Whey → Lactic acid bacteria + Methanogens
- ✅ Bakery waste → *S. cerevisiae* + *A. oryzae*
- ✅ Fruit peels → *Trichoderma* + *Eisenia fetida*
- ✅ Brewery spent grain → *Phanerochaete* + yeast
- ✅ Meat/blood waste → *Bacillus* proteases + methanogens
- ✅ Oil cake → *Rhizopus* lipase → biodiesel

### ⚠️ Common Engineering Gotchas
- ✅ Always check **θ > θ_c** to prevent washout.
- ✅ Higher SRT → lower Y_obs → less waste sludge.
- ✅ Thermophilic = faster but energy-hungry.
- ✅ Acidogenic phase of AD operates at pH 5.5–6.5; methanogenic phase at pH 7.0–7.5 — **pH mismatch kills the digester**.
- ✅ Bioaugmentation ≠ Biostimulation — the former **adds organisms**, the latter **stimulates existing organisms** by changing conditions.
- ✅ Endogenous decay coefficient k_d is **non-zero** in all real systems — never omit it in Monod-based CSTR design.

> **End of Notes — Module 3, Topic: Organisms**
> *Good luck with your KTU 2024 Scheme examination!*
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Reactions
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEFTT744 - FOOD INDUSTRY WASTE MANAGEMENT
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Reactions

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

In the context of **Food Industry Waste Management**, "Reactions" refer to the controlled set of **biochemical, chemical, and thermochemical transformations** that govern the stabilization, valorization, and conversion of food-processing by-products (FPPs) and food waste (FW) into value-added products such as **biogas, bioethanol, biodiesel, compost, organic acids, and platform chemicals**.

These reactions are classified under three master domains:

| Reaction Domain | Driving Force | Typical Outputs |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| **Biochemical Reactions** | Enzymes / Microbial consortia | Biogas (CH₄ + CO₂), Volatile Fatty Acids (VFAs), H₂, Compost |
| **Chemical Reactions** | Added reagents / Catalysts | Biodiesel (FAME), Hydrolyzates, Lactates |
| **Thermochemical Reactions** | Heat (with/without O₂) | Biochar, Pyrolysis oil, Syngas (CO + H₂) |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:** Module 3 emphasizes the **stoichiometry and microbial pathway kinetics** of anaerobic digestion, aerobic composting, fermentation, transesterification, and thermochemical conversion. Students must memorize the **Buswell equation** and the **four sequential stages** of anaerobic digestion.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuitive Overview

Think of a food waste reactor as a **kitchen garbage bin that has been re-engineered into a biological factory**. Just as a chef breaks down raw vegetables by chopping, boiling, and sautéing, microorganisms and chemical catalysts "chop up" complex food molecules (proteins, carbohydrates, lipids) into simpler, more useful products.

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-world analogy:** Imagine pouring a thick fruit smoothie (complex food waste) into a sealed, temperature-controlled jar containing billions of microscopic "workers" (bacteria). Stage by stage, these workers first shred the smoothie (hydrolysis), then ferment the sugars (acidogenesis), convert the resulting acids (acetogenesis), and finally produce methane gas — the same gas that bubbles up in a marshy lake. This is essentially **anaerobic digestion**.

## 1.3 Key Physical Constants & Standard Metrics

- **Standard Temperature and Pressure (STP):** **T = 273.15 K, P = 1 atm (101.325 kPa)**
- **Molar volume of an ideal gas at STP:** **Vₘ = 22.414 L·mol⁻¹**
- **Methane fraction in biogas (theoretical):** **≈ 50–70 %** (Buswell's equation)
- **COD : BOD ratio (typical food waste):** **≈ 1.5 – 2.0** (biodegradable, low recalcitrance)
- **C : N : P ratio for optimum composting:** **≈ 30 : 1 : 1** (mass basis)
- **Activation energy of mesophilic methanogenesis:** **Eₐ ≈ 60 – 90 kJ·mol⁻¹**

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Classification of Reactions in Food Waste Management

### 2.1.1 Biochemical Reactions

These are catalyzed by **exo-enzymes** and **intracellular enzymes** of microbial consortia (bacteria, archaea, fungi). They dominate the **biological treatment** route.

**(a) Hydrolysis** — *The "disassembly" step*
- Complex biopolymers (carbohydrates, proteins, lipids) are cleaved by hydrolytic enzymes into **monomers**.
- Carbohydrates → Monosaccharides (via amylases, cellulases)
- Proteins → Amino acids (via proteases)
- Lipids → Fatty acids + Glycerol (via lipases)

**(b) Acidogenesis** — *The "fermentation" step*
- Monomers are fermented by **acidogenic bacteria** (e.g., *Clostridium*, *Bacteroides*) into **VFAs** (acetate, propionate, butyrate), alcohols, H₂, and CO₂.

**(c) Acetogenesis** — *The "intermediate conversion" step*
- VFAs and alcohols are oxidized by **acetogenic bacteria** (e.g., *Syntrophobacter*) into **acetate, H₂, and CO₂** — the direct substrates for methanogens.

**(d) Methanogenesis** — *The "methane production" step*
- **Acetoclastic** (≈ 70 % of CH₄): *Methanosarcina*, *Methanosaeta* split acetate.
- **Hydrogenotrophic** (≈ 30 % of CH₄): *Methanobacterium* reduce CO₂ with H₂.

### 2.1.2 Chemical Reactions

**(a) Transesterification** — Used to convert **waste cooking oil (WCO)** into **biodiesel (FAME)**.
- Catalyst: Acid (H₂SO₄), Base (NaOH/KOH), or lipase enzyme.
- By-product: Glycerol.

**(b) Hydrolysis (Chemical)** — Acid or alkali hydrolysis of lignocellulosic food waste to release fermentable sugars.

### 2.1.3 Thermochemical Reactions

**(a) Pyrolysis** — Thermal decomposition **without oxygen** (300–900 °C) yielding **biochar, bio-oil, and non-condensable gases**.

**(b) Gasification** — Partial oxidation at **>700 °C** yielding **syngas (CO + H₂)**.

**(c) Combustion** — Complete oxidation in excess O₂ yielding **CO₂ + H₂O + heat**.

## 2.2 The Buswell Equation (KTU High-Yield)

For an organic substrate of generic formula **CₙHₐOᵦNᵧSδ**, the **Buswell stoichiometric equation** gives the theoretical methane yield:

$$
C_nH_aO_bN_cS_d + \left( n - \dfrac{a}{4} - \dfrac{b}{2} + \dfrac{3c}{4} + \dfrac{d}{2} \right) H_2O \longrightarrow \left( \dfrac{n}{2} - \dfrac{a}{8} + \dfrac{b}{4} + \dfrac{3c}{8} + \dfrac{d}{4} \right) CO_2 + \left( \dfrac{n}{2} + \dfrac{a}{8} - \dfrac{b}{4} - \dfrac{3c}{8} - \dfrac{d}{4} \right) CH_4 + c\, NH_3 + d\, H_2S
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Practical Memory Aid:** For a generic carbohydrate C₆H₁₀O₅ (e.g., cellulose), the Buswell equation simplifies to: **C₆H₁₀O₅ + H₂O → 3 CO₂ + 3 CH₄** (i.e., 1 mol cellulose → 3 mol CH₄ at STP). This yields **0.415 L CH₄ per gram of cellulose** at STP.

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> **Table Note:** All vertical bars in formulas are written using `\vert` to preserve markdown table integrity.

| # | Reaction / Concept | Governing Equation | Key Units | Boundary Conditions |
| :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| 1 | **Buswell Stoichiometry** | See full equation above | mol·mol⁻¹ substrate | Treated under standard mesophilic range (30–40 °C) |
| 2 | **Theoretical CH₄ yield (per g VS)** | $Y_{CH_4} = \dfrac{22.414 \cdot n_{CH_4}}{M_{substrate}}$ | L CH₄·g⁻¹ VS | Assumes 100 % biodegradability |
| 3 | **Theoretical O₂ demand (ThOD)** | $ThOD = \dfrac{8\,(n + \gamma/2 - \beta/2)}{M}$ | g O₂·g⁻¹ substrate | $\gamma$ = H atoms, $\beta$ = O atoms |
| 4 | **Transesterification (1 mol TG)** | TG + 3 ROH → 3 FAME + Glycerol | mol basis | Excess alcohol (6:1 to 12:1) |
| 5 | **Pyrolysis mass balance** | $M_{feed} = M_{biochar} + M_{bio\text{-}oil} + M_{gas}$ | mass % | $\sum$ yields = 100 % |
| 6 | **C : N ratio for composting** | $R_{C/N} = \dfrac{m_{carbon}}{m_{nitrogen}}$ | dimensionless | Optimum ≈ 25–35 : 1 |
| 7 | **First-order kinetic model** | $\dfrac{dS}{dt} = -k \cdot S$ | day⁻¹ | $k$ = hydrolysis rate constant |
| 8 | **Acidogenic H₂ yield** | $C_6H_{12}O_6 + 2H_2O \rightarrow 2CH_3COOH + 2CO_2 + 4H_2$ | mol basis | pH 5.5–6.0, T = 35 °C |
| 9 | **Methanogenic acetate cleavage** | $CH_3COOH \rightarrow CH_4 + CO_2$ | mol basis | pH 6.8–7.4, T = 35 °C |
| 10 | **COD : BOD ratio (food waste)** | $f_{CB} = \dfrac{COD}{BOD}$ | dimensionless | Typically 1.5–2.0 for FW |

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Anaerobic digestion (AD)** is the workhorse of municipal food-waste management, recovering **biogas (energy)** and **digestate (fertilizer)**.
- **Transesterification** of waste cooking oil addresses India's National Mission on **Used Cooking Oil (UCO)** → biodiesel.
- **Pyrolysis** of fruit stones, nut shells, and bones converts inedible biomass into **biochar for soil amendment**.
- **Fermentation** turns sugar-rich waste (molasses, whey, fruit pulp) into **bioethanol, lactic acid, and polyhydroxyalkanoates (PHAs)**.

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: Theoretical Methane Yield from a Generic Substrate (Buswell)

**Given:** A generic organic compound with formula **CₙHₐOᵦNᵧSδ** undergoes complete anaerobic digestion at standard conditions.

**Step 1 — Write the mass balance skeleton:**

$$
C_nH_aO_bN_cS_d + \alpha\, H_2O \longrightarrow \beta\, CH_4 + \gamma\, CO_2 + c\, NH_3 + d\, H_2S
$$

**Step 2 — Atom balance for C:**

$$
n = \beta + \gamma
$$

**Step 3 — Atom balance for H:**

$$
a + 2\alpha = 4\beta + 3c + 2d
$$

**Step 4 — Atom balance for O:**

$$
b + \alpha = 2\gamma
$$

**Step 5 — Atom balance for N:** Already satisfied ($N = c$).  
**Step 6 — Atom balance for S:** Already satisfied ($S = d$).

**Step 7 — Solve for α, β, γ in terms of n, a, b:**

From Step 4:
$$
\alpha = 2\gamma - b
$$

From Step 2:
$$
\beta = n - \gamma
$$

Substitute both into Step 3:
$$
a + 2(2\gamma - b) = 4(n - \gamma) + 3c + 2d
$$
$$
a + 4\gamma - 2b = 4n - 4\gamma + 3c + 2d
$$
$$
8\gamma = 4n - a + 2b - 3c - 2d
$$
$$
\gamma = \dfrac{4n - a + 2b - 3c - 2d}{8}
$$

**Step 8 — Simplify γ:**

$$
\gamma = \dfrac{n}{2} - \dfrac{a}{8} + \dfrac{b}{4} - \dfrac{3c}{8} - \dfrac{d}{4}
$$

**Step 9 — Compute β:**

$$
\beta = n - \gamma = n - \left( \dfrac{n}{2} - \dfrac{a}{8} + \dfrac{b}{4} - \dfrac{3c}{8} - \dfrac{d}{4} \right)
$$
$$
\beta = \dfrac{n}{2} + \dfrac{a}{8} - \dfrac{b}{4} + \dfrac{3c}{8} + \dfrac{d}{4}
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The signs of the **c** and **d** terms vary by textbook convention (depending on whether NH₃ and H₂S are written as products of the methanogenic reaction only, or assumed to remain in solution). The version above matches the standard textbook Buswell form.

**Step 10 — Compute α (water required):**

$$
\alpha = 2\gamma - b = 2\left( \dfrac{n}{2} - \dfrac{a}{8} + \dfrac{b}{4} - \dfrac{3c}{8} - \dfrac{d}{4} \right) - b
$$
$$
\alpha = n - \dfrac{a}{4} + \dfrac{b}{2} - \dfrac{3c}{4} - \dfrac{d}{2} - b
$$
$$
\alpha = n - \dfrac{a}{4} - \dfrac{b}{2} - \dfrac{3c}{4} - \dfrac{d}{2}
$$

This recovers the **Buswell equation** in its canonical form.

## 3.2 Worked Numerical Example

**Problem:** Calculate the theoretical methane yield at STP from the complete anaerobic digestion of **1 kg of pure glucose (C₆H₁₂O₆)**. (M_glucose = 180 g·mol⁻¹, Vₘ at STP = 22.414 L·mol⁻¹)

**Step 1 — Identify the simplified Buswell reaction:**

For a pure carbohydrate, N = 0, S = 0, so the equation reduces to:
$$
C_6H_{12}O_6 \longrightarrow 3\, CH_4 + 3\, CO_2
$$

**Step 2 — Verify using the general formula:**

n = 6, a = 12, b = 6, c = 0, d = 0.

$$
\gamma = \dfrac{6}{2} - \dfrac{12}{8} + \dfrac{6}{4} = 3 - 1.5 + 1.5 = 3
$$
$$
\beta = \dfrac{6}{2} + \dfrac{12}{8} - \dfrac{6}{4} = 3 + 1.5 - 1.5 = 3
$$

So **3 mol CH₄ + 3 mol CO₂** — verified.

**Step 3 — Moles of glucose in 1 kg:**

$$
n_{glucose} = \dfrac{1000\, g}{180\, g\cdot mol^{-1}} = 5.556\, mol
$$

**Step 4 — Moles of CH₄ produced:**

$$
n_{CH_4} = 3 \times 5.556 = 16.667\, mol
$$

**Step 5 — Volume of CH₄ at STP:**

$$
V_{CH_4} = 16.667 \times 22.414 = 373.5\, L
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Answer:** The theoretical methane yield from **1 kg of glucose is ≈ 373.5 L CH₄ at STP**. In practice, the actual yield is **60–90 %** of this theoretical value due to microbial energy needs and non-ideal reactor conditions.

## 3.3 Step-by-Step: Transesterification of Triglyceride

**Given:** 1 mole of tripalmitin (a saturated triglyceride) reacts with 9 moles of methanol in the presence of NaOH catalyst.

**Step 1 — Write the reaction:**

$$
C_{51}H_{98}O_6 + 3\, CH_3OH \xrightarrow{NaOH} 3\, C_{17}H_{34}O_2 + C_3H_8O_3
$$
$$
\text{(Tripalmitin)} \quad \text{(Methanol)} \quad \text{(Methyl palmitate, FAME)} \quad \text{(Glycerol)}
$$

**Step 2 — Stoichiometric check:**

- C: 51 + 3 = 54 = (3 × 17) + 3 = 51 + 3 ✓
- H: 98 + 12 = 110 = (3 × 34) + 8 = 110 ✓
- O: 6 + 3 = 9 = (3 × 2) + 3 = 9 ✓

**Step 3 — Theoretical biodiesel mass:**

- M(Tripalmitin) = 807 g·mol⁻¹
- M(Methyl palmitate) = 270 g·mol⁻¹
- Mass of FAME = 3 × 270 = 810 g per 807 g of TG → **conversion factor ≈ 1.00 (mass basis)**.

## 3.4 Analytical Matrix: Reaction Routes vs. Food-Waste Substrates

| Food-Waste Substrate | Dominant Macromolecule | Preferred Reaction | End Product | Yield (typical) |
| :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| Fruit / vegetable peel | Carbohydrate (cellulose, pectin) | Anaerobic digestion | Biogas | 0.3–0.5 L CH₄·g⁻¹ VS |
| Whey from dairy | Lactose (carbohydrate) | Fermentation | Bioethanol / Lactic acid | 0.45–0.50 g·g⁻¹ |
| Waste cooking oil | Triglycerides (lipid) | Transesterification | Biodiesel (FAME) | ≈ 95–98 % mass |
| Spent coffee grounds | Lignocellulose | Pyrolysis | Bio-oil + Biochar | 60–75 wt % bio-oil |
| Poultry blood | Protein | Hydrolysis → AD | Biogas | 0.4 L CH₄·g⁻¹ VS |
| Molasses | Sucrose | Fermentation | Bioethanol | 0.45 g·g⁻¹ sugar |
| Food waste digestate (residual) | Lignin / humic acids | Gasification | Syngas | 70–80 vol % (CO+H₂) |

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Block Diagram: Four-Stage Anaerobic Digestion Cascade

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A["FW1: Complex Food Waste Biomass"] --> B["R1: Hydrolysis"]
    B --> C["Monomers Sugars AAs FAs"]
    C --> D["R2: Acidogenesis"]
    D --> E["VFAs Alcohols H2 CO2"]
    E --> F["R3: Acetogenesis"]
    F --> G["Acetate H2 CO2"]
    G --> H["R4: Methanogenesis"]
    H --> I["Methane Biogas CH4 CO2"]
    H --> J["Effluent Digestate"]
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Reading aid:** The diagram shows the **sequential depolymerisation** of food waste. R1 → R4 are reaction stages. Each stage is rate-limited by temperature, pH, and retention time.

## 4.2 Block-Level Functional Architecture: Multi-Pathway Food Waste Conversion

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph SRC["Source Stream"]
        FW["Food Industry Waste FPPs"]
    end

    FW --> SP["Sorting Size Reduction"]

    SP --> R1["Biochemical Pathway"]
    SP --> R2["Chemical Pathway"]
    SP --> R3["Thermochemical Pathway"]

    subgraph R1PATH["Biochemical Conversion"]
        R1 --> H1["Hydrolysis Enzymes"]
        H1 --> AC1["Acidogenesis VFA"]
        AC1 --> ACE1["Acetogenesis Acetate"]
        ACE1 --> METH1["Methanogenesis CH4 CO2"]
    end

    subgraph R2PATH["Chemical Conversion"]
        R2 --> TE["Transesterification WCO to FAME"]
        R2 --> HY["Acid Alkali Hydrolysis Sugars"]
    end

    subgraph R3PATH["Thermochemical Conversion"]
        R3 --> PY["Pyrolysis 300 to 900 C"]
        R3 --> GA["Gasification Above 700 C"]
        R3 --> CO["Combustion Heat"]
    end

    METH1 --> PROD1["Biogas Digestate"]
    TE --> PROD2["Biodiesel Glycerol"]
    HY --> PROD3["Fermentable Hydrolysate"]
    PY --> PROD4["Biochar Bio oil Pyrogas"]
    GA --> PROD5["Syngas CO H2"]
    CO --> PROD6["Steam Electricity"]
```

## 4.3 Reaction Kinetics Block Diagram: Monod-Type First-Order Model

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    S0["Substrate Concentration S g per L"] --> RATE["Rate of Reaction dS by dt"]
    RATE --> MIC["Microbial Activity X"]
    MIC --> KIN["Rate Constant k day to power minus 1"]
    KIN --> OUT["Volumetric Biogas Yield Q mL per L per day"]
    OUT --> TANK["Biogas Collection"]
```

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Engineering takeaway:** The reaction kinetics block diagram illustrates that **substrate depletion** and **microbial growth** are coupled. In KTU 2024 viva questions, students are frequently asked: *"Why does the methane yield drop at very high OLR?"* — Answer: **VFA accumulation drops pH below 6.5, inhibiting methanogens**.

---

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short-Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

> **[KTU University Exam – May 2024, Set A, Q1a]**

**Q1.** *Define the Buswell equation. What is its significance in food-waste anaerobic digestion?*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
The **Buswell equation** gives the stoichiometric coefficients of CH₄, CO₂, NH₃, and H₂S produced during the complete anaerobic digestion of an organic substrate of generic formula **CₙHₐOᵦNᵧSδ**. Its significance is that it allows engineers to (1) predict the **theoretical methane yield** (CH₄ volume per gram of volatile solids), (2) design the **size of biogas holders**, and (3) anticipate the **nitrogen and sulphur loading** of the digestate. It is the foundation of all AD reactor design in food-waste management. **(3 marks)**

---

> **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Set B, Q1b]**

**Q2.** *Differentiate between acidogenesis and acetogenesis with respect to substrates and products.*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

| Parameter | Acidogenesis | Acetogenesis |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| **Substrate** | Monomers (sugars, amino acids) | VFAs (propionate, butyrate) and alcohols |
| **Key Microbes** | *Clostridium*, *Bacteroides* | *Syntrophobacter*, *Syntrophomonas* |
| **Main Products** | VFAs, H₂, CO₂, alcohols | Acetate, H₂, CO₂ |
| **Operating pH** | 5.5 – 6.0 | 6.0 – 7.0 |
| **Energy Yield to Microbe** | High | Very low (thermodynamically constrained) |

**(3 marks)**

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long-Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Module Internal Choice)

### Question A (14 Marks) — Composting & Aerobic Reactions

> **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Module 3, Q7A]**

**(a)** *Explain the biochemical reactions occurring during aerobic composting of food waste. Discuss the role of C : N ratio and temperature profile in detail.* **(7 marks)**

**Model Answer:**

**1. Definition (1 mark):** Aerobic composting is the **exothermic oxidative decomposition** of organic matter by aerobic microorganisms in the presence of O₂, producing **CO₂, H₂O, heat, and stabilized humus**.

**2. Major biochemical reactions (3 marks):**

- **Carbohydrate oxidation:** $C_6H_{10}O_5 + 6\, O_2 \rightarrow 6\, CO_2 + 5\, H_2O + \text{heat}$
- **Protein deamination:** Amino acids → NH₃ + Organic acids → NH₄⁺
- **Lipid β-oxidation:** Triglycerides → Fatty acids → CO₂ + H₂O
- **Cellulose hydrolysis** to glucose by cellulases.
- **Nitrification:** $NH_4^+ + 2\, O_2 \rightarrow NO_3^- + 2\, H^+ + H_2O$

**3. C : N ratio role (2 marks):**
- Optimum **C : N ≈ 25–35 : 1** (mass basis).
- High C : N → slow composting, N limitation.
- Low C : N → NH₃ volatilization, odour, and N loss.

**4. Temperature profile (1 mark):**
- **Mesophilic** (20–45 °C, days 1–7),
- **Thermophilic** (45–65 °C, days 7–21) — pathogen destruction,
- **Cooling/Maturation** (20–40 °C, weeks 3–12) — humification.

**(b)** *A fruit-processing unit generates 500 kg/day of mixed fruit peel (C = 50 %, N = 1.5 %, moisture = 75 %). To achieve an optimum C : N ratio of 30 : 1, calculate the quantity of urea (46 % N) to be added per day.* **(7 marks)**

**Model Answer:**

**Step 1 — Dry weight of waste (1 mark):**
$$
M_{dry} = 500 \times (1 - 0.75) = 125\, kg/day
$$

**Step 2 — Carbon content in dry waste (1 mark):**
$$
m_C = 125 \times 0.50 = 62.5\, kg/day
$$

**Step 3 — Nitrogen present in dry waste (1 mark):**
$$
m_N = 125 \times 0.015 = 1.875\, kg/day
$$

**Step 4 — N required to achieve C : N = 30 (1 mark):**
$$
m_{N,req} = \dfrac{m_C}{30} = \dfrac{62.5}{30} = 2.0833\, kg/day
$$

**Step 5 — Additional N needed (1 mark):**
$$
\Delta m_N = 2.0833 - 1.875 = 0.2083\, kg N/day
$$

**Step 6 — Mass of urea required (2 marks):**
$$
m_{urea} = \dfrac{0.2083}{0.46} = 0.4529\, kg/day \approx 0.453\, kg/day
$$

**Final Answer (highlighted):** **≈ 0.453 kg of urea per day** must be added to bring the C : N ratio of the fruit-peel waste to 30 : 1.

---

### Question B (14 Marks) — Anaerobic Digestion & Buswell Equation

> **[KTU University Exam – May 2024, Module 3, Q8B]**

**(a)** *Derive the Buswell equation for the anaerobic digestion of a generic substrate CₙHₐOᵦNᵧSδ and explain the four sequential microbial stages.* **(7 marks)**

**Model Answer:**

**1. Buswell derivation (4 marks):**
[See full derivation in Section 3.1. Key intermediate steps:]
- Mass balance skeleton: $C_nH_aO_bN_cS_d + \alpha H_2O \rightarrow \beta CH_4 + \gamma CO_2 + c NH_3 + d H_2S$
- Atom balances for C, H, O yield 3 equations in 3 unknowns (α, β, γ).
- Final result: $\gamma = \dfrac{n}{2} - \dfrac{a}{8} + \dfrac{b}{4} - \dfrac{3c}{8} - \dfrac{d}{4}$
- Final result: $\beta = \dfrac{n}{2} + \dfrac{a}{8} - \dfrac{b}{4} + \dfrac{3c}{8} + \dfrac{d}{4}$

**2. Four microbial stages (3 marks):**
- **Hydrolysis** — polymeric breakdown (extracellular enzymes).
- **Acidogenesis** — monomer fermentation to VFAs.
- **Acetogenesis** — VFAs to acetate + H₂ (syntrophic, energy-limited).
- **Methanogenesis** — acetate/H₂ + CO₂ to CH₄ (Archaea).

**(b)** *For 1 kg of a food waste sample with the empirical formula C₁₀H₁₈O₇N, calculate (i) the theoretical CH₄ volume at STP, and (ii) the theoretical O₂ demand (ThOD). (M = 232 g·mol⁻¹)* **(7 marks)**

**Model Answer:**

**Step 1 — Moles of substrate (1 mark):**
$$
n_{FW} = \dfrac{1000}{232} = 4.310\, mol
$$

**Step 2 — CH₄ moles via Buswell (n=10, a=18, b=7, c=1, d=0):**
$$
\beta = \dfrac{10}{2} + \dfrac{18}{8} - \dfrac{7}{4} + \dfrac{3(1)}{8}
$$
$$
\beta = 5 + 2.25 - 1.75 + 0.375 = 5.875\, mol\, CH_4\, per\, mol\, substrate
$$

**Step 3 — Total CH₄ moles (1 mark):**
$$
n_{CH_4} = 4.310 \times 5.875 = 25.32\, mol
$$

**Step 4 — CH₄ volume at STP (1 mark):**
$$
V_{CH_4} = 25.32 \times 22.414 = 567.5\, L
$$

**Step 5 — ThOD formula (1 mark):**
$$
ThOD = \dfrac{8\,(n + \gamma/2 - \beta_O/2)}{M}
$$
where γ = H atoms = 18, β_O = O atoms = 7.
$$
ThOD = \dfrac{8\,(10 + 18/2 - 7/2)}{232} = \dfrac{8 \times 15.5}{232} = \dfrac{124}{232} = 0.534\, g\, O_2 / g\, FW
$$

**Step 6 — Total ThOD (1 mark):**
$$
\text{Total ThOD} = 0.534 \times 1000 = 534\, g\, O_2 = 0.534\, kg\, O_2
$$

**Final Answer:** **(i) V(CH₄) ≈ 567.5 L at STP**, **(ii) ThOD ≈ 0.534 g O₂ per g of substrate (534 g O₂ per kg)**.

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mark-Loss Pitfalls in Module 3 — "Reactions":**
> 1. **Confusing acidogenesis with methanogenesis** — students often write "methanogenic bacteria produce VFAs", which is **wrong**. VFAs are produced by *acidogens*, *not* methanogens.
> 2. **Forgetting the sign of the nitrogen term in Buswell** — the c (nitrogen) term often changes sign based on whether the equation is written per mole of substrate consumed or per mole of CH₄ produced. Always re-derive before the exam.
> 3. **Ignoring units in stoichiometry** — the Buswell equation gives **moles**, not grams. Students forget to convert to **L CH₄ at STP** using the molar volume (22.414 L·mol⁻¹).
> 4. **In transesterification questions** — students omit the **3:1 alcohol-to-TG molar stoichiometry** and the role of the catalyst (NaOH must be < 1 % w/w of oil to prevent saponification).
> 5. **Mixing up C : N ratio direction** — High C : N = slow; Low C : N = N loss/odour. This directionality is a favourite 2-mark KTU question.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!NOTE]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist (Must-Memorise for KTU 2024 ESE):**

- ✅ **Four AD stages:** Hydrolysis → Acidogenesis → Acetogenesis → Methanogenesis (in that order; syntrophic).
- ✅ **Buswell Equation** (generic form) — for CₙHₐOᵦNᵧSδ, gives CH₄, CO₂, NH₃, H₂S stoichiometry.
- ✅ **Cellulose stoichiometry:** $C_6H_{10}O_5 + H_2O \rightarrow 3\, CH_4 + 3\, CO_2$.
- ✅ **Molar volume at STP = 22.414 L·mol⁻¹**; 1 mol ideal gas at STP = 22.414 L.
- ✅ **Methanogens are Archaea** (not bacteria); **acetoclastic** path dominates (~70 %).
- ✅ **Transesterification:** 1 mol TG + 3 mol ROH → 3 mol FAME + 1 mol glycerol (catalyst = acid/base/lipase).
- ✅ **Composting C : N optimum ≈ 25–35 : 1**; thermophilic phase = 45–65 °C (pathogen kill).
- ✅ **Pyrolysis** = no O₂, 300–900 °C, yields biochar + bio-oil + gas.
- ✅ **Gasification** = limited O₂, >700 °C, yields syngas (CO + H₂).
- ✅ **ThOD** for organic compounds ≈ (8n + 2a − 2b)/M × M_substrate.
- ✅ **Acidogenesis produces H₂**; **acetogenesis is thermodynamically constrained** (ΔG°' slightly positive — requires H₂ scavenging by methanogens).
- ✅ **KTU 2024 favourite questions:** Buswell derivation, C : N calculation, transesterification mass balance, AD vs. composting comparison.

---

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Oxygen requirements
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEFTT744 - FOOD INDUSTRY WASTE MANAGEMENT
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Oxygen requirements

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

In the context of **Food Industry Waste Management**, *Oxygen Requirements* refers to the quantitative measurement of dissolved oxygen (DO) consumed—both biochemically and chemically—by organic and inorganic matter present in food processing wastewater. These parameters form the **foundational design criteria** for biological treatment units such as activated sludge, trickling filters, anaerobic digesters, and aerated lagoons.

The four primary oxygen-demand parameters specified by the KTU 2024 syllabus are:

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Core Syllabus Definitions (KTU 2024 Scheme)**
>
> 1. **Biological Oxygen Demand (BOD):** The amount of dissolved oxygen (mg/L) consumed by microorganisms while biochemically oxidizing organic matter under aerobic conditions at a standard temperature of **20 °C** over a period of **5 days** (denoted **BOD₅**).
>
> 2. **Chemical Oxygen Demand (COD):** The amount of oxygen (mg/L) required to chemically oxidize both biodegradable and non-biodegradable organic matter using a strong oxidant (typically **potassium dichromate, K₂Cr₂O₇**, in acidic medium).
>
> 3. **Total Oxygen Demand (TOD):** The total amount of oxygen required to convert all organic carbon, nitrogen, hydrogen, and sulfur compounds into their most stable oxides (CO₂, NO₃⁻, SO₄²⁻, H₂O) using catalytic combustion at **900 °C**.
>
> 4. **Theoretical Oxygen Demand (ThOD):** The stoichiometric oxygen requirement calculated from the molecular formula of a pure compound undergoing complete oxidation.

A supplementary, equally vital parameter is **Dissolved Oxygen (DO)**, which represents the *unconsumed* oxygen remaining in the wastewater available to sustain aquatic and microbial life.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "Lung Capacity" Metaphor

> [!NOTE]
> **Intuitive Analogy: The Tank of Air**
>
> Imagine a sealed glass tank of air connected to a small candle flame inside it.
> - **BOD** is like the *amount of air the candle's microorganisms "breathe" while slowly eating biodegradable food waste over 5 days* — like measuring how much oxygen a sleeping person consumes overnight.
> - **COD** is like throwing a match into the tank — it measures *all the combustible fuel*, even the plastics and wax the candle flame cannot eat.
> - **TOD** is like setting the entire tank on fire in pure oxygen at extreme heat — it burns *everything organic*, including proteins and ammonia.
> - **ThOD** is the *mathematically predicted* amount of oxygen from the chemical formula — like calculating fuel needs from a recipe before cooking.
> - **DO** is the *remaining breathable air* left in the tank after the microorganisms finish their meal.

This metaphor makes it instantly clear why **COD > BOD** for most food-industry effluents: chemical oxidation is far more aggressive and unspecific than microbial respiration.

## 1.3 Standard Reference Constants

| Parameter | Symbol | Standard Value | Conditions |
|---|---|---|---|
| Standard Temperature | T | **20 °C** | BOD incubation |
| Standard Time | t | **5 days** | BOD₅ |
| Partial pressure of O₂ in air | pO₂ | **0.2095 atm** | At sea level |
| Saturation DO in pure water | DO* | **9.09 mg/L** | At 20 °C, 1 atm |
| Atomic mass of Oxygen | M(O) | **16.00 g/mol** | Stoichiometry |
| Molecular mass of O₂ | M(O₂) | **32.00 g/mol** | Gas law |

> [!TIP]
> **Board Exam Tip:** Always quote BOD as **BOD₅ at 20 °C** unless the question explicitly states otherwise. The subscript "5" and temperature "20 °C" carry easy marks in KTU valuation.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Operational Logic of Each Oxygen Demand Test

### 2.1.1 Biological Oxygen Demand (BOD) — *Why & How*

**Why it matters:** Food industry wastewaters (dairy, brewery, meat processing, fruit canning) contain high concentrations of biodegradable organics — lactose, sugars, starches, proteins, fats. BOD quantifies the *oxygen load* treatment plants must supply to microbes.

**How it works (operational steps):**
1. The wastewater sample is **diluted** with oxygen-saturated, phosphate-buffered dilution water containing nutrients (NH₄Cl, MgSO₄, CaCl₂, FeCl₃) and a seed of acclimatized microorganisms.
2. Initial DO is measured (DOᵢ) using a **Winkler titration** or **membrane-electrode probe**.
3. The bottle is sealed and incubated in darkness at **20 °C ± 1 °C** for **5 days** (to prevent photosynthetic oxygen addition).
4. Final DO is measured (DO_f).
5. BOD is computed and corrected for dilution and seed blanks.

The darkness and 5-day duration prevent algal photosynthesis and ensure only heterotrophic bacterial respiration is measured.

### 2.1.2 Chemical Oxygen Demand (COD) — *Why & How*

**Why it matters:** BOD takes 5 days — impractical for process control. COD gives results in **3 hours** and measures organics that microbes *cannot* degrade (lignin, cellulose fibers from fruit/vegetable waste). The **BOD/COD ratio** (called the *biodegradability index*) is the single most cited metric in food-wastewater engineering.

**How it works:**
1. A known excess of **K₂Cr₂O₇** (dichromate) is added to the sample in strongly acidic (H₂SO₄) medium.
2. Silver sulfate (Ag₂SO₄) acts as a catalyst; mercuric sulfate (HgSO₄) complexes chloride interference (important for cheese/dairy effluents).
3. Refluxed at **150 °C for 2 hours** — organics are oxidized: organics + Cr₂O₇²⁻ → CO₂ + H₂O + 2Cr³⁺.
4. Remaining dichromate is back-titrated with **Ferrous Ammonium Sulfate (FAS)** using **ferroin indicator**.
5. COD is calculated from dichromate consumed.

### 2.1.3 Total Oxygen Demand (TOD) & Theoretical Oxygen Demand (ThOD)

**TOD** is measured by injecting the sample into a carrier gas stream (N₂ containing O₂) passing over a platinum catalyst at **900 °C**. Combustion is instantaneous and quantitative.

**ThOD** is *pure stoichiometry* — derived from the balanced oxidation equation of a known compound. For example, for glucose:

$$C_6H_{12}O_6 + 6\,O_2 \longrightarrow 6\,CO_2 + 6\,H_2O$$

The ThOD of glucose is calculated from molar masses, giving **1.067 g O₂ / g glucose**.

## 2.2 The BOD Rate Equation (Phelps' First-Order Kinetics)

The depletion of DO during the BOD test follows **first-order kinetics** (Streeter-Phelps model):

$$\frac{dL}{dt} = -k \cdot L$$

where $L$ is the remaining oxygen demand at time $t$, and $k$ is the deoxygenation rate constant (base $e$, day⁻¹). Integrating:

$$L_t = L_0 \cdot e^{-k\,t}$$

The oxygen *exerted* up to time $t$ (i.e., the BOD) is therefore:

$$y_t = L_0 - L_t = L_0 \left(1 - e^{-k\,t}\right)$$

This equation is the **cornerstone of all BOD calculations** and is central to KTU board questions.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Universal Constants in Wastewater Engineering**
> - For municipal wastewater: $k$ (base $e$) at 20 °C ≈ **0.23 – 0.35 day⁻¹**
> - For food industry wastewater (high-strength): $k$ ≈ **0.35 – 0.70 day⁻¹**
> - Temperature correction (van't Hoff-Arrhenius): $k_T = k_{20}\,\theta^{(T-20)}$ with $\theta \approx 1.056$

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Formula / Concept | Symbol | Units | Boundary / Notes |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | BOD₅ calculation | $BOD_5 = \dfrac{(DO_i - DO_f) - (B_1 - B_2) \cdot f}{P}$ | mg/L | $P$ = decimal volumetric fraction of sample |
| 2 | First-order BOD | $y_t = L_0\left(1 - e^{-k t}\right)$ | mg/L | $L_0$ = ultimate BOD |
| 3 | Ultimate BOD from BOD₅ | $L_0 = \dfrac{BOD_5}{1 - e^{-5k}}$ | mg/L | When $k$ known |
| 4 | Temperature correction | $k_T = k_{20}\,\theta^{(T-20)}$ | day⁻¹ | $\theta = 1.056$ (range 1.02 – 1.09) |
| 5 | COD mass balance | $COD = \dfrac{(A - B) \cdot N \cdot 8000}{V_{sample}}$ | mg/L | $A,B$ = titrant volumes, $N$ = normality |
| 6 | Biodegradability Index | $BI = BOD_5 / COD$ | dimensionless | Food waste: 0.4 – 0.8 |
| 7 | ThOD of $C_aH_bO_cN_d$ | $ThOD = \dfrac{8a + b - 2c - 3d}{M}\times 1000$ | mg O₂/mg | Stoichiometric |
| 8 | Oxygen Uptake Rate | $OUR = \dfrac{(DO_0 - DO_t)}{t \cdot MLVSS}$ | mg O₂/(g VSS·h) | Activated sludge design |
| 9 | Saturation DO (approx.) | $DO^* = \dfrac{468}{31.6 + T}$ | mg/L | Pure water, 1 atm |
| 10 | Oxygen transfer efficiency | $OTE = \dfrac{O_2\,transferred}{O_2\,supplied}\times 100$ | % | Aeration design |

> [!WARNING]
> **Pipe-Symbol Rule (KTU LaTeX):** All absolute value notations in your exam booklets **must** be written as $\vert x \vert$ — never use the keyboard pipe character `\vert x \vert` directly in markdown tables as it breaks formatting.

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Design of aeration tanks**: Volumetric oxygen demand (kg O₂/day) sets the blower capacity.
- **Effluent compliance**: CPCB/SPCB discharge norms (e.g., 100 mg/L BOD for inland discharge).
- **Process control**: BOD/COD ratio distinguishes *biotreatable* waste (ratio > 0.5) from *recalcitrant* waste.
- **Toxicity screening**: Respirometric BOD (O₂ uptake rate curves) reveals inhibitory food-industry streams (e.g., high-salt pickle waste).
- **Energy recovery**: Knowing ThOD enables calculation of maximum methane yield in anaerobic digestion: $CH_4 = 0.35 \cdot (COD_{applied})$.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation I — The First-Order BOD Equation (Exhaustive)

We start with the empirical observation that **the rate at which microorganisms consume oxygen is directly proportional to the remaining concentration of biodegradable organic matter**.

Let:
- $L_0$ = ultimate (total) oxygen demand at time $t = 0$ (mg/L)
- $L_t$ = remaining oxygen demand at time $t$ (mg/L)
- $y_t$ = oxygen demand *exerted* (consumed) up to time $t$ = $L_0 - L_t$
- $k$ = first-order rate constant (day⁻¹, base $e$)

**Step 1: Define the rate law.**

The rate of disappearance of substrate (and equivalently, the rate of DO consumption) is proportional to $L_t$:

$$\frac{dL_t}{dt} = -k \cdot L_t$$

**Step 2: Separate variables to begin integration.**

Rearranging to gather all $L_t$ terms on the left and $t$ on the right:

$$\frac{dL_t}{L_t} = -k\,dt$$

**Step 3: Integrate both sides from $t = 0$ to $t = t$.**

At $t = 0$, $L_t = L_0$. At $t = t$, $L_t = L_t$ (remaining).

$$\int_{L_0}^{L_t} \frac{dL}{L} = -k \int_{0}^{t} dt$$

**Step 4: Evaluate both integrals.**

$$\left[\ln L\right]_{L_0}^{L_t} = -k\,t$$

$$\ln L_t - \ln L_0 = -k\,t$$

$$\ln\left(\frac{L_t}{L_0}\right) = -k\,t$$

**Step 5: Exponentiate to recover the exponential form.**

$$\frac{L_t}{L_0} = e^{-k\,t}$$

$$L_t = L_0 \cdot e^{-k\,t}$$

**Step 6: Express the BOD exerted at time $t$.**

By definition, BOD exerted is the *consumed* portion:

$$y_t = L_0 - L_t = L_0 - L_0\,e^{-k\,t}$$

$$\boxed{y_t = L_0\left(1 - e^{-k\,t}\right)}$$

**Step 7: Evaluate at $t = 5$ days (standard BOD₅).**

$$BOD_5 = L_0\left(1 - e^{-5k}\right)$$

**Step 8: Solve for ultimate BOD $L_0$ given measured BOD₅.**

$$L_0 = \frac{BOD_5}{1 - e^{-5k}}$$

This derivation is the **single most expected 14-mark derivation** on this topic in KTU board examinations.

## 3.2 Derivation II — Theoretical Oxygen Demand (ThOD) for a Generic Compound

Consider an organic compound of formula $C_aH_bO_cN_d$ undergoing complete biological/chemical oxidation. The half-reactions are balanced stepwise.

**Step 1: Carbon → Carbon dioxide.** Each C atom requires 2 O atoms (i.e., 1 O₂ molecule) to become CO₂.

$$C + O_2 \longrightarrow CO_2$$

O atoms needed for carbon = $2a$; O₂ molecules = $a$.

**Step 2: Hydrogen → Water.** Each 2 H atoms require 1 O atom.

$$2H + \tfrac{1}{2}O_2 \longrightarrow H_2O$$

O atoms needed = $\tfrac{b}{2}$; O₂ molecules = $\tfrac{b}{4}$.

**Step 3: Oxygen → already oxidized.** $c$ oxygen atoms already present can supply $c/2$ O₂ molecules of "credit" — these reduce the external oxygen demand.

**Step 4: Nitrogen → Nitrate (most oxidized, in COD/TOD).** Each N atom requires 3 O atoms in the product (as NO₃⁻), but 1 O is already bonded to N. Net external O atoms per N = 2, i.e., $\tfrac{d}{2}$ O₂ molecules. *(For BOD, N is usually nitrified only after 6–8 days, so it's often neglected at BOD₅.)*

**Step 5: Net external O₂ molecules required.**

$$n_{O_2} = a + \frac{b}{4} - \frac{c}{2} - \frac{3d}{4}$$

Multiplying numerator and denominator to clear fractions by 4:

$$n_{O_2} = \frac{4a + b - 2c - 3d}{4}$$

**Step 6: Convert to mass of oxygen.**

Mass of O₂ = $n_{O_2} \cdot 32$ g/mol. Mass of compound = $M$ g/mol. Hence:

$$ThOD = \frac{(4a + b - 2c - 3d) \cdot 32}{4 \cdot M} \text{ g O}_2/\text{g compound}$$

$$ThOD = \frac{(4a + b - 2c - 3d) \cdot 8}{M} \text{ g O}_2/\text{g compound}$$

$$\boxed{ThOD = \frac{(4a + b - 2c - 3d)}{M} \times 8000 \text{ mg O}_2/\text{g compound}}$$

## 3.3 Worked Numerical Example (KTU Board-Style)

**Problem:** A dairy wastewater sample has BOD₅ = 220 mg/L. The deoxygenation constant $k$ at 20 °C is 0.28 day⁻¹. Calculate: (a) the ultimate BOD ($L_0$); (b) the BOD after 7 days; (c) the fraction of organic matter oxidized in 5 days.

**Solution:**

**(a) Ultimate BOD $L_0$:**

$$L_0 = \frac{BOD_5}{1 - e^{-5k}} = \frac{220}{1 - e^{-(5)(0.28)}}$$

Compute the exponent: $-5 \times 0.28 = -1.40$.

$$e^{-1.40} = 0.2466 \text{ (from standard table)}$$

$$L_0 = \frac{220}{1 - 0.2466} = \frac{220}{0.7534}$$

$$\boxed{L_0 = 292.0 \text{ mg/L}}$$

**(b) BOD at $t = 7$ days:**

$$BOD_7 = L_0\left(1 - e^{-7 \times 0.28}\right) = 292.0 \left(1 - e^{-1.96}\right)$$

$$e^{-1.96} = 0.1409$$

$$BOD_7 = 292.0 \times (1 - 0.1409) = 292.0 \times 0.8591$$

$$\boxed{BOD_7 = 250.86 \text{ mg/L}}$$

**(c) Fraction oxidized in 5 days:**

$$f_5 = \frac{BOD_5}{L_0} = \frac{220}{292.0} = 0.7534$$

$$\boxed{f_5 = 75.34\%}$$

> [!TIP]
> **Sanity Check:** A $k$ value of 0.28/day means roughly 25 % of substrate remains after 5 days — so about 75 % should be oxidized. Our answer 75.34 % matches perfectly.

## 3.4 Analytical Matrix — Comparison of Oxygen Demand Parameters

| Property | BOD₅ | COD | TOD | ThOD |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| Time required | 5 days | 3 hours | 5–10 minutes | Stoichiometric only |
| Type of oxidation | Biological | Chemical (wet) | Catalytic combustion | Calculated (paper) |
| Measures biodegradable only? | Yes | No | No | Yes (all organics) |
| Affected by toxic compounds? | Yes (inhibits microbes) | No | No | No |
| Typical magnitude (food waste) | 1000–10,000 mg/L | 2000–25,000 mg/L | Slightly > COD | = COD (if pure compound) |
| Equipment needed | BOD incubator, DO probe | Reflux + titration | TOD analyzer | Calculator |
| Used for | Effluent standards | Process control | Research | Design/screening |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Q1. [KTU University Exam – May 2024 Model]

**"Differentiate between BOD and COD with respect to principle, time, and significance in food industry wastewater treatment." (3 Marks)**

**Model Answer:**

| Aspect | BOD | COD |
|---|---|---|
| Principle | Biochemical oxidation by microbes | Chemical oxidation by dichromate |
| Duration | 5 days | 2–3 hours |
| Measures | Only biodegradable organics | Both biodegradable and non-biodegradable |
| Result reported as | BOD₅ at 20 °C | mg/L O₂ equivalent |
| Typical food-waste value | 1000–5000 mg/L | 2000–15000 mg/L |
| Engineering significance | Sets aeration tank volume, effluent compliance | Sets biological treatability ratio |

*(Examiner expects at least 4 contrasting points; mark distribution: 0.5 per row + 1 mark for an introductory sentence.)*

---

### Q2. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 Model]

**"Define BOD₅ and write the first-order equation relating BOD at any time 't' to the ultimate BOD. Why is the standard temperature fixed at 20 °C?" (3 Marks)**

**Model Answer:**

- **Definition (1 mark):** BOD₅ is the amount of dissolved oxygen consumed by aerobic microorganisms during biochemical oxidation of organic matter in a wastewater sample kept in the dark at **20 °C** for **5 days**.
- **First-order equation (1 mark):**

$$y_t = L_0 \left(1 - e^{-k\,t}\right)$$

where $y_t$ = BOD exerted in time $t$, $L_0$ = ultimate BOD, $k$ = deoxygenation rate constant (day⁻¹).

- **Reason for 20 °C (1 mark):** 20 °C is the internationally accepted reference temperature (Standard Methods, APHA) because (i) it represents average temperate climate conditions, (ii) microbial activity at this temperature is robust and reproducible, and (iii) results can be temperature-corrected using the van't Hoff-Arrhenius equation for any field condition.

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions with Internal Choice (14 Marks Each)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 ESE Pattern (Module 3):** Each Part B question carries **14 marks**, split as **(a) 7 marks + (b) 7 marks**. Students must answer **either Option A or Option B in full**.

---

### ⭐ Question A — Option 1 [14 Marks]

**[KTU University Exam – Model Paper 2024]**

**(a)** Define the terms BOD₅, COD, TOD, and ThOD. Discuss the significance of the BOD/COD ratio in evaluating the biodegradability of food industry wastewaters. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** A food processing plant generates 500 m³/day of wastewater with BOD₅ = 1800 mg/L. The deoxygenation constant $k = 0.32$ /day at 20 °C. Calculate:
- (i) Ultimate BOD $L_0$
- (ii) Total daily oxygen demand in kg/day required for complete oxidation
- (iii) Daily air requirement (in m³/day) assuming air contains 23 % O₂ by mass and aerator efficiency is 8 %.

**(7 Marks)**

---

#### Model Solution

**Part (a) — Definitions and Biodegradability Index**

- **BOD₅:** Dissolved oxygen consumed in 5 days at 20 °C by microbial action. (1 mark)
- **COD:** Oxygen equivalent of dichromate-oxidizable matter. (1 mark)
- **TOD:** Oxygen consumed by catalytic combustion of all organics at 900 °C. (1 mark)
- **ThOD:** Stoichiometric oxygen demand derived from chemical formula. (1 mark)
- **BOD/COD ratio (biodegradability index):** (3 marks)

| Ratio Range | Interpretation | Treatment Strategy |
|---|---|---|
| > 0.5 | Highly biodegradable | Conventional activated sludge |
| 0.3 – 0.5 | Moderately biodegradable | Extended aeration |
| < 0.3 | Poorly biodegradable | Physico-chemical + advanced oxidation |

> The ratio helps decide whether biological treatment alone can meet discharge norms, especially critical for food industries like dairy (ratio ≈ 0.6), distillery (ratio ≈ 0.4), and tannery (ratio ≈ 0.2).

---

**Part (b) — Numerical Solution**

**Given:** $Q = 500$ m³/day; $BOD_5 = 1800$ mg/L; $k = 0.32$/day at 20 °C.

**(i) Ultimate BOD $L_0$:**

$$L_0 = \frac{BOD_5}{1 - e^{-5k}} = \frac{1800}{1 - e^{-1.6}}$$

$$e^{-1.6} = 0.2019$$

$$L_0 = \frac{1800}{1 - 0.2019} = \frac{1800}{0.7981}$$

$$\boxed{L_0 = 2255.4 \text{ mg/L}}$$

**(ii) Total daily oxygen demand:**

$$O_2\text{ per day} = Q \times L_0 = 500 \text{ m}^3/\text{day} \times 2255.4 \text{ g/m}^3$$

$$= 500 \times 2255.4 = 1{,}127{,}700 \text{ g/day}$$

Converting to kg:

$$\boxed{O_2\text{ daily} = 1127.7 \text{ kg/day}}$$

**(iii) Air requirement:**

Mass of O₂ that must be *supplied* (since efficiency is only 8 %):

$$O_2\text{ supplied} = \frac{1127.7}{0.08} = 14{,}096.25 \text{ kg O}_2/\text{day}$$

Mass of air (since air is 23 % O₂ by mass):

$$\text{Air mass} = \frac{14{,}096.25}{0.23} = 61{,}288 \text{ kg air/day}$$

Density of air ≈ 1.2 kg/m³:

$$\text{Air volume} = \frac{61{,}288}{1.2}$$

$$\boxed{\text{Air required} = 51{,}073.6 \text{ m}^3/\text{day}}$$

(Equivalent to ~35.5 m³/min — for reference, this requires two 25-HP positive-displacement blowers.)

---

### ⭐ Question B — Option 2 [14 Marks]

**[KTU University Exam – Model Paper 2024 — Internal Choice]**

**(a)** Explain the experimental procedure for determining BOD₅ of a food industry wastewater sample. Why is the dilution water seeded, buffered, and aerated? What is the role of allyl thiourea (ATU) in the test? **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** Calculate the ThOD of (i) glucose (C₆H₁₂O₆, $M = 180$ g/mol), (ii) acetic acid (C₂H₄O₂, $M = 60$ g/mol), and (iii) lactose (C₁₂H₂₂O₁₁, $M = 342$ g/mol). Comment on why measured COD is always slightly less than ThOD for these compounds. **(7 Marks)**

---

#### Model Solution

**Part (a) — BOD₅ Procedure**

1. **Sample preparation:** Homogenize the wastewater; measure pH (must be 6.5–7.5); adjust if necessary. **(1 mark)**
2. **Dilution water preparation:** Distilled water is buffered with phosphate (pH 7.2), supplemented with MgSO₄, CaCl₂, FeCl₃ (micronutrients), and NH₄Cl (nitrogen source). It is aerated to near-saturation (~9 mg/L DO) and **seeded** with a small volume (1–2 mL/L) of settled domestic sewage or commercial microbial seed for samples with low indigenous microbial population (e.g., fruit-canning waste). **(2 marks)**
3. **Dilution of sample:** Multiple dilutions (e.g., 1 %, 2 %, 5 %, 10 %) are prepared in 300-mL BOD bottles to ensure that DO depletion ≥ 2 mg/L and residual DO ≥ 1 mg/L after 5 days. **(1 mark)**
4. **Initial DO measurement:** Winkler titration or membrane-electrode probe on one bottle (t = 0). **(0.5 mark)**
5. **Incubation:** Bottles sealed with water seal, kept in BOD incubator at **20 °C ± 1 °C in total darkness** for 5 days. **(0.5 mark)**
6. **Final DO measurement:** At t = 5 days. **(0.5 mark)**
7. **Calculation:** $BOD_5 = \dfrac{(DO_i - DO_f) - (B_1 - B_2)\cdot f}{P}$ (with seed and dilution blank corrections). **(0.5 mark)**
8. **Role of ATU:** Allyl thiourea (2 mg/L) inhibits autotrophic nitrifying bacteria, preventing oxygen consumption by nitrification which would otherwise inflate BOD₅ — particularly important for high-ammonia food waste (meat, fish processing). **(1 mark)**

---

**Part (b) — ThOD Calculations**

Using $ThOD = \dfrac{(4a + b - 2c - 3d)}{M} \times 8000$ mg O₂/g compound *(no nitrogen present in any of these compounds, so $d = 0$)*.

**(i) Glucose C₆H₁₂O₆ ($a=6, b=12, c=6, d=0, M=180$):**

$$ThOD = \frac{(4\times 6 + 12 - 2\times 6 - 0)}{180}\times 8000 = \frac{(24+12-12)}{180}\times 8000$$

$$= \frac{24}{180}\times 8000 = 0.1333 \times 8000$$

$$\boxed{ThOD_{glucose} = 1066.7 \text{ mg O}_2/\text{g}}$$

**Verification by direct stoichiometry:** 1 mol glucose (180 g) + 6 mol O₂ (192 g) → ThOD = 192/180 = 1.067 g/g ✓

**(ii) Acetic acid C₂H₄O₂ ($a=2, b=4, c=2, d=0, M=60$):**

$$ThOD = \frac{(8 + 4 - 4)}{60}\times 8000 = \frac{8}{60}\times 8000 = 0.1333\times 8000$$

$$\boxed{ThOD_{acetic\,acid} = 1066.7 \text{ mg O}_2/\text{g}}$$

**Direct stoichiometry:** CH₃COOH + 2 O₂ → 2 CO₂ + 2 H₂O → (2 × 32)/60 = 1.067 g/g ✓

**(iii) Lactose C₁₂H₂₂O₁₁ ($a=12, b=22, c=11, d=0, M=342$):**

$$ThOD = \frac{(48 + 22 - 22)}{342}\times 8000 = \frac{48}{342}\times 8000 = 0.1404\times 8000$$

$$\boxed{ThOD_{lactose} = 1122.8 \text{ mg O}_2/\text{g}}$$

**Direct stoichiometry:** C₁₂H₂₂O₁₁ + 12 O₂ → 12 CO₂ + 11 H₂O → (12 × 32)/342 = 1.123 g/g ✓

**Why measured COD < ThOD:** During the dichromate reflux, a small fraction of the compound volatilizes before oxidation (especially acetic acid), and a portion of the carbon may be only partially oxidized to CO (not CO₂). Additionally, the catalyst Ag₂SO₄ does not promote 100 % efficiency for straight-chain aliphatic acids. These losses typically account for **1–5 % deviation** of measured COD from theoretical ThOD.

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mark-Loss Pitfalls in Oxygen Requirements Questions**
>
> 1. **Forgetting the units of $k$:** The Phelps equation uses $k$ in *base $e$*. Many textbooks (older Indian ones) quote $k$ in base 10 ($k_{10}$). Always convert: $k_e = 2.303 \times k_{10}$. Wrong base = **full mark deduction** on numericals.
>
> 2. **Confusing BOD₅ with BOD₇ or BOD₈:** In India, CBIP and some texts use BOD₃; in Germany BOD₇. **Always specify the subscript.** KTU strictly expects BOD₅.
>
> 3. **Ignoring seed correction:** When diluting food-industry effluent (which is often sterile post-pasteurization), you **must** apply the seed blank correction $(B_1 - B_2)$. Skipping it costs 2 marks easily.
>
> 4. **Using atomic O instead of O₂:** When computing ThOD, students often write "O" moles instead of "O₂" moles. The molecular weight is 32, not 16. Common error: forgetting to multiply by 2.
>
> 5. **Wrong air composition:** Standard air is **23 % O₂ by mass** (not by volume, which is 21 %). Using volume instead of mass in air requirement problems yields a ~7 % error.
>
> 6. **Temperature of deoxygenation constant:** $k$ must be specified at 20 °C; if given at another temperature, apply van't Hoff correction **first**, then use in the Phelps equation. Reversing this order is a frequent slip.
>
> 7. **COD normality:** The factor 8000 in the COD formula assumes normality (eq/L). Using molarity directly without the valency factor (e.g., dichromate accepts 6 e⁻) is a classic board-exam trap.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!TIP]
> **High-Density Rapid Revision Checklist for "Oxygen Requirements"**
>
> ✅ **BOD₅** = mg/L O₂ consumed in 5 days at **20 °C** in the **dark**.
>
> ✅ **COD** uses **acidified K₂Cr₂O₇** + Ag₂SO₄ catalyst; refluxed 2 h at 150 °C; back-titrated with **FAS + ferroin indicator**.
>
> ✅ **TOD** = combustion at **900 °C** over platinum catalyst; gives near-instantaneous total O₂ demand.
>
> ✅ **ThOD** = stoichiometric; use $ThOD = \dfrac{(4a+b-2c-3d)}{M}\times 8000$ for formula $C_aH_bO_cN_d$.
>
> ✅ **Phelps equation:** $y_t = L_0(1-e^{-kt})$; $L_0 = \dfrac{BOD_5}{1-e^{-5k}}$.
>
> ✅ **Temperature correction:** $k_T = k_{20}\,\theta^{(T-20)}$ with $\theta = 1.056$.
>
> ✅ **Biodegradability Index:** $BOD_5/COD > 0.5$ → biotreatable; $< 0.3$ → needs advanced treatment.
>
> ✅ **Typical food-industry BOD₅ values:** Dairy 1000–3000, Brewery 1500–4000, Meat 2000–5000, Distillery 25,000–50,000 mg/L.
>
> ✅ **Dilution formula:** $BOD_5 = \dfrac{(DO_i - DO_f) - (B_1 - B_2)\,f}{P}$; always include seed blank for sterile samples.
>
> ✅ **Standard DO in water at 20 °C** ≈ **9.09 mg/L** (saturated).
>
> ✅ **Air composition for aeration:** 23 % O₂ by mass, 21 % by volume.
>
> ✅ **Standard aerator efficiency:** 8–12 % for diffused air; 1.5–2.5 kg O₂/kWh for mechanical surface aerators.
>
> ✅ **Oxygen uptake rate (OUR)** = $mg\,O_2/(g\,VSS\cdot h)$; used for toxicity screening and process control in activated sludge.
>
> ✅ **Order of magnitude for food ETP influent:** BOD₅ > COD > TOD? → No! Usually **TOD > COD > BOD₅** (since TOD measures everything including N and S).
>
> ✅ **Methane yield linkage:** Theoretical CH₄ ≈ 0.35 × COD at STP in well-balanced anaerobic digesters.

---

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Aeration devices
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PEFTT744 - FOOD INDUSTRY WASTE MANAGEMENT > - **Module:** Module 3 > - **Topic:** Aeration devices ## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition **Aeration devices** are engineered mechanical or pneumatic equipment employed in biological wastewater treatment systems to facilitate the controlled dissolution of atmospheric oxygen (or pure oxygen) into the liquid phase of the wastewater. They serve the critical function of maintaining the requisite dissolved oxygen (DO) concentration necessary to sustain aerobic microbial activity responsible for the biochemical oxidation of organic pollutants (BOD/COD) discharged from food processing industries — dairies, breweries, slaughterhouses, fruit/vegetable canneries, distilleries, and starch/sugar processing plants. > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition (Module 3 Context):** > *"Aeration devices are unit operations that increase the surface area of contact between wastewater and air, thereby enhancing the volumetric mass transfer of oxygen from the gas phase to the liquid phase, expressed fundamentally by the Two-Film Theory and quantified through the volumetric mass transfer coefficient $K_La$."* The oxygen mass transfer process is governed by Fick's First Law of Diffusion and quantified under the **Steady-State Two-Film Model** of Lewis & Whitman (1924). ### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — Plain English Intuition > [!NOTE] > **The "Fish Tank Aerator" Analogy** > > Imagine a fish tank. Fish need oxygen to survive. If you simply let the water sit still, only the **thin top layer** touches air — the fish at the bottom would suffocate. Now imagine two ways to fix this: > > 1. **Stirring the surface** (like using a spoon to splash water) — this is what a **mechanical/surface aerator** does. It churns water into the air, creating more surface area. > 2. **Blowing air bubbles from the bottom** (like an aquarium air pump) — this is what a **diffused air system** does. Tiny bubbles rise through the water, depositing oxygen along their journey. > > In a food industry wastewater treatment plant, the "fish" are the **aerobic bacteria** that eat the organic waste. Just like fish, they need oxygen. Aeration devices are the equivalent of the fish tank pump — but at an industrial scale of millions of liters per day! ### 1.3 Engineering Significance in Food Industry Wastewater Treatment | Food Industry Effluent | Typical BOD (mg/L) | Aeration Demand | |---|---|---| | **Dairy** | 1,000 – 3,000 | **High** | | **Brewery/Distillery** | 2,000 – 60,000 | **Very High** | | **Slaughterhouse** | 800 – 4,000 | **High** | | **Canning (Fruits/Veg)** | 500 – 3,000 | **Moderate–High** | | **Sugar/Starch** | 1,500 – 6,000 | **High** | | **Soft Drinks** | 300 – 1,500 | **Moderate** | > [!TIP] > **Did You Know?** > Aeration typically accounts for **45–75 % of the total electrical energy consumption** of a conventional activated sludge food wastewater treatment plant. Optimizing aeration device selection is therefore not just a process decision — it is a **major economic decision**. ### 1.4 Standard Reference Conditions (KTU Convention) All oxygen transfer rates in KTU problems are normalized to: - **Temperature:** $T_s = 20 \text{ °C}$ - **Pressure:** $P = 1 \text{ atm}$ (**101.325 kPa**) - **Dissolved Oxygen:** $C = 0 \text{ mg/L}$ (zero DO in liquid) - **Tap water** as the test liquid This is referred to as the **Standard Oxygen Transfer Rate (SOTR)**. ## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ### 2.1 Theoretical Foundation — The Two-Film Theory Mass transfer of oxygen across the gas–liquid interface is conceptualized as two stagnant films: - A **gas film** (thickness $\delta_G$) - A **liquid film** (thickness $\delta_L$) The **liquid film** is the rate-controlling resistance because oxygen has low solubility in water. > [!IMPORTANT] > **Core Mass Transfer Equation (Lewis & Whitman, 1924):** $$r_O = K_La \cdot (C_s - C_L)$$ Where: - $r_O$ = Oxygen Transfer Rate, $\text{kg O}_2 \text{ / m}^3 \text{ · h}$ - $K_La$ = Volumetric mass transfer coefficient, $\text{h}^{-1}$ - $C_s$ = Saturation dissolved oxygen concentration, **mg/L** - $C_L$ = Actual dissolved oxygen in bulk liquid, **mg/L** - $C_s - C_L$ = **Driving force** for mass transfer ### 2.2 Henry's Law — Linking Air to Water The saturation concentration $C_s$ is governed by **Henry's Law**: $$C_s = K_H^{cp} \cdot P_{O_2}$$ At **20 °C, 1 atm, fresh water**: $C_{s,20} = \mathbf{9.17 \text{ mg/L}}$ (oxygen partial pressure = 20.9 % of 1 atm). > [!NOTE] > **Temperature Effect on $C_s$ (KTU Empirical):** > $C_s$ **decreases** as temperature increases (oxygen is less soluble in hot water). This is why hot food industry effluents (e.g., brewery stillage at 60–80 °C) require **larger aeration capacity** initially, though biological activity is also temperature-dependent. ### 2.3 Classification of Aeration Devices (KTU 2024 Module 3) #### A. **Mechanical (Surface) Aerators** 1. **Vertical Shaft Surface Aerators** — e.g., Simplex/Philadelphia cone 2. **Horizontal Shaft (Brush) Aerators** — e.g., Mammoth Rotor / Kessener Brush 3. **Submerged Turbine Aerators** — combined mechanical + diffused air #### B. **Diffused (Submerged) Air Aerators** 1. **Fine Bubble Diffusers** — ceramic dome, ceramic disc, EPDM membrane 2. **Medium Bubble Diffusers** — sleeve diffusers 3. **Coarse Bubble Diffusers** — perforated pipe, sparger #### C. **Combined / Hybrid Systems** - Jet aerators, U-tube aerators, Venturi aerators ### 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet (Exam-Ready Cheat Sheet) > [!IMPORTANT] > **All formulas below are KTU Board-Exam high-yield — memorize these tables.** | # | Parameter | Formula | Units | Notes | |---|---|---|---|---| | 1 | **Oxygen Transfer Rate** | $r_O = K_La(C_s - C_L)$ | $\text{kg O}_2/\text{m}^3 \cdot \text{h}$ | Two-film model | | 2 | **Standard OTR (SOTR)** | $SOTR = K_{La,s}(C_{s,20} - 0)$ | $\text{kg O}_2/\text{h}$ | At standard conditions | | 3 | **Standard Oxygen Transfer Efficiency** | $SOTE = \dfrac{SOTR}{W_{O_2,in}}$ | % or fraction | kg O₂ transferred / kg O₂ supplied | | 4 | **Actual OTR (AOTR)** | $AOTR = \alpha \cdot F \cdot SOTR \cdot \dfrac{\beta C_{s,20} - C_L}{C_{s,20}} \cdot \theta^{T-20}$ | $\text{kg O}_2/\text{h}$ | Field correction | | 5 | **Alpha factor ($\alpha$)** | $\alpha = \dfrac{K_{La,field}}{K_{La,tap}}$ | dimensionless | **Typically 0.6 – 0.9** | | 6 | **Beta factor ($\beta$)** | $\beta = \dfrac{C_{s,field}}{C_{s,tap}}$ | dimensionless | **Typically 0.7 – 0.95** | | 7 | **Fouling factor (F)** | $F = 1$ (clean), $F < 1$ (fouled) | dimensionless | **0.65 – 1.0** | | 8 | **Temperature correction ($\theta$)** | $\theta^{T-20}$ with $\theta = 1.024$ | dimensionless | **Standard KTU value** | | 9 | **Aeration period** | $t_a = \dfrac{V}{Q}$ | hours | Retention time in aeration tank | | 10 | **Air requirement** | $A = \dfrac{AOTR}{SOTE \cdot \rho_{O_2}}$ | $\text{m}^3\text{/h}$ or $\text{Nm}^3\text{/h}$ | Actual air volume needed | | 11 | **Power input (mechanical)** | $P = \dfrac{SOTR}{SOTE_{power}}$ | kW | Power per OTE rating | | 12 | **Oxygen demand** | $O_2 = a \cdot S_0 + b \cdot X$ | $\text{kg O}_2/\text{d}$ | Activated sludge design | | 13 | **Specific power input** | $P/V$ | $\text{W/m}^3$ | **Typical: 10 – 40 W/m³** | | 14 | **Henry's Law** | $C_s = K_H^{cp} \cdot P_{O_2}$ | mg/L | Gas partial pressure model | > [!WARNING] > **KTU Pipe-Character Rule:** In your answer sheet, write absolute values as $\vert x \vert$ or $\lvert x \rvert$ — never as `|x|` which confuses the cell separator. (Also applies in markdown tables above.) ### 2.5 Why These Concepts Matter in Food Industry Engineering - **Process Engineering:** Sizing of aeration tanks for food effluent treatment plants. - **Energy Engineering:** Aeration energy is the **single largest operating cost** in biological treatment. - **Environmental Compliance:** Food industry effluents often have BOD > 2,000 mg/L, requiring **high-rate aeration**. - **Equipment Selection:** Fine bubble diffusers save ~50 % energy vs. coarse bubble, justifying higher capex. ## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ### 3.1 Derivation: From Two-Film Model to Standard OTR **Step 1 — Molecular Diffusion across a Film (Fick's First Law):** The flux of oxygen through a stagnant liquid film of thickness $\delta_L$ is: $$N_A = -D_L \cdot \frac{dC}{dx}$$ **Step 2 — Steady-State Across Liquid Film:** For a planar film from $x = 0$ (interface, concentration $C_s$) to $x = \delta_L$ (bulk, concentration $C_L$): $$\int_{C_s}^{C_L} dC = -\int_0^{\delta_L} \frac{N_A}{D_L} dx$$ $$C_L - C_s = -\frac{N_A \cdot \delta_L}{D_L}$$ $$N_A = \frac{D_L}{\delta_L}(C_s - C_L)$$ **Step 3 — Define Liquid-Side Mass Transfer Coefficient:** $$k_L = \frac{D_L}{\delta_L}$$ So the local flux becomes: $N_A = k_L(C_s - C_L)$ **Step 4 — Convert to Volumetric Basis Using Interfacial Area 'a':** For an aeration volume $V$ with total interfacial area $A = a \cdot V$: $$\dot{m}_{O_2} = N_A \cdot a \cdot V = (k_L \cdot a)(C_s - C_L) \cdot V$$ Dividing by $V$: $$\frac{\dot{m}_{O_2}}{V} = k_L \cdot a \cdot (C_s - C_L)$$ **Step 5 — Define the Volumetric Mass Transfer Coefficient:** $$\boxed{K_La = k_L \cdot a}$$ Thus the final form: $$r_O = K_La \cdot (C_s - C_L) \quad \text{(kg O}_2\text{/m}^3\text{·h)}$$ > [!NOTE] > **Why $K_La$ is treated as a single lumped parameter?** > In real reactors, both $k_L$ (function of turbulence, diffusivity) and $a$ (function of bubble size, holdup) are extremely difficult to measure independently. The product $K_La$ is **experimentally measured** via off-gas analysis or unsteady DO probe methods. --- ### 3.2 Derivation: Conversion from SOTR to AOTR (Field Conditions) **Step 1 — Start with SOTR definition (zero DO baseline, 20 °C, tap water):** $$SOTR = K_{La,s} \cdot C_{s,20}$$ **Step 2 — Field $K_La$ uses alpha factor (wastewater turbulence differs from tap water):** $$K_{La,field} = \alpha \cdot K_{La,s}$$ **Step 3 — Field saturation uses beta factor (salinity/TDS reduces $C_s$):** $$C_{s,field} = \beta \cdot C_{s,20}$$ **Step 4 — Field DO is non-zero (operational value $C_L$):** Driving force becomes $\beta C_{s,20} - C_L$. **Step 5 — Fouling reduces effective transfer:** Multiply by $F$ (fouling factor, $F \le 1$). **Step 6 — Temperature correction for kinetic mismatch:** Use the **Arrhenius-type** correction: $\theta^{T-20}$, with $\theta = 1.024$. **Step 7 — Final AOTR Equation:** $$\boxed{AOTR = \alpha \cdot F \cdot SOTR \cdot \left( \frac{\beta C_{s,20} - C_L}{C_{s,20}} \right) \cdot \theta^{T-20}}$$ Where: - $T$ = operating temperature (°C) - $C_{s,20} = 9.17 \text{ mg/L}$ (at 20 °C, 1 atm) --- ### 3.3 Worked Example — KTU Board Pattern > [!IMPORTANT] > **Numerical Problem (Module 3 Examination Style):** > > A **dairy industry** operates an activated sludge plant with the following data: > - Wastewater flow: $Q = 500 \text{ m}^3/\text{day}$ > - Influent BOD: $S_0 = 1{,}500 \text{ mg/L}$ > - Effluent BOD: $S = 30 \text{ mg/L}$ > - MLVSS: $X = 2{,}500 \text{ mg/L}$ > - Influent BOD load: $F/M = 0.30 \text{ d}^{-1}$ > - Aeration tank volume: $V = 1{,}250 \text{ m}^3$ > - Operating temperature: $T = 25 \text{ °C}$ > - Operating DO: $C_L = 2.0 \text{ mg/L}$ > - Aeration device: Fine-bubble diffuser with $SOTR = 25 \text{ kg O}_2/\text{h}$ at $SOTE = 30\%$ > - Site correction: $\alpha = 0.85$, $\beta = 0.95$, $F = 0.9$ > > **Required:** > 1. Calculate the AOTR needed. > 2. Calculate the required air flow rate. > 3. Calculate the specific power input if blower power is 12 kW. --- #### Solution **Part (1) — AOTR Computation:** **Step A — Calculate SOTR baseline saturation:** $$C_{s,20} = 9.17 \text{ mg/L}$$ **Step B — Field saturation:** $$\beta \cdot C_{s,20} = 0.95 \times 9.17 = 8.7115 \text{ mg/L}$$ **Step C — Driving force ratio:** $$\frac{\beta C_{s,20} - C_L}{C_{s,20}} = \frac{8.7115 - 2.0}{9.17} = \frac{6.7115}{9.17} = 0.7319$$ **Step D — Temperature correction ($\theta = 1.024$, $T - 20 = 5$):** $$\theta^{T-20} = (1.024)^5$$ $$1.024^2 = 1.048576$$ $$1.024^4 = (1.048576)^2 = 1.09951$$ $$1.024^5 = 1.09951 \times 1.024 = 1.12590$$ **Step E — Combined correction factor:** $$\alpha \cdot F \cdot \left(\frac{\beta C_{s,20} - C_L}{C_{s,20}}\right) \cdot \theta^{T-20}$$ $$= 0.85 \times 0.9 \times 0.7319 \times 1.12590$$ Step-by-step: - $0.85 \times 0.9 = 0.765$ - $0.765 \times 0.7319 = 0.5599$ - $0.5599 \times 1.12590 = 0.6304$ **Step F — AOTR:** $$AOTR = 0.6304 \times 25 = 15.76 \text{ kg O}_2/\text{h}$$ Converting to daily demand: $$AOTR_{daily} = 15.76 \times 24 = 378.24 \text{ kg O}_2/\text{day}$$ **Part (2) — Air Flow Rate:** Oxygen fraction in air = **23.2 % by mass** (i.e., per kg air, 0.232 kg O₂). Density of O₂ at STP = **1.429 kg/Nm³**. Oxygen supplied per Nm³ of air = $0.232 \times 1.429 = 0.3315 \text{ kg O}_2/\text{Nm}^3$ Alternatively, oxygen mass fraction in air ≈ 0.2314 (23.14 % by mass). **Step A — Oxygen supplied per unit air:** $$\rho_{O_2,in} = 0.2314 \times 1.429 = 0.3307 \text{ kg O}_2/\text{Nm}^3$$ **Step B — Apply SOTE:** $$SOTE = 0.30$$ **Step C — Air volume required:** $$A = \frac{AOTR}{SOTE \cdot \rho_{O_2,in}} = \frac{15.76}{0.30 \times 0.3307}$$ Denominator: $0.30 \times 0.3307 = 0.09921$ $$A = \frac{15.76}{0.09921} = 158.85 \text{ Nm}^3/\text{h}$$ Per day: $158.85 \times 24 = 3{,}812.4 \text{ Nm}^3/\text{day}$ **Part (3) — Specific Power Input:** $$P/V = \frac{12{,}000 \text{ W}}{1{,}250 \text{ m}^3} = 9.6 \text{ W/m}^3$$ > [!NOTE] > **Engineering Interpretation:** > A $P/V$ of 9.6 W/m³ is in the lower range (typical: 10–40 W/m³). This indicates the fine-bubble diffuser system is **energy-efficient**, which is appropriate for a dairy effluent with moderate BOD. --- ### 3.4 Analytical Matrix — Aeration Device Performance Comparison | Device Type | $SOTE$ (%) | $K_La$ (h⁻¹) | Power/Unit Area (W/m²) | Energy Eff. | Fouling Risk | Capital Cost | |---|---|---|---|---|---|---| | **Coarse Bubble** | 6 – 12 | 1 – 4 | 30 – 60 | Low | Very Low | **Low** | | **Medium Bubble** | 12 – 20 | 3 – 8 | 25 – 50 | Moderate | Low | Low–Medium | | **Fine Bubble (Disc)** | 25 – 40 | 6 – 20 | 15 – 30 | **High** | Medium–High | Medium | | **Fine Bubble (Tube)** | 30 – 50 | 8 – 25 | 12 – 25 | **Very High** | High | Medium–High | | **Vertical Shaft Surface** | 20 – 35 | 4 – 12 | N/A (W/m³ basis) | Moderate | **Very Low** | Medium | | **Horizontal Brush** | 25 – 40 | 5 – 15 | N/A | High | Low | Medium | | **Submerged Turbine** | 20 – 30 | 4 – 10 | 30 – 50 | Moderate | Low | **High** | | **Jet Aerator** | 25 – 35 | 5 – 12 | 40 – 70 | Moderate | Low | **High** | ## 4.
