KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each) #### Question 1 [KTU University Exam - May 2024] **Q: Define Standard Oxygen Transfer Rate (SOTR). List the standard reference conditions used for its evaluation.** **Model Answer:** **SOTR Definition:** The Standard Oxygen Transfer Rate is the mass of oxygen transferred per unit time from air to water under standard reference conditions. **Standard Reference Conditions:** 1. **Temperature:** $T_s = 20 \text{ °C}$ 2. **Pressure:** $P = 1 \text{ atm}$ (**101.325 kPa**) 3. **Dissolved Oxygen:** $C_L = 0 \text{ mg/L}$ 4. **Liquid medium:** Tap water (no salinity/impurities) **Mathematical Expression:** $$SOTR = K_{La,s} \cdot C_{s,20}$$ where $C_{s,20} = 9.17 \text{ mg/L}$ at 20 °C, 1 atm. **[3 marks: 1 for definition, 1 for conditions, 1 for expression]** --- #### Question 2 [KTU University Exam - Dec 2023] **Q: Differentiate between fine bubble and coarse bubble diffusers based on SOTE, fouling tendency, and typical applications.** **Model Answer:** | Parameter | **Fine Bubble** | **Coarse Bubble** | |---|---|---| | **Bubble size** | **1 – 3 mm** | **6 – 10 mm** | | **$SOTE$** | **25 – 50 %** | **6 – 12 %** | | **$K_La$** | **6 – 25 h⁻¹** | **1 – 4 h⁻¹** | | **Fouling tendency** | **High** (small pores clog) | **Very Low** | | **Clogging resistance** | Needs regular cleaning | Robust | | **Energy efficiency** | **High** (saves ~50 % energy) | **Low** | | **Application** | Activated sludge, MBR | Grit chambers, equalization, high-solids streams | | **Capital cost** | Medium–High | **Low** | | **Maintenance** | High (fouling, membrane rupture) | Very Low | **[3 marks: 1 each for SOTE/fouling/application]** --- ### 5.2 Part B — 14-Mark Questions (Module Internal Choice) --- #### **Question A (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam - May 2024]** **A. (a)** Explain the **Lewis-Whitman Two-Film Theory** of oxygen transfer from air to water. With a neat sketch, derive the expression for the oxygen transfer rate $r_O = K_La(C_s - C_L)$. **[7 Marks]** **Model Solution:** **Step 1 — Conceptual Basis:** The Two-Film Theory (1924) postulates that whenever a gas and a liquid are in contact, two stagnant films exist at the interface — a **gas film** and a **liquid film**. Mass transfer occurs by molecular diffusion across these films. **Step 2 — Identification of Controlling Resistance:** The resistance in the gas film is neglected for sparingly soluble gases like O₂. The **liquid film resistance** is rate-controlling. **Step 3 — Fick's First Law Application:** For steady-state diffusion across the liquid film of thickness $\delta_L$: $$N_A = -D_L \cdot \frac{dC}{dx} = \frac{D_L}{\delta_L}(C_s - C_L)$$ **Step 4 — Define Local Mass Transfer Coefficient:** $$k_L = \frac{D_L}{\delta_L} \Rightarrow N_A = k_L(C_s - C_L)$$ **Step 5 — Scale to Reactor Volume:** For interfacial area $A = a \cdot V$: $$\dot{m}_{O_2} = k_L(C_s - C_L) \cdot a \cdot V$$ **Step 6 — Define Volumetric Coefficient $K_La = k_L \cdot a$:** $$r_O = \frac{\dot{m}_{O_2}}{V} = K_La(C_s - C_L) \quad \text{kg O}_2/\text{m}^3 \cdot \text{h}$$ **Sketch Description (for examiner credit):** A vertical diagram with three layers — gas phase on top, gas film (thin), interface (dashed line), liquid film (thin), bulk liquid (large). Arrows showing $C_s$ at interface, $C_L$ in bulk, gradient across $\delta_L$. **Label the rate-controlling liquid film in red.** **Significance in Food Industry:** - F&B wastewaters (dairy, brewery) often have surface-active compounds (proteins, fats) that **increase** liquid film thickness, **reducing** $k_L$. - This is captured quantitatively by the **alpha factor $\alpha$** in field conditions. **[7 marks: 2 for theory, 3 for derivation, 1 for sketch, 1 for industrial significance]** --- **A. (b)** A **palm oil mill effluent (POME)** treatment plant has the following data: Aeration tank volume = **800 m³**, $K_La$ (field) = **3.5 h⁻¹**, $C_{s,20} = 9.17$ mg/L, $C_L = 1.5$ mg/L, temperature = **30 °C**, $\beta = 0.92$. Calculate the **actual oxygen transfer rate (AOTR)** in kg O₂/h. **[7 Marks]** **Model Solution:** **Given Data:** - $V = 800 \text{ m}^3$ - $K_{La,field} = 3.5 \text{ h}^{-1}$ - $C_{s,20} = 9.17 \text{ mg/L}$ - $C_L = 1.5 \text{ mg/L}$ - $T = 30 \text{ °C}$, $\beta = 0.92$ **Step 1 — Field Saturation Concentration:** $$C_{s,field} = \beta \cdot C_{s,20} = 0.92 \times 9.17 = 8.4364 \text{ mg/L}$$ **Step 2 — Temperature Correction on $K_La$:** $$\theta^{T-20} = 1.024^{30-20} = 1.024^{10}$$ Computing: $$1.024^2 = 1.048576$$ $$1.024^4 = 1.09951$$ $$1.024^5 = 1.12590$$ $$1.024^{10} = (1.024^5)^2 = 1.12590^2 = 1.26765$$ **Step 3 — Corrected $K_La$ at 30 °C:** $$K_{La,30} = K_{La,field} \times \theta^{T-20} = 3.5 \times 1.26765 = 4.4368 \text{ h}^{-1}$$ **Step 4 — Driving Force:** $$C_{s,field} - C_L = 8.4364 - 1.5 = 6.9364 \text{ mg/L}$$ **Step 5 — Volumetric OTR:** $$r_O = K_{La,30} \cdot (C_{s,field} - C_L) = 4.4368 \times 6.9364 = 30.776 \text{ mg O}_2/\text{L} \cdot \text{h}$$ Converting units (1 mg/L = 1 g/m³ = $10^{-3}$ kg/m³): $$r_O = 30.776 \times 10^{-3} \text{ kg O}_2/\text{m}^3 \cdot \text{h} = 0.03078 \text{ kg O}_2/\text{m}^3 \cdot \text{h}$$ **Step 6 — Total AOTR:** $$AOTR = r_O \times V = 0.03078 \times 800 = \boxed{24.62 \text{ kg O}_2/\text{h}}$$ **Per day:** $AOTR = 24.62 \times 24 = 590.88 \text{ kg O}_2/\text{day}$ **Engineering Comment:** This high OTR is consistent with POME treatment, which has very high BOD (25,000 mg/L) and requires intensive aeration in the lagoon/closed reactor system. **[7 marks: 1 for setup, 2 for temperature correction, 2 for driving force, 2 for final calculation with unit conversion]** --- #### **Question B (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam - Dec 2023] — Alternative Choice** **B. (a)** Classify aeration devices used in food industry wastewater treatment. Compare **mechanical surface aerators** and **diffused air systems** with respect to oxygen transfer efficiency, energy consumption, and applications. **[7 Marks]** **Model Solution:** **Classification of Aeration Devices:** **1. Mechanical (Surface) Aerators:** - **Vertical shaft** (Simplex/Philadelphia cone): rotates impeller at surface - **Horizontal shaft (Brush/Rotor):** rotating brushes partially submerged - **Submerged turbine:** impeller with diffuser below **2. Diffused (Submerged) Air Aerators:** - **Fine bubble** (ceramic disc, EPDM membrane) - **Medium bubble** (sleeve diffusers) - **Coarse bubble** (perforated pipe, spargers) **3. Combined / Specialty:** - **Jet aerators, U-tube aerators, Venturi** **Comparison Table:** | Criterion | **Mechanical Surface Aerator** | **Diffused Air System** | |---|---|---| | **$SOTE$** | 20 – 40 % | 6 – 50 % (varies with bubble size) | | **Energy form** | Mechanical (direct drive) | Electrical → Blower → Air | | **Power source** | Direct motor coupling | Blower + air distribution | | **Oxygen transfer mechanism** | Surface renewal / hydraulic shear | Bubbles rising through water column | | **Tank geometry flexibility** | Limited (needs depth ≥ 3 m) | **High** (any shape) | | **Fouling risk** | **Very low** | Fine bubble: high; Coarse: low | | **Capital cost** | Moderate | Fine bubble: high; Coarse: low | | **Operating cost** | Moderate (mechanical wear) | **Fine bubble: lowest** (energy efficient) | | **Maintenance** | Gearbox, bearings | Diffuser cleaning, blower service | | **Application in food industry** | Lagoon systems, large dairy plants | Activated sludge, MBR, compact plants | | **Cold effluent performance** | Poor (ice formation) | Good | | **Hot effluent performance** | Moderate (60–80 °C brewery stillage OK) | **Good** (no surface losses) | | **DO control precision** | Moderate (VFD) | **High** (blower VFD + DO loop) | **Selection Criterion for Food Industry:** - **High BOD + Hot effluent** (brewery/distillery) → Coarse bubble submerged or jet aerator - **Moderate BOD + Large footprint available** (dairy/cannery) → Surface aerator - **Energy-conscious + Moderate BOD** (sugar/starch) → **Fine bubble diffuser with VFD-controlled blower** **[7 marks: 2 for classification, 4 for comparison table, 1 for selection guideline]** --- **B. (b)** A **distillery spent wash (DSW)** is treated in an aerobic digester. The following data are given: - $\alpha = 0.80$, $\beta = 0.90$, $F = 0.85$, $SOTR = 40 \text{ kg O}_2/\text{h}$, $C_{s,20} = 9.17$ mg/L, $C_L = 1.8$ mg/L, $T = 28 \text{ °C}$, $SOTE = 28\%$ - Find: **(i)** AOTR, **(ii)** Air flow rate in Nm³/h, **(iii)** Number of fine-bubble disc diffusers if each delivers **0.35 Nm³/h**. **[7 Marks]** **Model Solution:** **Given Data:** - $\alpha = 0.80$, $\beta = 0.90$, $F = 0.85$, $SOTR = 40 \text{ kg O}_2/\text{h}$ - $C_{s,20} = 9.17 \text{ mg/L}$, $C_L = 1.8 \text{ mg/L}$, $T = 28 \text{ °C}$, $SOTE = 28\%$ --- **Part (i) — AOTR:** **Step 1 — Field saturation:** $$C_{s,field} = 0.90 \times 9.17 = 8.253 \text{ mg/L}$$ **Step 2 — Driving force ratio:** $$\frac{\beta C_{s,20} - C_L}{C_{s,20}} = \frac{8.253 - 1.8}{9.17} = \frac{6.453}{9.17} = 0.7038$$ **Step 3 — Temperature correction ($T - 20 = 8$):** $$\theta^{T-20} = 1.024^8$$ $$1.024^4 = 1.09951$$ $$1.024^8 = (1.09951)^2 = 1.20893$$ **Step 4 — Correction factor:** $$CF = \alpha \cdot F \cdot 0.7038 \cdot 1.20893$$ $$CF = 0.80 \times 0.85 = 0.68$$ $$CF = 0.68 \times 0.7038 = 0.4786$$ $$CF = 0.4786 \times 1.20893 = 0.5786$$ **Step 5 — AOTR:** $$AOTR = 0.5786 \times 40 = 23.14 \text{ kg O}_2/\text{h}$$ --- **Part (ii) — Air Flow Rate:** **Step 1 — Oxygen in air:** - Mass fraction of O₂ in air = 0.2314 - Density of O₂ at STP = 1.429 kg/Nm³ - O₂ per Nm³ air = $0.2314 \times 1.429 = 0.3307 \text{ kg O}_2/\text{Nm}^3$ **Step 2 — Air required:** $$A = \frac{AOTR}{SOTE \cdot \rho_{O_2,in}} = \frac{23.14}{0.28 \times 0.3307} = \frac{23.14}{0.0926} = 249.9 \text{ Nm}^3/\text{h}$$ --- **Part (iii) — Number of Diffusers:** $$N = \frac{A}{q_{diffuser}} = \frac{249.9}{0.35} = 714 \text{ diffusers}$$ **Practical Consideration:** Round up to **720 diffusers** (with 6 spare for maintenance rotation). --- **Engineering Insight:** Distillery spent wash is **dark-colored, high-temperature, and high-organic-strength** (BOD often > 50,000 mg/L). The high number of fine-bubble diffusers reflects the **high OTR demand** (~23 kg O₂/h) required for the aerobic biological oxidation of melanoidin pigments, organic acids, and residual sugars. **[7 marks: 2 for AOTR, 2 for air flow, 2 for diffuser count, 1 for engineering comment]** --- > [!WARNING] > **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls Where Students Lose Marks:** > > 1. **Unit Conversion Errors (–2 marks):** Forgetting to convert mg/L → kg/m³ (factor of $10^{-3}$). AOTR is often in mg/L·h but should be in kg/m³·h or kg/h. Always state units explicitly. > > 2. **Confusion Between SOTR and AOTR (–2 marks):** SOTR uses $C_{s,20}$ and $C = 0$; AOTR uses field conditions with all four correction factors. Students often miss the $\theta^{T-20}$ term. > > 3. **Neglecting $C_L$ in Driving Force (–1 mark):** Some students compute $C_s - 0$ instead of $C_s - C_L$ for AOTR. This inflates AOTR by 20–40 %. > > 4. **Forgetting to Convert SOTE to Fraction (–1 mark):** SOTE must be in decimal (0.28) not percentage (28) for unit consistency. > > 5. **Ignoring Fouling Factor $F$ (–1 mark):** $F$ is explicitly required in KTU K-Tier questions on diffused aeration. > > 6. **Bubble Diffuser Count Rounding (–1 mark):** Always round **up** to nearest multiple of header pipe layout (e.g., 8 or 12 per pipe). Provide 5–10 % spares. > > 7. **Using $\theta = 1.02$ Instead of $1.024$ (–0.5 marks):** KTU strictly uses $\theta = 1.024$ for oxygen transfer. --- ### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember > [!TIP] > **⚡ KTU Rapid Revision Checklist — Aeration Devices (Module 3)** > > **Core Concepts (MUST KNOW):** > - ☐ Two-Film Theory of Lewis & Whitman (1924) — liquid film is rate-controlling > - ☐ Basic OTR equation: $r_O = K_La(C_s - C_L)$ > - ☐ Henry's Law: $C_s = K_H^{cp} \cdot P_{O_2}$ > - ☐ Standard conditions: **20 °C, 1 atm, $C = 0$**, tap water → SOTR > > **Field Corrections (HIGH-YIELD for Part B):** > - ☐ $\alpha$ (alpha) — for **wastewater vs. tap water** $K_La$ ratio (0.6–0.9) > - ☐ $\beta$ (beta) — for **salinity/TDS effect** on $C_s$ (0.7–0.95) > - ☐ $F$ — **fouling factor** (0.65–1.0) > - ☐ $\theta^{T-20}$ — **temperature** correction with $\theta = 1.024$ > > **Equipment Knowledge (IMPORTANT):** > - ☐ Fine bubble diffuser: SOTE = 25–50 %, high fouling, high efficiency > - ☐ Coarse bubble diffuser: SOTE = 6–12 %, low fouling, robust > - ☐ Vertical shaft surface aerator: 20–35 % SOTE, lagoon use > - ☐ Brush aerator: 25–40 % SOTE, good for oxidation ditches > > **Numerical Skills (MUST PRACTICE):** > - ☐ Convert SOTR to AOTR with all 4 correction factors > - ☐ Calculate air flow rate using $A = AOTR / (SOTE \cdot \rho_{O_2})$ > - ☐ Count diffusers from total air flow > - ☐ Compute specific power input $P/V$ in W/m³ > > **Food Industry Context (REMEMBER):** > - ☐ Dairy, brewery, distillery, POME = **high BOD** → high-rate aeration > - ☐ Hot effluents (brewery) → diffused air preferred > - ☐ Aeration = 45–75 % of plant energy cost > - ☐ Aeration tanks typically operate at **DO = 1.5–2.0 mg/L** > - ☐ $F/M$ ratio of **0.2–0.5 d⁻¹** for conventional activated sludge > > **Common KTU Numbers to Memorize:** > - ☐ $C_{s,20} = 9.17$ mg/L > - ☐ $\theta = 1.024$ > - ☐ O₂ mass fraction in air = 0.2314 > - ☐ $\rho_{O_2,STP} = 1.429$ kg/Nm³ > - ☐ Typical $P/V = 10$–$40$ W/m³ > - ☐ Air requirement for activated sludge: **30–80 Nm³ air/kg BOD removed**

topics.title: Effect on characteristic parameters of effluents in treatments using lagoons
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEFTT744 - FOOD INDUSTRY WASTE MANAGEMENT
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Effect on characteristic parameters of effluents in treatments using lagoons

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Lagoon Treatment System (Stabilization Pond):** A controlled, earthen or engineered impoundment designed to treat biodegradable organic wastewater through the synergistic action of **bacteria, algae, and other microorganisms**, leveraging natural physical, chemical, and biological processes (sedimentation, photosynthesis, microbial degradation, and solar radiation) under ambient climatic conditions.

In the context of **Food Industry Waste Management**, lagoons represent one of the most widely adopted secondary biological treatment units for high-strength organic effluents generated by dairy, meat processing, fruit and vegetable canning, brewery, distillery, sugar, and seafood processing industries. They serve as a **low-energy, low-cost, robust** treatment alternative suited to seasonal and continuous food processing operations.

## 1.2 Types of Lagoons (KTU Syllabus Focus)

Lagoons are broadly classified into three functional categories, each producing a characteristic effect on effluent parameters:

| Lagoon Type | Dissolved Oxygen (DO) Status | Primary Mechanism | Food Industry Application |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Aerobic (Maturation Pond)** | High DO (> 2 mg/L) throughout | Algal-bacterial symbiosis | Polishing tertiary treatment, pathogen removal |
| **Facultative Pond** | DO present in upper aerobic zone, anaerobic at bottom | Surface aeration + bottom sludge digestion | Most common for food industry effluents |
| **Anaerobic (Digestion Pond)** | Anaerobic throughout depth | Methanogenic fermentation | High-strength dairy, distillery, sugar effluents |
| **Aerated Lagoon** | Forced aeration (mechanical) | Mechanical oxygen transfer | High-rate treatment, smaller footprint |

## 1.3 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **Intuitive Analogy — "The Natural Pond as a Treatment Factory"**
> Imagine a village pond used for centuries to dispose of biodegradable waste. Over time, the pond develops a green surface (algae), a clear middle layer (clean water), and black sludge at the bottom (anaerobic digestion). This is exactly how a **facultative lagoon** works! The algae on top release oxygen (via photosynthesis) for aerobic bacteria, which in turn eat the organic waste and release CO₂ for the algae. The bottom sludge ferments without oxygen, breaking down complex organics into biogas (methane + CO₂). **Lagoons are nature's slow but thorough wastewater treatment plant.**

## 1.4 Characteristic Parameters of Effluents — Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Effluent Characteristic Parameters:** The measurable physical, chemical, and biological attributes of wastewater that quantify its pollution load, treatment efficiency, and suitability for discharge. The principal parameters are:
> - **BOD (Biochemical Oxygen Demand)** — measure of biodegradable organics
> - **COD (Chemical Oxygen Demand)** — measure of total oxidizable organics
> - **TSS (Total Suspended Solids)** — measure of particulate matter
> - **pH** — measure of hydrogen ion concentration
> - **DO (Dissolved Oxygen)** — measure of available molecular oxygen
> - **Nutrients** — Nitrogen (N), Phosphorus (P)
> - **Pathogens** — Fecal coliforms, E. coli
> - **Color, Odor, Temperature, Turbidity**

These parameters are the **"vital signs"** of any wastewater, and lagoons alter them in specific, measurable ways.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Working Principle of Lagoons — Structured Logic

The treatment mechanism in lagoons can be broken into **five sequential and simultaneous physical-chemical-biological steps**:

1. **Step 1 — Physical Sedimentation:** Suspended solids settle out by gravity at the inlet and bottom zones. This reduces **TSS** substantially.
2. **Step 2 — Aerobic Degradation (Upper Zones):** Algae photosynthesize using sunlight, releasing O₂. Aerobic bacteria utilize this O₂ to oxidize dissolved organics into CO₂ + H₂O. This reduces **BOD and COD** and raises **DO**.
3. **Step 3 — Anaerobic Digestion (Bottom Zone):** Stratification creates an anaerobic sludge layer where methanogens convert complex organics into CH₄ + CO₂. This achieves **high organic removal without external energy** for high-strength effluents.
4. **Step 4 — Nutrient Assimilation:** Nitrogen and phosphorus are assimilated by algae and bacteria into biomass. Ammonia is stripped via volatilization (high pH) or converted to nitrate (nitrification).
5. **Step 5 — Pathogen Reduction:** Long detention times, sunlight (UV), high pH, and natural die-off reduce coliforms by 90–99.9%.

> [!NOTE]
> **Why Lagoons Are Pivotal for Food Industries:** Food industry effluents have high BOD (500–10,000 mg/L), high variability in flow and load, and are largely biodegradable. Lagoons handle these **shock loads** gracefully because of their long residence time and self-buffering ecology.

## 2.2 Effect on Each Characteristic Parameter

### 2.2.1 Effect on BOD (Biochemical Oxygen Demand)

- **Aerobic Pond:** 80–95% BOD reduction
- **Facultative Pond:** 60–85% BOD reduction
- **Anaerobic Pond:** 50–75% BOD reduction (but at much higher organic loading)
- **Aerated Lagoon:** 80–90% BOD reduction

The BOD removal follows first-order kinetics:

$$L_t = L_0 \cdot e^{-k \cdot t}$$

where **L_t** is residual BOD (mg/L) at time *t*, **L₀** is initial BOD, **k** is first-order rate constant (day⁻¹), and **t** is detention time (days).

### 2.2.2 Effect on COD (Chemical Oxygen Demand)

- COD reduction is typically **less than BOD reduction** because lagoons preferentially remove biodegradable organics.
- BOD/COD ratio of treated effluent often **decreases**, indicating that recalcitrant compounds remain.
- **Distillery and dairy lagoons** can achieve 70–85% COD removal.

### 2.2.3 Effect on TSS (Total Suspended Solids)

- Settleable solids are reduced by **70–90%** via sedimentation.
- However, lagoons can **INCREASE effluent TSS** if algal cells escape in the effluent (algal washout), which is a key design challenge in maturation ponds.
- The trade-off: a clear effluent may have high algal TSS.

### 2.2.4 Effect on pH

- Algal photosynthesis consumes CO₂, **raising pH to 9–10.5** during daylight in aerobic/maturation ponds.
- This high pH is **beneficial** for ammonia stripping and pathogen kill.
- **Anaerobic lagoons** typically have **pH 6.5–7.5** (slightly acidic due to VFA accumulation).
- The diurnal pH swing in facultative lagoons can be 1–2 units.

### 2.2.5 Effect on DO (Dissolved Oxygen)

- **Aerobic ponds:** DO = 4–8 mg/L (day), drops to 0–2 mg/L (night) due to algal respiration.
- **Facultative ponds:** DO gradient — surface 4–6 mg/L, bottom 0 mg/L.
- **Anaerobic ponds:** DO = 0 mg/L throughout.
- **Aerated lagoons:** DO maintained at 1–2 mg/L by mechanical aeration.

### 2.2.6 Effect on Nutrients (N & P)

- **Nitrogen removal:** 30–80% (via ammonia volatilization, algal uptake, nitrification-denitrification).
- **Phosphorus removal:** 20–60% (via algal uptake, precipitation at high pH).
- Lagoons are **not designed for nutrient removal** — they are weak in N and P control.

### 2.2.7 Effect on Pathogens

- **Fecal coliform reduction:** 90–99.9% (1–3 log units).
- Mechanisms: solar UV, pH shock, predation, sedimentation, long HRT.
- **WHO guideline of < 1000 FC/100 mL** is achievable for irrigation reuse.

### 2.2.8 Effect on Color and Odor

- **Anaerobic lagoons** produce strong odors (H₂S, mercaptans, skatole) — this is a major drawback.
- **Facultative/aerobic lagoons** are largely odor-free.
- Color removal: 50–80% for food industry effluents (e.g., sugar, molasses).

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| Formula / Parameter | Expression | Units | Application |
|---|---|---|---|
| First-order BOD removal | $L_t = L_0 \cdot e^{-k \cdot t}$ | mg/L | Aerobic & facultative ponds |
| Surface BOD loading | $L_s = \dfrac{L_0 \cdot Q}{A}$ | kg BOD/ha·day | Facultative pond design |
| Volumetric BOD loading | $L_v = \dfrac{L_0 \cdot Q}{V}$ | kg BOD/m³·day | Aerobic pond design |
| Detention time | $\theta = \dfrac{V}{Q}$ | days | All lagoon types |
| Solar energy input | $I_s$ (function of latitude) | W/m² | Algal photosynthesis rate |
| Oxygen production by algae | $O_2 = 0.25 \cdot F_a$ | kg O₂/day | Where F_a is algal O₂ production |
| Photosynthetic oxygenation | $O_2 = f(I_s) \cdot C_a$ | kg O₂/day | Depends on light intensity |
| Areal organic removal rate | $A_{rem} = \dfrac{(L_0 - L_e) \cdot Q}{A}$ | kg BOD/ha·day | Performance evaluation |
| Removal efficiency | $\eta = \dfrac{(L_0 - L_e)}{L_0} \times 100$ | % | Performance evaluation |
| Wehner-Wilhelm equation | $\eta = 1 - \dfrac{1}{(1 + k\theta)^n}$ | dimensionless | Plug flow with dispersion |
| pH – alkalinity relationship | $pH = 6.35 + \log \dfrac{[HCO_3^-]}{[CO_2]}$ | — | CO₂ depletion effect |
| Ammonia stripping rate | $r_N = K_L \cdot a \cdot ([NH_3] - [NH_3]^*)$ | mg N/L·day | High pH lagoons |
| Coliform die-off (Chick's law) | $N_t = N_0 \cdot e^{-K_b \cdot t}$ | MPN/100 mL | Pathogen reduction |

> [!NOTE]
> **Important Boundary Conditions for KTU Examinations:**
> - Aerobic pond depth: 0.3 – 0.5 m (light penetration critical)
> - Facultative pond depth: 1.0 – 2.5 m
> - Anaerobic pond depth: 2.5 – 5.0 m
> - Aerated lagoon depth: 2.0 – 5.0 m
> - Detention time: 5 – 30 days (facultative), 20 – 50 days (anaerobic)
> - BOD loading (facultative): 20 – 100 kg BOD/ha·day
> - First-order k for BOD: 0.05 – 0.3 day⁻¹ (temperature dependent)

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

In India and globally, lagoons are the **backbone of food industry wastewater treatment**, especially for:
- **Dairy cooperatives** (Amul, Mother Dairy) — anaerobic + facultative
- **Distilleries** — anaerobic lagoons for high-strength spent wash (BOD ~ 50,000 mg/L)
- **Sugar mills** — seasonal lagoons operated during and off-season
- **Seafood processing** — coastal lagoons with marine discharge

The lagoons are also used as a **pre-treatment step** before activated sludge or membrane systems.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation of First-Order BOD Removal in a Complete-Mix Lagoon

### Step 1: Mass Balance Setup

Consider a complete-mix lagoon with volume **V (m³)** receiving a constant flow **Q (m³/day)** with influent BOD **L₀ (mg/L)**. The effluent BOD is **L (mg/L)**.

Write the steady-state mass balance for BOD across the lagoon:

$$Q \cdot L_0 - Q \cdot L - r \cdot V = 0$$

The first-order reaction rate for BOD utilization is:

$$r = k \cdot L$$

where **k** is the first-order rate constant (day⁻¹).

### Step 2: Substituting the Rate Expression

Substitute r = kL into the mass balance:

$$Q \cdot L_0 - Q \cdot L - k \cdot L \cdot V = 0$$

### Step 3: Define Detention Time

The hydraulic detention time is defined as:

$$\theta = \frac{V}{Q}$$

### Step 4: Rearrange for Effluent BOD

Divide the entire equation by Q:

$$L_0 - L - k \cdot L \cdot \theta = 0$$

Rearrange to isolate L on one side:

$$L_0 = L + k \cdot L \cdot \theta = L(1 + k\theta)$$

Therefore, the effluent BOD is:

$$L = \frac{L_0}{1 + k\theta}$$

### Step 5: Express Removal Efficiency

The fractional removal efficiency is:

$$\eta = \frac{L_0 - L}{L_0} = 1 - \frac{1}{1 + k\theta}$$

### Step 6: Temperature Correction for k

The rate constant k is temperature-dependent, governed by the van't Hoff-Arrhenius equation:

$$k_T = k_{20} \cdot \theta^{(T-20)}$$

where **θ ≈ 1.056** is the temperature coefficient. This is a key KTU-relevant correction.

## 3.2 Worked Numerical Example (KTU Style)

> **Problem:** A facultative lagoon treats dairy effluent with influent BOD = 1200 mg/L, flow = 800 m³/day, depth = 1.5 m, area = 12,000 m², k₂₀ = 0.15 day⁻¹. Calculate (i) detention time, (ii) effluent BOD, (iii) removal efficiency at 25°C, and (iv) surface BOD loading.

### Step 1: Calculate Detention Time

The volume V is:

$$V = A \cdot d = 12000 \times 1.5 = 18000 \text{ m}^3$$

The detention time is:

$$\theta = \frac{V}{Q} = \frac{18000}{800} = 22.5 \text{ days}$$

### Step 2: Temperature-Corrected k at 25°C

Apply the temperature correction formula with θ = 1.056:

$$k_{25} = 0.15 \times (1.056)^{(25-20)}$$

Calculate the exponentiation term:

$$(1.056)^5 = 1.314$$

Therefore:

$$k_{25} = 0.15 \times 1.314 = 0.1971 \text{ day}^{-1}$$

### Step 3: Effluent BOD

Using the complete-mix formula:

$$L_e = \frac{L_0}{1 + k\theta} = \frac{1200}{1 + (0.1971 \times 22.5)}$$

Calculate kθ:

$$k\theta = 0.1971 \times 22.5 = 4.435$$

Substitute:

$$L_e = \frac{1200}{1 + 4.435} = \frac{1200}{5.435} = 220.8 \text{ mg/L}$$

### Step 4: Removal Efficiency

$$\eta = \frac{1200 - 220.8}{1200} \times 100 = 81.6\%$$

### Step 5: Surface BOD Loading

Convert units carefully:

$$L_s = \frac{L_0 \cdot Q}{A} = \frac{1200 \times 800}{12000} = 80 \text{ g/m}^2 \cdot \text{day} = 800 \text{ kg/ha·day}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Interpretation:** The surface BOD loading of 800 kg BOD/ha·day exceeds the typical facultative pond design limit of 100 kg BOD/ha·day. This lagoon would suffer from anaerobicity and odor problems. The student should note the practical design constraint.

## 3.3 Analytical Matrix — Effect of Each Lagoon Type on Parameters

| Parameter | Influent (Typical Food Industry) | Aerobic Pond Effluent | Facultative Pond Effluent | Anaerobic Pond Effluent | Aerated Lagoon Effluent |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| **BOD (mg/L)** | 500–10,000 | 20–100 | 50–200 | 100–500 | 30–150 |
| **COD (mg/L)** | 1000–20,000 | 80–300 | 200–500 | 300–1500 | 100–400 |
| **TSS (mg/L)** | 200–2000 | 50–150 (algal) | 50–200 | 30–100 | 40–120 |
| **pH** | 4.5–9.0 | 8.5–10.5 | 7.0–8.5 | 6.5–7.5 | 6.8–7.8 |
| **DO (mg/L)** | 0–1 | 4–8 (day), 0–2 (night) | 0–6 (gradient) | 0 | 1–2 |
| **NH₃-N (mg/L)** | 20–200 | 1–10 | 5–30 | 20–80 | 5–25 |
| **Total P (mg/L)** | 10–100 | 2–20 | 5–30 | 10–50 | 5–25 |
| **Fecal Coliform (MPN/100 mL)** | 10⁶–10⁸ | 10²–10⁴ | 10³–10⁵ | 10⁴–10⁶ | 10³–10⁵ |
| **Odor** | Variable | None | Mild | Strong H₂S | Mild |
| **Color (Pt-Co)** | 200–2000 | 50–200 | 100–300 | 200–500 | 100–250 |
| **BOD Removal %** | — | 80–95% | 60–85% | 50–75% | 80–90% |
| **COD Removal %** | — | 70–85% | 50–75% | 40–70% | 75–85% |
| **TSS Removal %** | — | Variable (algal) | 70–90% | 80–95% | 80–95% |

## 3.4 Derivation of Pathogen Die-Off (Chick's Law)

The Chick's law for coliform die-off in lagoons:

$$N_t = N_0 \cdot e^{-K_b \cdot t}$$

where **N₀** is initial coliform count, **N_t** is count at time t, and **K_b** is die-off rate constant.

For a complete-mix lagoon with HRT θ:

$$N_e = \frac{N_0}{1 + K_b \cdot \theta}$$

The die-off constant K_b has contributions from multiple mechanisms:

$$K_b = K_d + K_s + K_p + K_{uv}$$

where **K_d** = natural death, **K_s** = sedimentation, **K_p** = predation, **K_uv** = solar UV. UV is the dominant mechanism in maturation ponds at high pH (9–10).

> [!NOTE]
> **Why Diurnal pH Variation Matters:** At high pH (>9), the equilibrium NH₃/NH₄⁺ shifts toward free ammonia, which is toxic to pathogens. Combined with UV exposure during daytime, this creates a powerful disinfection environment unique to aerobic/maturation lagoons.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Functional Architecture Flow — Effect of Lagoons on Effluent Parameters

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Raw Food Industry Effluent]:::inputNode
    B[Influent Characterization: BOD, COD, TSS, pH, Temp, Nutrients]:::paramNode
    C{Select Lagoon Type Based on Strength}:::decisionNode
    D[Aerobic Maturation Pond]:::aerobicNode
    E[Facultative Stabilization Pond]:::facultativeNode
    F[Anaerobic Digestion Pond]:::anaerobicNode
    G[Aerated Lagoon]:::aeratedNode
    H[BOD Reduction 80-95%]:::effectNode
    I[Algal TSS Increase Possible]:::effectNode
    J[High pH 9-10.5]:::effectNode
    K[BOD Reduction 60-85%]:::effectNode
    L[DO Gradient 0-6 mg/L]:::effectNode
    M[Stratification Aerobic Anaerobic]:::effectNode
    N[BOD Reduction 50-75%]:::effectNode
    O[High Methane Production]:::effectNode
    P[pH 6.5-7.5, Strong Odor]:::effectNode
    Q[BOD Reduction 80-90%]:::effectNode
    R[Mechanical Aeration]:::effectNode
    S[DO Maintained 1-2 mg/L]:::effectNode
    T[Treated Effluent: Polishing, Reuse, Discharge]:::outputNode
    U[Pathogen Kill 1-3 log]:::sideNode
    V[Nutrient Reduction 30-80%]:::sideNode
    W[Energy Recovery via Biogas]:::sideNode

    A --> B
    B --> C
    C -->|Low BOD < 200| D
    C -->|Medium BOD 200-1000| E
    C -->|High BOD > 1000| F
```

# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### **Question 1** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`
**List the characteristic parameters of food industry effluents that are significantly affected by lagoon treatment.**

**Model Answer:**
The characteristic parameters of food industry effluents significantly affected by lagoon treatment are:
1. **BOD (Biochemical Oxygen Demand)** — reduced by 60–95% depending on lagoon type.
2. **COD (Chemical Oxygen Demand)** — reduced by 50–85% depending on biodegradability.
3. **TSS (Total Suspended Solids)** — reduced by 70–90% by sedimentation; may increase due to algal biomass in aerobic ponds.
4. **pH** — shifts towards alkaline (8.5–10.5) in aerobic ponds due to algal photosynthesis consuming CO₂; remains near neutral (6.5–7.5) in anaerobic ponds.
5. **DO (Dissolved Oxygen)** — increases in aerobic zones due to algal and mechanical aeration.
6. **Nutrients (N & P)** — partially reduced by algal uptake and ammonia volatilization.
7. **Pathogens (Fecal coliforms)** — reduced by 1–3 log units via UV, pH shock, and natural die-off.
8. **Color and Odor** — color reduced by 50–80%; odor eliminated in aerobic ponds but may worsen in anaerobic ponds.

### **Question 2** `[KTU University Exam - June 2024]`
**Differentiate between aerobic, facultative, and anaerobic lagoons with respect to DO, BOD removal, and depth.**

**Model Answer:**

| Feature | Aerobic Pond | Facultative Pond | Anaerobic Pond |
|---|---|---|---|
| **DO (mg/L)** | > 2 (throughout) | 0 (bottom) to 6 (top) | 0 (throughout) |
| **BOD Removal (%)** | 80–95% | 60–85% | 50–75% |
| **Depth (m)** | 0.3 – 0.5 | 1.0 – 2.5 | 2.5 – 5.0 |
| **Primary Mechanism** | Algal-bacterial symbiosis | Stratified multi-zone | Methanogenic fermentation |
| **Energy Input** | Solar (passive) | Solar (passive) | Solar (passive) |
| **Odor** | None | Mild | Strong (H₂S) |
| **HRT (days)** | 5–15 | 15–30 | 20–50 |
| **Best for** | Polishing, pathogen removal | Medium-strength effluents | High-strength effluents |

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each)

### **Question A** `[KTU University Exam - Model Paper 2024]`
**(a)** Explain the working principle of a facultative lagoon with a neat labeled diagram. Discuss the effect of this lagoon on (i) BOD, (ii) pH, (iii) DO, and (iv) pathogens. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A dairy industry discharges 1500 m³/day of wastewater with BOD 1500 mg/L into a facultative lagoon. The lagoon area is 20,000 m² and depth is 1.8 m. The first-order BOD removal rate constant k₂₀ = 0.18 day⁻¹. Calculate (i) detention time, (ii) effluent BOD at 20°C, (iii) effluent BOD at 30°C, and (iv) BOD removal efficiency at 30°C. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution for Part (a)

**Working Principle of Facultative Lagoon:**
A facultative lagoon is a stratified impoundment that operates in three vertically distinct zones:

- **Surface Aerobic Zone:** Sunlight penetrates the upper 0.3–0.5 m, supporting algal photosynthesis. Algae release O₂ which is utilized by aerobic bacteria to oxidize dissolved BOD.
- **Middle Facultative Zone:** A transitional zone where conditions fluctuate between aerobic and anaerobic, hosting a mixed microbial flora.
- **Bottom Anaerobic Zone:** Below the light penetration depth, settled sludge undergoes anaerobic digestion by methanogenic bacteria, producing CH₄ and CO₂.

**Effect on Parameters:**

**(i) BOD:** Facultative lagoons achieve 60–85% BOD removal. The surface aerobic zone oxidizes soluble BOD via bacterial action, while the bottom anaerobic zone digests settled organics. Overall, the BOD of a food industry effluent can be reduced from 1500 mg/L to 200–400 mg/L.

**(ii) pH:** During daylight, algal photosynthesis removes CO₂, raising the pH to 8.0–9.0. At night, respiration lowers the pH slightly. The diurnal variation is typically 0.5–1.5 pH units. The slight alkalinity favors ammonia volatilization and pathogen destruction.

**(iii) DO:** A vertical DO gradient develops: surface 4–6 mg/L (daytime), dropping to 0 mg/L at the bottom. This gradient is the defining feature of facultative ponds.

**(iv) Pathogens:** Fecal coliforms are reduced by 1–3 log units through combined action of elevated pH, solar UV radiation, long detention time, sedimentation, and microbial predation. The effluent typically has 10³–10⁵ MPN/100 mL.

#### Model Solution for Part (b)

**Given Data:**
- Q = 1500 m³/day
- L₀ = 1500 mg/L
- A = 20,000 m²
- d = 1.8 m
- k₂₀ = 0.18 day⁻¹

**(i) Detention Time:**

Volume of lagoon:

$$V = A \times d = 20000 \times 1.8 = 36000 \text{ m}^3$$

Detention time:

$$\theta = \frac{V}{Q} = \frac{36000}{1500} = 24 \text{ days}$$

**(ii) Effluent BOD at 20°C:**

Using the complete-mix lagoon equation:

$$L_e = \frac{L_0}{1 + k\theta}$$

Substitute values:

$$L_e = \frac{1500}{1 + (0.18 \times 24)} = \frac{1500}{1 + 4.32} = \frac{1500}{5.32}$$

$$L_e = 281.95 \text{ mg/L}$$

**(iii) Effluent BOD at 30°C:**

Apply temperature correction (θ coefficient = 1.056):

$$k_{30} = k_{20} \times (1.056)^{(30-20)} = 0.18 \times (1.056)^{10}$$

Calculate (1.056)¹⁰:

$$(1.056)^{10} = 1.724$$

Therefore:

$$k_{30} = 0.18 \times 1.724 = 0.3103 \text{ day}^{-1}$$

Effluent BOD:

$$L_e = \frac{1500}{1 + (0.3103 \times 24)} = \frac{1500}{1 + 7.447} = \frac{1500}{8.447}$$

$$L_e = 177.6 \text{ mg/L}$$

**(iv) BOD Removal Efficiency at 30°C:**

$$\eta = \frac{L_0 - L_e}{L_0} \times 100 = \frac{1500 - 177.6}{1500} \times 100 = 88.16\%$$

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfalls for Question A:**
> - Students often forget to convert the temperature coefficient formula correctly. Use **k_T = k₂₀ × (1.056)^(T-20)**, NOT a linear correction.
> - Many students omit the area/depth to volume conversion; this is a 1-mark deduction in the KTU scheme.
> - Do not confuse the surface BOD loading (kg BOD/ha·day) with volumetric loading (kg BOD/m³·day).

---

### **Question B** `[KTU University Exam - Model Paper 2024]`
**(a)** With the help of a flowchart, describe how a multi-stage lagoon system (anaerobic + facultative + maturation) affects the characteristic parameters of a high-strength food industry effluent. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A sugar mill operates an anaerobic lagoon followed by a facultative lagoon in series. The flow is 2000 m³/day, influent BOD = 5000 mg/L. The anaerobic lagoon has V₁ = 20,000 m³ with k₁ = 0.10 day⁻¹ (anaerobic), and the facultative lagoon has V₂ = 40,000 m³ with k₂ = 0.20 day⁻¹ (facultative). Calculate the overall BOD removal efficiency at 25°C. Use temperature coefficient θ = 1.056. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution for Part (a)

**Multi-Stage Lagoon System for High-Strength Food Industry Effluents:**

A multi-stage lagoon system is designed to progressively reduce the organic and pathogenic load of high-strength food industry effluents. The three stages operate as:

**Stage 1 — Anaerobic Lagoon (Primary):**
- Receives raw effluent with BOD 5000–10,000 mg/L.
- Functions as a high-rate anaerobic digester.
- **Effect on parameters:**
  - BOD reduced by 50–70% (to 1500–3000 mg/L).
  - COD reduced by 40–60%.
  - TSS reduced by 60–80% (sedimentation).
  - pH drops to 6.5–7.2 (volatile fatty acid accumulation).
  - Strong odor (H₂S, mercaptans).
  - Methane production (energy recovery opportunity).
  - DO remains at 0 mg/L.

**Stage 2 — Facultative Lagoon (Secondary):**
- Receives anaerobic effluent for further polishing.
- **Effect on parameters:**
  - BOD reduced by 60–85% from input (additional 50–70% of remaining BOD).
  - COD reduced by 50–75%.
  - TSS further reduced by 70–90%.
  - pH rises to 7.5–8.5 (algal activity).
  - DO present in upper zone (2–6 mg/L).
  - Color reduced significantly.

**Stage 3 — Maturation Pond (Tertiary):**
- Shallow aerobic pond for pathogen kill and polishing.
- **Effect on parameters:**
  - BOD further reduced to < 30 mg/L.
  - Pathogens reduced by 2–3 log units.
  - pH rises to 9–10.5 (UV + pH disinfection).
  - Nutrients (N) reduced by ammonia volatilization.
  - Produces irrigation-quality water.

The cumulative BOD removal across three stages can reach 95–99%, with fecal coliform reduction up to 4–5 log units.

#### Model Solution for Part (b)

**Given Data:**
- Q = 2000 m³/day
- L₀ = 5000 mg/L
- V₁ = 20,000 m³, k₁₂₅ = 0.10 day⁻¹ (after temperature correction)
- V₂ = 40,000 m³, k₂₂₅ = 0.20 day⁻¹ (after temperature correction)
- T = 25°C, θ_coefficient = 1.056

**Note:** The problem states k₁ and k₂ at 25°C; verify by applying temperature correction if k values are given at 20°C.

**Step 1: Detention Time of Anaerobic Lagoon**

$$\theta_1 = \frac{V_1}{Q} = \frac{20000}{2000} = 10 \text{ days}$$

**Step 2: Effluent BOD from Anaerobic Lagoon (L₁)**

$$L_1 = \frac{L_0}{1 + k_1 \theta_1} = \frac{5000}{1 + (0.10 \times 10)} = \frac{5000}{1 + 1} = \frac{5000}{2} = 2500 \text{ mg/L}$$

**Step 3: Detention Time of Facultative Lagoon**

$$\theta_2 = \frac{V_2}{Q} = \frac{40000}{2000} = 20 \text{ days}$$

**Step 4: Final Effluent BOD (L₂)**

$$L_2 = \frac{L_1}{1 + k_2 \theta_2} = \frac{2500}{1 + (0.20 \times 20)} = \frac{2500}{1 + 4} = \frac{2500}{5} = 500 \text{ mg/L}$$

**Step 5: Overall BOD Removal Efficiency**

$$\eta_{overall} = \frac{L_0 - L_2}{L_0} \times 100 = \frac{5000 - 500}{5000} \times 100 = 90\%$$

**Verification of Surface Loadings:**

Anaerobic lagoon surface loading:

$$L_{s1} = \frac{5000 \times 2000}{A_1}$$

If depth d = 4 m, A₁ = V₁/d = 5000 m² = 0.5 ha:

$$L_{s1} = \frac{5000 \times 2000}{0.5 \times 10^4} = 2000 \text{ kg BOD/ha·day}$$

This is consistent with high-rate anaerobic lagoon design.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfalls for Question B:**
> - In series lagoon problems, students frequently forget to use the effluent of the first lagoon as the influent of the second lagoon — this is the **most common error** in KTU series-treatment calculations.
> - Don't apply the temperature correction twice; apply it only once based on the reference temperature of given k values.
> - Always specify the units at every step; unit omission results in partial credit loss in KTU valuation.

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- [ ] **Lagoons** are controlled earthen impoundments using natural processes to treat biodegradable food industry wastewater.
- [ ] **Four main types:** Aerobic, Facultative, Anaerobic, Aerated — each with a distinct DO profile and depth.
- [ ] **BOD removal efficiency** is the most tracked parameter: Aerobic 80–95%, Facultative 60–85%, Anaerobic 50–75%.
- [ ] **Complete-mix model** for effluent BOD: $L_e = \dfrac{L_0}{1 + k\theta}$
- [ ] **Temperature correction:** $k_T = k_{20} \times (1.056)^{(T-20)}$ — this is a **favourite KTU question**.
- [ ] **Algal photosynthesis** raises pH to 9–10.5 in aerobic/maturation ponds, which enhances pathogen kill and ammonia stripping.
- [ ] **Anaerobic lagoons** produce **biogas (CH₄ + CO₂)** and **strong H₂S odor** — design with gas collection and odor control.
- [ ] **TSS can increase** in aerobic pond effluents due to algal washout — this is a key design limitation.
- [ ] **Nutrient removal (N & P) is poor** in lagoons; consider algal harvesting or chemical precipitation for advanced nutrient control.
- [ ] **Pathogen die-off** follows Chick's Law: $N_t = N_0 \cdot e^{-K_b \cdot t}$; mechanisms include UV, pH, sedimentation, predation.
- [ ] **Multi-stage lagoon systems** (Anaerobic + Facultative + Maturation) can achieve 95–99% BOD removal and 4–5 log coliform reduction.
- [ ] **Design depths to memorize:** Aerobic 0.3–0.5 m, Facultative 1.0–2.5 m, Anaerobic 2.5–5.0 m, Aerated 2.0–5.0 m.
- [ ] **Detention time to memorize:** Aerobic 5–15 d, Facultative 15–30 d, Anaerobic 20–50 d.
- [ ] **Surface BOD loading limit** for facultative ponds: 20–100 kg BOD/ha·day.
- [ ] **Lagoons are the most energy-efficient** food industry treatment option but require **large land area** and are **climate-sensitive**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Final KTU Examination Strategy:** For numerical problems, always state the formula, substitute values step-by-step, and conclude with units. For descriptive questions, structure the answer as: (1) definition, (2) mechanism, (3) effect on each parameter, (4) limitations/applications. This structure aligns with KTU's marking scheme and earns full credit.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEFTT744 - FOOD INDUSTRY WASTE MANAGEMENT
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Trickling filters

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# Module 4 — Trickling Filters

## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Formal KTU Definition**
> A *Trickling Filter (TF)* is a **fixed-film biological wastewater treatment reactor** in which wastewater is distributed over a bed of coarse, inert packing media. Microorganisms grow as a *slimy biofilm* (zoogleal film) on the surface of the media. As the wastewater trickles down through the bed, organic pollutants diffuse into the biofilm and are metabolised aerobically, while treated effluent and sloughed biomass are collected by an underdrain system.

### 1.1 Conceptual Analogy — Plain-English Intuition

Imagine a **vertical multi-storey building where every floor is packed with wet, mossy stones** and rainwater is sprayed from the top.

- The stones are the *filter media* → they provide a permanent "home" (surface area) for the bacteria and fungi to attach.
- The moss is the *biofilm* → a living skin of microbes that eats the organic dirt in the water.
- The spray is the *distributor arm* → it spreads the wastewater evenly so every stone is fed.
- The water dripping out the bottom is *partially treated effluent* + *fallen moss pieces* (sloughed biomass).

> [!NOTE]
> **Key insight for the exam:** Unlike the *activated sludge process* (where microbes float freely in a tank), trickling filters **immobilise the microbes on a surface**. This makes the system more resilient to shock loads, which is a major reason it is favoured in the **food industry** (dairy, brewery, distillery, starch, fruit/veg processing) where influent strength fluctuates hourly.

### 1.2 Core Components of a Trickling Filter (Must-Memorise)

| # | Component | Function | Common Construction |
|---|-----------|----------|---------------------|
| 1 | **Distributor (rotary arms)** | Sprays wastewater uniformly over the media; rotation is hydraulic (reaction of jets) | 2 or 4 arms on a central column |
| 2 | **Filter media** | Provides surface area for biofilm attachment | Stones, slag, plastic rings, PVC cross-flow media |
| 3 | **Biofilm (zoogleal slime layer)** | Active biological agent — degrades organics | Thickness $\approx$ 0.1–2.0 mm |
| 4 | **Underdrain system** | Collects effluent + fallen biofilm (sloughing) and admits air | Half-round tile blocks with open joints |
| 5 | **Ventilation ducts / stack** | Provides oxygen for aerobic respiration | Open vents to atmosphere |
| 6 | **Final clarifier (humus tank)** | Settles sloughed solids from filter effluent | Settling tank downstream |

### 1.3 Key Operating Parameters (Examination High-Yield)

- **Hydraulic Loading Rate (HLR)** — $\mathbf{Q/A}$ in m³/m²·d
- **Organic Loading Rate (OLR)** — $\mathbf{Q\,S_o / V}$ in kg BOD/m³·d
- **Wetting rate / wetting time** — the fraction of time the biofilm is submerged vs air-exposed; controls oxygen transfer.
- **Recirculation ratio (R)** — fraction of treated effluent returned to the filter inlet.

> [!TIP]
> Food-industry wastewaters (e.g., from dairies with 1 500–4 000 mg/L BOD, or from distilleries with 20 000–50 000 mg/L COD) are **highly concentrated**. Hence **high-rate or roughing trickling filters** are preferred so that the system is not overwhelmed.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Process Mechanism — Step-by-Step

When the rotating distributor sprays influent over the top of the media, the following micro-processes occur **simultaneously and continuously**:

1. **Wetting & Adsorption** — The thin liquid film adheres to the biofilm; soluble organic matter (BOD) diffuses from the bulk liquid into the slime layer (driven by Fick's law).
2. **Aerobic Oxidation** — Bacteria (chiefly *Zoogloea*, *Pseudomonas*, *Alcaligenes*) oxidise the organics using dissolved oxygen; oxygen diffuses inward from air. End products are $\text{CO}_2$, $\text{H}_2\text{O}$, $\text{NH}_3$, and new cell mass.
3. **Nitrification (in deeper, mature filters)** — In the lower portion of a low-loaded filter, *Nitrosomonas* and *Nitrobacter* convert $\text{NH}_4^+$ → $\text{NO}_2^-$ → $\text{NO}_3^-$.
4. **Sloughing** — When biofilm thickness exceeds $\approx$ 2 mm, internal mass-transfer limitations starve the deeper layer, the slime detaches, and is washed out by the trickling liquid. The sloughed solids are called **humus**.
5. **Ventilation & Aeration** — Natural draft (chimney effect: cold air enters at the bottom, hot moist air exits at the top) provides the $\text{O}_2$ needed.

## 2.2 Classification of Trickling Filters

> [!IMPORTANT]
> KTU typically asks for a **comparison table** between Standard-Rate, High-Rate, and Roughing filters — **memorise these numerical values**.

| Parameter | Standard-Rate TF | High-Rate TF | Roughing TF (Super-High Rate) |
|-----------|------------------|--------------|-------------------------------|
| OLR (kg BOD/m³·d) | 0.08 – 0.32 | 0.32 – 1.0 | 1.0 – 4.0 |
| HLR (m³/m²·d) | 1 – 4 | 4 – 30 | 30 – 100 (with recirculation) |
| Recirculation ratio (R) | 0 | 1 – 3 | 3 – 8 |
| BOD removal efficiency | 80 – 90 % | 65 – 85 % | 40 – 65 % |
| Media depth (m) | 1.5 – 3.0 | 1.0 – 2.0 | 0.5 – 1.5 |
| Distributor speed (rpm) | 0.03 – 0.3 | 0.3 – 2 | > 2 |
| Sloughing frequency | Continuous | Heavy | Very heavy |
| Application | Municipal / weak industrial | Dairy, food canning | Pre-treatment of strong distillery/brewery |

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet (Examination Cheat-Sheet)

> [!NOTE]
> Three canonical design equations are accepted by KTU examiners. **Pick the one that matches the data given in the problem**. Never use more than one in a single problem unless explicitly asked.

$$
\begin{array}{|l|c|c|c|}
\hline
\textbf{Model} & \textbf{Equation} & \textbf{Use} & \textbf{Constants} \\
\hline
\text{Eckenfelder} & \dfrac{S_e}{S_o} = \exp\!\left[-k\,\left(\dfrac{Q}{A}\right)^{n}\right] & \text{Plastic/} & k=0.21,\; n=0.50 \text{ (BOD-based)} \\
& & \text{low-load TF} & \text{or } k=0.20,\; n=0.67 \\
\hline
\text{NRC} & \dfrac{S_e}{S_o} = \dfrac{1}{1 + \left(\dfrac{Q}{V}\right)^{n}} & \text{Standard-rate} & n=0.5 \\
& & \text{stone media} & k \text{ folded in } \\
\hline
\text{Galler–Levaat} & \dfrac{S_e}{S_o} = \exp\!\left[-k\,\left(\dfrac{D}{Q^{n}}\right)\right] & \text{High-rate} & k,n \text{ from pilot} \\
\hline
\end{array}
$$

### Auxiliary Design Formulas

- **Hydraulic Loading Rate (HLR):**
$$HLR = \dfrac{Q}{A} \quad \left[\dfrac{\text{m}^3}{\text{m}^2 \cdot \text{d}}\right]$$

- **Organic Loading Rate (OLR):**
$$OLR = \dfrac{Q\,S_o}{V} \quad \left[\dfrac{\text{kg BOD}}{\text{m}^3 \cdot \text{d}}\right]$$

- **Volume of filter bed:**
$$V = A \times D \quad [\text{m}^3]$$

- **Recirculation-adjusted influent BOD** (when $R$ is the recirculation ratio $Q_r / Q$):
$$S'_o = \dfrac{S_o + R\,S_e}{1 + R}$$

- **BOD removal efficiency:**
$$\eta = \dfrac{S_o - S_e}{S_o} \times 100\,\%$$

- **BOD of recirculated stream** (assuming effluent is fully nitrified → use BOD₅):
$$S_e \text{ (returned)} \approx S_e \text{ (filter effluent)}$$

### 2.4 Real-World Utility in the Food Industry

| Food-Industry Sub-Sector | Why TF is Chosen |
|--------------------------|------------------|
| **Dairy** | Tolerates high fat, protein; slime layer handles shock load of milk washouts |
| **Brewery / Distillery** | Pre-treats strong BOD before anaerobic lagoon / UASB |
| **Fruit & Vegetable Canning** | Handles seasonal, particulate-laden wastewater without clogging |
| **Meat & Poultry** | Robust to high ammonia; double-stage TF gives nitrification |
| **Sugar / Starch** | Smooths out high-carbohydrate peaks that would crash AS systems |

> [!TIP]
> If the question specifies a **highly variable organic load**, justify the use of TF over activated sludge by quoting: *"Fixed-film biomass in TFs is several times thicker and more diverse than flocs in AS, so the system buffers sudden COD spikes — a daily occurrence in food processing."*
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation of the Eckenfelder Kinetic Model (Board-Standard)

The Eckenfelder equation assumes the filter behaves as a **first-order plug-flow reactor with respect to the wetted area**, and the rate of BOD removal is proportional to the contact time $t_c$ between the liquid film and the biofilm.

### Step 1 — Rate expression

The rate of substrate removal per unit volume is:
$$r = -k\,S$$

where $k$ is a pseudo first-order rate constant $\left[\text{time}^{-1}\right]$.

### Step 2 — Residence time on the film

The contact (or residence) time is approximated as the film thickness $\delta$ divided by the superficial velocity:
$$t_c = \dfrac{\delta}{v_s} = \dfrac{\delta \cdot A}{Q}$$

### Step 3 — Plug-flow integration

For a differential slice of filter height $dD$, the substrate balance on the liquid film is:
$$\dfrac{dS}{S} = -k\,\dfrac{dD}{v_s}$$

### Step 4 — Integrate from inlet to outlet

Integrating over the full depth $D$ from $S_o$ to $S_e$:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\int_{S_o}^{S_e} \dfrac{dS}{S} &= -\dfrac{k\,D}{v_s} \\
\ln\!\left(\dfrac{S_e}{S_o}\right) &= -k\,D\,A/Q \\
\dfrac{S_e}{S_o} &= \exp\!\left[-k\,D\left(\dfrac{A}{Q}\right)\right]
\end{aligned}
$$

### Step 5 — Empirical correction (full Eckenfelder form)

For a real trickling filter, surface overflow and bio-film heterogeneity introduce a non-linearity captured by an exponent $n$ on the hydraulic term:

$$
\boxed{\;\dfrac{S_e}{S_o} \;=\; \exp\!\left[-k\left(\dfrac{Q}{A}\right)^{\!n}\right]\;}
$$

This is the equation **you write on the board**. For plastic media use $k = 0.21$, $n = 0.5$.

## 3.2 Derivation of the NRC (National Research Council) Equation

The NRC equation is an *engineering simplification* of the plug-flow model, recast in terms of volumetric loading. Start from:

$$\ln\!\left(\dfrac{S_e}{S_o}\right) = -k\,D\left(\dfrac{A}{Q}\right)$$

Substitute $D \cdot A = V$ and rearrange:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\dfrac{S_e}{S_o} &= \exp\!\left[-k\left(\dfrac{V}{Q}\right)\right] \\
\text{Use series expansion: } \exp(-x) &\approx \dfrac{1}{1+x} \text{ for small } x \\
\therefore \dfrac{S_e}{S_o} &\approx \dfrac{1}{1 + k\,(V/Q)^{n}}
\end{aligned}
$$

For stone media with typical plug-flow behaviour, the empirical NRC form is:

$$
\boxed{\;\dfrac{S_e}{S_o} \;=\; \dfrac{1}{1 + (Q/V)^{n}}\;}
$$

with $n = 0.5$ for standard-rate filters and $V$ the effective filter volume in m³.

## 3.3 Worked Numerical Example (Practice Pattern for KTU)

> **Problem (Module 4 — KTU-pattern)**
> A dairy discharges $Q = 4\,000$ m³/d of wastewater with soluble BOD $S_o = 2\,500$ mg/L. Design a high-rate trickling filter using Eckenfelder equation to achieve 80 % BOD removal. Assume $k = 0.21$, $n = 0.5$, and allowable HLR = 20 m³/m²·d.

### Solution — Step-by-step

**Step (a):** Required effluent BOD

$$
S_e = (1 - 0.80)\times 2\,500 = 500 \text{ mg/L}
$$

**Step (b):** Apply Eckenfelder

$$
\begin{aligned}
\dfrac{S_e}{S_o} &= 0.20 = \exp\!\left[-k\left(\dfrac{Q}{A}\right)^{0.5}\right] \\
\ln(0.20) &= -0.21\left(\dfrac{Q}{A}\right)^{0.5} \\
-1.609 &= -0.21\left(\dfrac{Q}{A}\right)^{0.5} \\
\left(\dfrac{Q}{A}\right)^{0.5} &= \dfrac{1.609}{0.21} = 7.66 \\
\dfrac{Q}{A} &= 58.7 \text{ m}^3/\text{m}^2\cdot\text{d}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step (c):** Required filter area

$$
A = \dfrac{Q}{HLR} = \dfrac{4\,000}{58.7} = 68.1 \text{ m}^2
$$

**Step (d):** For depth $D = 2.0$ m, volume

$$
V = A \times D = 68.1 \times 2.0 = 136.2 \text{ m}^3
$$

**Step (e):** Check organic loading

$$
OLR = \dfrac{Q\,S_o}{V} = \dfrac{4\,000\times 2.5}{136.2} = 73.4 \text{ kg BOD/m}^3\cdot\text{d}
$$

This is **far above** the high-rate limit (1.0 kg/m³·d) → confirms the problem is academically constructed; in a real design, the engineer would **add recirculation** to bring $S_o'$ down:

$$
S_o' = \dfrac{S_o + R\,S_e}{1 + R} = \dfrac{2\,500 + R(500)}{1+R}
$$

For $R = 4$:

$$
S_o' = \dfrac{2\,500 + 2\,000}{5} = 900 \text{ mg/L}
$$

Re-running step (b) with $S_o' = 900$ gives $HLR = 22.4$ m³/m²·d → filter area $\approx 178$ m² → **feasible design**.

## 3.4 Analytical Decision Matrix — Which Model to Use?

$$
\begin{array}{|l|c|c|c|}
\hline
\textbf{Condition Given in Question} & \textbf{Use Model} & \textbf{Key Symbol} & \textbf{Examiner Tip} \\
\hline
HLR or Q/A in the data & Eckenfelder & k,\;n & \text{Check units of HLR} \\
\hline
Q/V (volumetric loading) in data & NRC & n & \text{Plug-flow assumption} \\
\hline
D and Q given, plastic media & Galler–Levaat & k & \text{Rare in Module 4} \\
\hline
\end{array}
$$
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Functional Block Diagram of a Trickling-Filter Plant

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    node1["INFLUENT PUMP STATION\n(Food-Industry WW)"]
    node2["SCREENING & GRIT\nREMOVAL"]
    node3["PRIMARY CLARIFIER\nRemoves 25 to 35 percent BOD"]
    node4["RECIRCULATION PUMP\nReturns R times Q"]
    node5["ROTARY DISTRIBUTOR\nSprays WW over media"]
    node6["TRICKLING FILTER BED\nBiofilm on plastic media"]
    node7["UNDERDRAIN SYSTEM\nCollects effluent plus humus"]
    node8["FINAL CLARIFIER\nHumus tank, removes sloughed biomass"]
    node9["DISINFECTION\nChlorine or UV"]
    node10["TREATED EFFLUENT TO DISPOSAL"]
    node11["SLUDGE TO DIGESTER\nor DEWATERING"]
    node12["VENTILATION\nNatural draft air supply"]

    node1 --> node2
    node2 --> node3
    node3 --> node5
    node4 --> node5
    node5 --> node6
    node6 --> node7
    node7 --> node8
    node7 --> node12
    node8 --> node9
    node9 --> node10
    node8 --> node11
    node8 -. recycle to node4 .-> node4
```

## 4.2 Biofilm Mechanism on Filter Media (Cross-Section View)

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    nodeA["AIR PHASE\nOxygen available"]
    nodeB["LIQUID FILM\nWastes diffuse inward"]
    nodeC["AEROBIC ZONE\nBacteria oxidise BOD\nto CO2 and H2O"]
    nodeD["ANOXIC ZONE\nDenitrification possible"]
    nodeE["ATTACHMENT\nSURFACE OF MEDIA"]
    nodeF["INFLOWING WASTEWATER\nSoluble and suspended organics"]

    nodeA --> nodeB
    nodeB --> nodeC
    nodeC --> nodeD
    nodeD --> nodeE
    nodeF --> nodeB
```

> [!NOTE]
> The double-line on the top of *nodeC* represents the **air–water interface** where oxygen transfer happens. The biofilm must stay thin (0.1–2.0 mm) so that oxygen and BOD can diffuse to all depths of the slime layer; otherwise the inner layer becomes anaerobic and sloughs off.

## 4.3 Process Flow with Recirculation Loop

```mermaid

    nodeR1["INFLUENT Q and So"]
    nodeR2["Mixing Box\nSo prime equals So plus R Se over 1 plus R"]

    nodeR6 --> nodeR8
```

## 4.4 Comparison of Trickling Filter Loading Regimes

```mermaid

    nodeL1["TF LOADING REGIME SELECTION"]

    nodeL3["HIGH RATE\nOLR 0.32 to 1.0\nBOD removal 65 to 85 percent\nR equals 1 to 3"]
    nodeL4["ROUGHING\nOLR greater than 1.0\nBOD removal 40 to 65 percent\nR equals 3 to 8\nPre-treatment of strong WW"]

    nodeL1 --> nodeL2
    nodeL1 --> nodeL3
    nodeL1 --> nodeL4
```

# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short-Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Q1. `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Module 4]`
**Define a trickling filter. Mention the role of the rotary distributor and the underdrain system.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
A trickling filter is a **fixed-film biological wastewater treatment unit** in which pre-settled wastewater is distributed over a bed of inert packing media supporting a microbial biofilm (1 mark).
The **rotary distributor** sprays the wastewater uniformly over the media bed; its rotation is powered by the reactive force of the jets, and it ensures the entire surface is wetted so that contact time between substrate and biofilm is maximised (1 mark).
The **underdrain system**, located at the base, performs three functions: (i) collects the treated effluent, (ii) channels sloughed biomass (humus) to the final clarifier, and (iii) admits air for natural-draft ventilation of the filter (1 mark).

### Q2. `[KTU University Exam – May 2024, Module 4]`
**What is hydraulic loading rate (HLR) and organic loading rate (OLR) of a trickling filter? State typical values for a high-rate filter.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
**HLR** is the volume of wastewater (including recirculation) applied per unit area of filter surface per day, defined as $HLR = Q/A$ in m³/m²·d (1 mark).
**OLR** is the BOD load applied per unit volume of filter media per day, defined as $OLR = Q S_o / V$ in kg BOD/m³·d (1 mark).
For a **high-rate** trickling filter, $HLR = 4–30$ m³/m²·d and $OLR = 0.32–1.0$ kg BOD/m³·d (1 mark).

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long-Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Internal Choice)

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall — TF Problems**
> • (–1 mark) **Mixing up units.** HLR is in m³/m²·d; OLR is in kg/m³·d. Convert $S_o$ from mg/L to kg/m³ by dividing by 1 000.
> • (–1 mark) **Forgetting recirculation.** If the problem says "with recirculation ratio $R$", use $S_o' = (S_o + R S_e)/(1+R)$ **before** applying Eckenfelder.
> • (–1 mark) **Choosing the wrong model.** Eckenfelder uses $Q/A$; NRC uses $Q/V$. Re-read the data column.
> • (–1 mark) **Not stating design check.** Always report the final HLR and OLR to confirm the filter regime is feasible.

### Question A (14 marks) — `[KTU University Exam – May 2023]`

> **(a)** With the help of a neat functional diagram, describe the working of a **high-rate trickling filter** used for treating dairy wastewater. Highlight the role of the biofilm, distributor, and underdrain. **(7 marks)**
>
> **(b)** A high-rate trickling filter is to be designed to treat $Q = 2\,500$ m³/d of food-processing wastewater with $S_o = 1\,200$ mg/L. Using the **Eckenfelder equation** with $k = 0.21$, $n = 0.5$, allowable HLR = 25 m³/m²·d, and filter depth $D = 1.8$ m, determine (i) the required filter area, (ii) the BOD removal efficiency if actual OLR is 0.6 kg/m³·d. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution — (a) 7 marks

**Working of a High-Rate Trickling Filter for Dairy Wastewater**

1. **Pre-treatment** (1 mark): Dairy wastewater first passes through screens, grit chambers, and a primary clarifier to remove fat, milk solids, and grit. This protects the filter from clogging.
2. **Distribution** (1 mark): A motor- or hydraulically-driven **rotary distributor** mounted on a central column sprays the clarified influent (and recirculated effluent) evenly over the top of the media bed. As the distributor rotates at 0.3–2 rpm, the four arms pass over the entire surface once every 20–60 seconds.
3. **Biofilm contact** (2 marks): The thin liquid film trickling down the plastic media encounters a **biofilm (zoogleal slime)** 0.1–2 mm thick. The film contains a mixed culture — chiefly *Zoogloea*, *Pseudomonas*, *Achromobacter*, *Beggiatoa*, and grazing protozoa (*Vorticella*, *Opercularia*). Soluble BOD (lactose, casein, peptides) diffuses inward; oxygen diffuses from the surrounding air. Aerobic oxidation yields $\text{CO}_2$, $\text{H}_2\text{O}$, and new cell mass according to:

$$
\text{C}_6\text{H}_{12}\text{O}_6 + 6\text{O}_2 \longrightarrow 6\text{CO}_2 + 6\text{H}_2\text{O}
$$

4. **Sloughing and ventilation** (1 mark): When the slime becomes too thick, it detaches (*sloughs*) and is flushed to the **underdrain**, which simultaneously admits air via natural-draft ventilation. The sloughed *humus* is captured in the **final clarifier**.
5. **Recirculation** (1 mark): In a high-rate TF, 1–3 volumes of treated effluent per volume of influent are returned to dilute the inlet BOD and to keep the biofilm moist during low-flow periods.
6. **Final effluent polishing** (1 mark): The clarified, disinfected effluent is discharged; sludge is sent to a digester or dewatering unit.

#### Model Solution — (b) 7 marks

**Step 1 — Convert influent BOD to kg/m³:**
$$S_o = 1\,200\text{ mg/L} = 1.2\text{ kg/m}^3$$

**Step 2 — Maximum allowable flow (cap by HLR):**
$$A_{\min} = \dfrac{Q}{HLR_{\max}} = \dfrac{2\,500}{25} = 100\text{ m}^2$$

**Step 3 — Eckenfelder equation for design area (assuming $S_e/S_o$ target = 0.30):**

For 70 % removal, $S_e = 360$ mg/L, hence $S_e/S_o = 0.30$:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\exp\!\left[-0.21\left(\dfrac{Q}{A}\right)^{0.5}\right] &= 0.30 \\
-0.21\left(\dfrac{Q}{A}\right)^{0.5} &= \ln 0.30 = -1.204 \\
\left(\dfrac{Q}{A}\right)^{0.5} &= 5.73 \\
\dfrac{Q}{A} &= 32.8\text{ m}^3/\text{m}^2\cdot\text{d}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Required area:**
$$A = \dfrac{2\,500}{32.8} = 76.2\text{ m}^2$$

Since 76.2 m² < 100 m², the design is governed by the HLR limit, so adopt $\boxed{A = 100\text{ m}^2}$ for safe design **(2 marks)**.

**Step 4 — Filter volume:**
$$V = A \times D = 100 \times 1.8 = 180\text{ m}^3$$

**Step 5 — Actual OLR (given = 0.6 kg/m³·d):**
$$S_{o,\text{actual}} = \dfrac{OLR \times V}{Q} = \dfrac{0.6 \times 180}{2\,500} = 0.0432\text{ kg/m}^3 = 43.2\text{ mg/L}$$

**Step 6 — BOD removal efficiency:**
$$\eta = \dfrac{1\,200 - 43.2}{1\,200} \times 100 = 96.4\,\%$$

**Interpretation:** The high removal is because 0.6 kg/m³·d is a *moderate* OLR for plastic-media filters; the actual efficiency is **96.4 %**, giving $S_e \approx 43$ mg/L, which is well within dairy discharge norms **(1 mark)**.

---

### Question B (14 marks) — `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2022]`

> **(a)** Compare **standard-rate**, **high-rate**, and **roughing** trickling filters in a tabular form with respect to (i) OLR, (ii) HLR, (iii) recirculation ratio, (iv) BOD removal efficiency, and (v) typical applications. **(7 marks)**
>
> **(b)** A brewery wastewater of $Q = 1\,000$ m³/d and $S_o = 3\,500$ mg/L is treated by a high-rate trickling filter with $D = 1.5$ m. Using the **NRC equation** ($n = 0.5$), determine the **filter diameter** required to achieve $S_e = 350$ mg/L. Also compute the OLR. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution — (a) 7 marks

| Parameter | Standard-Rate TF | High-Rate TF | Roughing TF |
|-----------|------------------|--------------|-------------|
| (i) OLR (kg BOD/m³·d) | 0.08 – 0.32 | 0.32 – 1.0 | 1.0 – 4.0 |
| (ii) HLR (m³/m²·d) | 1 – 4 | 4 – 30 | 30 – 100 |
| (iii) Recirculation ratio R | 0 | 1 – 3 | 3 – 8 |
| (iv) BOD removal efficiency | 80 – 90 % | 65 – 85 % | 40 – 65 % |
| (v) Typical applications | Domestic, weak food WW | Dairy, fruit-canning, bakeries | Strong brewery/distillery pre-treatment before anaerobic system |

(Marking: 1 mark per row × 6 rows — but capped at 7 marks; allow 1 mark for conclusion statement.)

> *(Sample conclusion, 1 mark)*: As loading rate increases from standard to roughing, the unit becomes more compact and less efficient, so roughing filters are used only as a pre-treatment step before a second biological stage.

#### Model Solution — (b) 7 marks

**Step 1 — Target removal ratio:**
$$\dfrac{S_e}{S_o} = \dfrac{350}{3\,500} = 0.10$$

**Step 2 — Apply NRC equation** (use filter volume $V$ in numerator — recall derivation):

$$
\begin{aligned}
\dfrac{S_e}{S_o} &= \dfrac{1}{1 + (Q/V)^{n}} \\
0.10 &= \dfrac{1}{1 + (Q/V)^{0.5}} \\
1 + (Q/V)^{0.5} &= 10 \\
(Q/V)^{0.5} &= 9 \\
Q/V &= 81 \\
V &= \dfrac{1\,000}{81} = 12.35\text{ m}^3
\end{aligned}
$$

**(3 marks for the algebraic steps.)**

**Step 3 — Filter area from volume and depth:**

$$
A = \dfrac{V}{D} = \dfrac{12.35}{1.5} = 8.23\text{ m}^2
$$

**Step 4 — Filter diameter** (circular filter):

$$
\begin{aligned}
A &= \dfrac{\pi D_f^{\,2}}{4} \\
D_f &= \sqrt{\dfrac{4A}{\pi}} = \sqrt{\dfrac{4 \times 8.23}{\pi}} = \sqrt{10.48} = 3.24\text{ m}
\end{aligned}
$$

**(2 marks for area → diameter.)**

**Step 5 — Organic Loading Rate:**

$$
OLR = \dfrac{Q \times S_o}{V} = \dfrac{1\,000 \times 3.5}{12.35} = 283.4\text{ kg BOD/m}^3\cdot\text{d}
$$

**Interpretation (1 mark):** This OLR is extremely high — confirming the design assumption (90 % removal with a single 1.5 m deep TF) is not realistic for brewery effluent. The student should conclude that for a real brewery WW, a **roughing TF must precede an anaerobic UASB reactor or an activated-sludge stage**.

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Examiner Deduction** — In the NRC equation some textbooks place $V$ in the **denominator** and others in the **numerator**, depending on how the original plug-flow integration is written. Always re-derive quickly on the answer sheet (2 lines) to avoid sign errors.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!NOTE]
> **Rapid-Revision Checklist for Trickling Filters**

- **Definition** — Fixed-film biological reactor with biofilm on coarse or plastic media. (Know it cold.)
- **Five must-know components:** Distributor, media, biofilm, underdrain, ventilation.
- **Three filter regimes (numerical ranges are exam-favourite):**
  • Standard-rate: OLR 0.08–0.32; removal 80–90 %; no recirculation.
  • High-rate: OLR 0.32–1.0; removal 65–85 %; $R = 1–3$.
  • Roughing: OLR > 1.0; removal 40–65 %; $R = 3–8$.
- **Two equations to remember (board-ready):**
  • Eckenfelder: $S_e/S_o = \exp[-k(Q/A)^n]$, with $k = 0.21$, $n = 0.5$ (plastic media).
  • NRC: $S_e/S_o = 1 / [1 + (Q/V)^n]$, with $n = 0.5$ (stone media).
- **Unit conversion** is the #1 mark-killer: $1$ mg/L $= 10^{-3}$ kg/m³. Multiply $Q$ (m³/d) by $S_o$ (kg/m³) to get kg BOD/d, then divide by $V$ (m³) for OLR.
- **Recirculation formula** for adjusted influent: $S_o' = (S_o + R\,S_e)/(1+R)$.
- **Biofilm thickness sweet spot:** 0.1–2.0 mm. Thicker → anaerobic inner layer → sloughing.
- **Why TF in food industry:** tolerance to shock loads, suspended solids, and high oil/grease from dairy and meat plants.
- **Limitation:** TFs cannot alone polish very strong (> 5 000 mg/L BOD) wastewaters — always follow with **anaerobic + activated sludge** in food-industry plants.
- **Design check order (always):** (i) compute HLR, (ii) compute OLR, (iii) confirm regime, (iv) confirm recirculation if used.
- **Mini-formula-crib for last-minute revision:**
$$HLR = Q/A \quad;\quad OLR = QS_o/V \quad;\quad \eta = (S_o - S_e)/S_o \times 100$$
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**

> - **Module:** Module 4

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# Module 4 — Activated Sludge Process (ASP)

## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Formal KTU Definition (Wastewater Engineering Terminology)**
> The **Activated Sludge Process (ASP)** is a **suspended-growth biological wastewater treatment** process in which a **biomass consortium** of aerobic microorganisms (the *activated sludge*) is maintained in continuous contact with the influent organic substrate in an **aeration tank**, supplied with **dissolved oxygen (DO)**, and subsequently separated in a **secondary clarifier**, with a fraction of the settled biomass (**return activated sludge, RAS**) recycled back to the aeration tank to sustain a high, metabolically active microbial population.

In the KTU 2024 scheme context for **PEFTT744 – Food Industry Waste Management**, ASP is treated as the **workhorse secondary treatment** for **dissolved and colloidal organic matter** (measured as **Biochemical Oxygen Demand, BOD₅** and **Chemical Oxygen Demand, COD**) generated by **dairy, brewery, distillery, fruit & vegetable, seafood, and meat processing** effluents.

### Conceptual Analogy — "The Restaurant Kitchen"
Imagine the aeration tank as a **busy restaurant kitchen**:
- **Food waste from the buffet (influent BOD)** keeps arriving at the door.
- The **chefs (microorganisms — bacteria, protozoa, rotifers)** eat the food and multiply.
- The **ventilation fans (aerators / diffusers)** pump in oxygen so the chefs can breathe and "burn" the food aerobically.
- The **bussing station (secondary clarifier)** separates the chefs (settled sludge) from the clean wash water (clarified effluent).
- A portion of chefs is **sent back to the kitchen (RAS)** to keep cooking tomorrow; a small portion is **discharged (waste activated sludge, WAS)** to prevent overcrowding.

> [!NOTE]
> **Standard KTU Metrics You Must Memorize**
> - **MLSS (Mixed Liquor Suspended Solids):** **2,000 – 4,000 mg/L** (conventional ASP)
> - **F/M ratio (Food-to-Microorganism):** **0.2 – 0.5 day⁻¹** (conventional)
> - **SRT (Solids Retention Time / Sludge Age):** **5 – 15 days** (conventional)
> - **HRT (Hydraulic Retention Time):** **4 – 8 hours** (conventional)
> - **DO in aeration tank:** **1.5 – 2.5 mg/L** (critical for nitrification)
> - **Recycle ratio (Qr/Q):** **0.25 – 1.0** (typical 0.5)
> - **BOD removal efficiency:** **85 – 95 %**

### Where ASP Fits in the Food Industry Effluent Train

| Stage | Function | Typical Equipment |
|---|---|---|
| Preliminary | Screening, grit removal | Bar screens, grit chambers |
| Primary | Settleable solids removal | Primary clarifier |
| **Secondary (ASP)** | **Dissolved/colloidal organics oxidation** | **Aeration tank + Secondary clarifier** |
| Tertiary | Polishing (N, P, disinfection) | Sand filter, chlorination |
| Sludge handling | Biosolids management | Thickener, digester, dewatering |

> [!TIP]
> For **high-strength food industry effluents** (e.g., distillery spent wash, BOD ~ 40,000–100,000 mg/L), ASP alone is **insufficient** — it is preceded by **anaerobic treatment (UASB / anaerobic lagoon)** in a **two-stage high-rate system**.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Process Components — Step-by-Step Logic

The activated sludge system is a **closed-loop biological reactor** with three coupled units:

### Step 1 — Aeration Tank (Biological Reactor)
- Influent wastewater + **RAS** enter the head of the tank.
- **Aeration system** (mechanical surface aerators OR fine-bubble diffused air) supplies **O₂** and provides mixing.
- Microbes assimilate soluble BOD → synthesize new cells + CO₂ + H₂O.
- Operating mode can be **plug-flow, completely-mixed, or sequencing batch (SBR)**.

### Step 2 — Secondary Clarifier (Solid-Liquid Separator)
- MLSS flows by gravity to the clarifier.
- Sludge settles as a blanket; clarified supernatant is discharged as **effluent**.
- Settled sludge is split: **RAS** (returned to aeration) and **WAS** (wasted).

### Step 3 — Sludge Recycle Line
- RAS concentration: typically **6,000 – 10,000 mg/L**.
- Maintains constant MLSS → decouples **HRT** from **SRT**.
- *This decoupling is the genius of ASP*: hydraulic surges don't wash out the biomass.

### Step 4 — Excess Sludge Wastage
- WAS controls **SRT (θc)**.
- Steady-state mass balance: rate of biomass growth = rate of biomass wastage.

### Why Each Step Exists — The "How & Why"

| Step | How it works | Why it matters for food industry |
|---|---|---|
| Aeration | O₂ transfer + mixing | High BOD effluents need high O₂ supply (≥ 1.5 × BOD removed per stoichiometry) |
| Clarification | Gravity settling | Required to meet discharge BOD < 30 mg/L (Indian CPCB norms) |
| RAS recycle | Pumped return loop | Decouples HRT/SRT → robust to shock loads (common in food processing) |
| WAS | Controlled bleed | Prevents sludge accumulation; sends biomass to sludge handling |

## 2.2 KTU High-Yield Formula Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The following **seven equations** constitute ~90 % of all numerical questions asked in KTU ESE on this topic. Memorize the form, the variable meaning, and the units.

| # | Formula | Description / Variables | Typical Units |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $\theta = \dfrac{V}{Q}$ | **HRT (Hydraulic Retention Time)**; $V$ = tank volume, $Q$ = influent flow | hours (h) |
| 2 | $\theta_c = \dfrac{V \cdot X}{Q_w \cdot X_w + Q_e \cdot X_e}$ | **SRT / Mean Cell Residence Time (MCRT)**; $X$ = MLSS, $Q_w$ = WAS flow, $X_w$ = WAS conc., $Q_e$ = effluent flow, $X_e$ = effluent SS | days (d) |
| 3 | $\dfrac{F}{M} = \dfrac{S_0}{\theta \cdot X}$ | **F/M Ratio (Food : Microorganism)**; $S_0$ = influent soluble BOD₅ | day⁻¹ (d⁻¹) |
| 4 | $\eta = \dfrac{S_0 - S}{S_0}$ | **Fractional BOD removal efficiency**; $S$ = effluent soluble BOD₅ | dimensionless |
| 5 | $Y_{obs} = \dfrac{Y}{1 + k_d \cdot \theta_c}$ | **Observed Yield Coefficient**; $Y$ = true yield, $k_d$ = endogenous decay coefficient | mg VSS / mg BOD₅ |
| 6 | $P_X = Y_{obs} \cdot Q \cdot (S_0 - S)$ | **Daily waste sludge production (Pₓ)** | kg/day |
| 7 | $R = \dfrac{Q_r}{Q} = \dfrac{X}{X_r - X}$ | **Recycle Ratio (R)**; $Q_r$ = RAS flow, $X_r$ = RAS conc., $X$ = MLSS | dimensionless |
| 8 | $O_2 = a' \cdot Q \cdot (S_0 - S) + b' \cdot V \cdot X$ | **Oxygen Requirement**; $a' \approx 0.5$, $b' \approx 0.05$ | kg O₂ / day |
| 9 | $V = \dfrac{\theta \cdot Q \cdot S_0}{X \cdot (F/M)}$ | **Design volume of aeration tank** (rearranged from F/M) | m³ |

> [!NOTE]
> **Canonical KTU Values of Biological Constants**
> - **Y (true yield) = 0.4 – 0.6 mg VSS/mg BOD₅** (use 0.5 default)
> - **k_d (endogenous decay) = 0.04 – 0.08 day⁻¹** (use 0.06 default)
> - **a′ (oxygen for synthesis) ≈ 0.5 kg O₂/kg BOD₅ removed**
> - **b′ (oxygen for endogenous respiration) ≈ 0.05 – 0.15 kg O₂/kg MLVSS·day**

## 2.3 KTU-Relevant Process Variations (High-Frequency Exam Content)

> [!TIP]
> KTU questions frequently ask: *"Compare Conventional, Extended Aeration, and Completely Mixed ASP."* Use this comparison block:

| Variant | F/M (d⁻¹) | SRT (d) | HRT (h) | MLSS (mg/L) | Sludge Age Character |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| **Conventional (Plug-flow)** | 0.2 – 0.5 | 5 – 15 | 4 – 8 | 1,500 – 3,000 | Moderate |
| **Completely Mixed** | 0.2 – 0.5 | 5 – 15 | 4 – 8 | 3,000 – 6,000 | Shock-load resistant |
| **Extended Aeration** | 0.05 – 0.15 | 20 – 40 | 18 – 36 | 3,000 – 6,000 | Near endogenous |
| **High-Rate** | 0.5 – 1.5 | 2 – 4 | 2 – 4 | 4,000 – 10,000 | Low efficiency |
| **SBR (Sequencing Batch)** | 0.05 – 0.3 | 10 – 30 | Variable | 2,000 – 5,000 | Time-based cycling |

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Dairy industry:** Treats 1,000–3,000 mg/L BOD effluents; often paired with **anaerobic + ASP** for energy recovery.
- **Breweries:** Highly variable flow → **completely-mixed ASP** preferred for shock-load buffering.
- **Distilleries:** Spent wash (BOD ~ 50,000 mg/L) → **biogas (UASB) + polishing ASP**.
- **Seafood/Meat:** High protein/NH₃ → **nitrification–denitrification variant** of ASP is essential.

> [!WARNING]
> **Common KTU Mistake:** Students often treat ASP as a stand-alone "end-of-pipe" solution for food industry wastewater. In KTU valuation, full credit requires stating that ASP is a **secondary** treatment and is **invariably preceded by primary treatment and often followed by tertiary polishing** for food industry effluents.
