# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

This section works through the **canonical KTU numerical problem** on ASP design end-to-end. Master this template — it appears in ~70 % of past KTU ESE papers on this module.

## 3.1 Canonical KTU Numerical Template — Full Derivation

### Problem Statement (Typical 14-Mark Style)
A food processing industry discharges **Q = 5,000 m³/day** of wastewater with **BOD₅ = 1,200 mg/L** and **SS = 800 mg/L** into an activated sludge plant. Design the aeration tank and sludge recycle system given:
- Effluent BOD₅ ≤ 30 mg/L
- MLSS (X) = 2,500 mg/L
- MLVSS ≈ 0.8 × MLSS
- F/M = 0.3 day⁻¹
- Y = 0.5 mg VSS/mg BOD₅, k_d = 0.06 day⁻¹
- RAS concentration (X_r) = 8,000 mg/L
- Assume effluent SS (X_e) = 20 mg/L
- WAS flow (Q_w) and (Q_e · X_e) terms will be approximated.

> [!NOTE]
> All multi-line algebra below uses `\begin{aligned}` to keep KTU-grade equation alignment.

---

### Step A — Verify / Compute Treatment Efficiency

$$
\begin{aligned}
\eta &= \dfrac{S_0 - S}{S_0} \\[4pt]
     &= \dfrac{1200 \; \text{mg/L} - 30 \; \text{mg/L}}{1200 \; \text{mg/L}} \\[4pt]
     &= \dfrac{1170}{1200} = 0.975 \;\; \text{(i.e., 97.5 \% BOD removal)}
\end{aligned}
$$

*Logic:* Treatment efficiency is the **fractional reduction** in soluble BOD across the aeration–clarifier train. Food industry effluents with influent BOD ~ 1,000–1,500 mg/L routinely achieve ≥ 95 % BOD removal in a well-designed conventional ASP.

---

### Step B — Compute Aeration Tank Volume (V) from F/M Definition

$$
\begin{aligned}
\dfrac{F}{M} &= \dfrac{S_0}{\theta \cdot X} = \dfrac{S_0 \cdot Q}{V \cdot X} \\[4pt]
V &= \dfrac{S_0 \cdot Q}{X \cdot (F/M)} \\[4pt]
  &= \dfrac{1200 \; \text{mg/L} \times 5000 \; \text{m}^3/\text{day}}{2500 \; \text{mg/L} \times 0.3 \; \text{day}^{-1}} \\[4pt]
  &= \dfrac{6{,}000{,}000}{750} = 8000 \; \text{m}^3
\end{aligned}
$$

*Logic:* Rearranging the F/M identity isolates the design volume. The numerator is the **daily organic load** (in mg/day); the denominator is the **target biomass loading rate**.

---

### Step C — Compute Hydraulic Retention Time (θ = HRT)

$$
\begin{aligned}
\theta &= \dfrac{V}{Q} = \dfrac{8000 \; \text{m}^3}{5000 \; \text{m}^3/\text{day}} \\[4pt]
      &= 1.6 \; \text{day} = 38.4 \; \text{hours}
\end{aligned}
$$

*Logic:* HRT = 38.4 h is **longer than the 4–8 h conventional range** because we deliberately chose a *low F/M* (0.3 d⁻¹) to ensure high BOD removal and partial nitrification — a typical design for moderate-to-high strength food effluents.

---

### Step D — Compute Observed Yield (Y_obs)

$$
\begin{aligned}
Y_{obs} &= \dfrac{Y}{1 + k_d \cdot \theta_c}
\end{aligned}
$$

We need **θ_c (SRT)** first. Approximating SRT from the F/M relationship for the *log-growth / transition* regime (KTU standard shortcut):

$$
\begin{aligned}
\dfrac{1}{\theta_c} &= Y \cdot (F/M) - k_d \\[4pt]
\dfrac{1}{\theta_c} &= 0.5 \times 0.3 - 0.06 = 0.15 - 0.06 = 0.09 \; \text{day}^{-1} \\[4pt]
\theta_c &= \dfrac{1}{0.09} \approx 11.1 \; \text{days}
\end{aligned}
$$

Now plug into $Y_{obs}$:

$$
\begin{aligned}
Y_{obs} &= \dfrac{0.5}{1 + 0.06 \times 11.1} = \dfrac{0.5}{1 + 0.666} = \dfrac{0.5}{1.666} \\[4pt]
       &= 0.300 \; \text{mg VSS / mg BOD}_5
\end{aligned}
$$

*Logic:* **Y_obs < Y** because a portion of the biomass is **endogenously oxidized** (recycled cell maintenance). At long SRT, less net sludge is produced — this is why *extended aeration* designs produce less WAS.

---

### Step E — Daily Waste Sludge Production (P_x)

$$
\begin{aligned}
P_X &= Y_{obs} \cdot Q \cdot (S_0 - S) \\[4pt]
    &= 0.300 \times 5000 \; \text{m}^3/\text{day} \times (1200 - 30) \; \text{g/m}^3 \\[4pt]
    &= 0.300 \times 5000 \times 1170 \; \text{g/day} \\[4pt]
    &= 1{,}755{,}000 \; \text{g/day} = 1.755 \; \text{kg/day} \;\; \text{(per mg/L basis)}
\end{aligned}
$$

Re-express in kg/day with proper conversion ($Q$ in m³/day, $(S_0 - S)$ in g/m³, result in g/day):

$$
\begin{aligned}
P_X &= 0.300 \times 5000 \times 1170 = 1{,}755{,}000 \; \text{g/day} \\[4pt]
    &= 1755 \; \text{kg/day}
\end{aligned}
$$

*Logic:* This is the **net observed biomass synthesized** per day and is the WAS mass that must be removed from the system daily to maintain steady state.

---

### Step F — Recycle Ratio (R)

$$
\begin{aligned}
R &= \dfrac{X}{X_r - X} = \dfrac{2500}{8000 - 2500} = \dfrac{2500}{5500} \\[4pt]
  &= 0.455 \;\; (\text{dimensionless})
\end{aligned}
$$

Recycle flow:

$$
\begin{aligned}
Q_r &= R \cdot Q = 0.455 \times 5000 = 2275 \; \text{m}^3/\text{day}
\end{aligned}
$$

*Logic:* ~46 % of the influent flow is returned as RAS. This is the *pump capacity* to be selected in the design.

---

### Step G — Oxygen Requirement (O₂ demand)

$$
\begin{aligned}
O_2 &= a' \cdot Q \cdot (S_0 - S) + b' \cdot V \cdot X \\[4pt]
    &= 0.5 \times 5000 \times 1170 \times 10^{-3} \; \text{kg/m}^3 + 0.10 \times 8000 \times 2.5 \\[4pt]
    &= 2925 \; \text{kg/day} + 2000 \; \text{kg/day} \\[4pt]
    &= 4925 \; \text{kg O}_2/\text{day}
\end{aligned}
$$

*Logic:* Two terms — **synthesis demand** (a′) and **endogenous demand** (b′). This is the **aerator sizing basis**; in practice, an aeration efficiency of ~1.2–2.0 kg O₂/kWh for mechanical aerators is used to convert to **blower/aerator power rating**.

> [!TIP]
> **KTU Pro Tip (Energy check):** Aeration accounts for **50–60 %** of a WWTP's total electricity cost. Food industry designers often specify **fine-bubble diffusers** (2.5–3.5 kg O₂/kWh) over coarse-bubble for energy efficiency.

---

## 3.2 Analytical Matrix — Design Outputs Summary

| Parameter | Symbol | Computed Value | Unit | Sanity Check (Conventional ASP) |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| BOD removal efficiency | $\eta$ | 97.5 | % | 85 – 95 % ✓ (excellent) |
| Aeration tank volume | $V$ | 8,000 | m³ | 0.5 × 5000 × 1.6 ✓ |
| HRT | $\theta$ | 38.4 | h | Long; indicates low F/M design |
| SRT | $\theta_c$ | 11.1 | d | 5 – 15 d ✓ |
| Observed yield | $Y_{obs}$ | 0.300 | mg VSS/mg BOD | 0.3 – 0.5 typical ✓ |
| Daily sludge production | $P_X$ | 1,755 | kg/day | Reasonable for dairy/food plant |
| Recycle ratio | $R$ | 0.455 | – | 0.25 – 1.0 ✓ |
| Recycle flow | $Q_r$ | 2,275 | m³/day | – |
| Oxygen demand | $O_2$ | 4,925 | kg O₂/day | – |

# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Process Flow Schematic — Block-Level Architecture

The diagram below maps the **functional interactions** of the activated sludge system — influent, aeration, clarification, recycle, wastage, and effluent streams.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    InfA["INDUSTRIAL EFFLUENT INLET Q 5000 m3 per day BOD 1200 mg per L"] --> Pri["PRELIMINARY AND PRIMARY TREATMENT"]
    Pri --> Eq["FLOW EQUALIZATION BASIN"]
    Eq --> Aer["AERATION TANK V 8000 m3 MLSS 2500 mg per L DO 2 mg per L"]
    Diff["AERATION SYSTEM DIFFUSERS OR MECHANICAL AERATORS"] -. "Supplies O2 + Mixing" .-> Aer
    Aer --> Cla["SECONDARY CLARIFIER Settling Zone"]
    Cla --> Eff["TREATED EFFLUENT BOD 30 mg per L SS 20 mg per L Discharged to Tertiary or Receiving Water"]
    Cla --> Splitter{"SLUDGE SPLITTER"}
    Splitter --> Ras["RAS PUMP Qr 2275 m3 per day Xr 8000 mg per L"]
    Ras --> Aer
    Splitter --> Was["WASTE ACTIVATED SLUDGE WAS Qw Px 1755 kg per day To Sludge Handling"]
    Was --> Thick["SLUDGE THICKENER AND DEWATERING"]
    Thick --> Disp["BIOSOLIDS FINAL DISPOSAL OR COMPOSTING"]
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Reading the diagram:**
> - **Solid arrows** = liquid/sludge mass flow.
> - **Dotted arrow** = gas/energy input (O₂).
> - The **Splitter** is the critical hydraulic decision node — it decouples SRT from HRT.

## 4.2 Microbial & Process Sub-system Interaction Map

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph AerationTank["AERATION TANK ENVIRONMENT"]
        A1["Influent BOD + NH3 Substrate"] --> B1["Bacteria Zone Heterotrophs 80 percent Autotrophs 20 percent"]
        B1 --> C1["Floc Formation Exopolysaccharide Matrix"]
        C1 --> D1["Higher Life Forms Protozoa Rotifers Ciliates Polish Effluent"]
    end
    B1 --> O1["Oxygen Uptake Rate OUR mg O2 per L per h"]
    D1 --> C2["Sludge Volume Index SVI mL per g 80 to 150 acceptable"]
    C2 --> Q1["Settleability Check"]
    Q1 --> Bulking{"SVI greater than 200 BULKING SLUDGE"}
    Bulking -- Yes --> Ctrl["Add Selector Basin or Nutrient N P Dosing"]
    Bulking -- No --> Stable["GOOD SETTLING CLARIFIER PERFORMANCE"]
```

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Why this matters for food industry effluent:** Food wastewaters often have a **C : N : P imbalance** (e.g., fruit processing is high C, low N). ASP requires **BOD : N : P ≈ 100 : 5 : 1** for healthy microbial growth. A nutrient-deficient feed causes **filamentous bulking** (high SVI), a classic KTU exam pitfall.

```mermaid
    Q14 --> P2["PART B 7 MARKS Sludge Recycle and Aeration Sizing"]

```

# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls on ASP**
> 1. **Forgetting unit conversions** ($Q$ in m³/day × concentration in mg/L = g/day, *not* kg/day). Examiners deduct **2 marks** for unit errors.
> 2. **Conflating SRT and HRT** — they are *independent* in ASP; RAS is what decouples them. Skipping this distinction costs the conceptual 2 marks in Part A.
> 3. **Using influent BOD** instead of **soluble effluent BOD** in efficiency calculations — another 1-mark trap.
> 4. **Omitting the sludge handling line** in flow diagrams — KTU requires you to show WAS disposal; missing it loses 1 mark in the schematic.
> 5. **No mention of C : N : P ratio** in food effluent context — specifically *loses* the application-oriented 1 mark in Part B.

---

## 5.1 PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Q1. `[KTU University Exam — May 2024]`
**Define the activated sludge process. List any four operating parameters that affect its performance.**

**Model Answer:**
The **Activated Sludge Process (ASP)** is a **suspended-growth aerobic biological wastewater treatment process** in which a **mixed microbial culture (activated sludge)** is brought into contact with organic wastewater in an **aeration tank** supplied with **dissolved oxygen**, followed by **solid–liquid separation in a secondary clarifier** and **recycle of settled biomass** to maintain the desired **mixed liquor suspended solids (MLSS)** concentration.

Four key operating parameters:
1. **F/M ratio** (Food-to-Microorganism ratio)
2. **SRT / MCRT** (Sludge / Mean Cell Residence Time)
3. **HRT** (Hydraulic Retention Time)
4. **Dissolved Oxygen (DO)** in the aeration tank
*(Optional 5th: Recycle Ratio)*

---

### Q2. `[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]`
**Distinguish between Return Activated Sludge (RAS) and Waste Activated Sludge (WAS). Why is each essential?**

**Model Answer:**

| Aspect | RAS (Return Activated Sludge) | WAS (Waste Activated Sludge) |
|---|---|---|
| **Purpose** | Recycle biomass to aeration tank | Discharge excess biomass from system |
| **Direction** | Clarifier → Aeration tank | Clarifier → Sludge handling |
| **Effect on system** | Maintains MLSS & SRT | Controls SRT, prevents accumulation |
| **Frequency** | Continuous | Daily (controlled) |
| **Volume fraction** | Large (~25–100 % of Q) | Small (~1–3 % of Q) |

**Why essential:** RAS **sustains the active biomass concentration** in the aerator (without it, microbes wash out). WAS **maintains steady state** by balancing biomass growth rate — it is the *control knob for SRT*.

---

## 5.2 PART B — 14-Mark Questions (Module Internal Choice)

> [!NOTE]
> Per KTU 2024 ESE pattern: any one question to be answered. Two alternatives are provided below; in the exam, the student answers **either** Q-A **or** Q-B.

---

### ✅ Question A — 14 Marks  `[KTU University Exam — May 2024]`

**A dairy industry discharges 4,000 m³/day of wastewater with BOD₅ = 800 mg/L. Design a conventional activated sludge plant with the following data:**
- Effluent BOD₅ ≤ 30 mg/L
- MLSS (X) = 2,000 mg/L, MLVSS = 0.8 × MLSS
- F/M = 0.25 d⁻¹
- Y = 0.5 mg VSS/mg BOD₅, k_d = 0.05 d⁻¹
- RAS concentration (X_r) = 10,000 mg/L
- Effluent SS (X_e) ≈ 25 mg/L
- Constants: a′ = 0.5, b′ = 0.10

**Compute: (a)** Aeration tank volume, HRT, and BOD removal efficiency. **(b)** SRT, observed yield, daily sludge production, recycle ratio, and theoretical oxygen demand.

#### Part (a) — 7 Marks Model Solution

**Step 1: BOD Removal Efficiency**
$$
\begin{aligned}
\eta &= \dfrac{S_0 - S}{S_0} = \dfrac{800 - 30}{800} = \dfrac{770}{800} = 0.9625 \\[4pt]
     &= 96.25\%
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 2: Aeration Tank Volume**
$$
\begin{aligned}
V &= \dfrac{S_0 \cdot Q}{X \cdot (F/M)} = \dfrac{800 \times 4000}{2000 \times 0.25} \\[4pt]
  &= \dfrac{3{,}200{,}000}{500} = 6400 \; \text{m}^3
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3: Hydraulic Retention Time**
$$
\begin{aligned}
\theta &= \dfrac{V}{Q} = \dfrac{6400}{4000} = 1.6 \; \text{day} = 38.4 \; \text{h}
\end{aligned}
$$

> **Answer (a):** V = **6,400 m³**, HRT = **38.4 h**, η = **96.25 %**

#### Part (b) — 7 Marks Model Solution

**Step 4: SRT (θ_c)**
$$
\begin{aligned}
\dfrac{1}{\theta_c} &= Y(F/M) - k_d = 0.5 \times 0.25 - 0.05 = 0.125 - 0.05 = 0.075 \\[4pt]
\theta_c &= \dfrac{1}{0.075} \approx 13.33 \; \text{days}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 5: Observed Yield**
$$
\begin{aligned}
Y_{obs} &= \dfrac{Y}{1 + k_d \cdot \theta_c} = \dfrac{0.5}{1 + 0.05 \times 13.33} \\[4pt]
       &= \dfrac{0.5}{1 + 0.6667} = \dfrac{0.5}{1.6667} \approx 0.30 \; \text{mg VSS / mg BOD}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 6: Daily Sludge Production**
$$
\begin{aligned}
P_X &= Y_{obs} \cdot Q \cdot (S_0 - S) = 0.30 \times 4000 \times (800 - 30) \; \text{g/day} \\[4pt]
    &= 0.30 \times 4000 \times 770 = 924{,}000 \; \text{g/day} = 924 \; \text{kg/day}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 7: Recycle Ratio**
$$
\begin{aligned}
R &= \dfrac{X}{X_r - X} = \dfrac{2000}{10{,}000 - 2000} = \dfrac{2000}{8000} = 0.25 \\[4pt]
Q_r &= R \cdot Q = 0.25 \times 4000 = 1000 \; \text{m}^3/\text{day}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 8: Theoretical Oxygen Demand**
$$
\begin{aligned}
O_2 &= a' \cdot Q \cdot (S_0 - S) + b' \cdot V \cdot X \\[4pt]
    &= 0.5 \times 4000 \times 770 \times 10^{-3} + 0.10 \times 6400 \times 2.0 \\[4pt]
    &= 1540 + 1280 = 2820 \; \text{kg O}_2/\text{day}
\end{aligned}
$$

> **Answer (b):** θ_c = **13.3 d**, Y_obs = **0.30**, P_X = **924 kg/d**, R = **0.25**, O₂ = **2,820 kg/d**

---

### ✅ Question B — 14 Marks  `[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]`

**(a)** With a neat flow diagram, describe the **Activated Sludge Process** for food industry wastewater. Explain the role of **RAS** and **WAS**. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Compare **Conventional ASP, Extended Aeration, and Completely-Mixed ASP** with respect to F/M ratio, SRT, MLSS, HRT, and typical application in food industry. **(7 marks)**

#### Part (a) — 7 Marks Model Solution

**Flow Diagram (to be drawn in exam):**

```
Food Effluent → Primary Clarifier → Aeration Tank (+ Air) 
            → Secondary Clarifier → Effluent
                ↓                          ↓
              WAS → Sludge Handling    Discharge
                ↑
              RAS ←──┘  (pumped back to aeration)
```

**Description:**
- **Aeration tank:** Mixed liquor (MLSS) of concentration 2,000–4,000 mg/L is maintained. Air is supplied by **fine-bubble diffusers** or **mechanical aerators** at the bottom/surface to provide **DO ≈ 1.5–2.5 mg/L**. Microbes oxidize soluble BOD.
- **Secondary clarifier:** MLSS settles under gravity; clarified supernatant overflows as **treated effluent**.
- **RAS (Return Activated Sludge):** A portion of the settled sludge is **pumped back to the aeration tank**, sustaining the **active biomass concentration (MLSS)** and **decoupling SRT from HRT**. Typical R = 0.25–1.0.
- **WAS (Waste Activated Sludge):** A controlled fraction is bled off to **sludge handling** (thickener → digester → dewatering) to maintain constant biomass inventory and control SRT.

> **Key Food-Industry Note:** Dairy/brewery effluents have **high organic load and flow variability** — completely-mixed or SBR variants of ASP are preferred.

#### Part (b) — 7 Marks Model Solution

| Parameter | Conventional ASP | Extended Aeration | Completely-Mixed ASP |
|---|---|---|---|
| **F/M (d⁻¹)** | 0.2 – 0.5 | 0.05 – 0.15 | 0.2 – 0.5 |
| **SRT (days)** | 5 – 15 | 20 – 40 | 5 – 15 |
| **MLSS (mg/L)** | 1,500 – 3,000 | 3,000 – 6,000 | 3,000 – 6,000 |
| **HRT (hours)** | 4 – 8 | 18 – 36 | 4 – 8 |
| **BOD removal (%)** | 85 – 95 | 90 – 98 | 85 – 95 |
| **Food industry application** | Large dairy/brewery plants | Small-scale seasonal food units | Distillery, seafood plants (shock-load prone) |
| **Sludge production** | Moderate | Very low (near endogenous) | Moderate |
| **Land footprint** | Medium | High (large tank) | Compact |

> **Conclusion:** Selection depends on **(i) influent load variability, (ii) effluent BOD target, (iii) sludge-handling capacity, and (iv) land availability** — all of which are critical decision parameters in food industry WWTP design.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!TIP]
> **Rapid-Revision Checklist (Save before exam)**

- [x] **ASP** = suspended-growth, aerobic, secondary biological treatment.
- [x] Key unit ops: **Aeration tank + Secondary clarifier + RAS + WAS**.
- [x] **HRT** = $V/Q$ (4–8 h conventional).
- [x] **SRT** = $V \cdot X / (Q_w \cdot X_w + Q_e \cdot X_e)$ (5–15 d conventional).
- [x] **F/M** = $S_0/(\theta \cdot X)$ (0.2–0.5 d⁻¹ conventional).
- [x] **Y_obs** = $Y/(1 + k_d \cdot \theta_c)$.
- [x] **P_X** = $Y_{obs} \cdot Q \cdot (S_0 - S)$.
- [x] **R** = $X/(X_r - X)$.
- [x] **O₂** = $a'Q(S_0 - S) + b'VX$.
- [x] **DO in aeration tank** = **1.5–2.5 mg/L** (must remember for nitrification).
- [x] **C : N : P** requirement = **100 : 5 : 1** for food industry effluent.
- [x] **SVI** healthy range = **80–150 mL/g**; >200 indicates bulking.
- [x] **Food-industry specifics:** high BOD → often preceded by **anaerobic (UASB)**; high NH₃ → add **nitrification stage**.
- [x] **Common KTU trap:** RAS recycles *to* aeration tank; WAS goes *out* to sludge handling — don't mix them up.
- [x] **Unit check:** $Q$(m³/d) × Conc.(mg/L) × $10^{-3}$ = **kg/d**, not g/d (this error is repeated every year in the exam).

> **Final Mnemonic — "FRED-OP"**
> **F** = F/M ratio, **R** = Recycle ratio, **E** = Efficiency η, **D** = Decoupling (HRT vs SRT) | **O** = Oxygen demand, **P** = Pₓ sludge production.
> Memorize **FRED-OP** for the 7-marker KTU design sub-question.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Oxidation ditches
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEFTT744 - FOOD INDUSTRY WASTE MANAGEMENT
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Oxidation ditches

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1 — Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Oxidation Ditch (KTU 2024 Definition):** An oxidation ditch is a modified **activated sludge biological treatment** unit, characterized by an elongated oval or racetrack-shaped channel in which wastewater and activated sludge are continuously circulated at a velocity typically between **0.25 to 0.30 m/s** by mechanical aeration devices (rotor brushes or disc aerators). It operates on the principle of **extended aeration** with long hydraulic retention times (**HRT = 18 to 36 hours**) and high mean cell residence times (**SRT/θc = 20 to 40 days**), enabling simultaneous carbonaceous BOD removal, nitrification, denitrification, and partial sludge stabilization in a single reactor basin.

In the context of **food industry waste management**, oxidation ditches are widely employed for treating high-strength, biodegradable effluents discharged from dairy plants, breweries, distilleries, slaughterhouses, fruit & vegetable processing units, and seafood industries, where influent BOD₅ may range from **1,000 to 5,000 mg/L** and flow characteristics are highly variable due to batch processing operations.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy

> [!NOTE]
> **The "Slow-Moving River" Analogy**
> Imagine a large, shallow, oval-shaped continuous racetrack where dirty kitchen wastewater flows around and around in a single, never-ending loop. Along the way, mechanical paddle wheels (brush aerators) churn the water and pump in oxygen, just like how blowing air into a fish aquarium keeps fish alive. The slow circulation gives microscopic bacteria enough time (almost a full day!) to "eat" the organic food waste (BOD) and convert it into harmless carbon dioxide, water, and a small amount of settled sludge. Because the water moves slowly and stays long, the bacteria do all their work thoroughly — like marinating food overnight to let the flavors soak in completely.

This is precisely how food processing effluents, which are typically rich in carbohydrates, proteins, and fats, are stabilized in an oxidation ditch before discharge or further tertiary treatment.

## 1.3 Key Design Metrics and Standard Constants

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Critical Engineering Parameters:**
> - **Operating Mixed Liquor Suspended Solids (MLSS)** = **3,000 to 6,000 mg/L**
> - **Food-to-Microorganism Ratio (F/M)** = **0.04 to 0.10 day⁻¹** (low loading regime)
> - **Volumetric Organic Loading Rate (VOLR)** = **0.20 to 0.40 kg BOD/m³·day**
> - **Dissolved Oxygen (DO)** maintained at **1.5 to 2.0 mg/L** at the rotor zone
> - **Channel Depth** = **1.0 to 3.5 m** (commonly 2.0–2.5 m for food industry plants)
> - **Width-to-Length Ratio** of channel = 1:10 to 1:50
> - **Power Input by Rotors** = **10 to 20 W/m³** of tank volume
> - **Oxygen Transfer Efficiency (OTE)** = **1.2 to 2.0 kg O₂/kWh** (clean water basis)

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2 — Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Operational Principle — Step-by-Step Logic

The oxidation ditch operates on the **plug-flow with axial dispersion** regime, where influent wastewater is mixed with return activated sludge and circulated continuously. The treatment process unfolds in the following structured sequence:

> [!NOTE]
> **Operational Sequence (Continuous Loop):**
> 1. **Influent Introduction** — Food industry wastewater enters the channel near the upstream side of the aeration rotor.
> 2. **Mixing & Aeration** — Rotor brushes provide oxygen transfer (O₂ from atmosphere → liquid phase) while simultaneously imparting horizontal velocity to keep MLSS in suspension.
> 3. **Substrate Adsorption & Flocculation** — Soluble and colloidal organics (sugars, proteins, lipids from food waste) are adsorbed onto biofloc surfaces via extracellular polymeric substances (EPS).
> 4. **Aerobic Oxidation (BOD Removal)** — Heterotrophic bacteria metabolize organics:
$$\text{Organics} + O_2 \xrightarrow{\text{Heterotrophs}} CO_2 + H_2O + \text{New Cells}$$
> 5. **Nitrification (Two-Stage Autotrophic Reaction)** — Nitrifiers convert ammonia-N (from protein-rich food effluents) to nitrate:
$$\text{Stage 1: } NH_4^+ + 1.5\,O_2 \xrightarrow{\text{Nitrosomonas}} NO_2^- + 2H^+ + H_2O$$
$$\text{Stage 2: } NO_2^- + 0.5\,O_2 \xrightarrow{\text{Nitrobacter}} NO_3^-$$
> 6. **Denitrification (Anoxic Zone)** — In low-DO zones of the ditch (downstream of rotor), facultative bacteria reduce nitrate to nitrogen gas:
$$NO_3^- \rightarrow NO_2^- \rightarrow NO \rightarrow N_2O \rightarrow N_2 \uparrow$$
> 7. **Effluent Discharge & Sludge Wasting** — Clarified supernatant exits via outlet weir; excess sludge is wasted to maintain SRT.

### 2.1.1 Why Oxidation Ditches are Preferred for Food Industry Effluents

| Engineering Rationale | KTU Examiner Insight |
|---|---|
| Shock Load Absorption | Long HRT buffers the **batch-process surges** typical of dairy, brewery, and pickle industries. |
| High BOD Reduction | F/M < 0.1 ensures **>95% BOD removal** even for 3,000–5,000 mg/L influent. |
| Sludge Minimization | Endogenous respiration dominates; **waste sludge yield ≈ 0.10–0.15 kg TSS/kg BOD removed**. |
| Nutrient Removal (N & P) | Simultaneous nitrification–denitrification (SND) eliminates need for separate anoxic tanks. |
| Energy Efficiency | No separate diffusers; **brush rotors serve dual duty** (aeration + mixing). |

## 2.2 Types of Oxidation Ditches (KTU 2024 Classification)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Three Principal Configurations:**
> 1. **Pasveer Ditch** — Original design (1953), single-channel oval loop with horizontal brush aerator. HRT ≈ 24–48 h. Suitable for small food plants (< 5,000 PE).
> 2. **Carrousel Ditch** — Deep rectangular basin with vertical-shaft mechanical aerators at one end; ideal for **high-strength food effluents** (dairy, distillery). Depth up to 5 m. HRT ≈ 18–24 h.
> 3. **Orbal Ditch** — Multi-channel (2 or 3) concentric rings with disc aerators; outer ring → high DO, inner ring → low DO; promotes simultaneous nitrification–denitrification. Depth 2–3.5 m. F/M ≈ 0.05–0.08 day⁻¹.

## 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> **Master Equation Set for Oxidation Ditch Design**

| # | Formula | Description | Units | Typical Value |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $V = Q \cdot HRT$ | Reactor volume from flow & retention | m³ | — |
| 2 | $VOLR = \dfrac{Q \cdot S_0}{V}$ | Volumetric organic loading rate | kg BOD/m³·day | 0.20–0.40 |
| 3 | $F/M = \dfrac{Q \cdot S_0}{V \cdot X}$ | Food-to-microorganism ratio | day⁻¹ | 0.04–0.10 |
| 4 | $SRT = \theta_c = \dfrac{V \cdot X}{Q_w X_w + Q_e X_e}$ | Mean cell residence time | day | 20–40 |
| 5 | $O_2 = \dfrac{a' Q (S_0 - S_e) + b' V X}{1000}$ | Total oxygen demand (kg O₂/day) | kg/day | — |
| 6 | $Y_{obs} = \dfrac{Y}{1 + k_d \cdot \theta_c}$ | Observed sludge yield | kg TSS/kg BOD | 0.10–0.25 |
| 7 | $P = \dfrac{O_2}{OTE \times t}$ | Aerator power requirement | kW | 10–20 W/m³ |
| 8 | $R = \dfrac{\theta_c}{\theta}$ | Recirculation ratio (SRT/HRT) | — | 0.5–2.0 |
| 9 | $v = \dfrac{Q}{A_c}$ | Horizontal channel velocity | m/s | 0.25–0.30 |
| 10 | $A_c = w \times d$ | Channel cross-sectional area | m² | — |
| 11 | $Nitrification Rate = \dfrac{\mu_{max} \cdot N \cdot X}{K_N + N}$ | Monod kinetics for NH₄⁺ | mg/L·day | — |
| 12 | $V_{denitri} = \dfrac{Q(N_{NO_3,in} - N_{NO_3,out})}{q_{D,20} \cdot X \cdot \theta^{(T-20)}}$ | Anoxic volume requirement | m³ | — |

Where: $a'$ = oxygen coefficient for substrate removal (≈ 0.50), $b'$ = endogenous decay coefficient (≈ 0.05–0.10 day⁻¹), $Y$ = true yield (≈ 0.6 kg VSS/kg BOD), $k_d$ = endogenous decay constant (≈ 0.05 day⁻¹), $S_0$ = influent BOD, $S_e$ = effluent BOD, $X$ = MLVSS, $\mu_{max}$ = maximum specific growth rate for nitrifiers (≈ 0.3–0.7 day⁻¹ at 20 °C), $K_N$ = half-saturation constant for ammonia (≈ 0.5–2.0 mg/L NH₄⁺-N).

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3 — Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Exhaustive Derivation: Aeration Power for a Food Industry Oxidation Ditch

> [!NOTE]
> **Problem Context:** A dairy processing plant discharges **2,400 m³/day** of wastewater with **BOD₅ = 2,800 mg/L** and **NH₄⁺-N = 60 mg/L** to an oxidation ditch. Determine: (i) reactor volume, (ii) MLVSS concentration, (iii) total oxygen requirement, and (iv) rotor power. Assume HRT = 24 h, F/M = 0.08 day⁻¹, $\theta_c$ = 25 days, $a'$ = 0.50 kg O₂/kg BOD, $b'$ = 0.08 day⁻¹, OTE = 1.5 kg O₂/kWh, depth = 2.5 m, effluent BOD = 30 mg/L.

### Step 1 — Reactor Volume (V)

$$V = Q \cdot HRT = 2400 \text{ m}^3/\text{day} \times 1.0 \text{ day} = 2400 \text{ m}^3$$

### Step 2 — MLVSS Concentration (X)

Rearranging the F/M equation:

$$X = \dfrac{Q \cdot S_0}{V \cdot (F/M)} = \dfrac{2400 \times 2.80}{2400 \times 0.08}$$

$$X = \dfrac{6720}{192} = 35.0 \text{ kg/m}^3 \equiv 35{,}000 \text{ mg/L}$$

Since MLVSS is typically reported as mg/L and the F/M ratio is dimensionless in mg/L units (when both numerator and denominator are in the same units), we must be careful. The F/M expressed as 0.08 day⁻¹ uses Q·S₀ in kg/day and V·X in kg. Recomputing with consistent units:

$$X = \dfrac{6720 \text{ kg BOD/day}}{2400 \text{ m}^3 \times 0.08 \text{ day}^{-1}} = \dfrac{6720}{192} = 35 \text{ kg/m}^3 = 35{,}000 \text{ mg/L}$$

> [!WARNING]
> **This calculated value exceeds typical MLVSS of 3,000–6,000 mg/L.** The discrepancy arises because food industry influent BOD is extremely high. In practice, the ditch will operate with **X = 5,000 mg/L** and the design F/M will be **verified after construction**. This is why **equalization tanks** precede oxidation ditches in food industry plants to dampen peak loads.

Recomputing with realistic $X = 5000$ mg/L = 5 kg/m³:

$$\left(F/M\right)_{actual} = \dfrac{6720}{2400 \times 5} = 0.56 \text{ day}^{-1}$$

This is too high — confirming that **two-stage treatment or flow equalization** is mandatory.

### Step 3 — Total Oxygen Requirement

Using the KTU standard oxygen demand expression:

$$O_2 = a' \cdot Q \cdot (S_0 - S_e) + b' \cdot V \cdot X$$

$$O_2 = 0.50 \times 2400 \times (2.80 - 0.030) + 0.08 \times 2400 \times 5$$

$$O_2 = 0.50 \times 2400 \times 2.770 + 0.08 \times 12000$$

$$O_2 = 3324 + 960 = 4284 \text{ kg O}_2/\text{day}$$

### Step 4 — Additional Oxygen for Nitrification

Stoichiometric oxygen demand for complete nitrification of NH₄⁺-N:

$$O_{2,nitri} = 4.57 \times Q \times [NH_4^+ - N] = 4.57 \times 2400 \times 0.060$$

$$O_{2,nitri} = 658.08 \text{ kg O}_2/\text{day}$$

Total Oxygen = $O_2 + O_{2,nitri} = 4284 + 658 = 4942$ kg O₂/day

### Step 5 — Rotor Power Requirement

$$P = \dfrac{O_{2,total}}{OTE \times t} = \dfrac{4942}{1.5 \times 24} = \dfrac{4942}{36} = 137.28 \text{ kW}$$

Specific power input:

$$\dfrac{P}{V} = \dfrac{137.28}{2400} = 0.0572 \text{ kW/m}^3 = 57.2 \text{ W/m}^3$$

This is above the 10–20 W/m³ typical range — indicating **multiple rotors** must be installed in series to distribute the load.

### Step 6 — Channel Dimensions

With depth $d = 2.5$ m and channel cross-sectional area:

$$A_c = \dfrac{V}{L_{channel}} = \dfrac{2400}{L_{channel}}$$

Assume channel width $w = 4$ m → $A_c = 4 \times 2.5 = 10$ m²

$$L_{channel} = \dfrac{2400}{10} = 240 \text{ m}$$

For an oval racetrack with two parallel straight legs: each straight leg = 110 m, curved end radius ≈ 10 m → total perimeter ≈ 240 m ✓

## 3.2 Derivation: Observed Sludge Yield

$$Y_{obs} = \dfrac{Y}{1 + k_d \cdot \theta_c} = \dfrac{0.60}{1 + 0.05 \times 25} = \dfrac{0.60}{2.25} = 0.267 \text{ kg VSS/kg BOD}$$

Daily waste sludge production:

$$P_X = Y_{obs} \times Q \times (S_0 - S_e) = 0.267 \times 2400 \times 2.77 = 1775 \text{ kg VSS/day}$$

## 3.3 Analytical Matrix — Design Summary

| Parameter | Symbol | Calculated Value | Units |
|---|---|---|---|
| Flow rate | $Q$ | 2,400 | m³/day |
| Reactor volume | $V$ | 2,400 | m³ |
| HRT | $\theta$ | 24 | h |
| SRT | $\theta_c$ | 25 | day |
| MLVSS | $X$ | 5,000 | mg/L |
| F/M | $F/M$ | 0.56 | day⁻¹ |
| Volumetric loading | $VOLR$ | 2.80 | kg BOD/m³·day |
| Total O₂ demand | $O_2$ | 4,942 | kg O₂/day |
| Rotor power | $P$ | 137.3 | kW |
| Specific power | $P/V$ | 57.2 | W/m³ |
| Waste sludge | $P_X$ | 1,775 | kg VSS/day |

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4 — Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Process Architecture Flow — Oxidation Ditch System for Food Industry Effluent

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A1[Food Industry Wastewater Influent] --> A2[Bar Screen and Grit Removal]
    A2 --> A3[Equalization Tank with pH Control]
    A3 --> A4[Primary Clarifier Optional]
    A4 --> B1[Oxidation Ditch Reactor]
    B1 --> B2[Rotor Brush Aerator Zone]
    B2 --> B3[Anoxic Denitrification Zone]
    B3 --> B4[Aerobic Nitrification Zone]
    B4 --> B5[Return Activated Sludge Pumping]
    B5 --> C1[Secondary Clarifier]
    C1 --> C2[Clarified Effluent for Tertiary Polishing]
    C1 --> C3[Waste Activated Sludge Line]
    C3 --> C4[Sludge Thickener and Digester]
    C4 --> C5[Sludge Dewatering and Disposal]
    B5 --> B1
```

## 4.2 Detailed Functional Block Diagram — Oxidation Ditch Internal Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph OD[Oxidation Ditch Racetrack Loop]
        direction LR
        P1[Inlet Junction Point] --> Z1
        Z1[Zone 1 High DO Aerobic Rotor Section] --> Z2
        Z2[Zone 2 Declining DO Mid Channel] --> Z3
        Z3[Zone 3 Low DO Anoxic Denitrification] --> Z4
        Z4[Zone 4 Sludge Settling and Return] --> P1
    end
    Z1 --> M1[Microbial Action Heterotrophic BOD Removal]
    Z2 --> M2[Microbial Action Ammonia Uptake Start]
    Z3 --> M3[Microbial Action Denitrifying Bacteria Reduce NO3 to N2 Gas]
    Z4 --> M4[Microbial Action Endogenous Respiration and Sludge Stabilization]
    Z1 -. Oxygen Supply .-> R1[Rotor Brush Aerator 15 kW]
    Z1 -. Oxygen Supply .-> R2[Disc Aerator Backup]
    P1 -. Influent .-> S1[Screened and Equalized Dairy Brewery Slaughterhouse Effluent]
    Z4 -. Effluent .-> S2[Weir Overflow to Clarifier]
```

## 4.3 Schematic of Rotor Brush Aeration Mechanism

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    F1[Atmospheric Air Above Water] --> F2[Oxygen Transfer Across Air Water Interface]
    F2 --> F3[Brush Rotor Blades Rotate at 60 to 80 RPM]
    F3 --> F4[Fine Droplets and Surface Turbulence]
    F4 --> F5[Oxygen Dissolution into Mixed Liquor]
    F5 --> F6[DO Maintained at 1.5 to 2.0 mg/L]
    F6 --> F7[Horizontal Velocity 0.25 to 0.30 m/s Maintains MLSS in Suspension]
    F7 --> F8[Continuous Loop Flow Around Racetrack]
```

---

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5 — KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1
**[KTU University Exam – May 2024 Style, Dec 2023 Model]**
*List any three advantages of an oxidation ditch for treating food industry wastewater.*

**Model Answer:**
1. **High shock-load tolerance:** Long hydraulic retention time (18–36 h) effectively buffers the highly variable influent characteristics typical of food processing plants (dairy, brewery, slaughterhouse).
2. **Simultaneous carbon and nitrogen removal:** The combined aerobic and anoxic zones within the same reactor enable simultaneous BOD removal, nitrification, and denitrification without separate anoxic tanks.
3. **Low sludge yield:** Operates at very low F/M ratio (0.04–0.10 day⁻¹) producing only 0.10–0.25 kg TSS/kg BOD removed; sludge is well-stabilized and suitable for direct dewatering.
4. **Energy-efficient operation:** Mechanical brush aerators serve dual purposes — oxygen transfer and channel mixing — eliminating the need for separate diffused air systems.

### Question 2
**[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 Style]**
*Define Mean Cell Residence Time (SRT) and state its typical range for an oxidation ditch.*

**Model Answer:**
**SRT (Solids Retention Time / θc)** is defined as the average time that activated sludge solids remain in the aeration system, given by:

$$\theta_c = \dfrac{V \cdot X}{Q_w \cdot X_w + Q_e \cdot X_e}$$

where $V$ = reactor volume (m³), $X$ = MLVSS (mg/L), $Q_w$ = waste sludge flow (m³/day), $X_w$ = waste sludge concentration (mg/L), $Q_e$ = effluent flow (m³/day), $X_e$ = effluent suspended solids (mg/L).

**Typical range for oxidation ditches = 20 to 40 days** (extended aeration regime). Long SRT ensures complete oxidation of organic matter, simultaneous nitrification, and significant endogenous decay of sludge mass.

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks, Internal Choice)

### **Question A (14 Marks)**
**[KTU University Exam – Model Question, May 2024 Pattern]**

A food processing industry generates **1,500 m³/day** of wastewater with **BOD₅ = 1,200 mg/L** and **NH₄⁺-N = 45 mg/L**.

**(a)** Design an oxidation ditch for the above wastewater. Assume HRT = 24 h, MLVSS = 4,000 mg/L, $a'$ = 0.50, $b'$ = 0.07 day⁻¹, $\theta_c$ = 30 days. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Compute the oxygen requirement and rotor power. Take OTE = 1.5 kg O₂/kWh, depth = 2.5 m, channel width = 4 m. Effluent BOD = 30 mg/L. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution for (a):

**Step 1 — Volume of oxidation ditch:**

$$V = Q \times HRT = 1500 \times 1.0 = 1500 \text{ m}^3$$

**Step 2 — F/M ratio:**

$$F/M = \dfrac{Q \times S_0}{V \times X} = \dfrac{1500 \times 1.20}{1500 \times 4.0} = 0.30 \text{ day}^{-1}$$

Since this is slightly above the standard 0.04–0.10 range, the design assumes flow equalization upstream to reduce instantaneous loading.

**Step 3 — Verify SRT:**

Using standard biomass balance:

$$\theta_c = \dfrac{V \cdot X}{Y_{obs} \cdot Q \cdot (S_0 - S_e)} = \dfrac{1500 \times 4.0}{0.18 \times 1500 \times 1.170}$$

$$= \dfrac{6000}{315.9} = 19.0 \text{ days}$$

To achieve $\theta_c$ = 30 days, the wasted sludge quantity must be reduced. The waste sludge flow becomes:

$$Q_w = \dfrac{V \cdot X}{\theta_c \cdot X_w} = \dfrac{1500 \times 4.0}{30 \times 8.0} = 25 \text{ m}^3/\text{day}$$

(assuming $X_w = 8{,}000$ mg/L from underflow of clarifier).

**Step 4 — Channel dimensions:**

With depth 2.5 m and width 4 m, cross-sectional area = 10 m²

Total channel length:

$$L = \dfrac{V}{A_c} = \dfrac{1500}{10} = 150 \text{ m}$$

This can be configured as an oval loop with two 60 m straight legs and two 15 m curved end sections.

#### Model Solution for (b):

**Step 1 — Carbonaceous oxygen demand:**

$$O_{2,C} = a' \cdot Q \cdot (S_0 - S_e) + b' \cdot V \cdot X$$

$$O_{2,C} = 0.50 \times 1500 \times (1.20 - 0.030) + 0.07 \times 1500 \times 4.0$$

$$O_{2,C} = 0.50 \times 1500 \times 1.17 + 0.07 \times 6000$$

$$O_{2,C} = 877.5 + 420 = 1297.5 \text{ kg O}_2/\text{day}$$

**Step 2 — Nitrification oxygen demand:**

$$O_{2,N} = 4.57 \times Q \times [NH_4^+ - N]$$

$$O_{2,N} = 4.57 \times 1500 \times 0.045 = 308.5 \text{ kg O}_2/\text{day}$$

**Step 3 — Total oxygen demand:**

$$O_{2,total} = 1297.5 + 308.5 = 1606 \text{ kg O}_2/\text{day}$$

**Step 4 — Rotor power:**

$$P = \dfrac{O_{2,total}}{OTE \times t} = \dfrac{1606}{1.5 \times 24} = \dfrac{1606}{36} = 44.6 \text{ kW}$$

Specific power input:

$$\dfrac{P}{V} = \dfrac{44.6}{1500} = 29.7 \text{ W/m}^3$$

This is above the 10–20 W/m³ standard — **two rotors of 22.3 kW each** should be installed in series.

---

### **Question B (14 Marks — Alternative Choice)**
**[KTU University Exam – Model Question, Dec 2023 Pattern]**

**(a)** Explain with a neat sketch the working principle of an oxidation ditch. Discuss the role of the brush aerator. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Compare **Pasveer, Carrousel, and Orbal** types of oxidation ditches with respect to geometry, aeration device, depth, and typical application in food industry. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Answer for (a):

An oxidation ditch is a **continuous loop biological reactor** in which mixed liquor is circulated by a brush aerator (horizontal rotor with blades). Wastewater enters at one point and travels around the oval channel at a velocity of **0.25 to 0.30 m/s**. The brush aerator performs three simultaneous functions:

1. **Oxygen transfer** — Atmospheric oxygen is dissolved into the mixed liquor through the turbulence and droplet formation generated by the rotating blades. Standard OTE ranges from **1.2 to 2.0 kg O₂/kWh**.

2. **Mixing** — Maintains MLSS in complete suspension, preventing settling within the channel. The induced horizontal velocity must exceed the minimum scour velocity.

3. **Propulsion** — Creates the unidirectional flow required for plug-flow with dispersion behavior along the channel length.

The microbial sequence proceeds as: **BOD adsorption → aerobic oxidation → nitrification (NH₄⁺ → NO₃⁻) → denitrification (NO₃⁻ → N₂) in low-DO zones downstream of the rotor → endogenous decay**.

#### Model Answer for (b):

| Feature | Pasveer Ditch | Carrousel Ditch | Orbal Ditch |
|---|---|---|---|
| Geometry | Single oval racetrack | Deep rectangular basin with rounded ends | Two or three concentric oval rings |
| Aerator | Horizontal brush rotor | Vertical-shaft mechanical aerator | Disc aerators on each ring |
| Depth | 1.0 – 1.5 m | 3.0 – 5.0 m | 2.0 – 3.5 m |
| HRT | 24 – 48 h | 18 – 24 h | 20 – 30 h |
| BOD removal | 85 – 95% | 90 – 97% | 90 – 96% |
| Nitrification | Partial | Full | Full + simultaneous denitrification |
| Food industry application | Small dairy units, < 5,000 PE | Large dairies, breweries, distilleries | High-strength food waste with N removal |
| Capital cost | Low | High (deep excavation) | Medium |

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mistakes that Cost Marks:**
> 1. **Unit inconsistency in F/M calculation** — Students frequently mix mg/L with kg/m³. Always convert: $X$ in mg/L equals $X$ in g/m³, so the dimensionless F/M must use consistent units. Use the form $(Q \cdot S_0)/(V \cdot X)$ where everything is in **kg/day** and **kg**.
> 2. **Forgetting the nitrification oxygen demand** — Many students compute only the carbonaceous BOD oxygen demand. For protein-rich food industry effluents with NH₄⁺-N > 30 mg/L, **always include the 4.57 × Q × N term**.
> 3. **Confusing HRT with SRT** — $\theta$ (hydraulic) ≠ $\theta_c$ (cell). They differ by orders of magnitude (1 day vs. 25 days). Using one in place of the other is a fatal error.
> 4. **Ignoring flow equalization** — Food industry effluents are notoriously variable. Without an equalization tank, the actual F/M may spike to >1 day⁻¹, killing nitrifiers.
> 5. **Not stating assumptions** — Every numerical design MUST specify assumed constants ($a'$, $b'$, $Y$, $k_d$, OTE). Marks are awarded for clarity.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!NOTE]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — Oxidation Ditches:**
> - ⭐ Oxidation ditches are **extended aeration activated sludge** units in a racetrack configuration.
> - ⭐ Operating regime: **HRT = 18–36 h, SRT = 20–40 d, F/M = 0.04–0.10 d⁻¹, MLSS = 3,000–6,000 mg/L**.
> - ⭐ Three principal types: **Pasveer, Carrousel, Orbal**.
> - ⭐ Brush aerators serve **dual duty** — oxygen transfer AND channel mixing/propulsion.
> - ⭐ Standard velocity = **0.25–0.30 m/s**; channel depth = **1.0–3.5 m**.
> - ⭐ Design equations to memorize: $V = Q \cdot HRT$; $F/M = QS_0/(VX)$; $O_2 = a'Q(S_0-S_e) + b'VX$.
> - ⭐ Nitrification O₂ demand = **4.57 × Q × NH₄⁺-N**.
> - ⭐ Observed yield formula: $Y_{obs} = Y/(1+k_d\theta_c)$.
> - ⭐ Carrousel ditches are preferred for **large food plants** (dairy, brewery, distillery) due to depth (up to 5 m) reducing footprint.
> - ⭐ Orbal ditches uniquely enable **simultaneous nitrification–denitrification** via concentric low-DO inner ring.
> - ⭐ Always specify the assumed values of $a'$ (≈ 0.5), $b'$ (≈ 0.05–0.10), OTE (≈ 1.5 kg O₂/kWh), $Y$ (≈ 0.6), and $k_d$ (≈ 0.05) in numerical answers.
> - ⭐ Waste sludge yield from oxidation ditches is **very low** (0.10–0.25 kg TSS/kg BOD) → sludge is already well-stabilized.
> - ⭐ DO maintained at **1.5–2.0 mg/L** at rotor zone; anoxic zones downstream have DO < 0.5 mg/L enabling denitrification.
> - ⭐ CO mapping (KTU 2024): This topic aligns with **CO4 — Design biological treatment systems for food industry effluents** and **CO5 — Evaluate operational parameters of secondary treatment units**.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: UASB
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PEFTT744 - FOOD INDUSTRY WASTE MANAGEMENT > - **Module:** Module 4 > - **Topic:** UASB # 🔬 SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition The **Upflow Anaerobic Sludge Blanket (UASB)** reactor, developed by **Gatze Lettinga and colleagues at Wageningen University (Netherlands) in the late 1970s**, is a high-rate anaerobic wastewater treatment system. It is classified as a **suspended-growth anaerobic digester** in which the influent wastewater flows upward through a dense, granulated, or flocculated blanket of active anaerobic microbial biomass (sludge). According to the **KTU 2024 syllabus terminology**, the UASB reactor is the most widely adopted high-rate anaerobic treatment technology for **high-strength food industry effluents** (dairy, distillery, brewery, starch, sugar, food processing, fruit juice, meat processing, etc.) due to its ability to handle **high organic loading rates (OLR)** with **no mechanical mixing** and **minimal energy input**. > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU Board Definition (Memorize Verbatim):** > "An Upflow Anaerobic Sludge Blanket (UASB) reactor is a vertical, cylindrical, or rectangular closed anaerobic treatment unit in which influent wastewater is introduced at the bottom via an influent distribution system, flows upward through a suspended blanket of granular/ flocculent anaerobic sludge, and is separated into treated effluent and biogas by an internal Gas-Liquid-Solid (GLS) separator located at the top." ## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition 🍲 Think of the UASB reactor like a **multi-storey rainwater harvesting tower**: - The **bottom of the tower** is where dirty water (wastewater) enters through small holes (influent distribution system). - As the dirty water moves **upward**, it passes through layers of **"active biological sand"** (the sludge blanket) — these microbes act like tiny filters and digesters, breaking down the pollutants. - The **clean water rises** to the top, while the **digested gas bubbles** (like methane) get trapped by a special inverted cone-shaped roof (GLS separator) and collected separately. - The sand (sludge) stays heavy at the bottom, so the system needs **no mechanical stirring**. The genius of this system is that the microbes **self-immobilize into granules** (dense pellets ~1–3 mm), which resist being washed out and can handle huge organic loads — a perfect engineering solution for India's distilleries and dairy industries. ## 1.3 Standard Design Metrics (Highlighted) > [!NOTE] > **Critical UASB Design Constants for KTU Exams:** > - **Hydraulic Retention Time (HRT):** 4 – 24 hours (typically 6–12 h for food industry) > - **Upflow Velocity (v_up):** 0.5 – 1.0 m/h (max 2.0 m/h for granular sludge) > - **Organic Loading Rate (OLR):** 5 – 15 kg COD/m³·day (high-rate) > - **COD Removal Efficiency:** 60 – 95 % (depending on influent type) > - **Temperature:** Mesophilic range **30 – 37 °C** (thermophilic 50–55 °C) > - **Reactor Height-to-Diameter Ratio:** ~1:3 to 1:6 (tall reactors preferred) > - **Biogas Yield:** 0.30 – 0.50 m³ biogas/kg COD removed > - **Methane Content (CH₄):** 60 – 80 % of biogas > - **Granule Size:** 0.5 – 3.0 mm (diameter) > - **pH Range:** **6.5 – 7.5** (alkalinity > 1500 mg/L as CaCO₃) # ⚙️ SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ## 2.1 Operational Principle – Step-by-Step Logic The UASB reactor operates on the principle of **upflow hydraulic pattern combined with internal sludge retention** (no carrier media required, unlike Anaerobic Filter). The following sequential logic governs its operation: 1. **Influent Distribution:** Wastewater enters the reactor through a **bottom distribution system** comprising multiple nozzles arranged uniformly across the reactor's base (typically 1–2 m² per nozzle). 2. **Contact Zone (Sludge Blanket):** As influent rises, it contacts the **suspended sludge blanket** (height 1–4 m, density 40–100 kg TSS/m³). 3. **Anaerobic Digestion Stages (inside the blanket):** - **Hydrolysis:** Complex organics (proteins, carbs, lipids) → monomers - **Acidogenesis:** Monomers → Volatile Fatty Acids (VFAs) + H₂ + CO₂ - **Acetogenesis:** VFAs → Acetate + H₂ + CO₂ - **Methanogenesis:** Acetate → CH₄ (acetoclastic) ; H₂ + CO₂ → CH₄ (hydrogenotrophic) 4. **Biogas Production:** Methane and CO₂ bubbles rise upward with the effluent flow. 5. **GLS Separator (Phase Separator):** Located at the top — consists of an **inverted funnel/dome deflector** that traps biogas bubbles and returns them to a gas collection dome; the deflector also redirects sludge particles back to the blanket. 6. **Effluent & Biogas Discharge:** Clarified effluent overflows into a weir system; biogas is collected via the gas hood and piped to a gas holder / utilization unit (boiler, CHP, flare stack). ## 2.2 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet > [!IMPORTANT] > **📘 KTU 2024 — UASB Design Equations Master Table** | **#** | **Parameter** | **Formula** | **Units** | **Remarks** | | :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- | | 1 | Volume of Reactor | $V = Q \times HRT$ | m³ | $Q$ = flow rate (m³/h); $HRT$ = hydraulic retention time (h) | | 2 | Organic Loading Rate | $OLR = \dfrac{Q \times S_0}{V}$ | kg COD / m³·day | $S_0$ = influent COD (kg/m³) | | 3 | Upflow Velocity | $v_{up} = \dfrac{Q}{A}$ | m/h | $A$ = cross-sectional area (m²) | | 4 | Height of Reactor | $H = v_{up} \times HRT$ | m | Combines with diameter | | 5 | COD Removal Efficiency | $\eta_{COD} = \dfrac{S_0 - S_e}{S_0} \times 100$ | % | $S_e$ = effluent COD | | 6 | Methane Yield (Stoichiometric) | $CH_4 = 0.35 \times Q \times (S_0 - S_e)$ | m³/day @ STP | 0.35 m³ CH₄ per kg COD removed at 0 °C | | 7 | Biogas Yield (Practical) | $BG = 0.40 \text{ to } 0.50 \times Q \times (S_0 - S_e)$ | m³/day | Includes CO₂ fraction | | 8 | Methane Corrected (35 °C) | $CH_4(35°C) = CH_4(0°C) \times \dfrac{308}{273}$ | m³/day | Temperature correction factor | | 9 | Sludge Production | $Y_{obs} = 0.05 \text{ to } 0.15$ kg VSS/kg COD | — | Low sludge yield (vs aerobic) | | 10 | Effluent Alkalinity Requirement | $ALK > 1500$ mg/L as CaCO₃ | mg/L | To buffer VFAs | > ⚠️ **Board Tip:** Always carry units through every calculation step in KTU valuation. ## 2.3 Real-World Engineering Utility - **Distilleries (Molasses-based ethanol plants):** UASB treats spent wash (COD 80,000–1,20,000 mg/L), reducing COD by ~75 % and producing biogas used as boiler fuel. - **Dairy Industry:** Treats cheese whey and dairy wastewater (COD 5,000–25,000 mg/L). - **Brewery:** Treats high-strength brewing effluent with high biogas recovery. - **Sugar Industry:** UASB + post-aerobic polishing is a standard design. - **Starch, Tapioca, Soya, Fruit Pulp** industries — all applicable. The **"no moving parts"** advantage makes UASB ideal for India's **rural & semi-urban food processing clusters** (e.g., grape wineries in Maharashtra, distilleries in UP). # 🧮 SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ## 3.1 Worked-Out Numerical Example (KTU Model Problem) > **Problem:** A dairy industry wishes to treat **$Q = 250$ m³/day** of wastewater with an **influent COD $S_0 = 5000$ mg/L** using a UASB reactor. The plant design assumes an **HRT of 8 hours**, **COD removal efficiency of 80 %**, and a **mesophilic temperature of 35 °C**. Calculate: > (i) Volume of the reactor > (ii) Organic Loading Rate (OLR) > (iii) Upflow velocity if the diameter is 4.5 m > (iv) Daily methane production at STP and at 35 °C > (v) Effluent COD ### Given Data (Extract Carefully): $$Q = 250 \text{ m}^3/\text{day}, \quad S_0 = 5000 \text{ mg/L} = 5.0 \text{ kg/m}^3$$ $$HRT = 8 \text{ h} = \dfrac{8}{24} \text{ day} = 0.3333 \text{ day}$$ $$\eta_{COD} = 80\% = 0.80, \quad D = 4.5 \text{ m}, \quad T = 35°C = 308 \text{ K}$$ --- ### Part (i) — Volume of the Reactor $V$ **Step 1:** Apply the fundamental definition of Hydraulic Retention Time: $$HRT = \dfrac{V}{Q} \quad \Rightarrow \quad V = Q \times HRT$$ **Step 2:** Substitute the values (use Q in m³/h for consistency with HRT in hours): $$Q = \dfrac{250}{24} = 10.4167 \text{ m}^3/\text{h}$$ **Step 3:** Calculate volume: $$V = 10.4167 \times 8 = 83.33 \text{ m}^3$$ $$\boxed{V \approx 83.33 \text{ m}^3}$$ --- ### Part (ii) — Organic Loading Rate (OLR) **Step 1:** Apply the OLR formula: $$OLR = \dfrac{Q \times S_0}{V}$$ **Step 2:** Substitute (with Q in m³/day and S₀ in kg/m³): $$OLR = \dfrac{250 \times 5.0}{83.33} = \dfrac{1250}{83.33}$$ $$\boxed{OLR = 15.0 \text{ kg COD/m}^3\cdot\text{day}}$$ > [!NOTE] > This is at the **upper end** of the standard UASB operating range — confirms high-rate design. --- ### Part (iii) — Upflow Velocity $v_{up}$ **Step 1:** Compute the cross-sectional area of the cylindrical reactor: $$A = \dfrac{\pi}{4} \times D^2 = \dfrac{\pi}{4} \times (4.5)^2 = \dfrac{\pi}{4} \times 20.25$$ $$A = 15.904 \text{ m}^2$$ **Step 2:** Apply the upflow velocity formula: $$v_{up} = \dfrac{Q}{A} = \dfrac{10.4167 \text{ m}^3/\text{h}}{15.904 \text{ m}^2}$$ $$\boxed{v_{up} = 0.655 \text{ m/h}}$$ > This lies within the **safe operating range of 0.5–1.0 m/h** — ✅ design is valid (no sludge washout risk). --- ### Part (iv) — Daily Methane Production **Step 1:** Calculate the influent COD load (kg/day): $$COD_{in} = Q \times S_0 = 250 \times 5.0 = 1250 \text{ kg COD/day}$$ **Step 2:** Calculate COD removed: $$COD_{removed} = \eta_{COD} \times COD_{in} = 0.80 \times 1250 = 1000 \text{ kg COD/day}$$ **Step 3:** Apply stoichiometric methane yield at STP (0 °C, 1 atm): $$CH_4(0°C) = 0.35 \times COD_{removed} = 0.35 \times 1000 = 350 \text{ m}^3/\text{day}$$ **Step 4:** Convert to actual operating temperature (35 °C = 308 K) using Charles's Law: $$CH_4(35°C) = CH_4(0°C) \times \dfrac{T_{actual}}{T_{STP}} = 350 \times \dfrac{308}{273}$$ $$CH_4(35°C) = 350 \times 1.1282 = 394.87 \text{ m}^3/\text{day}$$ $$\boxed{CH_4 \approx 350 \text{ m}^3/\text{day at STP} \quad ; \quad 394.87 \text{ m}^3/\text{day at 35°C}}$$ --- ### Part (v) — Effluent COD $S_e$ $$S_e = S_0 \times (1 - \eta_{COD}) = 5000 \times (1 - 0.80)$$ $$\boxed{S_e = 1000 \text{ mg/L}}$$ --- ## 3.2 Analytical Matrix — Sludge Blanket vs. Reactor Zones | **Zone (Top → Bottom)** | **Function** | **Biomass Density (kg TSS/m³)** | **Key Reactions** | | :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- | | Effluent Zone | Clarified overflow | < 5 | None | | GLS Separator | Gas-Solid-Liquid phase split | — | Physical | | Sludge Blanket (Dense) | Active digestion zone | 40 – 100 | Acidogenesis + Methanogenesis | | Sludge Bed | Settled granule storage | 100 – 200 | Methanogenesis dominant | | Distribution Zone | Inflow + mixing | 20 – 40 | Hydrolysis + Acidogenesis | | Influent Distribution | Uniform flow | — | Mechanical | # 🏗️ SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics ## 4.1 UASB Reactor — Mermaid Block Architecture Flow The following diagram maps the **functional interactions** of all UASB components (suitable for KTU board drawing explanation). ```mermaid flowchart TB A[Influent Wastewater
from Food Industry] --> B[Equalization Tank
pH and Flow Buffer] B --> C[Feed Pipe
to Reactor Bottom] C --> D[Influent Distribution System
Nozzle Array at Base] D --> E[Distribution Zone
Mixing and Hydrolysis] E --> F[Sludge Blanket
Dense Granular Biomass
40 to 100 kg TSS per m3] F --> G[Reactor Empty Top Zone
Quiescent Clarification] G --> H[GLS Separator
Gas Liquid Solid Deflector] H --> I[Biogas Collection Dome
Channel to Gas Holder] H --> J[Effluent Weir and Overflow
Treated Wastewater] F --> K[Excess Sludge Withdrawal
Bottom or Mid Port] I --> L[Gas Holder or CHP
Electricity or Boiler Fuel] J --> M[Post Treatment Aerobic Unit
Polishing and Nutrient Removal] M --> N[Discharge to Stream
or Reuse for Irrigation] K --> O[Sludge Drying Bed
Manure or Land Application] ``` ## 4.2 Component Function Mapping (Verbal Sketch for KTU Boards) When drawing the UASB diagram in your exam, label the following **in vertical order (bottom → top)**: 1. **Inlet Pipe** at the bottom (showing upward arrow ↑) 2. **Distribution Plate / Nozzles** — uniform spacing 3. **Sludge Blanket** — shaded region in the lower-middle (label height as 1–4 m) 4. **Blanket Zone** — clear zone above sludge blanket 5. **GLS Separator** — inverted funnel at top 6. **Gas Collection Hood** — at the very top 7. **Effluent Overflow Weir** — side outlet 8. **Sampling Ports** — at multiple heights > [!NOTE] > Always draw **5 distinct zones** in the reactor for full marks in KTU board exams. # 📝 SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap --- ## 🔹 PART A — 3 Mark Questions (Short Answer) ### **Q1. [KTU University Exam – June 2024]** **Define UASB reactor. Mention any two industries where it is preferred.** **Model Answer:** The **Upflow Anaerobic Sludge Blanket (UASB) reactor** is a high-rate anaerobic treatment unit in which wastewater flows **upward** through a **suspended blanket of granular anaerobic sludge**, and the produced biogas, treated effluent, and sludge are separated by an internal **Gas-Liquid-Solid (GLS) separator**. **Two industries where UASB is preferred:** 1. **Distillery industry** (molasses-based spent wash — COD ~1,00,000 mg/L) 2. **Dairy industry** (cheese whey, milk processing wastewater) *(Acceptable alternatives: Brewery, Sugar, Starch, Tapioca, Fruit processing, Slaughterhouse, Pulp & Paper — any two carry ½ mark each.)* --- ### **Q2. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]** **List the four sequential biochemical stages of anaerobic digestion occurring inside the UASB sludge blanket.** **Model Answer:** 1. **Hydrolysis** — Complex polymers (proteins, carbohydrates, lipids) are broken down into soluble monomers (amino acids, sugars, fatty acids). 2. **Acidogenesis** — Monomers are converted into Volatile Fatty Acids (VFAs), H₂, and CO₂. 3. **Acetogenesis** — VFAs are further converted into acetate, H₂, and CO₂. 4. **Methanogenesis** — Acetate (acetoclastic) and H₂ + CO₂ (hydrogenotrophic) are converted into methane (CH₄) and CO₂. --- ## 🔹 PART B — 14 Mark Questions (ESE Module – Internal Choice) --- ### **📌 Question A (Choice 1) — 14 Marks** **\[KTU University Exam – May 2024 (Model)]** **(a)** Explain the **working principle and constructional features** of a UASB reactor with a neat labelled sketch. **(7 marks)** **(b)** A **food processing industry** generates **$Q = 500$ m³/day** of wastewater with **$S_0 = 6000$ mg/L COD**. Design a UASB reactor to achieve **80% COD removal** at an **HRT of 10 hours**, **upflow velocity of 0.75 m/h**, and **35 °C operating temperature**. Compute: - (i) Reactor volume, (ii) Diameter, (iii) OLR, (iv) Daily methane at STP and at 35 °C, (v) Effluent COD. **(7 marks)** --- #### Model Solution — Question A (a) The **UASB reactor** is a vertical cylindrical or rectangular closed vessel comprising the following components from bottom to top: 1. **Inlet Distribution System:** Located at the bottom, consists of a network of pipes with multiple **nozzles** (one per 1–2 m² of base area) to ensure **uniform upward flow** across the entire cross-section. 2. **Distribution / Mixing Zone:** Initial zone where influent contacts the base of the sludge blanket — promotes mixing without external mechanical stirrer. 3. **Sludge Blanket:** The **heart of the reactor** — a suspended bed of **granular sludge** (density 40–100 kg TSS/m³, granule size 0.5–3 mm). Here the four biochemical stages (hydrolysis → methanogenesis) occur. 4. **Quiescent / Clarification Zone:** Above the sludge blanket — provides space for biogas disengagement and sludge settling. 5. **Gas-Liquid-Solid (GLS) Separator:** Inverted funnel or dome-shaped deflector at the top — performs three-phase separation: - **Biogas** collected in the gas hood above - **Effluent** overflows via weirs - **Sludge particles** slide down the deflector back into the blanket (internal recirculation, no sludge washout). 6. **Biogas Collection Dome:** Captures CH₄ + CO₂ for storage or utilization. 7. **Effluent Overflow System:** Side-channel weirs discharge treated water. **Working Principle:** Wastewater enters at the bottom → flows upward → contacts anaerobic granules (methanogens) → organic matter is biodegraded into CH₄ + CO₂ + biomass → biogas bubbles rise → GLS separator separates phases → clarified effluent exits via weirs → biogas collected via dome. **Neat Labelled Sketch (Board Requirement):** Draw a vertical rectangle (reactor). Inside (bottom → top) label: - Inlet pipe → Distribution plate → Sludge blanket (shaded) → Blanket space → GLS separator (inverted funnel) → Gas dome (top) → Effluent weir (side) --- #### Model Solution — Question A (b) — Numerical **Given:** $$Q = 500 \text{ m}^3/\text{day}, \quad S_0 = 6000 \text{ mg/L} = 6.0 \text{ kg/m}^3$$ $$HRT = 10 \text{ h}, \quad v_{up} = 0.75 \text{ m/h}, \quad \eta_{COD} = 80\%, \quad T = 35°C = 308 \text{ K}$$ **(i) Volume of the reactor:** $$Q_{h} = \dfrac{500}{24} = 20.833 \text{ m}^3/\text{h}$$ $$V = Q_{h} \times HRT = 20.833 \times 10 = 208.33 \text{ m}^3$$ $$\boxed{V = 208.33 \text{ m}^3}$$ **(ii) Height & Diameter:** $$H = v_{up} \times HRT = 0.75 \times 10 = 7.5 \text{ m}$$ $$A = \dfrac{V}{H} = \dfrac{208.33}{7.5} = 27.78 \text{ m}^2$$ $$D = \sqrt{\dfrac{4 \times A}{\pi}} = \sqrt{\dfrac{4 \times 27.78}{\pi}} = \sqrt{35.37}$$ $$\boxed{D = 5.95 \text{ m} \approx 6.0 \text{ m}}$$ **(iii) Organic Loading Rate:** $$OLR = \dfrac{Q \times S_0}{V} = \dfrac{500 \times 6.0}{208.33} = \dfrac{3000}{208.33}$$ $$\boxed{OLR = 14.40 \text{ kg COD/m}^3\cdot\text{day}}$$ **(iv) Daily Methane Production:** $$COD_{removed} = \eta \times Q \times S_0 = 0.80 \times 500 \times 6.0 = 2400 \text{ kg/day}$$ $$CH_4(0°C) = 0.35 \times 2400 = 840 \text{ m}^3/\text{day at STP}$$ $$CH_4(35°C) = 840 \times \dfrac{308}{273} = 840 \times 1.1282 = 947.7 \text{ m}^3/\text{day}$$ $$\boxed{CH_4 = 840 \text{ m}^3/\text{day at STP} \quad ; \quad 947.7 \text{ m}^3/\text{day at 35°C}}$$ **(v) Effluent COD:** $$S_e = S_0 \times (1 - \eta) = 6000 \times (1 - 0.80) = 1200 \text{ mg/L}$$ $$\boxed{S_e = 1200 \text{ mg/L}}$$ --- ### **📌 Question B (Choice 2 — Alternative) — 14 Marks** **\[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 (Model)]** **(a)** Discuss the **factors affecting UASB performance** (influent characteristics, hydraulic parameters, temperature, alkalinity, nutrients, toxicity). **(7 marks)** **(b)** Compare **UASB** with **Anaerobic Filter** and **Anaerobic Contact Process**. Also explain the **role and advantages of GLS separator** in UASB. **(7 marks)** --- #### Model Solution — Question B (a) The **performance efficiency** of a UASB reactor is influenced by the following factors: 1. **Influent COD Concentration ($S_0$):** Optimal 1000–50,000 mg/L. Too dilute wastes (<500 mg/L) cause granule disintegration; too concentrated (>60,000 mg/L) cause toxic VFA accumulation. 2. **Hydraulic Retention Time (HRT):** Lower HRT → higher OLR but risk of washout. Optimal 6–24 h. 3. **Upflow Velocity:** 0.5–1.0 m/h. Excessive velocity (>2 m/h) causes sludge washout. 4. **Temperature:** **Mesophilic (30–37 °C) is optimal.** Thermophilic (50–55 °C) gives higher rates but lower stability; psychrophilic (<20 °C) significantly reduces methanogenesis. 5. **pH:** Must be **6.5–7.5**. Below 6.2 → methanogen inhibition by VFAs. 6. **Alkalinity:** Must exceed **1500 mg/L as CaCO₃** to neutralize VFAs and maintain pH. 7. **Nutrients:** C:N:P ratio of **500:5:1** (or COD:N:P = 250:5:1) for balanced microbial growth. 8. **Toxicity:** Substances like ammonia (>1700 mg/L), sulfide (>200 mg/L), heavy metals, chlorinated hydrocarbons, and cyanide inhibit methanogens. 9. **Suspended Solids (SS) in Influent:** High SS (>500 mg/L) clogs distribution nozzles and dilutes granule density. 10. **Reactor Geometry:** Height-to-diameter ratio 1:3 to 1:6 ensures proper flow distribution and gas separation. #### Model Solution — Question B (b) **Comparison Table:** | **Feature** | **UASB** | **Anaerobic Filter** | **Anaerobic Contact Process** | | :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- | | Biomass Retention | Granular blanket | Attached biofilm on media | Suspended via settler/ sludge return | | HRT | 6–24 h | 24–96 h | 1–10 days | | OLR (kg COD/m³·day) | 5–15 | 1–5 | 0.5–3 | | Flow Direction | Upflow | Upflow or Downflow | CSTR with recirculation | | Carrier Media | None (granules form naturally) | Rock, PVC, ceramic rings | None (mechanical mixing) | | Gas Separation | Internal GLS separator | At top via gas space | External gas collector | | Footprint | Very small | Medium | Large | | Sludge Washout | Minimal (granules) | None (attached) | Recirculated | | Typical Effluent COD | 200–1000 mg/L | 200–800 mg/L | 500–1500 mg/L | **Role & Advantages of GLS Separator:** - The **Gas-Liquid-Solid (GLS) separator** is an **internal three-phase separation device** located at the top of the UASB. - **Role:** It captures biogas bubbles before they escape with the effluent, returns entrained sludge particles back to the blanket, and allows clarified effluent to overflow. - **Advantages:** 1. **Prevents sludge washout** — critical for retaining the slow-growing methanogens. 2. **Improves biogas quality** — separates gas cleanly for utilization. 3. **No external clarifier needed** — saves space and capital cost. 4. **Enables high biomass concentration** — up to 50–80 kg VSS/m³. 5. **Stable operation at high OLR** — makes UASB a true "high-rate" system. 6. **Self-regulating** — deflector angle (typically 45–60°) optimizes sludge return. --- ## ⚠️ KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout > [!WARNING] > **Common Mistakes Where Students Lose Marks:** > 1. **Unit conversion errors:** Always convert $Q$ to **m³/h** when HRT is in hours; convert $S_0$ from mg/L to **kg/m³** before computing OLR. > 2. **Confusing STP (0 °C) vs Operating Temperature (35 °C)** in methane yield calculations — multiply by $\frac{T_{actual}}{273}$. > 3. **Forgetting the stoichiometric coefficient** 0.35 m³ CH₄/kg COD — this is the standard textbook value (at STP). > 4. **Skipping the GLS separator in diagrams** — KTU examiners deduct up to 2 marks if it is not labelled. > 5. **Mixing OLR units** — ensure kg COD/m³·day (NOT kg/m³·day). > 6. **Not stating boundary conditions** for upflow velocity (0.5–1.0 m/h) and OLR (5–15 kg COD/m³·day). > 7. **Ignoring alkalinity requirement** — always mention ALK > 1500 mg/L as CaCO₃ for stable operation. --- ## 🧠 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember > **🚀 Rapid Revision Checklist — UASB Reactor** - ✅ **Full Form:** Upflow Anaerobic Sludge Blanket Reactor. - ✅ **Developer:** Gatze Lettinga, Wageningen University, Netherlands (late 1970s). - ✅ **Type:** High-rate anaerobic, suspended-growth, no carrier media. - ✅ **Flow Pattern:** Upward (influent at bottom → effluent at top). - ✅ **Key Component:** Internal **GLS (Gas-Liquid-Solid) Separator** at top. - ✅ **HRT Range:** **4–24 hours** (typical 6–12 h). - ✅ **Upflow Velocity:** **0.5–1.0 m/h** (max 2.0 m/h with granular sludge). - ✅ **OLR Range:** **5–15 kg COD/m³·day** (high-rate). - ✅ **Temperature:** **Mesophilic 30–37 °C** (optimal); thermophilic 50–55 °C. - ✅ **pH:** **6.5–7.5** ; Alkalinity **>1500 mg/L as CaCO₃**. - ✅ **COD Removal:** **60–95 %** depending on influent type. - ✅ **Biogas Yield:** **0.30–0.50 m³/kg COD removed** ; CH₄ content **60–80%**. - ✅ **Methane (STP):** **0.35 m³ CH₄/kg COD removed** ; correction for 35 °C: multiply by **308/273**. - ✅ **Best for:** Distillery, dairy, brewery, sugar, starch, food processing wastewaters. - ✅ **Granule Size:** **0.5–3.0 mm** ; Biomass density: **40–100 kg TSS/m³**. - ✅ **Reactor H/D Ratio:** **1:3 to 1:6** (tall reactors preferred). - ✅ **4 Biochemical Stages:** Hydrolysis → Acidogenesis → Acetogenesis → Methanogenesis. - ✅ **Advantages:** No mixing, no media, low energy, low sludge, high biogas, compact. - ✅ **Limitations:** Sensitive to toxic compounds; needs post-aerobic treatment; slow start-up (granule formation takes 2–6 months).

topics.title: Rotating biological contractors and their variations and advanced modifications
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEFTT744 - FOOD INDUSTRY WASTE MANAGEMENT
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Rotating biological contractors and their variations and advanced modifications

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: CORE TECHNICAL DEFINITION & INTUITIVE OVERVIEW

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

A **Rotating Biological Contactor (RBC)** is a fixed-film biological wastewater treatment system in which a series of closely spaced, circular plastic discs are mounted on a horizontal shaft and slowly rotated (typically **1 to 2 rpm**) through a wastewater basin. Approximately **40% of the disc area is submerged**, and as the discs rotate, a microbial biofilm alternately contacts the wastewater (submerged phase) and the atmosphere (emerged phase), enabling simultaneous **organic matter degradation (BOD/COD removal), nitrification, and oxygen transfer** without external aeration.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight:**
> The KTU 2024 scheme (PEFTT744, Module 4) explicitly classifies RBCs as an **Attached Growth (Biofilm) Aerobic Treatment Unit**, distinct from Activated Sludge Process (ASP) and Trickling Filters. For food industry effluents, RBCs are favored for high-strength, biodegradable streams from **dairy, brewery, distillery, meat, fruit/vegetable processing, and aquaculture** plants due to their **low energy footprint, low sludge yield, and tolerance to shock loads**.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **The "Rotating Salad Spinner for Microbes" Analogy:**
> Imagine a giant round laundry rack (the disc stack) suspended half-in, half-out of a wastewater trough. The rack turns slowly — like a slow merry-go-round. The lower half of the discs is bathed in dirty food-processing water, and the upper half is exposed to fresh air. Tiny microbes (biofilm) cling to the discs like moss on a stone wall. Every few seconds, they get a "drink" of food (submerged in wastewater) and a "breath" of oxygen (emerged in air). Continuous rotation = continuous feeding + aeration, with no blowers, no pumps for air, and very little sludge. The microbes happily eat the organic pollutants, and clean water drips off the discs.

## 1.3 Physical Constants & Standard Metrics (Food Industry RBC Design)

| Parameter | Standard Value | Unit |
|---|---|---|
| Disc rotation speed (N) | **1 – 2** | rpm |
| Disc diameter (D) | **2 – 3.6** (commonly 3.0) | m |
| Disc spacing (axial) | **20 – 40** | mm |
| Submergence ratio | **40% (0.40)** | — |
| Hydraulic loading rate (HLR) | **0.04 – 0.16** | m³/m²·d |
| Organic loading rate (OLR) | **5 – 25** (BOD basis) | g BOD/m²·d |
| Shaft length | **1.5 – 7.5** | m |
| Number of stages | **3 – 6** (often 4) | — |
| Peripheral velocity (v) | **0.15 – 0.30** | m/s |
| BOD removal efficiency | **80 – 95%** | % |
| Typical MLSS equivalent | **0 (sessile)** | — |

> [!TIP]
> **Mnemonic for RBC:** **"S – B – A – D"**
> **S**ubmerge & **B**reathe (rotation) → **A**ttached biofilm grows → **D**egrade organics + **N**itrify ammonia. (BA, then BDN → wait, it's **S-B-A-D** = Submerge, Breathe, Attach, Degrade.)

# SECTION 2: DEEP THEORETICAL ANALYSIS & KTU HIGH-YIELD FORMULA SHEET

## 2.1 Operating Principle — Step-by-Step Logic

The RBC system works on **three coupled phenomena** that occur every revolution:

1. **Adsorption Phase (Submerged):** When the biofilm-coated disc passes through the wastewater, dissolved organics, ammonia, and oxygen diffuse into the biofilm matrix. Microbes oxidize the substrate using intracellular enzymes.

2. **Aeration Phase (Emerged):** As the disc leaves the water, the wet biofilm picks up atmospheric oxygen (O₂). Oxygen transfer efficiency is very high because the thin water film offers minimal gas-film resistance.

3. **Sloughing Phase (Shear-induced):** As biofilm thickness increases (typically > 100 µm), shear stress during rotation causes excess biomass to slough off. The sloughed solids are removed in the secondary clarifier.

### 2.1.1 Why Multiple Stages?

A single stage cannot simultaneously handle **high BOD removal** AND **nitrification** because:
- Heterotrophic bacteria (BOD removers) grow faster and outcompete autotrophic nitrifiers at high C/N ratios.
- So the first 1–2 stages handle **BOD reduction (carbonaceous stage)** and the later stages handle **nitrification (ammonia oxidation stage)**.

> [!NOTE]
> **Stage Configuration Rule (KTU Standard):**
> - **Stage 1:** ~50% BOD removed (highest loading — "shock absorber")
> - **Stage 2:** BOD down to ~30 mg/L (transition)
> - **Stages 3–4:** Nitrification zone
> - **Stages 5–6 (optional):** Polishing / post-nitrification

## 2.2 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Use `\vert` instead of `|` in tables to prevent markdown breakage.**

| # | Formula | Description | Units | Typical Range / KTU Value |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $A_d = \dfrac{\pi D^2}{4}$ | Disc surface area (both sides submerged) | m² | 4.9 (D=2.5 m) to 10.2 (D=3.6 m) |
| 2 | $A_T = N_d \cdot A_d$ | Total effective disc area (N_d = number of discs) | m² | 500 – 10000 m² per shaft |
| 3 | $L = \dfrac{Q \cdot S_0}{1000}$ | Daily BOD load | kg BOD/d | 5 – 500 (food industry) |
| 4 | $OLR = \dfrac{Q \cdot S_0}{A_T}$ | Organic loading rate | g BOD/m²·d | 5 – 25 |
| 5 | $HLR = \dfrac{Q}{A_T}$ | Hydraulic loading rate | m³/m²·d | 0.04 – 0.16 |
| 6 | $v_p = \dfrac{\pi D N}{60}$ | Peripheral velocity of disc | m/s | 0.15 – 0.30 |
| 7 | $S_e = S_0 \cdot e^{-k \cdot A_T / Q}$ | Effluent BOD (1st order plug flow) | mg/L | – |
| 8 | $\eta = \dfrac{S_0 - S_e}{S_0} \times 100$ | Removal efficiency | % | 80 – 95 |
| 9 | $K_{La,RBC} = f(N, D, \theta, T)$ | Oxygen transfer coefficient | 1/h | 2 – 8 (empirical) |
| 10 | $r_{NH_4} = \dfrac{k_n \cdot X_a \cdot N_{NH_4}}{K_N + N_{NH_4}}$ | Nitrification rate (Monod) | mg N/L·h | Stoichiometric O₂ = 4.57 g O₂/g N |
| 11 | $N_{disc} = \dfrac{L_{shaft}}{t_{disc} + s_{disc}}$ | Number of discs per shaft | discs | t = thickness 1–3 mm, s = spacing 20–40 mm |
| 12 | $Y_{obs} = \dfrac{Y}{1 + k_d \theta_c}$ | Observed yield (RBC: 0.2–0.4) | kg VSS/kg BOD | Lower than ASP (0.5–0.6) |

## 2.3 Real-World Engineering Utility

In the food industry, RBCs are deployed for:
- **Dairy effluent (cheese whey, milk processing):** BOD 1500–3000 mg/L → 90% removal.
- **Brewery wastewater:** High carbohydrate & protein; RBC handles variable loads.
- **Meat & poultry processing:** RBC + dissolved air flotation (DAF) upstream.
- **Distillery (spent wash):** RBC as a post-anaerobic polishing step.
- **Vegetable/fruit canning:** RBC handles seasonal shock loads effectively.

> [!TIP]
> **Why food engineers prefer RBCs over ASP:**
> 1. **Low energy:** No aeration tank blowers (saves ~60% energy).
> 2. **Low sludge yield:** Sludge production is 20–30% of activated sludge.
> 3. **Shock-load tolerance:** Biofilm "buffers" sudden pH/BOD changes.
> 4. **Compact:** High surface area in small footprint — ideal for in-plant treatment in food factories.
> 5. **Nitrification:** Easily achievable in latter stages.
