**(b) [7 marks]** A **1000 m³/day** distillery spent wash has **COD = 50,000 mg/L** and **color = 80,000 Pt–Co units**. Using the Fenton process with H₂O₂ dose = 0.6 g per g of COD removed and Fe²⁺ : H₂O₂ molar ratio of 1:10, calculate:
- (i) H₂O₂ required (kg/day) for 60 % COD reduction.
- (ii) FeSO₄·7H₂O required (kg/day). (MW H₂O₂ = 34, MW FeSO₄·7H₂O = 277.85)

---

#### **Model Solution to Question B (a):**

**Fenton Process Explanation (7 marks):**

The **Fenton process** is a homogeneous **Advanced Oxidation Process (AOP)** in which a mixture of **ferrous ion (Fe²⁺)** and **hydrogen peroxide (H₂O₂)** generates **hydroxyl radicals ($\cdot$OH)** — the most powerful practical aqueous oxidant ($E^{\circ}$ = **+2.80 V**) — which attack refractory organics in food industry wastewater such as distillery spent wash, oil-mill effluent, and fermentation residues.

**Process Flow Steps:**
1. **Effluent collection** in equalization tank.
2. **pH adjustment to 2.5–3.5** using H₂SO₄ (critical — at pH > 4, iron precipitates as Fe(OH)₃ before reacting; at pH < 2, $\cdot$OH formation is suppressed).
3. **FeSO₄ dosing** (typical dose 200–1000 mg/L).
4. **H₂O₂ dosing** in a stirred reactor with 30–120 min HRT.
5. **Reaction:** $\text{Fe}^{2+} + \text{H}_2\text{O}_2 \rightarrow \text{Fe}^{3+} + \cdot\text{OH} + \text{OH}^-$
6. **Chain propagation:** $\text{Fe}^{2+} + \cdot\text{OH} \rightarrow \text{Fe}^{3+} + \text{OH}^-$ (regenerates Fe²⁺).
7. **Neutralization to pH 7–8** with NaOH to precipitate Fe(OH)₃.
8. **Sedimentation/clarification** to remove iron sludge.
9. **Polishing** by sand filter and activated carbon.

**Optimum Parameters:**
- **pH = 3.0** (most cited optimum)
- **Fe²⁺ : H₂O₂ molar ratio = 1:10** (typical operating range 1:5 to 1:20)
- **Reaction time = 60 min** at 25–35 °C.

**Role of Fe²⁺ catalyst:** Fe²⁺ catalytically decomposes H₂O₂ via a one-electron transfer, generating the hydroxyl radical. Without Fe²⁺, H₂O₂ alone is a relatively weak oxidizer. The Fe²⁺ is regenerated during the chain cycle, so it acts as a true catalyst.

**Diagram:** Refer to **Section 4.4** (Fenton Reactor Process Flow).

---

#### **Model Solution to Question B (b):**

**Given:**
- Q = 1000 m³/day
- COD_in = 50,000 mg/L
- Target reduction = 60 % → COD_removed = 50,000 × 0.60 = **30,000 mg/L**
- H₂O₂ : COD = 0.6 g/g
- Fe : H₂O₂ molar = 1:10

**Step 1 — Daily COD mass removed.**
$$m_{COD,removed} = 30{,}000 \text{ mg/L} \times 10^6 \text{ L/day} = 3 \times 10^{10} \text{ mg/day} = 30{,}000 \text{ kg/day}$$

**Step 2 — H₂O₂ dose.**
$$m_{H_2O_2,pure} = 0.6 \times 30{,}000 = \textbf{18,000 kg/day}$$

**Step 3 — Moles of H₂O₂.**
$$n_{H_2O_2} = \dfrac{18{,}000 \times 10^3}{34} = 529{,}412 \text{ mol/day}$$

**Step 4 — Moles of Fe²⁺ (1:10 ratio).**
$$n_{Fe^{2+}} = 529{,}412 / 10 = 52{,}941 \text{ mol/day}$$

**Step 5 — Mass of FeSO₄·7H₂O.**
$$m_{FeSO_4 \cdot 7H_2O} = 52{,}941 \times 277.85 = 14{,}711{,}000 \text{ g/day} = \textbf{14,711 kg/day} \approx \textbf{14.7 tonnes/day}$$

**Summary:**
- (i) **18,000 kg/day (18 tonnes/day)** of pure H₂O₂ (corresponding to ~60,000 L/day of 30 % commercial H₂O₂).
- (ii) **14,711 kg/day (14.7 tonnes/day)** of FeSO₄·7H₂O.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall — Top Reasons Students Lose Marks:**
> 1. **Forgetting the 2-equivalent nature of H₂SO₄** in neutralization problems (most common error — costs 1–2 marks).
> 2. **Using wrong stoichiometric ratio** in P precipitation (5:3 Ca:P for hydroxyapatite, not 1:1).
> 3. **Forgetting to convert pH to molar concentration** (pH is log scale!).
> 4. **Confusing coagulant dose and coagulant + alkalinity demand** (must always add lime for alkalinity support when natural alkalinity is low).
> 5. **Failing to specify operating pH** in Fenton — without pH 2.5–3.5, the design is incomplete (loses 1 mark).
> 6. **Confusing sweep coagulation and charge neutralization** — these are distinct mechanisms.
> 7. **Forgetting the units** in the Jar test design (mg/L, mg/day, kg/day conversions are tested every year).
> 8. **Using $\vert x \vert$ vertical bars in tables** (breaks markdown and loses presentation mark in some evaluations).

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-density revision checklist — KTU Module 4:**

- **Physicochemical treatments** = combination of physical separation + chemical reaction. **Chemical treatments** = purely chemical transformations.
- **pH neutralization** is **mandatory first**; target pH 6.5–8.5; use 2-equivalent balance for H₂SO₄, 1-equivalent for HCl.
- **Coagulation–flocculation** removes colloids and TSS; **rapid mix (G ≈ 800 s⁻¹, 2 min)** then **slow mix (G ≈ 50 s⁻¹, 30 min)**.
- **Alum** optimum pH range = **5.5–7.5**; **FeCl₃** range = **4.5–6.5**; both form hydroxides that trap colloids.
- **Schulze–Hardy rule:** coagulation power of an ion is proportional to the **sixth power of its valence** ($z^6$).
- **Phosphorus precipitation** via **hydroxyapatite** — Ca:P molar ratio = **5:3**, sludge MW = **502.31**.
- **Standard oxidant potentials (V):** O₃ (2.07) > H₂O₂ (1.78) > MnO₄⁻ (1.51) > Cl₂ (1.36) > ClO₂ (0.95) > HOCl (0.89).
- **Fenton** optimum: pH 3.0, Fe²⁺:H₂O₂ = 1:10, 60 min HRT, dose 0.5 g H₂O₂ per g COD removed (range 0.3–0.8).
- **Avoid chlorine** for food industry effluents due to **THM (trihalomethane)** formation risk.
- **AOPs** generate $\cdot$OH radicals ($E^{\circ}$ = **+2.80 V**); include Fenton, Photo-Fenton, O₃/H₂O₂ (Peroxone), O₃/UV, TiO₂/UV.
- **Langmuir isotherm** assumes monolayer, **Freundlich** assumes heterogeneous multilayer; linearized Freundlich = $\log q_e$ vs $\log C_e$.
- **Camp–Stein velocity gradient** $G = \sqrt{P / \mu V}$ — defines the rapid-mix and flocculation intensity.
- **Standard performance:** PCT alone gives 60–90 % COD, 80–95 % TSS, color < 100 Pt–Co.
- **ZLD** (Zero Liquid Discharge) mandates PCT for water reuse in food plants.
- **Frequent KTU keywords:** "design the dose", "calculate the daily consumption", "state the operating pH", "explain with a flow diagram" — answer each verbatim.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Coagulation and flocculation
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PEFTT744 - FOOD INDUSTRY WASTE MANAGEMENT > - **Module:** Module 4 > - **Topic:** Coagulation and flocculation # SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology) **Coagulation** is the physico-chemical unit operation in which a chemical destabilizing agent (coagulant) is added to a colloidal or finely divided suspension to neutralize the surface electrostatic charges on suspended colloidal particles, thereby eliminating the natural repulsive forces that keep them dispersed. This charge neutralization process destabilizes the colloidal system within a time scale of **microseconds to seconds** and converts the *primary charge barrier* (Zeta potential) into a state where inter-particle collisions become thermodynamically favorable. **Flocculation** is the subsequent gentle hydrodynamic mixing process that promotes the aggregation of these destabilized micro-particles into larger, settleable macro-flocs (typically **0.1 mm to 3 mm** in size) by promoting inter-particle collisions through fluid shear. The flocculation process has a much slower kinetic regime, requiring **20 to 45 minutes** in conventional wastewater treatment. > [!IMPORTANT] > **Syllabus Highlight (KTU 2024 Scheme - Module 4):** > Coagulation and flocculation are tertiary/pretreatment unit operations extensively used in the food industry for: > - Treatment of dairy effluent (whey, casein wash water) > - Brewery and distillery wastewater clarification > - Fruit and vegetable processing wastewater (high suspended solids) > - Slaughterhouse and meat processing effluents (high protein/colloid content) > - Sugar industry effluent decolorization > - Edible oil refinery wastewater ## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy (Intuitive Plain-English Explanation) Imagine you have a glass of muddy water where the **fine clay particles refuse to settle** even after hours of standing. This happens because each tiny clay particle carries a **negative electrical charge** (just like two magnets repelling each other if you try to push the same poles together). They cannot clump together and cannot sink. **Now think of Coagulation like adding "anti-static spray" to the muddy water:** - When you add a coagulant (like alum or ferric chloride), the positively charged metal ions (Al³⁺ or Fe³⁺) act like tiny "charge-canceling magnets." - They neutralize the negative charges on the clay colloids, removing the electrical "force field" that was keeping them apart. - Result: The particles are now *destabilized* and *bare*, ready to touch each other. **Now think of Flocculation like gently stirring a pot of sauce to thicken it:** - Once the charges are neutralized, gentle, slow stirring causes the destabilized particles to **bump into each other repeatedly**. - Each successful collision makes them stick together (just like wet snowflakes stick into a snowball). - Over time, millions of these tiny particles snowball into large, heavy **"flocs"** that gravity can finally pull down to the bottom of the tank. ## 1.3 Physical Constants & Standard Metrics | Parameter | Standard Value | Units | |---|---|---| | Zeta potential for stable colloids | $\vert \zeta \vert > \pm 30$ | mV | | Zeta potential for destabilized colloids | $-5 < \zeta < +5$ | mV | | Coagulant dose range (Alum) | **10 – 150** | mg/L | | Coagulant dose range (Ferric Chloride) | **5 – 150** | mg/L | | Rapid mixing time | **30 – 120** | seconds | | Flocculation detention time | **20 – 45** | minutes | | Velocity gradient (Rapid mix) | **G = 700 – 1000** | s⁻¹ | | Velocity gradient (Flocculation) | **G = 20 – 75** | s⁻¹ | | Camp dimensionless number (Gt) | **10⁴ – 10⁵** | dimensionless | | Dynamic viscosity of water (20 °C) | $\mu = 1.002 \times 10^{-3}$ | Pa·s | > [!NOTE] > **Key Conceptual Distinction:** > - **Coagulation** = *Chemical* step (charge neutralization via coagulant addition) > - **Flocculation** = *Physical* step (particle aggregation via gentle mixing) > - The two processes are **sequential, not interchangeable**, and require different mixing intensities. # SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ## 2.1 The Four Mechanisms of Coagulation Modern theory (Stumm & Morgan, 1962 framework) recognizes four primary destabilization mechanisms. KTU examiners frequently test this classification. ### Mechanism 1: Double-Layer Compression - **Why:** High ionic strength compresses the diffuse layer around colloids. - **How:** Inert electrolytes (e.g., NaCl) compress the electrical double layer, reducing the repulsion barrier. - **Limitation:** Requires very high concentrations (impractical for food wastewater); mainly a theoretical concept. ### Mechanism 2: Adsorption and Charge Neutralization - **Why:** Specific adsorption of hydrolyzed metal species onto colloid surfaces. - **How:** Cationic species (Al³⁺, Fe³⁺, polymeric hydroxo-complexes) adsorb directly on the negative colloid surface, **neutralizing the surface charge stoichiometrically**. - **Application:** Most common mechanism with low to moderate coagulant doses. ### Mechanism 3: Sweep Coagulation (Enmeshment/Precipitation) - **Why:** Massive precipitation of metal hydroxide floc physically entrains colloids. - **How:** At high coagulant doses (above solubility limit), a heavy floc of **Al(OH)₃ or Fe(OH)₃** forms in bulk. Colloidal particles get "swept" along as the hydroxide settles. - **Optimal pH range:** Alum → **6.5 – 7.5**; FeCl₃ → **5.0 – 8.5** ### Mechanism 4: Inter-Particle Bridging (Polymer Bridging) - **Why:** Long-chain polymers physically tether multiple particles. - **How:** High-molecular-weight polyelectrolytes (e.g., polyacrylamide) have multiple active sites that adsorb onto different particles simultaneously, **bridging** them into a 3D matrix. - **Application:** Used as coagulant aids; especially effective in food industry sludge dewatering. > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU Board Exam Tip:** The selection of mechanism depends on: > 1. **pH of the wastewater** (governs speciation of metal ions) > 2. **Coagulant dose** (low dose = charge neutralization; high dose = sweep) > 3. **Colloid concentration** (low colloids favor sweep; high colloids favor neutralization) ## 2.2 Chemistry of Common Coagulants ### Alum (Aluminum Sulphate) — Al₂(SO₄)₃·14H₂O $$\text{Al}_2(\text{SO}_4)_3 \cdot 14\text{H}_2\text{O} + 3\text{Ca}(\text{HCO}_3)_2 \rightarrow 2\text{Al}(\text{OH})_3 \downarrow + 3\text{CaSO}_4 + 14\text{H}_2\text{O} + 6\text{CO}_2$$ - **Effective pH range:** 6.0 – 7.8 - **Alkalinity consumed:** 1 mg/L alum requires **0.45 mg/L CaCO₃** alkalinity - **Stoichiometric Al(OH)₃ produced per mg alum:** **0.234 mg** ### Ferric Chloride — FeCl₃·6H₂O $$\text{FeCl}_3 + 3\text{H}_2\text{O} \rightarrow \text{Fe}(\text{OH})_3 \downarrow + 3\text{HCl}$$ - **Effective pH range:** 5.0 – 11.0 (wider than alum) - **Better for food industry:** Effective in high-strength, variable pH wastewaters - **Drawback:** Chloride ion adds to TDS in treated effluent ### Polyaluminium Chloride (PAC) — [Al₂(OH)ₙCl₆₋ₙ]ₘ - Pre-hydrolyzed; less pH-dependent; lower sludge volume - Typical dose: **2 – 25 mg/L** (much lower than alum) ## 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet | # | Formula | Description / Use | Units | |---|---|---|---| | 1 | $\zeta = \frac{4\pi \eta u}{D} \cdot \frac{1}{E}$ | Zeta potential from electrophoretic mobility $u$ | mV | | 2 | $P = G^2 \cdot \mu \cdot V$ | **Camp-Stein Equation** — Power dissipated in flocculation | W | | 3 | $G = \sqrt{\dfrac{P}{\mu \cdot V}}$ | Velocity gradient (rms) in flocculation tank | s⁻¹ | | 4 | $G_t = G \cdot t$ | **Camp Number** — dimensionless flocculation criterion | dimensionless | | 5 | $V = Q \cdot t_d$ | Flocculation tank volume from flow $Q$ and detention time $t_d$ | m³ | | 6 | $P = \rho \cdot C_D \cdot A \cdot \dfrac{v^3}{2}$ | Power input by paddle stirrer | W | | 7 | $G = \sqrt{\dfrac{\gamma}{\mu} \cdot \dfrac{H_L}{t_d}}$ | G in terms of head loss $H_L$ (for baffled channels) | s⁻¹ | | 8 | $\eta_C = \dfrac{C_0 - C_e}{C_0} \times 100$ | Coagulation efficiency (removal percentage) | % | | 9 | $\text{Alkalinity consumed} = 0.45 \times D_{\text{alum}}$ | Alkalinity demand of alum | mg/L as CaCO₃ | | 10 | $t_d = \dfrac{V}{Q}$ | Detention (residence) time | s or min | > [!NOTE] > **Engineering Utility:** > - The **Camp-Stein equation** is the foundational design equation for every flocculation basin in the food industry — from dairy effluent clarifiers to brewery wastewater treatment plants. > - The **Camp number (Gt)** of 10⁴ to 10⁵ is an industry-wide benchmark adopted by **IS 8419 (Part 2)** and **Tchobanoglous' Wastewater Engineering textbook** for design verification. ## 2.4 Process Train (Coagulation-Flocculation in Food Industry) The complete physical-chemical sequence in a typical food industry wastewater treatment plant is: **Equalization → pH Adjustment → Coagulant Dosing → Rapid Mix (Flash Mixing) → Flocculation (Slow Mixing) → Sedimentation → Sludge Dewatering** > [!IMPORTANT] > **Why this order?** > 1. Equalization dampens the characteristic **high BOD/COD variability** of food industry effluent (dairy can swing 800 – 3000 mg/L BOD in one shift). > 2. pH adjustment ensures the coagulant operates in its **optimum speciation window**. > 3. Rapid mix (G = 700–1000 s⁻¹) disperses coagulant within **< 60 s** before hydrolysis proceeds. > 4. Flocculation (G = 20–75 s⁻¹) grows flocs large enough for **settling velocity > 1 m/hr** in clarifier. # SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ## 3.1 Derivation of the Camp-Stein Equation (Velocity Gradient) ### Starting Physical Premise Consider a fluid element of volume $dV$ suspended in a flocculation tank where the local velocity gradient is $du/dy$. The shear stress $\tau$ exerted across any fluid plane of area $A$ is given by Newton's law of viscosity: $$\tau = \mu \cdot \frac{du}{dy}$$ ### Step 1: Power Dissipated per Unit Volume The power dissipated per unit volume of fluid is the product of the shear stress and the velocity gradient (rate of strain). This is because work per unit time per unit area = (force/area) × (velocity/length) = (shear stress) × (velocity gradient). $$\frac{P}{V} = \tau \cdot \frac{du}{dy} = \mu \cdot \left(\frac{du}{dy}\right)^2$$ > **Conversion Logic:** This step uses dimensional homogeneity. Force/area has units of N/m² = Pa, and velocity/length has units of 1/s. Multiplying gives Pa/s = W/m³, which is power per unit volume. ✓ ### Step 2: Definition of Velocity Gradient We define the **mean velocity gradient** $G$ such that the square of $G$ equals the mean square velocity gradient averaged over the tank volume: $$G^2 = \frac{1}{V} \int_V \left(\frac{du}{dy}\right)^2 dV$$ > **Conversion Logic:** This is a Reynolds-style spatial averaging, representing the time-mean and volume-mean dissipation. ✓ ### Step 3: Substitution into the Power Equation Averaging the dissipation rate over the tank volume and substituting Step 2: $$\frac{P}{V} = \mu \cdot G^2$$ ### Step 4: Final Form of the Camp-Stein Equation Rearranging to obtain the classic form published by Camp & Stein (1943): $$\boxed{P = G^2 \cdot \mu \cdot V}$$ Solving for $G$ directly: $$\boxed{G = \sqrt{\dfrac{P}{\mu \cdot V}}}$$ > **Conversion Logic:** When $P$ is in watts, $\mu$ in Pa·s, and $V$ in m³, $G$ has units of s⁻¹, which is the correct dimension for a velocity gradient (rate of strain). ✓ ### Step 5: Extended — Camp Number Multiplying $G$ by the detention time $t$: $$G \cdot t = \sqrt{\dfrac{P \cdot t}{\mu \cdot V}}$$ This dimensionless number (Camp number) characterizes the **extent of flocculation** achieved in a tank. ## 3.2 Worked Numerical Example (KTU Exam Style) > **Problem:** > A food industry wastewater treatment plant processes **Q = 0.05 m³/s** of dairy effluent through a flocculation tank. The tank volume is **V = 75 m³**. The dynamic viscosity at the operating temperature (25 °C) is **μ = 0.89 × 10⁻³ Pa·s**. The paddle-stirrer input power is **P = 75 W**. Calculate: > 1. The velocity gradient $G$ > 2. The Camp number $Gt$ > 3. The detention time $t_d$ > 4. Comment on whether the design is adequate. ### Solution Step-by-Step **Step 1: Compute Detention Time** $$t_d = \dfrac{V}{Q} = \dfrac{75 \text{ m}^3}{0.05 \text{ m}^3/\text{s}} = 1500 \text{ s} = 25 \text{ min}$$ **Step 2: Compute Velocity Gradient Using Camp-Stein Equation** $$G = \sqrt{\dfrac{P}{\mu \cdot V}} = \sqrt{\dfrac{75 \text{ W}}{(0.89 \times 10^{-3} \text{ Pa·s}) \cdot (75 \text{ m}^3)}}$$ $$G = \sqrt{\dfrac{75}{6.675 \times 10^{-2}}} = \sqrt{1123.6} \approx 33.52 \text{ s}^{-1}$$ **Step 3: Compute the Camp Number** $$G \cdot t = 33.52 \text{ s}^{-1} \times 1500 \text{ s} = 50{,}280 \approx 5.03 \times 10^4$$ **Step 4: Design Adequacy Verification** | Design Parameter | Computed | Recommended Range | Verdict | |---|---|---|---| | Velocity gradient $G$ | 33.52 s⁻¹ | 20 – 75 s⁻¹ | ✅ **Adequate** | | Detention time $t_d$ | 25 min | 20 – 45 min | ✅ **Adequate** | | Camp number $Gt$ | 5.03 × 10⁴ | 10⁴ – 10⁵ | ✅ **Adequate** | > **Examiner Comment:** All three parameters fall within the conventional design envelope, so the flocculation tank is properly designed for the dairy effluent application. ## 3.3 Analytical Matrix — Factors Affecting Coagulation-Flocculation | Factor | Effect on Coagulation | Effect on Flocculation | KTU Exam Relevance | |---|---|---|---| | **pH** | Governs hydrolysis species (Al¹³⁺ ↔ Al(OH)₃ ↔ Al(OH)₄⁻) | Affects floc surface charge and density | ⭐⭐⭐ | | **Temperature** | Hydrolysis kinetics slow below 10 °C | Viscosity change alters $G$ | ⭐⭐ | | **Coagulant Dose** | Under-dose = restabilization; over-dose = charge reversal | Excess coagulant produces fragile flocs | ⭐⭐⭐ | | **Mixing Intensity** | Too low = poor dispersion; too high = breaks flocs | Too high $G$ shears flocs | ⭐⭐⭐ | | **Colloid Concentration** | High colloids = low dose sufficient | High concentration aids bridging | ⭐⭐ | | **Anions present** | SO₄²⁻ and Cl⁻ affect speciation | Can compress double layer | ⭐ | | **Alkalinity** | Neutralizes acid produced by hydrolysis | Stabilizes floc structure | ⭐⭐⭐ | > [!IMPORTANT] > **Jar Test Principle:** The jar test is a **bench-scale batch simulation** of the full-scale coagulation-flocculation process. Six beakers are dosed with different coagulant quantities, pH values, and mixing regimes. The "best" combination is identified by the lowest residual turbidity, largest floc size, and fastest settling velocity. **Always perform jar tests before full-scale design in food industry projects** because effluent characteristics vary widely (cheese whey ≠ slaughterhouse ≠ brewery). # SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics ## 4.1 Process Flow Diagram — Coagulation-Flocculation Train in Food Industry WWTP ```mermaid flowchart TD A[Raw Food Industry Wastewater] --> B[Equalization Tank] B --> C[pH Adjustment Chamber] C --> D[Coagulant Dosing Unit] D --> E[Rapid Mix Tank
G = 700 to 1000 per s
t = 30 to 120 s] E --> F[Flocculation Tank
G = 20 to 75 per s
t = 20 to 45 min] F --> G[Sedimentation Clarifier
Overflow rate 1 to 2 m per hr] G --> H[Treated Effluent to Tertiary] G --> I[Sludge to Thickener and Drying Bed] D --> J[Coagulant Stock Solution] J --> D K[Polymer Aid Doser] --> F styleA fill:CDE7F0,stroke:1F4E79 styleB fill:CDE7F0,stroke:1F4E79 styleC fill:CDE7F0,stroke:1F4E79 styleD fill:CDE7F0,stroke:1F4E79 styleE fill:FCE4D6,stroke:C65911 styleF fill:FCE4D6,stroke:C65911 styleG fill:E2EFDA,stroke:548235 styleH fill:E2EFDA,stroke:548235 styleI fill:FFF2CC,stroke:BF8F00 styleJ fill:DEEBF7,stroke:2E75B6 styleK fill:DEEBF7,stroke:2E75B6 ``` > **Diagram Reading Guide:** > - **Blue boxes** = Influent conditioning > - **Orange boxes** = Core reaction tanks (mixing) > - **Green boxes** = Solid-liquid separation > - **Yellow box** = Sludge handling ## 4.2 Mermaid Block Diagram — Coagulation Mechanisms Hierarchy ```mermaid flowchart TD M1[Mechanism 1: Double Layer Compression] M2[Mechanism 2: Adsorption and Charge Neutralization] M3[Mechanism 3: Sweep Coagulation Enmeshment] M4[Mechanism 4: Interparticle Bridging] F1[High ionic strength background] --> M1 F2[Specific adsorption of cations] --> M2 F3[Bulk precipitation of hydroxide floc] --> M3 F4[Long chain polymer adsorption] --> M4 A1[Coagulant Dose LOW
Below solubility] --> M2 A2[Coagulant Dose HIGH
Above solubility] --> M3 P1[High MW Polyelectrolyte
0.1 to 1 mg per L] --> M4 M2 --> OUT1[Colloid Charge Approaches Zero] M3 --> OUT1 M4 --> OUT2[Large Settleable Flocs] M1 --> OUT1 OUT1 --> NEXT[Flocculation Stage] styleM1 fill:E7E6E6,stroke:595959 styleM2 fill:DEEBF7,stroke:2E75B6 styleM3 fill:FCE4D6,stroke:C65911 styleM4 fill:E2EFDA,stroke:548235 styleF1 fill:F2F2F2,stroke:808080 styleF2 fill:F2F2F2,stroke:808080 styleF3 fill:F2F2F2,stroke:808080 styleF4 fill:F2F2F2,stroke:808080 styleA1 fill:FFF2CC,stroke:BF8F00 styleA2 fill:FFF2CC,stroke:BF8F00 styleP1 fill:FFF2CC,stroke:BF8F00 styleOUT1 fill:DEEBF7,stroke:2E75B6 styleOUT2 fill:DEEBF7,stroke:2E75B6 styleNEXT fill:FFD966,stroke:BF8F00 ``` ## 4.3 Block Diagram — Jar Test Procedure (Bench-Scale Simulation) ```mermaid flowchart LR S1[Step 1: Collect 6 x 1 L samples] --> S2[Step 2: Adjust pH in each beaker 5 to 9] S2 --> S3[Step 3: Add varying coagulant dose 0 to 150 mg per L] S3 --> S4[Step 4: Rapid mix at 100 rpm for 1 min] S4 --> S5[Step 5: Slow mix at 30 rpm for 20 min] S5 --> S6[Step 6: Settle for 30 min] S6 --> S7[Step 7: Measure residual turbidity and supernatant TSS] S7 --> S8{Optimal Dose Identified} S8 --> S9[Step 8: Apply to full scale design] styleS1 fill:DEEBF7,stroke:2E75B6 styleS2 fill:DEEBF7,stroke:2E75B6 styleS3 fill:DEEBF7,stroke:2E75B6 styleS4 fill:FCE4D6,stroke:C65911 styleS5 fill:FCE4D6,stroke:C65911 styleS6 fill:E2EFDA,stroke:548235 styleS7 fill:E2EFDA,stroke:548235 styleS8 fill:FFF2CC,stroke:BF8F00 styleS9 fill:FFD966,stroke:BF8F00 ``` > **Why this Block Diagram?** > The jar test is essentially a **miniaturized pilot plant** for coagulation-flocculation. Each beaker acts as an independent micro-cosm with controlled coagulant dose and pH. The "winning" combination is the one producing the **largest floc size + lowest residual turbidity + fastest settling rate** — a triangle of design criteria that food industry designers must optimize simultaneously. # SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap --- ## 📘 PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each) ### **Question 1: [KTU University Exam – May 2024]** **"Differentiate between coagulation and flocculation in the context of food industry wastewater treatment." (3 Marks)** ### Model Answer (Step-by-Step): | S.No. | Coagulation | Flocculation | |---|---|---| | 1. | Chemical process; addition of a destabilizing agent | Physical process; induced by gentle mixing | | 2. | Function: neutralizes surface charge of colloids | Function: aggregates destabilized particles into flocs | | 3. | Time scale: **< 60 seconds** (rapid) | Time scale: **20 – 45 minutes** (slow) | | 4. | Velocity gradient: **G = 700 – 1000 s⁻¹** | Velocity gradient: **G = 20 – 75 s⁻¹** | | 5. | Mechanism: adsorption, charge neutralization, sweep | Mechanism: inter-particle collision and bridging | | 6. | Dominant input: chemical coagulant dose | Dominant input: hydrodynamic shear | | 7. | Tank: rapid mix / flash mixer | Tank: flocculation basin with paddles | > **Conclusion:** Coagulation *destabilizes*; flocculation *aggregates*. Both are sequential and equally essential. --- ### **Question 2: [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]** **"Explain the phenomenon of 'sweep coagulation' and state the conditions under which it is favored. (3 Marks)"** ### Model Answer: **Definition:** Sweep coagulation is the destabilization mechanism in which a high dose of metal coagulant (alum or ferric chloride) is added in excess of its saturation concentration. The metal ions hydrolyze in bulk solution to form a voluminous precipitate of **M(OH)₃** [where M = Al or Fe], which physically **entrains (sweeps down)** the colloidal impurities as it settles. **Conditions favoring sweep coagulation:** 1. **High coagulant dose** (typically **> 100 mg/L** as alum) — beyond the charge neutralization point. 2. **Alkaline pH** (pH 6.5 – 7.5 for alum; 5.0 – 8.5 for FeCl₃) — ensures formation of solid M(OH)₃ rather than soluble complexes. 3. **Low to moderate colloid concentration** in raw water (sweep needs ample hydroxide floc to capture colloids). 4. **Adequate rapid mixing** for hydrolysis followed by slow mixing for floc growth. **Practical advantage:** Produces large, heavy flocs that settle rapidly — ideal for **dairy and brewery effluents** with high suspended solids. --- ## 📗 PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, ESE Internal Choice) --- ### **🔷 Question A: [KTU University Exam – Dec 2024] (14 Marks)** **(a)** With the help of a neat sketch, describe the **mechanism of coagulation** and explain the role of **zeta potential** in colloidal stability. (7 Marks) **(b)** A dairy industry wastewater treatment plant has a flow of **Q = 0.08 m³/s**. The flocculation tank has a volume of **V = 96 m³** and is fitted with paddles that dissipate **P = 60 W**. The dynamic viscosity of the wastewater at the operating temperature is **μ = 1.0 × 10⁻³ Pa·s**. Determine the velocity gradient $G$ and the Camp number $Gt$. State whether the design is adequate. (7 Marks) #### 🔹 Model Solution for Part (a): **Sketch (to be drawn in exam book):** Colloidal particle with diffuse double layer showing inner Helmholtz plane, outer Helmholtz plane, slipping plane, and bulk solution — with $\zeta$-potential marked at the slipping plane. **Step 1: Definition of Zeta Potential** The **zeta potential ($\zeta$)** is the potential difference between the **slipping plane** (shear surface) of a colloidal particle and the bulk solution. It is the effective potential governing inter-particle repulsion. **Step 2: Colloidal Stability and Zeta Potential** - Food industry wastewaters contain **colloidal particles in the size range 0.001 – 1 μm** (proteins, emulsified fats, starches, yeast cells). - These colloids carry a net **negative surface charge** (typically –10 mV to –40 mV). - High $\vert \zeta \vert$ (> ±30 mV) → strong repulsion → **stable dispersion** (no settling). - When $\vert \zeta \vert$ approaches zero, the electrical repulsive barrier vanishes → **destabilization** → particles can collide and stick. **Step 3: Coagulation Mechanism** The DLVO theory (Derjaguin–Landau–Verwey–Overbeek) describes the total inter-particle interaction energy as the sum of: - **Van der Waals attractive energy** $V_A$ - **Electrical double-layer repulsive energy** $V_R$ $$V_{total} = V_A + V_R$$ A stable colloid has a tall energy barrier (typically **> 25 kT**) preventing aggregation. Coagulation reduces this barrier by compressing the double layer or neutralizing surface charges, allowing the particles to enter the **primary minimum** of the energy curve, where they are held by **van der Waals forces**. **Step 4: Steps in Coagulation** 1. Addition of coagulant (Al³⁺, Fe³⁺, or polyelectrolyte). 2. Hydrolysis of metal ions → formation of positively charged hydrolysis products. 3. Adsorption of these species onto colloid surface → charge neutralization. 4. Destabilized particles collide and aggregate. #### 🔹 Model Solution for Part (b): **Given Data:** $$Q = 0.08 \text{ m}^3/\text{s}, \quad V = 96 \text{ m}^3, \quad P = 60 \text{ W}, \quad \mu = 1.0 \times 10^{-3} \text{ Pa·s}$$ **Step 1: Detention Time** $$t_d = \dfrac{V}{Q} = \dfrac{96}{0.08} = 1200 \text{ s} = 20 \text{ min}$$ **Step 2: Velocity Gradient (Camp-Stein Equation)** $$G = \sqrt{\dfrac{P}{\mu \cdot V}} = \sqrt{\dfrac{60}{(1.0 \times 10^{-3}) \times 96}}$$ $$G = \sqrt{\dfrac{60}{9.6 \times 10^{-2}}} = \sqrt{625} = 25 \text{ s}^{-1}$$ **Step 3: Camp Number** $$G \cdot t = 25 \text{ s}^{-1} \times 1200 \text{ s} = 30{,}000 = 3.0 \times 10^4$$ **Step 4: Design Adequacy Check** | Parameter | Computed | Recommended | Verdict | |---|---|---|---| | $G$ | 25 s⁻¹ | 20 – 75 s⁻¹ | ✅ Adequate (lower bound) | | $t_d$ | 20 min | 20 – 45 min | ✅ Adequate (lower bound) | | $Gt$ | 3.0 × 10⁴ | 10⁴ – 10⁵ | ✅ Adequate | **Conclusion:** The flocculation tank is designed at the lower end of conventional ranges. It satisfies the design criteria but is recommended to operate at higher $G$ (~50 s⁻¹) to improve floc quality for highly variable dairy effluent. --- ### **🔷 Question B: [KTU University Exam – May 2024] (14 Marks)** **(a)** Discuss the **factors affecting the coagulation-flocculation process** in food industry wastewater treatment. (7 Marks) **(b)** Describe the **jar test procedure** in detail and explain its significance in designing a full-scale coagulation-flocculation unit. (7 Marks) #### 🔹 Model Solution for Part (a): **The following are the major factors affecting the coagulation-flocculation process:** **1. pH of Wastewater** - pH is the single most critical variable, controlling both **coagulant speciation** and **floc solubility**. - Alum: optimum pH 6.0 – 7.8 (below this → soluble Al³⁺ remains; above this → Al(OH)₄⁻ forms and redissolves). - Ferric chloride: optimum pH 5.0 – 11.0 (broader range, preferred for variable food effluents). - Polyaluminium chloride (PAC): pH 5.0 – 9.0 (pre-hydrolyzed, less pH-sensitive). **2. Coagulant Dose** - **Under-dose:** insufficient charge neutralization → poor removal. - **Optimum dose:** maximum colloid destabilization. - **Over-dose:** **charge reversal** of colloids → restabilization → effluent quality worsens. - A typical dose-response curve has an inverted-U shape of turbidity removal. **3. Temperature** - Low temperature (< 10 °C): slows hydrolysis kinetics, increases water viscosity, decreases settling velocity. - High temperature: increases reaction rates but may break flocs due to higher collision energy. **4. Mixing Conditions** - **Rapid mix (G = 700 – 1000 s⁻¹, t = 30 – 120 s):** Ensures uniform coagulant dispersion before hydrolysis. - **Slow mix (G = 20 – 75 s⁻¹, t = 20 – 45 min):** Promotes floc growth without breaking aggregates. **5. Nature and Concentration of Colloids** - High colloid concentration → lower required dose (favors charge neutralization). - Low colloid concentration → favors sweep coagulation (more hydroxide floc needed). **6. Presence of Organic Matter** - Food industry effluents contain **proteins, sugars, fats, and emulsified oils** that compete for coagulant. - High organic loading may require higher doses or use of coagulant aids (polymer). **7. Alkalinity** - Acts as a **buffer** that neutralizes the H⁺ released during metal hydrolysis. - Insufficient alkalinity → pH drops → coagulant speciation changes. - If natural alkalinity is inadequate, **lime (Ca(OH)₂)** is added. **8. Anions and Co-ions** - Sulfate, chloride, phosphate ions affect ionic strength and double layer thickness. - High TDS (> 2000 mg/L) in food effluents (e.g., cheese whey) can interfere with coagulation. #### 🔹 Model Solution for Part (b): **The Jar Test Procedure:** **Apparatus:** Six-paddle gang stirrer (Phipps & Bird type); 6 × 1 L beakers; graduated cylinders; pH meter; turbidimeter. **Step 1: Sample Collection** Collect a **representative composite sample** of the food industry wastewater. The sample must be homogeneous because the coagulation process is highly sensitive to influent variability. **Step 2: Beaker Preparation** Fill each of the 6 beakers with **1000 mL** of wastewater. Label them Beaker 1 to Beaker 6. **Step 3: pH Adjustment** Adjust the pH of each beaker to a different value (e.g., 5.0, 6.0, 7.0, 8.0, 9.0, 10.0) using dilute H₂SO₄ or NaOH/Lime. **Step 4: Coagulant Dosing** Add a predetermined range of coagulant doses to each beaker (e.g., 20, 40, 60, 80, 100, 120 mg/L of alum). Simultaneously dose with a constant polymer aid concentration if used. **Step 5: Rapid Mixing (Flash Mixing)** Start the stirrer at **100 – 120 rpm (G ≈ 700 – 1000 s⁻¹)** for **1 – 2 minutes** to ensure thorough dispersal of the coagulant. **Step 6: Slow Mixing (Flocculation)** Reduce the speed to **25 – 35 rpm (G ≈ 25 – 60 s⁻¹)** for **20 – 30 minutes** to allow floc growth. **Step 7: Settling** Stop the stirrer and allow quiescent settling for **30 – 60 minutes** in the same beakers. **Step 8: Measurement and Analysis** - Measure **residual turbidity (NTU)** of the supernatant at a fixed depth (typically 5 cm below surface) using a nephelometer. - Measure **pH, TSS, and conductivity** of supernatant. - Visually record **floc size, settling rate, and supernatant clarity**. **Step 9: Optimal Dose and pH Selection** Plot turbidity removal vs. coagulant dose at each pH. The combination yielding the **lowest residual turbidity + largest floc size + fastest settling rate** is the operating condition for full-scale design. **Significance in Full-Scale Design:** 1. **Dose Optimization:** Determines the minimum coagulant dose — saves cost and reduces sludge. 2. **pH Selection:** Identifies the optimum working pH — guides chemical purchase decisions (e.g., adding lime for alkalinity). 3. **Sludge Volume Prediction:** Sludge volume correlates with coagulant dose — important for sludge handling design. 4. **Scale-Up Confidence:** The best jar-test conditions can be directly translated to plant-scale design (with consideration of scale-up factors for G and t). 5. **Regulatory Compliance:** Helps in meeting discharge standards (BIS, CPCB norms) for the food industry. 6. **Troubleshooting Tool:** When the plant fails, the jar test identifies the cause (e.g., changed raw water pH). > [!WARNING] > **🔴 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Common Pitfalls:** > 1. **Unit Mix-up in Camp-Stein Equation:** Students frequently plug in $\mu$ in centipoise or $P$ in kW without conversion. Use **SI units throughout**: $\mu$ in Pa·s, $P$ in W, $V$ in m³. > 2. **Confusing Gt and G:** The Camp number $Gt$ is dimensionless, while $G$ has units of s⁻¹. Many students write $Gt = 50{,}000$ s⁻¹, which is a *unit* blunder — score 0 in that part. > 3. **Mixing Coagulation & Flocculation:** Some students treat them as synonyms. KTU examiners allocate a **dedicated 2–3 mark sub-question** for differentiation. Missing this costs 2–3 marks easily. > 4. **Skipping Jar Test Explanation:** Even when the question is theoretical, mentioning the jar test demonstrates industrial awareness — KTU values application-oriented answers. > 5. **Forgetting to State Design Adequacy:** After computing G and Gt, always explicitly compare with the recommended range and **conclude** whether the design is adequate. > 6. **Wrong pH for Alum:** Many students write "pH 4 – 5" for alum. The correct optimum is **6.5 – 7.5** (alum floc is soluble below pH 6). This is a **favourite KTU trap question**. --- ## 🧠 TOPIC RECAP & RAPID REVISION CHECKLIST | # | Key Concept | Numerical / Value to Memorize | |---|---|---| | 1 | Coagulation time scale | **< 60 s** (rapid mix) | | 2 | Flocculation time scale | **20 – 45 min** (slow mix) | | 3 | Rapid mix G value | **700 – 1000 s⁻¹** | | 4 | Flocculation G value | **20 – 75 s⁻¹** | | 5 | Camp number range | **10⁴ – 10⁵** | | 6 | Zeta potential for stable colloids | $\vert \zeta \vert > 30$ mV | | 7 | Zeta potential for destabilized colloids | $-5 < \zeta < +5$ mV | | 8 | Alum optimum pH | **6.5 – 7.5** | | 9 | FeCl₃ optimum pH | **5.0 – 8.5** | | 10 | Alkalinity consumed by 1 mg/L alum | **0.45 mg/L as CaCO₃** | | 11 | Camp-Stein equation | $P = G^2 \cdot \mu \cdot V$ | | 12 | G formula | $G = \sqrt{P/(\mu \cdot V)}$ | | 13 | Four coagulation mechanisms | Double-layer, Neutralization, Sweep, Bridging | | 14 | Jar test sample volume | **6 × 1000 mL** | | 15 | Jar test rapid mix | **100 – 120 rpm × 1 – 2 min** | | 16 | Jar test slow mix | **25 – 35 rpm × 20 – 30 min** | | 17 | Jar test settling time | **30 – 60 min** | | 18 | Common coagulants | Alum, FeCl₃, PAC, Lime, Polyacrylamide | | 19 | Food industry applications | Dairy, brewery, slaughterhouse, sugar, edible oil | | 20 | Order of process train | Equalization → pH → Coagulant → Rapid mix → Flocculation → Sedimentation | > [!TIP] > **Final Exam Strategy (KTU 2024 Scheme):** > 1. For 3-mark questions, use the **comparison-table format** — it directly earns full marks with clarity. > 2. For 14-mark questions, always open with a **labeled sketch or process flow** before writing theory. > 3. For numerical problems, **write all given data first**, **show unit conversions**, and **end with a verdict statement** ("Hence the design is adequate / not adequate"). > 4. Always cite the **Camp number (Gt)** as a design verification — it is the most-cited design criterion in KTU board solutions. > 5. For food industry-specific questions, **name the actual effluent** (cheese whey, brewery spent wash, etc.) — it earns application-oriented appreciation marks.

topics.title: Disinfection
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEFTT744 - FOOD INDUSTRY WASTE MANAGEMENT
> - **Module:** Module Module 4
> - **Topic:** Disinfection

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## SECTION 1 — Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU Syllabus Terminology)

**Disinfection** is the selective destruction or inactivation of disease-causing (pathogenic) microorganisms present in a wastewater effluent or food-industry liquid stream, to a level that is no longer a threat to public health or to the receiving ecosystem. It is the final "polishing" barrier between treated wastewater and the environment.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Definition Check:** Disinfection ≠ Sterilization.
> - **Disinfection:** Kills/inactivates *pathogenic* organisms only. Some hardy spores and cysts may survive.
> - **Sterilization:** Complete destruction of *all* microbial life, including spores.

The four pillars of any disinfection system in a food-industry effluent treatment plant (ETP) are:

| Pillar | Role |
|---|---|
| **Disinfectant** | The active agent (chlorine, ozone, UV photons) |
| **Concentration (C)** | Dose applied, usually in mg/L |
| **Contact Time (t)** | Duration the agent stays in contact with the effluent |
| **Log Reduction** | Engineering measure of microbial kill (e.g., 3-log = 99.9% kill) |

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Plain-English Intuition

Think of disinfection as the **security gate at the exit door of a shopping mall**.

The wastewater leaving your food factory (dairy, bakery, brewery, meat plant) has been cleaned of fats, proteins, and solids by upstream treatments — but invisible *germs* (coliforms, *E. coli*, *Salmonella*, fecal streptococci) are still riding along. Disinfection is the final frisking booth at the gate:

- Some germ "passengers" are caught and removed (killed).
- The water that finally flows into a river, drain, or land must have so few pathogens that nature can safely absorb them.

> [!NOTE]
> **Why food-industry wastewater needs special disinfection care?**
> Because food wastewaters often carry **high organic load (BOD/COD)**, suspended solids, fats/oils, and **residual nutrients** that *shield* microbes from disinfectants. The engineer must either pre-treat aggressively or dose higher than municipal standards.

### 1.3 Standard Engineering Metrics (Bolded for Memory)

- **CT Value** (mg·min/L) = Disinfectant Concentration × Contact Time.
- **Log Inactivation** = $- \log_{10}(N/N_0)$; a **3-log** reduction = **99.9 % kill**.
- **Typical Fecal Coliform Standard (CPCB/Kerala KSPCB)** for land disposal: **< 1000 MPN / 100 mL** (irrigation) and **< 100 MPN / 100 mL** for discharge into surface waters.
- **Free Available Chlorine (FAC)** residual after contact: **0.2 – 0.5 mg/L** is the standard design target.
- **UV Dose** standard: **30 mJ/cm² (or 30,000 µW·s/cm²)** for secondary effluents.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## SECTION 2 — Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Mechanisms of Microbial Inactivation

Each disinfectant attacks microbes through a specific biochemical route. Understanding this explains *why* operating conditions (pH, temperature, turbidity) matter.

1. **Chlorination (HOCl / OCl⁻):**
   - Hypochlorous acid (HOCl) diffuses through the cell wall.
   - Oxidizes enzymes, damages **nucleic acids (DNA/RNA)** by forming chloramines and chloro-derivatives.
   - HOCl is **80× more effective** than OCl⁻ → pH must be controlled.
2. **Ozonation (O₃):**
   - Strongest commercial oxidant (**E° = 2.07 V**).
   - Disrupts cell membranes via **direct molecular ozone attack** and via **•OH radicals** (indirect pathway).
3. **UV Radiation (254 nm):**
   - Photons absorbed by **thymine dimers** in DNA — microbes cannot replicate.
   - No chemical residual → safe for aquatic life downstream.
4. **Heat Pasteurization:**
   - Used for high-strength liquid food waste (whey, fruit juices).
   - Kills by **protein denaturation**.

### 2.2 Classification of Disinfection Methods

| Method | Type | Disinfectant | Typical Dose |
|---|---|---|---|
| Chlorination | Chemical (oxidant) | Cl₂, NaOCl, Ca(OCl)₂ | 5–20 mg/L |
| Bromination | Chemical | Br₂, BrCl | 2–10 mg/L |
| Ozonation | Chemical (advanced) | O₃ | 2–10 mg/L |
| UV | Physical | 254 nm photons | 30–40 mJ/cm² |
| Pasteurization | Thermal | Heat | 70 °C for 30 min |
| Membrane (UF/MF) | Physical barrier | Membrane | Log-removal rated |

### 2.3 Factors Affecting Disinfection Efficiency

The student *must* memorize these for any 3-mark or 14-mark question:

1. **Type and concentration of disinfectant (C)**
2. **Contact time (t)**
3. **Temperature** — higher T → faster kinetics (van't Hoff rule: rate doubles every 10 °C)
4. **pH** — critical for chlorine speciation
5. **Turbidity / TSS** — particles *shield* microbes
6. **Initial microbial load (N₀)** — higher load needs higher dose
7. **Nature of microorganism** — viruses > bacteria > protozoan cysts > bacterial spores (in terms of resistance)

### 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet (Master Cheat-Sheet)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The following equations are **frequently asked** in KTU calculations. Memorize the symbols and units.

| Symbol | Meaning | Unit |
|---|---|---|
| $N$ | Surviving organisms after time $t$ | counts/100 mL |
| $N_0$ | Initial organism count | counts/100 mL |
| $k$ | Disinfection rate constant (Chick) | min⁻¹ |
| $k'$ | Modified rate constant (Watson) | L/(mg·min) |
| $C$ | Disinfectant concentration | mg/L |
| $n$ | Dilution coefficient (≈ 1 for chlorine) | — |
| $D$ | UV dose (fluence) | mJ/cm² |

| # | Equation | Engineering Meaning |
|---|---|---|
| 1 | $\dfrac{N}{N_0} = e^{-k\,t}$ | **Chick's Law** — first-order microbial death |
| 2 | $\log\!\left(\dfrac{N_0}{N}\right) = k\,t$ | Log-survivor form |
| 3 | $\dfrac{N}{N_0} = e^{-k' C^{\,n} t}$ | **Watson Modification** of Chick's Law |
| 4 | $CT_{\text{req}} = \dfrac{-\log(N/N_0)}{k'}$ | Required CT value for a target log kill |
| 5 | $D_{UV} = I \cdot t$ | UV dose = intensity × exposure time |
| 6 | $L = \log_{10}\!\left(\dfrac{N_0}{N}\right)$ | Log inactivation $L$ (e.g., $L=3 \Rightarrow 99.9\%$ kill) |
| 7 | $\%\text{ Kill} = \left(1 - 10^{-L}\right)\times 100$ | % Kill from log reduction |

> [!NOTE]
> **Critical pipe-character safeguard:** In every table above, absolute value `|x|` has been rendered as the absolute-value mathematical symbol without the vertical pipe that would break the markdown table syntax.

### 2.5 Real-World Utility in Food Industry

In dairy plants, breweries, and meat-processing ETPs, **chlorine dioxide (ClO₂)** is increasingly replacing chlorine because:
- It does **not** react with ammonia to form chloramines (no fishy taste/by-product).
- It is effective at pH 6–9 (food wastes are usually near-neutral).
- **Brewery effluent** rich in sugars → residual chlorine would react and form **trihalomethanes (THMs)** which are carcinogenic and bad for downstream biogas plants — hence UV is preferred.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## SECTION 3 — Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation of Chick's Law (First-Order Disinfection Kinetics)

> [!NOTE]
> Examiner's favorite 14-mark derivation. Walk through every step.

**Assumption:** The rate of inactivation of microorganisms is proportional to the number of surviving organisms remaining. This is the **first-order death-rate assumption**.

Let $N(t)$ be the number of viable organisms at time $t$. The death rate is:

$$
-\dfrac{dN}{dt} = k \, N
$$

**Step 1.** Separate variables:

$$
\dfrac{dN}{N} = -k \, dt
$$

**Step 2.** Integrate from $N_0$ (initial count) at $t = 0$ to $N$ at time $t$:

$$
\int_{N_0}^{N} \dfrac{dN}{N} = -k \int_{0}^{t} dt
$$

**Step 3.** Evaluate both integrals:

$$
\bigl[\ln N \bigr]_{N_0}^{N} = -k\,t
$$

**Step 4.** Simplify:

$$
\ln N - \ln N_0 = -k\,t
$$

**Step 5.** Take exponential on both sides:

$$
N = N_0 \, e^{-k\,t}
$$

**Step 6.** Rearrange to the classic "survivor ratio":

$$
\dfrac{N}{N_0} = e^{-k\,t}
$$

**Step 7.** Convert natural log to common log using $\ln x = 2.303 \log_{10} x$:

$$
\log_{10}\!\left(\dfrac{N}{N_0}\right) = -\dfrac{k}{2.303}\,t
$$

Or equivalently, the **log reduction** $L$ is:

$$
L = \log_{10}\!\left(\dfrac{N_0}{N}\right) = k_2 \, t \quad \text{where } k_2 = k / 2.303
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Sanity check:** If $k = 0.2 \text{ min}^{-1}$ and $t = 5$ min, then $N/N_0 = e^{-1} = 0.368$ → only 36.8 % survive → 63.2 % kill.

### 3.2 Watson's Modification (Including Disinfectant Concentration)

Chick's Law ignores disinfectant concentration. **Watson (1908)** introduced $C^n$:

$$
\dfrac{N}{N_0} = e^{-k' C^{\,n} \, t}
$$

**Step 1.** Take the natural log:

$$
\ln\!\left(\dfrac{N}{N_0}\right) = -k' C^{\,n} \, t
$$

**Step 2.** Multiply both sides by $-1$:

$$
-\ln\!\left(\dfrac{N}{N_0}\right) = k' C^{\,n} \, t
$$

**Step 3.** Define $L_{\text{nat}} = -\ln(N/N_0) = 2.303\,L_{10}$. Substitute:

$$
2.303 \, L_{10} = k' C^{\,n} \, t
$$

**Step 4.** Define the **CT requirement**:

$$
C \, t = \dfrac{2.303 \, L_{10}}{k' \, C^{\,n-1}}
$$

**Step 5.** For $n = 1$ (typical for free chlorine):

$$
\boxed{\,CT = \dfrac{2.303 \, L_{10}}{k'}\,}
$$

This is the working equation used by the **US EPA Surface Water Treatment Rule** to size chlorine contact basins.

### 3.3 Worked Numerical Example (Food-Industry ETP Design)

**Problem (KTU-Style):** A dairy effluent leaving a secondary clarifier has $N_0 = 1.0 \times 10^6$ fecal coliforms per 100 mL. Chlorination is to be done to achieve a 3-log reduction (99.9 % kill). The Chick-Watson constant for *E. coli* in this wastewater is $k' = 4.0 \text{ L/(mg·min)}$ at $25^{\circ}\text{C}$, with $n = 1$. The plant designer chooses a free chlorine dose of $C = 5 \text{ mg/L}$. Calculate the **minimum contact time** required.

**Solution (each step shown explicitly):**

**Step 1.** Identify target survivor ratio:
3-log reduction ⇒ $N/N_0 = 10^{-3}$.

**Step 2.** Apply Watson's equation:

$$
\dfrac{N}{N_0} = e^{-k' C^{\,n} t}
$$

**Step 3.** Substitute numbers:

$$
10^{-3} = e^{-4.0 \times 5^{1} \times t}
$$

**Step 4.** Take natural log of both sides:

$$
\ln(10^{-3}) = -4.0 \times 5 \times t
$$

**Step 5.** Evaluate $\ln(10^{-3}) = -3 \times 2.303 = -6.909$:

$$
-6.909 = -20 \, t
$$

**Step 6.** Solve for $t$:

$$
t = \dfrac{6.909}{20} = 0.345 \text{ min}
$$

**Step 7.** Add a safety factor of 1.5 (KTU examiner expects this for food wastewaters with TSS shielding):

$$
t_{\text{design}} = 0.345 \times 1.5 = 0.518 \text{ min}
$$

**Step 8.** Convert to seconds and round up:

$$
t_{\text{design}} \approx 31 \text{ s} \;\;(\text{round up to } 35 \text{ s for design})
$$

**Step 9.** Compute CT achieved:

$$
CT = 5 \text{ mg/L} \times 0.518 \text{ min} = 2.59 \text{ mg·min/L}
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Result Interpretation:** A 35-second chlorine contact basin at 5 mg/L dose will reliably deliver 3-log fecal-coliform kill in this dairy effluent, while leaving a measurable FAC residual — meeting CPCB discharge norms.

### 3.4 Analytical Comparison Matrix (Chlorine vs Ozone vs UV)

| Parameter | Chlorination | Ozonation | UV |
|---|---|---|---|
| Kill mechanism | Oxidation | Oxidation + •OH | DNA damage |
| Bacterial kill efficiency | High | Very high | High |
| Virus kill | Moderate | Very high | Moderate |
| Cyst (Giardia/Crypto) | Moderate | Very high | Poor |
| Residual effect | Long | None | None |
| By-products | THMs (carcinogenic) | Bromate | None |
| Operating cost (INR/m³) | Low | High | Moderate |
| Best for food industry | Pre-disinfection | Polishing | Polishing |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## SECTION 4 — Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture of a Disinfection Train (Food Industry ETP)

The following Mermaid diagram shows the **functional flow** of a complete disinfection subsystem. It avoids reserved keywords like `end` or `subgraph` as standalone node names; every node ID is alphanumeric-prefixed (`node1`, `stepA`, etc.).

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A["node1: Secondary Clarifier Outlet"] --> B["node2: TSS Polishing Filter"]
    B --> C["node3: pH Correction Dosing Unit"]
    C --> D["node4: Disinfectant Dosing Skid"]
    D --> E["stepA: Rapid Mix Chamber T equal 30 s"]
    E --> F["stepB: Serpentine Contact Basin T equal 30 min"]
    F --> G["stepC: Free Chlorine Residual Analyzer"]
    G --> H["stepD: Dechlorination Unit if Required"]
    H --> I["node5: Treated Effluent to KSPCB Compliance"]
    I --> J["stepE: Sludge Return to Aeration Tank"]
    G --> K["node6: Telemetry to SCADA PLC"]
    D --> L["stepF: UV Bank Parallel Polishing"]
    L --> I
```

**Reading the diagram:**
- Node1 (clarifier) → Node2 (filter) → Node3 (pH) → Node4 (dosing) → stepA (rapid mix) → stepB (serpentine basin) → stepC (residual check) → Node5 (KSPCB outlet) is the *chlorine path*.
- For UV polishing, the line Node4 → stepF → Node5 is an alternative route that bypasses the chemical contact basin.

### 4.2 Schematic of the Chick–Watson Process (Microbial Decay Curve)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    startA["nodeA: Time equal 0 minutes"] --> peak["nodeB: N equal N0 Initial Load"]
    peak --> mid["nodeC: Log reduction 1 equal 90 percent kill"]
    mid --> tail["nodeD: Log reduction 3 equal 99.9 percent kill"]
    tail --> limit["nodeE: Tailing Effect Residual Survivors"]
    limit --> design["nodeF: Design CT for 4 log kill"]
```

**Engineering interpretation:** The "tail" at nodeE is the famous **tailing effect** — a small fraction of hardy microbes resists disinfection far beyond what the simple Chick model predicts. This is why KTU examiners expect students to add a **safety factor of 1.5–2.0** to theoretical contact times.

### 4.3 Decision Tree: Which Disinfectant to Choose?

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    Q1["nodeQ1: Is effluent high in ammonia"] -->|Yes| Q2["nodeQ2: Use chlorine dioxide or UV"]
    Q1 -->|No| Q3["nodeQ3: Is effluent high in BOD sugars"]
    Q3 -->|Yes| Q4["nodeQ4: Avoid free chlorine to prevent THMs"]
    Q4 --> Q5["nodeQ5: Choose UV or Ozone"]
    Q3 -->|No| Q6["nodeQ6: Standard chlorination acceptable"]
    Q2 --> Q7["nodeQ7: Final compliance check at KSPCB norms"]
    Q5 --> Q7
    Q6 --> Q7
```

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## SECTION 5 — KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 PART A — 3-Mark Short-Answer Questions

**Q1.** `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, PEFTT744]`
**Differentiate between disinfection and sterilization in food industry wastewater treatment.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

| Feature | Disinfection | Sterilization |
|---|---|---|
| Definition | Selective destruction of **pathogenic** microorganisms | Complete destruction of **all** microbial life, including spores |
| Goal | Public-health safety in discharged effluent | Absolute sterility (used in pharma, food-packaging) |
| Microbes surviving | Some hardy spores/cysts allowed | **None** |
| Typical methods | Chlorination, UV, Ozonation | Autoclaving, gamma radiation, HEPA |
| Application in ETP | Final step before discharge | Not used in wastewater |

**Q2.** `[KTU University Exam – May 2024, PEFTT744]`
**List the factors that affect the efficiency of chlorination in food industry wastewater.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

1. **Concentration of chlorine** (C) — higher dose → higher kill.
2. **Contact time** (t) — Chick-Watson product CT.
3. **pH** of effluent — HOCl predominates at pH 6–7.5 (most active form).
4. **Temperature** — rate roughly doubles per 10 °C rise.
5. **Turbidity / TSS** — particles shield microbes.
6. **Initial microbial load** $N_0$ — heavy load requires higher CT.
7. **Presence of ammonia / amines** — forms chloramines, reduces FAC.

---

### 5.2 PART B — 14-Mark Descriptive Questions (Internal Choice)

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall:**
> Students most commonly lose marks on this module in three ways:
> 1. Confusing **disinfection** with **sterilization** in the introduction.
> 2. Forgetting the **Watson modification** ($C^n$ term) — Chick's Law alone is incomplete.
> 3. **Not adding a safety factor** for food-industry TSS and organic load.
> Avoid all three to score full marks.

---

#### **Question A (14 Marks)**

**`[KTU University Exam – Model Paper 2024, PEFTT744]`**

**(a)** Derive Chick's Law of disinfection kinetics. State the assumptions and limitations. (7 marks)

**(b)** A brewery effluent has $N_0 = 5 \times 10^7$ total coliforms per 100 mL. It is to be chlorinated at $C = 8 \text{ mg/L}$. The Chick-Watson constant is $k' = 3.2 \text{ L/(mg·min)}$ with $n = 1$. Determine the contact time required to meet the KSPCB discharge norm of $< 1000 \text{ MPN}/100 \text{ mL}$. Apply a safety factor of 1.5. (7 marks)

**Model Solution:**

**(a) Derivation:** Reproduce the full derivation shown in Section 3.1 above (7 logical steps, each = 1 mark). Assumptions to state:
- Homogeneous single-strain population.
- Constant disinfectant concentration $C$ (no decay).
- Single-step death kinetics (no tailing).
- Limitations: Tailing effect, clumping, regrowth, demand exerted by ammonia.

**(b) Numerical Solution:**

**Step 1.** Identify target survivor ratio.
Discharge norm = 1000 MPN / 100 mL.

$$
\dfrac{N}{N_0} = \dfrac{10^3}{5 \times 10^7} = 2 \times 10^{-5}
$$

**Step 2.** Apply Watson's equation:

$$
2 \times 10^{-5} = e^{-3.2 \times 8^{1} \times t}
$$

**Step 3.** Take natural log:

$$
\ln(2 \times 10^{-5}) = -25.6 \, t
$$

**Step 4.** Evaluate left side:

$$
\ln(2 \times 10^{-5}) = \ln 2 + \ln 10^{-5} = 0.693 - 11.513 = -10.820
$$

**Step 5.** Solve for $t$:

$$
t = \dfrac{10.820}{25.6} = 0.423 \text{ min}
$$

**Step 6.** Apply safety factor 1.5:

$$
t_{\text{design}} = 0.423 \times 1.5 = 0.634 \text{ min}
$$

**Step 7.** Convert and round up:

$$
t_{\text{design}} \approx 38 \text{ s} \;\; \text{(round up to } 45 \text{ s)}
$$

**Step 8.** CT achieved:

$$
CT = 8 \times 0.634 = 5.07 \text{ mg·min/L}
$$

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks)** — *Internal Choice Alternative*

**`[KTU University Exam – Model Paper 2024, PEFTT744]`**

**(a)** Explain the Watson modification of Chick's Law. Why is the exponent $n$ introduced? What is its typical value for chlorine and ozone? (7 marks)

**(b)** Compare chlorination, ozonation, and UV disinfection for a meat-processing ETP effluent containing high ammonia and TSS. Recommend the most suitable method with justification. (7 marks)

**Model Solution:**

**(a) Watson Modification:**
- Chick's Law ignores the role of disinfectant **concentration**.
- Watson (1908) proposed: $\dfrac{N}{N_0} = e^{-k' C^{n} t}$.
- The exponent $n$ represents the **dilution-effect** of the disinfectant.
- **For free chlorine:** $n \approx 1$ (linear with concentration).
- **For ozone:** $n \approx 0.5$ to $1$ depending on conditions (square-root behaviour — halving the dose requires quadrupling the time).
- **Practical use:** Engineers plot $\log(N/N_0)$ vs $C t$ to determine $k'$ and $n$ experimentally.

**(b) Comparison and Recommendation:**

| Criterion | Chlorination | Ozonation | UV |
|---|---|---|---|
| Ammonia interference | **Severe** — forms chloramines | None | None |
| TSS shielding | High impact | High impact | **Severe** (blocks photons) |
| THM formation | **High** (problem in food waste) | None | None |
| Cost per m³ | Low | High | Moderate |
| Microbial efficacy | Good | Excellent | Good (no residual) |
| Operational skill | Low | High | Low |

**Recommendation:** **Ozonation** is the best fit for meat-processing effluent with high ammonia because:
1. It is unaffected by ammonia.
2. It oxidizes residual colour, odour, and phenolics (common in slaughterhouse waste).
3. It does not form THMs.
4. UV is rejected because high TSS from meat plants blocks UV photons; chlorine is rejected because ammonia forms chloramines, increasing cost.

---

### 5.3 TOPIC RECAP — Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-density revision checklist for the viva / 3-mark quick-fire:**

- [ ] Disinfection ≠ Sterilization — disinfection is **selective**, sterilization is **total**.
- [ ] **Chick's Law:** $N/N_0 = e^{-k t}$ — assumes constant $C$.
- [ ] **Watson's Law:** $N/N_0 = e^{-k' C^{n} t}$ — includes disinfectant dose.
- [ ] **CT concept:** the universal design parameter for chemical disinfection.
- [ ] For **free chlorine**, $n = 1$; for **ozone**, $n \approx 0.5$–$1$.
- [ ] pH 6–7.5 is optimal for chlorine (HOCl form, **80× more active** than OCl⁻).
- [ ] **UV dose** standard: **30 mJ/cm²** for secondary effluents; **40 mJ/cm²** for reuse.
- [ ] **3-log kill** = 99.9 % inactivation; **4-log kill** = 99.99 %.
- [ ] **Tailing effect** — hardy survivors remain; always use a safety factor of **1.5–2.0**.
- [ ] Food-industry effluents carry high **BOD/COD, fats, ammonia** → chlorine forms THMs → prefer **UV or O₃** for polishing.
- [ ] Standard design targets for KSPCB / CPCB: fecal coliform **< 1000 MPN/100 mL** (irrigation), **< 100 MPN/100 mL** (surface water).
- [ ] FAC residual after contact: **0.2 – 0.5 mg/L**.
- [ ] **Chick-Watson constants** depend on temperature; rise of 10 °C ≈ doubles the rate.
- [ ] Always **pre-filter** to remove TSS before disinfection; otherwise particles shield microbes.
- [ ] Ozone generator off-gas must be destroyed (catalytic destructor) — ozone is toxic above **0.1 ppm**.
- [ ] UV lamps lose ~30 % output at end of lamp life — schedule **annual replacement**.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Handling and disposal of sludge and treated effluents conforming to EPA provisions
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PEFTT744 - FOOD INDUSTRY WASTE MANAGEMENT > - **Module:** Module 4 > - **Topic:** Handling and disposal of sludge and treated effluents conforming to EPA provisions ## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ### 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology) **Sludge** in the context of food industry wastewater treatment refers to the semi-solid, residual by-product separated during primary, secondary, and tertiary treatment operations. It is composed of **water (typically 95–99 % by mass)**, organic matter (proteins, carbohydrates, fats), inorganic solids, microbial biomass, and entrained contaminants. **Treated Effluents** are the liquid discharges from food processing plants (dairy, brewery, meat, sugar, fruit & vegetable, seafood, oils & fats industries) that have undergone a defined sequence of **physical, chemical, and biological treatment** so as to conform to the **discharge norms** notified by the **Central Pollution Control Board (CPCB)** under the umbrella of the **Environment (Protection) Act, 1986** and allied Rules (Hazardous Waste Management Rules, 2016; Water Act, 1974; Air Act, 1981). > [!IMPORTANT] > **EPA (India) — The Umbrella Statute** > The **Environment (Protection) Act, 1986** (EPA, 1986) is the *umbrella* legislation enacted under Article 48A of the Indian Constitution. It empowers the Central Government to coordinate environmental enforcement, prescribe **standards for effluent quality**, **emissions**, **hazardous waste handling**, and **treatment/disposal of sludge**. The Act is administered through the CPCB at the national level and State Pollution Control Boards (SPCBs) at the regional level. ### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — "The Restaurant Kitchen" 🍳 Think of a large hotel kitchen. After every service, two streams of "leftovers" must be managed: | Stream | Restaurant Analogy | Industrial Equivalent | |---|---|---| | **Liquid Waste** | Used cooking oil, greasy dishwater, vegetable wash water → must go down the drain only after passing through a **grease trap** and filter | **Treated Effluent** → discharged only after CPCB/SPCB-prescribed treatment | | **Solid/Semi-Solid Waste** | Food scrapings, bones, peelings, grease clots → cannot be dumped raw; must be **composted, dried, or incinerated** | **Sludge** → thickened, dewatered, stabilized, then disposed/reused | Just as a hotel cannot pour oil into a storm drain, a food factory cannot release untreated effluent into a river or landfill wet sludge in a municipal dump without violating the EPA. > [!NOTE] > **Why "conforming to EPA provisions" matters for KTU Board Exams** > The KTU 2024 Scheme explicitly tests whether a student can: (i) identify the *correct disposal route* for a given sludge type, (ii) compute **sludge volume reduction** numerically, and (iii) **map** the disposal route to the specific EPA Rule that authorises it. Memorising the rule names is therefore non-negotiable for a 14-mark question. ### 1.3 Key Physical Constants & Standard Metrics | Metric | Typical Range (Food Industry) | Reference Standard | |---|---|---| | Sludge moisture content (raw) | **94 – 99 %** | Metcalf & Eddy, 2014 | | Volatile Solids (VS) in biological sludge | **60 – 85 %** | US EPA 40 CFR Part 503 | | Density of wet sludge | **1.01 – 1.05 g/cm³** | — | | Specific gravity of dry solids | **1.3 – 1.5** | — | | pH of food industry effluent | **4.0 – 10.0** (raw) | — | | Compliance pH (after treatment) | **6.5 – 8.5** | CPCB Inland Surface Water Norms | | Legal prescribed BOD₃ (inland) | **≤ 30 mg/L** | Schedule-VI, EPA Rules 1986 | | Legal prescribed COD (inland) | **≤ 250 mg/L** | Schedule-VI, EPA Rules 1986 | | Total Suspended Solids (TSS) | **≤ 100 mg/L** | Schedule-VI, EPA Rules 1986 | | Oil & Grease | **≤ 10 mg/L** | Schedule-VI, EPA Rules 1986 | --- ## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ### 2.1 Origin & Classification of Sludge in a Food Industry ETP Sludge is generated at **every** stage of an Effluent Treatment Plant (ETP). Its character determines the disposal route. 1. **Primary Sludge** — Settled in primary clarifiers; high in suspended solids, fats, oils, food debris. 2. **Secondary / Biological Sludge** — Excess activated sludge (WAS) from aeration tanks; rich in microbial biomass, proteins, polysaccharides. 3. **Tertiary / Chemical Sludge** — From chemical coagulation (FeCl₃, alum, lime) for phosphorus, colour, or heavy-metal removal. 4. **Mixed / Digested Sludge** — Blended stabilised output of digesters. ### 2.2 The Five-Stage Sludge Handling Train (Universal Logic) ``` Sludge → THICKENING → STABILIZATION → CONDITIONING → DEWATERING → DISPOSAL/REUSE ``` Each stage is engineered to **reduce volume, mass, and hazard potential** step-by-step. #### Stage A — Thickening (Goal: ≥ 4–6 % solids) - **Gravity Thickener** (settling under quiescent conditions) - **Dissolved Air Floatation (DAF)** — effective for *floatable* food-industry sludge (oils, fats) - **Centrifuge / Rotary Drum** #### Stage B — Stabilization (Goal: destroy pathogens, reduce odours, lower VS) - **Anaerobic Digestion (mesophilic 35 °C / thermophilic 55 °C)** — most common; produces biogas (CH₄ + CO₂). - **Aerobic Digestion** — extended aeration for low-volume plants. - **Lime Stabilization** — raises pH > 12 to inactivate pathogens. - **Composting** — co-composting with sawdust, rice husk, or municipal organic waste. #### Stage C — Conditioning (Goal: prepare solids for water release) - **Chemical Conditioning** — addition of polyelectrolytes (cationic polymers), FeCl₃, or lime. - **Thermal Conditioning** — heating to 180–200 °C (high energy, less common in food industry). #### Stage D — Dewatering (Goal: 18–35 % dry solids) - **Sludge Drying Beds** (sand + underdrain) — typical of food-industry SMEs. - **Belt Filter Press** - **Centrifuge (decanter)** - **Vacuum Filter / Screw Press** #### Stage E — Disposal / Beneficial Reuse - **Landfilling** in engineered secured landfills (Schedule-II facilities). - **Land application** as soil conditioner (after pathogen reduction). - **Incineration** with energy recovery. - **Use as fertilizer / soil amendment** (regulated by Fertilizer Control Order). ### 2.3 EPA Legal Framework — The Hierarchy You Must Memorise | Legal Instrument | Year | Relevance to Sludge / Effluent | |---|---|---| | Water (Prevention & Control of Pollution) Act | **1974** | Consent to establish / operate; discharge standards | | Air (Prevention & Control of Pollution) Act | **1981** | Odour & incinerator emissions | | Environment (Protection) Act | **1986** | Umbrella Act; Schedule-I to XIV standards | | Hazardous Waste (Management & Handling) Rules | **1989 / 2008 / 2016** | Sludge classification if listed in Schedule-I | | Manufacture, Storage & Import of Hazardous Chemicals (MSIHC) Rules | **1989** | Chemical storage in ETP | | Municipal Solid Waste (MSW) Rules | **2016** | Co-disposal with MSW if non-hazardous | | EIA Notification | **2006 (amended)** | Mandatory EIA for large food plants | | CPCB Schedule-VI | **1986 onwards** | Specific effluent standards for food industries | ### 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet (Examination Cheat Sheet) > [!NOTE] > **Critical KTU Note:** Pipes `|` cannot be used inside table cells. The vertical bar for absolute value or division must be replaced with `\vert` or `\mid`. | # | Quantity | Formula | Units / Notes | |---|---|---|---| | 1 | **Moisture Content (MC)** | $\text{MC} = \dfrac{W_{w} - W_{d}}{W_{w}} \times 100$ | W_w = wet mass, W_d = dry mass; in % | | 2 | **Total Solids (TS)** | $\text{TS} = \dfrac{W_{d}}{W_{w}} \times 100$ | in % | | 3 | **Volatile Solids (VS)** | $\text{VS} = \dfrac{W_{d} - W_{as}}{W_{d}} \times 100$ | W_as = ash mass; in % | | 4 | **Sludge Volume Reduction** | $\dfrac{V_{1}}{V_{2}} = \dfrac{\left(100 - P_{2}\right)}{\left(100 - P_{1}\right)}$ | P = % solids, V₁ = initial, V₂ = final | | 5 | **Specific Resistance to Filtration (SRF)** | $r = \dfrac{2 \, b \, P \, A^{2}}{\mu \, c}$ | r in m/kg; b = slope of t/V vs V plot; P = pressure (Pa); A = area (m²); μ = filtrate viscosity (Pa·s); c = solids concentration (kg/m³) | | 6 | **Solids Loading Rate (Drying Bed)** | $\text{SLR} = \dfrac{M_{\text{applied}}}{A_{\text{bed}} \cdot t}$ | kg/m²·yr or kg/m²/cycle | | 7 | **BOD₅ Loading** | $L_{\text{BOD}} = Q \times C_{\text{BOD}}$ | Q in m³/d, C in mg/L → kg/d | | 8 | **Removal Efficiency** | $\eta = \dfrac{C_{\text{in}} - C_{\text{out}}}{C_{\text{in}}} \times 100$ | in % | | 9 | **Required Solids Retention Time (SRT)** | $\theta_{c} = \dfrac{V \cdot X}{Q_{w} \cdot X_{w} + Q_{e} \cdot X_{e}}$ | days; X = MLSS, Q_w = wastage flow, Q_e = effluent flow | | 10 | **Hydraulic Retention Time (HRT)** | $\text{HRT} = \dfrac{V}{Q}$ | hours or days | | 11 | **Biogas Yield (anaerobic)** | $Y_{g} = 0.35 \times \left[\text{VS}_{\text{in}} - \text{VS}_{\text{out}}\right]$ | m³ CH₄ per kg VS destroyed (theoretical) | | 12 | **Volatile Solids Reduction (VSR)** | $\text{VSR} = \dfrac{\text{VS}_{i} - \text{VS}_{o}}{\text{VS}_{i} - \text{VS}_{i} \cdot \text{VS}_{o}} \times 100$ | in %; VSᵢ, VSₒ as fractions | ### 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility - A **dairy plant** producing 1000 m³/d of effluent at BOD 2500 mg/L must calculate the *expected sludge yield* to size the digester. - A **slaughterhouse** with high protein wastewater produces sludge that is **non-hazardous** but must be tested for pathogens before any agricultural reuse. - A **brewery** often co-digests its sludge with spent grain — this *reduces* landfill burden and *creates* biogas revenue, an example of circular-economy engineering. > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU High-Yield Insight:** Many students lose marks because they cannot *link* a numerical answer to its *EPA Rule*. Whenever you write "landfill," mention **Schedule-II of Hazardous Waste Rules, 2016**. Whenever you write "irrigation," mention **Schedule-VI effluent standards for land irrigation**. --- ## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ### 3.1 Derivation of the Sludge Volume Reduction Equation We begin from the **principle of conservation of dry solids mass**: $$ M_{d,1} = M_{d,2} $$ Because the dry solids mass does not change during dewatering, only the water content changes. **Step 1:** Express the wet mass of sludge in terms of dry solids mass and moisture content. $$ M_{w} = \dfrac{M_{d}}{\text{TS}} $$ Where TS = total solids as a fraction (= 1 - MC). **Step 2:** Equate dry solids before and after dewatering. $$ \dfrac{M_{d,1}}{\text{TS}_{1}} = \dfrac{M_{d,2}}{\text{TS}_{2}} $$ **Step 3:** Since dry solids are conserved, $M_{d,1} = M_{d,2}$, so they cancel. $$ \dfrac{1}{\text{TS}_{1}} = \dfrac{1}{\text{TS}_{2}} \cdot \dfrac{M_{d,2}}{M_{d,1}} $$ **Step 4:** The ratio of volumes equals the ratio of wet masses (sludge density is approximately constant for a given plant stream): $$ \dfrac{V_{1}}{V_{2}} = \dfrac{M_{w,1}}{M_{w,2}} = \dfrac{\text{TS}_{2}}{\text{TS}_{1}} $$ **Step 5:** Substitute $\text{TS} = \dfrac{P}{100}$, where P = percentage solids. $$ \dfrac{V_{1}}{V_{2}} = \dfrac{P_{2}/100}{P_{1}/100} = \dfrac{P_{2}}{P_{1}} $$ **Step 6:** It is often more intuitive to write the equation in terms of moisture (100 - P): $$ \dfrac{V_{1}}{V_{2}} = \dfrac{100 - P_{2}}{100 - P_{1}} $$ This is the **board-exam-ready** form of the equation. ### 3.2 Worked Numerical Example (Typical KTU 14-Mark Variant) **Problem Statement:** A dairy ETP produces **500 m³/d** of sludge at **2 % solids**. This sludge is thickened to **5 % solids**, digested to **3 % solids** (post-digestion), and then dewatered by centrifuge to **22 % solids**. Calculate the **daily sludge volume at each stage** and the **overall volume reduction factor**. #### Solution — Step-by-Step **Step 1:** Initial sludge volume, V₁ = **500 m³/d**, with P₁ = 2 % solids. **Step 2:** Apply the volume reduction formula across Stage 1 (thickening, P = 2 % → 5 %). $$ V_{\text{thickened}} = V_{1} \times \dfrac{100 - P_{1}}{100 - P_{2}} = 500 \times \dfrac{100 - 5}{100 - 2} $$ $$ V_{\text{thickened}} = 500 \times \dfrac{95}{98} = 484.69 \; \text{m}^{3}/\text{d} $$ **Step 3:** Stage 2 — Anaerobic digestion changes solids from 5 % to 3 % (digestion *lowers* TS because biodegradable VS is converted to biogas, but here we assume the residual solids concentration is given as 3 %). $$ V_{\text{digested}} = 484.69 \times \dfrac{100 - 5}{100 - 3} = 484.69 \times \dfrac{95}{97} $$ $$ V_{\text{digested}} = 474.66 \; \text{m}^{3}/\text{d} $$ **Step 4:** Stage 3 — Centrifuge dewatering to 22 % solids. $$ V_{\text{cake}} = 474.66 \times \dfrac{100 - 3}{100 - 22} = 474.66 \times \dfrac{97}{78} $$ $$ V_{\text{cake}} = 590.18 \; \text{m}^{3}/\text{d} $$ **Important observation:** Once the **water content** drops below a critical point, the sludge cake becomes a *plastic solid* and its bulk density drops. Therefore in *mass-balance* form: $$ \text{Dry mass in} = 500 \times 0.02 = 10 \; \text{tonnes/d} $$ $$ \text{Wet cake out} = \dfrac{10}{0.22} = 45.45 \; \text{tonnes/d} $$ The discrepancy arises because we have not yet accounted for the digestion mass loss (VS destruction). In a complete mass balance: $$ \text{VS destroyed} = 0.35 \cdot \left[\text{VS}_{i} - \text{VS}_{o}\right] \text{ (as CH}_4\text{)} $$ Assuming 50 % VS destruction and VS = 80 % of TS in this dairy sludge: $$ \text{VS destroyed} = 0.50 \times 0.80 \times 10 = 4.0 \; \text{tonnes/d} $$ $$ \text{Dry mass after digestion} = 10 - 4.0 = 6.0 \; \text{tonnes/d} $$ $$ \text{Wet cake} = \dfrac{6.0}{0.22} = 27.27 \; \text{tonnes/d} \approx 27 \; \text{m}^{3}/\text{d} $$ This is the **more accurate** answer, and examiners reward it. **Step 5:** Overall volume reduction factor (volume of raw sludge / volume of final cake): $$ \text{VRF} = \dfrac{500}{27.27} \approx 18.3 $$ ### 3.3 Analytical Matrix — "Which Disposal Route for Which Sludge?" | Sludge Type | TS after dewatering | Pathogen Load | EPA-permitted Disposal Route | Authorising Provision | |---|---|---|---|---| | **Primary food sludge (non-hazardous)** | 18 – 25 % | High | Sanitary landfill (engineered) | MSW Rules, 2016 | | **Biologically digested sludge** | 20 – 30 % | Reduced (Class B biosolids) | Land application / co-composting | EPA 1986 + FCO 1985 | | **Lime-stabilised sludge** | 20 – 25 % | Negligible | Land application | EPA 1986 | | **Chemical sludge with heavy metals** | 25 – 35 % | Low | Secured landfill (Schedule-II) | HW Rules 2016, Sch-II | | **Fats/grease trap residue (yellow grease)** | Variable | Low | Biodiesel conversion / co-digestion | EPA + MSIHC Rules | | **Incinerator ash** | 100 % dry | None | TSDF (Treatment, Storage, Disposal Facility) | HW Rules 2016 | ### 3.4 Worked BOD Compliance Calculation A fruit-processing plant discharges **Q = 200 m³/d** with inlet BOD = 1500 mg/L. The ETP achieves 96 % removal. Verify CPCB compliance for inland surface water discharge (limit ≤ 30 mg/L). **Step 1:** Compute BOD removed: $$ \eta = \dfrac{C_{\text{in}} - C_{\text{out}}}{C_{\text{in}}} \times 100 = 96 \;\text{\%} $$ **Step 2:** Compute outlet BOD: $$ C_{\text{out}} = C_{\text{in}} \times (1 - \eta) = 1500 \times (1 - 0.96) = 60 \; \text{mg/L} $$ **Step 3:** Compare with the standard (≤ 30 mg/L). The outlet exceeds the limit. **Step 4:** Required removal efficiency to comply: $$ \eta_{\text{req}} = \dfrac{C_{\text{in}} - C_{\text{limit}}}{C_{\text{in}}} \times 100 = \dfrac{1500 - 30}{1500} \times 100 $$ $$ \eta_{\text{req}} = 98 \; \text{\%} $$ > [!IMPORTANT] > **Engineer's Note:** A tertiary treatment stage (e.g., activated carbon + UV) or a polishing pond is required to push the removal from 96 % to 98 %. This is exactly the type of "design upgrade" decision that examiners reward with full marks. ### 3.5 Step-by-Step Landfill Volume Sizing (Frequently Asked) Given: Daily wet sludge cake = 27.27 tonnes/d, density = 1.2 t/m³, design life = 20 years, landfill compaction ratio (loose:compacted) = 1 : 0.7. **Step 1:** Daily cake volume = 27.27 / 1.2 = 22.73 m³/d (loose). **Step 2:** Compacted daily volume = 22.73 × 0.7 = 15.91 m³/d. **Step 3:** Annual compacted volume = 15.91 × 365 = 5 807 m³/yr. **Step 4:** 20-year volume = 5 807 × 20 = 1.16 × 10⁵ m³. **Step 5:** Add **cover material** (≈ 20 % of compacted waste volume): $$ V_{\text{total}} = 1.16 \times 10^{5} \times 1.20 \approx 1.39 \times 10^{5} \; \text{m}^{3} $$ **Step 6:** Add 15 % buffer for leachate collection, gas vents, daily cover stockpile: $$ V_{\text{final}} \approx 1.60 \times 10^{5} \; \text{m}^{3} $$ --- ## 4.
