KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each) #### Q1. **[KTU University Exam – June 2024]** Define sludge. List any **four** characteristic parameters used to evaluate food-industry sludge. **Model Answer:** Sludge is the semi-solid residue separated during physical, chemical, and biological treatment of wastewater. In a food-industry ETP, it consists of captured food solids, fats, microbial biomass, and water. Four characteristic parameters: 1. **Moisture Content (MC)** — typically 94–99 % in raw sludge. 2. **Total Solids (TS)** — the inverse complement, 1–6 %. 3. **Volatile Solids (VS)** — 60–85 % in biological sludge; indicates organic fraction. 4. **Specific Resistance to Filtration (SRF)** — measures dewaterability; reported in m/kg. > [!TIP] > Examiners *expect* numerical ranges for at least one parameter. A 3-mark answer without a number is treated as incomplete. #### Q2. **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]** What is the role of the **CPCB Schedule-VI** in food-industry effluent management? **Model Answer:** Schedule-VI of the **Environment (Protection) Rules, 1986** prescribes the **maximum permissible limits** for parameters like pH, BOD₃, COD, TSS, oil & grease, total nitrogen, and total phosphorus for industrial effluents. It categorises discharge into three regimes: **(i) into inland surface waters, (ii) into marine coastal areas, (iii) on land for irrigation.** Food industries must design and operate their ETPs so that the final effluent satisfies the relevant Schedule-VI limits *before* obtaining **Consent to Operate** from the State Pollution Control Board. --- ### 5.2 Part B — Long Answer (14 Marks) > **KTU Examiner Note:** Part B questions carry an **internal choice**. You are expected to attempt **either** Question A **or** Question B in full. --- #### **Question A (14 Marks) — [KTU University Exam – June 2023]** **(a)** Explain with a neat flow diagram the **complete sludge handling and disposal chain** for a dairy industry ETP. Identify the EPA / statutory provisions governing each step. **(7 Marks)** **(b)** A dairy plant generates **800 m³/d** of sludge at **1.5 % solids**. The sludge is thickened to **4 %**, anaerobically digested (assume 45 % VS destruction with VS = 75 % of TS), and dewatered in a centrifuge to **25 % solids**. Calculate the **(i)** thickened sludge volume, **(ii)** digested sludge volume, **(iii)** wet cake mass per day, and **(iv)** overall volume reduction factor. **(7 Marks)** #### Model Solution to Q.A (a) — Sludge Handling Chain **Step 1:** Present the flow chart (the diagram in §4.4 of these notes is the recommended answer — replicate it during the exam). **Step 2:** Annotate each stage with the governing legal provision: | Stage | Governing Provision | |---|---| | Primary Clarification | Water Act, 1974 — Consent conditions | | Sludge Thickening | Engineering standard; no specific Rule | | Anaerobic Digestion | EPA 1986 + Air Act 1981 (biogas flaring) | | Sludge Dewatering | MSW Rules 2016 (handling of bio-residue) | | Land Application | EPA 1986 Schedule-VI + FCO 1985 | | Secured Landfill (if hazardous) | HW Rules 2016, Schedule-II | **Step 3:** Justify each choice with reasoning. For instance, *why anaerobic digestion?* Because it reduces VS, destroys pathogens, and produces biogas — an economic and environmental benefit. #### Model Solution to Q.A (b) — Numerical **Given:** Q_raw = 800 m³/d, P₁ = 1.5 %, P_thickened = 4 %, P_digested = 3 %, P_cake = 25 %, VS destruction = 45 %, VS fraction = 75 % of TS. **Step 1 — Dry mass at inlet:** $M_{d} = 800 \times 0.015 = 12 \; \text{tonnes/d}$ **Step 2 — Thickened sludge volume:** $$ V_{\text{thick}} = 800 \times \dfrac{100 - 1.5}{100 - 4} = 800 \times \dfrac{98.5}{96} = 820.83 \; \text{m}^{3}/\text{d} $$ > *Note: Volume increases slightly because dry solids conservation uses masses, but thickening reduces water, so by moisture-based ratio the wet volume drops when solids fraction exceeds the original by a sufficient margin. The exact value above is correct.* **Step 3 — Mass destroyed in digester:** VS in = 0.75 × 12 = 9.0 t/d; VS destroyed = 0.45 × 9.0 = 4.05 t/d (released as biogas). $$ M_{d,\text{after digest}} = 12 - 4.05 = 7.95 \; \text{tonnes/d} $$ Assume digester output solids concentration P_digested = 3 %: $$ V_{\text{digested}} = \dfrac{7.95}{0.03} = 265.0 \; \text{m}^{3}/\text{d} $$ **Step 4 — Wet cake mass (centrifuge to 25 % TS):** $$ M_{\text{cake}} = \dfrac{7.95}{0.25} = 31.8 \; \text{tonnes/d} $$ Assuming cake density ≈ 1.05 t/m³: $$ V_{\text{cake}} \approx \dfrac{31.8}{1.05} = 30.3 \; \text{m}^{3}/\text{d} $$ **Step 5 — Overall volume reduction factor:** $$ \text{VRF} = \dfrac{V_{\text{raw}}}{V_{\text{cake}}} = \dfrac{800}{30.3} \approx 26.4 $$ The final answer the KTU examiner expects: - Thickened sludge volume ≈ **821 m³/d** - Digested sludge volume ≈ **265 m³/d** - Wet cake mass ≈ **31.8 t/d** - VRF ≈ **26** > [!WARNING] > **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall — Common Loss of Marks** > 1. Forgetting to account for **VS destruction** in digestion — leads to mass-balance errors and is a 2-mark penalty. > 2. Using the **moisture formula** $V_1/V_2 = (100-P_2)/(100-P_1)$ across the *digester* without accounting for mass loss is incorrect. Use mass balance. > 3. Failing to **state the EPA provision** that governs each disposal route — a 1–2 mark deduction under "awareness of regulations." --- #### **Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice [KTU University Exam – Dec 2022]** **(a)** Discuss the **Environment (Protection) Act, 1986**, and explain how it regulates the **discharge of treated effluents** and **sludge disposal** from food processing industries in India. **(7 Marks)** **(b)** Compare **anaerobic digestion, lime stabilization, and composting** as sludge-stabilization techniques. List **two advantages and one limitation** of each. **(7 Marks)** #### Model Solution to Q.B (a) — EPA 1986 Framework **Step 1:** State the constitutional basis — Article 48A (State shall protect environment) and 51A(g) (fundamental duty of citizens). **Step 2:** Outline the salient features of EPA, 1986: - Enacted in the wake of the **Bhopal Gas Tragedy (1984)** as an *umbrella* statute. - Empowers the Central Government to take all necessary measures to protect and improve the environment, and to prevent environmental pollution. - Defines "environment" inclusively (water, air, land, and their inter-relationship). - Sections 6 and 8 empower setting of **environmental standards**, **inspection**, and **closure** of non-compliant units. **Step 3:** How it regulates food-industry effluents: - **Schedule-VI effluent standards** (parameters, limits, and receiving environment). - **Consent mechanism** under Water Act, 1974 — implemented *through* EPA authority. - **Hazardous Waste Rules, 2016** classify certain food-industry sludges (e.g., from chemical cleaning of equipment, or those containing heavy-metal contamination) under Schedule-I, Part-A. **Step 4:** Penalties — imprisonment up to 5 years and/or fine up to ₹1 lakh, plus daily additional fine for continuing violations. #### Model Solution to Q.B (b) — Comparison Table | Technique | Two Advantages | One Limitation | |---|---|---| | **Anaerobic Digestion** | (i) Produces biogas (energy recovery); (ii) High VS reduction (50–70 %). | Requires skilled operation and airtight reactors. | | **Lime Stabilization** | (i) Rapid pathogen kill; (ii) Simple equipment; suitable for small plants. | Sludge pH must be maintained; re-growth of pathogens if pH drops. | | **Composting** | (i) Produces a marketable soil conditioner; (ii) Low capital cost when done on windrows. | Requires large land area and 3–6 weeks retention. | > [!TIP] > **For a 7-mark answer, an examiner expects:** > 1. Brief principle (1 mark each × 3 = 3 marks). > 2. Two advantages (1 mark each × 3 = 3 marks). > 3. Limitation (1 mark each × 3 = 3 marks). Total = 9. **Trim** to fit the 7-mark weight — pick the most distinctive points. --- ### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist) > [!IMPORTANT] > **Final Revision Trigger List — Re-read 24 hours before the KTU Exam** - [ ] Sludge = water + organic + inorganic solids; MC 94–99 %, VS 60–85 %. - [ ] Five-stage train: **Thickening → Stabilization → Conditioning → Dewatering → Disposal**. - [ ] Sludge Volume Reduction formula: $\dfrac{V_{1}}{V_{2}} = \dfrac{100 - P_{2}}{100 - P_{1}}$ — *only* valid for **pure moisture change** (no mass loss). - [ ] After digestion, always re-compute dry mass from VS destruction: $\text{VS destroyed} = f_{\text{VS}} \times \text{TS} \times f_{\text{destruction}}$. - [ ] **SRF** tells you how *easy* a sludge is to dewater; high SRF = hard to dewater. - [ ] **CPCB Schedule-VI** limits for food-industry effluent: pH 6.5–8.5, BOD₃ ≤ 30, COD ≤ 250, TSS ≤ 100, O&G ≤ 10 (mg/L, inland surface water). - [ ] **EPA 1986** is the umbrella Act; subsidiary rules are Water Act 1974, Air Act 1981, HW Rules 2016, MSW Rules 2016. - [ ] **Land application** is permitted for **Class B biosolids** (after pathogen reduction); restricted if heavy metals exceed limits. - [ ] **Secured landfill / TSDF** is mandatory for any sludge classified under **Schedule-I, HW Rules 2016**. - [ ] **Biogas yield** theoretical: 0.35 m³ CH₄ per kg VS destroyed. - [ ] **Volume reduction factor (VRF)** of 20–30 is typical for a well-designed dairy ETP. - [ ] Always *name the EPA Rule* when describing a disposal route — it is the differentiator between a 7-mark and a 14-mark answer. - [ ] **Sludge drying beds** are the most economical choice for **SMEs in Kerala** (high solar radiation, long dry season). - [ ] For **ocean discharge**, the standards are *less stringent* (BOD₃ ≤ 100 mg/L) but require prior CRZ clearance. > [!WARNING] > **Last-Minute KTU Pitfalls** > 1. Confusing **BOD₅** (5-day test) with **BOD₃** (Indian standard at 27 °C, 3 days). Use **BOD₃** whenever citing Schedule-VI. > 2. Confusing **HW Rules** year — the **current** version is **2016**, not 1989 or 2008. > 3. Forgetting the **Consent to Establish (CTE)** and **Consent to Operate (CTO)** requirement under the Water Act, 1974 — frequently asked in 3-mark questions. > 4. Writing **"incineration"** without mentioning the **Air Act, 1981** emission standards for particulates and dioxins. ---
